actions.thought: AKo is a premium hand, especially in a 6-max game. Being UTG, I want to open for a standard raise of 3x the big blind (300) to build the pot and discourage light callers. This establishes a strong range and allows me to play the hand aggressively or react to 3-bets effectively.
K7o is a weak offsuit hand facing a UTG open (typically a tight range). Not strong enough to call or 3-bet profitably from middle position with several players left to act behind. Easy fold.

I hold 7c 4d in the Cutoff, which is a very weak hand (74 offsuit). Facing an Under-The-Gun (UTG) raise to 300 indicates a strong range from the opponent. Given it is the first hand of the tournament and I have no reads to justify a loose play or a light 3-bet bluff, the standard and most profitable play is to fold. There is no reason to get involved with junk cards against early position aggression.

Preflop on the button with Q6o facing an UTG raise to 300 (3x BB). Q6o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and dominated by better queens or any pair in Flash's likely tight UTG opening range (pairs 66+, AJo+, KQo+). Even in position, the pot odds (calling 300 into 450, needing ~40% equity) are insufficient as Q6o has <35% equity against that range. SB and BB still to act could squeeze or call, increasing risk. Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament with no reads.

Facing an UTG 3bb open from the small blind with 8♣4♠, we’re out of position versus a strong, tight range and this hand has poor playability and domination issues. Calling invites the big blind in and realizes equity poorly; 3-bet bluffing this combo is too low quality without reads. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack.

Facing a single UTG open to 300 with everyone else folded. My hand is K♠6♠ (K6 offsuit), which is marginal but reasonable to defend from the big blind heads-up. The pot is 450 and I need to call 200 more (≈31% equity required), and stacks are deep (~99 BB), so calling to realize equity and keep the pot controllable is the best line. A 3-bet with K6o would be spewy — I’d be OOP with a weak kicker and would bloat the pot early in the tournament. Folding is too tight given the pot odds. Plan: call to 300, see the flop, and proceed cautiously postflop (value/defend when I connect, give up to heavy aggression on boards where I’m likely dominated).

I have Kh6s on Js-9h-Jd — no pair, no real draw (only a backdoor spade). Pot is 650 and there is no bet to face. The preflop opener (UTG) has a fairly strong range and their check here can be either weakness or a trap, but I have very little showdown value and don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a weak hand early in the tournament. With deep stacks and only the first hand, pot control is the priority: check and see a turn card. Plan: check now, fold to meaningful aggression on the flop, and only continue to the turn if it improves my hand (K or 6) or if the opponent gives a clear free card by checking back again.

The flop is Js-9h-Jd, which is a paired board that generally favors my UTG range as the pre-flop aggressor. I hold AK high, which has significant showdown value but can be vulnerable to random floats or small pairs. By placing a continuation bet of 250 (roughly 38% of the pot), I represent a strong range including overpairs (QQ-AA) and Jacks (AJ, KJ, QJ), forcing Minni to fold hands like small pocket pairs or straight draws (like QT or 87) that have equity against me. If called, I have two overcards to improve and can potentially check back the turn.

I checked the flop with Kh6s and planned to fold to meaningful aggression. Flash bets 250 into the ~650 pot (pot now 900), so calling 250 to win 900 requires ~27.8% equity. On Js-9h-Jd I have no pair and only a backdoor spade; UTG's c-bet range is heavy on Jx and strong pocket pairs that dominate me. Out of position, with deep stacks early in the tournament, calling or turning this into a bluff/raise is unjustified. Preserve the stack and fold.
J6o from UTG is a very weak hand. UTG requires a tight opening range, and J6 offsuit is nowhere near playable from this position. Easy fold.

I hold Queen-Six offsuit (Q6o) in Middle Position. This is a statistically weak hand that is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop. With four players left to act behind me, including the button and blinds, opening with this hand carries negative expected value (EV), especially this early in the tournament (Hand 2/150). Standard strategy dictates folding here to preserve chips for better spots.

9h2s is an extremely weak offsuit hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Even in late position (cutoff) after two folds, it has poor playability post-flop and low equity against typical blind defenses or raises. With deep stacks (100BB) early in the tournament (hand 2/150), discipline is key to maximize stack long-term. Folding avoids spew and waits for premium spots.

Folds to me on the button with blinds only in the pot. 9♦7♣ is not a premium but it’s within a profitable button opening range: we have strong positional advantage, immediate fold equity, and reasonable playability when called (can make pairs/straights and apply pressure on many flops). I’ll use a small steal sizing to risk less while still pressuring the blinds. Versus a 3-bet from the more aggressive BB, I’m generally folding this hand.

I have A♥K♠ in the small blind facing a button open to 225 (pot 375, to call 175) with ~96bb effective. This is a premium hand that should be 3-bet for value/isolation rather than just calling out of position. A minimum reraise (350) is too small — it gives opponents good odds to call and often leaves me playing multiway OOP with a hand I want to extract value from. I will 3-bet to ~3x the open (raise to 675). That sizing is big enough to charge speculative hands and weaker Ax/Kx, builds the pot when I have fold equity, and still leaves plenty of stack to play postflop. Plan: if I get isolated heads-up I’ll proceed aggressively OOP; if the BB cold-calls I’ll proceed more cautiously vs multiway; if I face a large 4-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing (fold to shove-sized 4-bets, consider calling moderate 4-bets).

I am in the Big Blind with pocket fours. FiveTwo opened from the Button, and Minni 3-bet from the Small Blind. While pocket fours have set-mining potential, calling a 3-bet out of position against a range that is likely very strong (JJ+, AK, AQ) is generally a losing play. The price to call (575) is high relative to the pot, and the implied odds are not sufficient to justify calling given I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Folding is the safest and most mathematically sound decision here.

We opened the button light, but SB’s 3-bet puts us in a tough spot with 9♦7♣ offsuit. This hand has no blocker value, is frequently dominated when we make a pair, and doesn’t realize equity well versus a 3-bet range (even if Minni is capable of squeezing light). Calling invites difficult postflop decisions and reverse implied odds; 4-bet bluffing is also poor with this combo. Best play is to release and preserve stack for better defend/4-bet candidates (suited connectors, broadways, blocker Ax/Kx).

I am holding 8c 2d in UTG position. This is statistically one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em (offsuit, unconnected, low ranks). Being in early position, I have no positional advantage and face the entire table yet to act. There is no justification for playing this hand. Folding is the only correct strategic option.

Qd6d is a marginal suited hand in middle position with 6-handed play and deep stacks (100BB). Standard MP opening range includes stronger suited hands like QJs+, suited broadways, or better connectors. Q6s is gappy, plays poorly postflop OOP against likely calling/3betting ranges, especially with aggressive opponents like Minni observed in prior hands. Early in tournament (hand 3/150), better to wait for premium spots to build stack without risking unnecessary equity. Pro folded UTG, no action yet; folding preserves stack.

We’re first in from the cutoff after two folds, but J4 suited is still too dominated (crushed by most Jx that continue) and doesn’t play well in multiway pots versus the button and blinds. Limping is weak and invites isolation; raising relies heavily on fold equity and creates tough postflop spots when called/3-bet. With plenty of better steal candidates available, I’ll pass and preserve stack.

I’m on the button with 8♥3♠ — a very weak offsuit hand, but I have position and deep stacks. Rather than limp/calling 100, I’ll open for fold equity and initiative: raise to 300 (≈3x the BB). This size is large enough to shrink defenders’ calling ranges and pick up the blinds often, but small relative to our 100+ BB stack. Plan: fold to any sizeable 3-bet (hand plays poorly vs aggression). If called, proceed cautiously postflop — continuation-bet only on favorable, high-equity or highly favorable textures; otherwise check/fold to significant resistance. Steal attempt with a clear plan to abandon the hand to strong action.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Ten offsuit. The Button (Minni) has opened to 3x the big blind. While KTo is a decent hand, it performs poorly out of position, especially against an active opponent who showed aggression in the previous hand. Calling often leads to difficult post-flop situations or invites a squeeze from the Big Blind. Folding is the most disciplined play early in the tournament to avoid playing a bloated pot with a dominated or marginal hand out of position.
72o is one of the worst hands in poker. Even against a wide button opening range, this hand has poor equity and plays terribly postflop with no connectivity or flush potential. Easy fold.

UTG in a 6-handed game with K9 offsuit. This is a weak hand for early position: king-high with a poor kicker, offsuit, and vulnerable to domination by better kings (KQ, KT, KJ). Standard play is to fold to preserve stack early in the tournament when deep-stacked (~100BB). No reads justify a limp or raise; pot odds don't warrant calling the 100 into a 150 pot multiway. Wait for stronger holdings.

Tc4o is far too weak to enter from middle position, especially with aggressive players still to act (Minni in CO, Flash on BTN) and two blinds who can defend. Limping creates multiway pots where this hand realizes equity poorly, and opening would be an unnecessary low-equity bluff early in a deep-stacked tournament. Preserve stack for better spots.

5♣7♥ is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability out of position if called or 3‑betted. With 101bb effective and the button + blinds yet to act, raising from the cutoff with 5‑7o will often get isolated by better hands or put me into multiway pots where my hand has low equity. Limping (calling 100) is also undesirable — it invites callers and leaves me facing tougher postflop decisions with little fold equity. Early in the tournament there's no need to gamble marginal spots; preserve the stack and wait for a better opening or a suited connector/broadway. Fold and conserve chips.

Action has folded to me on the button. Ten-Three offsuit is a very weak hand and does not justify an open-raise even from the button position in a 6-max game. I will fold and wait for a better starting hand.

Folded to me in the SB with T6 suited. Heads up against the BB, this is a standard raise spot. Suited connectorish hand has playability, and I can apply pressure to potentially take down the pot. Raising to 3x is standard.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise to 300 from the Small Blind (Claude). The pot odds are 200 to call into a pot of 400 (plus my call makes it 600), requiring 33% equity. In a Blind vs Blind scenario, I have position on the post-flop streets, which is a significant advantage. My hand, Q2s, is speculative but has flush potential and decent raw equity against a wide stealing range from the SB. While a 3x open is larger than a min-raise, folding a suited Queen in position would be over-folding. I will call to see a flop.

I flopped a flush draw with Td6d on Jd9dJs. I have 9 outs to the flush which gives me good equity (~35%). Heads up in position as the preflop aggressor, this is a standard c-bet spot. I'll bet around 1/3 pot to continue my aggression and build the pot with my draw while also potentially taking it down now.

I hold a Queen-high flush draw (Qd2d) on a board of Jd9dJs. The opponent (SB) has c-bet 200 into a pot of 600, giving me 5:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 1000). My equity with the flush draw is approximately 36% by the river and ~18-19% on the next card, which justifies the call based on direct odds alone (requiring 20%). The board is paired, which introduces some full house risk, but a flush is still very strong in a Blind vs Blind scenario. Raising provides little benefit as it folds out bluffs I beat or dominates me against Jx. Calling allows me to realize my equity cheaply and keep his range wide.

Turn gave me a pair of tens to go with my flush draw. I now have a pair + flush draw, giving me strong equity (9 flush outs + maybe some T outs). Board is Jd9dJsTs - pretty coordinated with straight possibilities. I should continue betting as the aggressor to charge draws and build the pot with my decent equity. Betting around 50% pot.

I hold Qd2d on a board of Jd9dJsTs, giving me a massive combo draw: a Queen-high flush draw and an open-ended straight draw (I hold a Q, board has 9-T-J, needing an 8 or K). I have approximately 15 outs (9 diamonds + 3 non-diamond Kings + 3 non-diamond Eights), giving me roughly 30% equity going to the river. The opponent bets 500 into 1000, offering me 3:1 odds (25% required equity). Since my equity exceeds the pot odds, a call is mathematically profitable. Raising is unnecessary as it might isolate me against trips or full houses (given the paired board) and deny me the chance to bluff-catch or realize my equity cheaply in position.

River Qh completed the board: Jd9dJsTsQh. My flush draw bricked and I'm left with just a pair of tens. This is a terrible spot to bet - any K makes a straight, any Q has two pair, any J has trips, and 89 has a straight. Pro called both flop and turn, suggesting they have something. Betting would only get called/raised by better hands. Best to check and potentially win at showdown against missed draws.

On the river, the board is [Jd 9d Js Ts Qh]. I have improved to Two Pair (Queens and Jacks) with my [Qd 2d]. However, the board is extremely dangerous, containing four cards to a straight (9-T-J-Q). Any King or Eight makes a straight, and any Jack makes trips. The opponent (SB) has checked to me after barreling the turn. His range likely consists of missed draws (which I beat), trips/straights (which beat me), or marginal made hands he is pot-controlling. Betting here achieves nothing: worse hands like missed flush draws or Ace-high will fold, and better hands like trips or straights will certainly call or raise. I have sufficient showdown value to beat his bluffs, so checking back is the optimal play to realize my equity without risking more chips.

UTG in a 6-max game, 9♢5♣ offsuit is a weak, disconnected hand that plays poorly out of position and is frequently dominated when it makes a pair. Limping invites multiway pots with low equity, and opening would be unnecessary risk for minimal fold equity this early. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack.

6♠2♠ in middle facing an unopened pot — pot 150, to call 100. This is a very weak, low suited hand with poor playability out of position and high chance of being dominated. Raising to 200 is overly loose and will often get 3‑bet or create multiway pots where 6‑2s has almost no equity; calling 100 is cheap but likely leads to difficult postflop decisions OOP against multiple players. With ~100BB I can wait for a much better spot, so folding preserves my stack and avoids negative expected‑value situations.

I am in the Cutoff position with Jd 8h. Two players have folded before me. While the Cutoff is a good position to open-raise and steal the blinds, J8o is a weak hand that does not play well post-flop and is generally outside the standard opening range for this position in a 6-max game. With three players behind me, including active players like Claude and Pro, it is more prudent to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot.
JTo on the button with everyone folding to me. This is a clear steal opportunity with a solid hand. Standard button open to 2.5x.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 6h, facing a raise to 250 from Claude on the Button. This is a weak off-suit one-gapper. Playing this hand out of position against a button raiser is generally a losing proposition due to lack of playability and equity. The cost to call is 200 chips, but the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make folding the correct decision.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 250 after folds around the table and SB fold. My hand is 2d Ts, offsuit with poor connectivity and low cards, one of the worst possible hands with minimal playability or showdown value. Pot odds are 400:150 (~2.67:1 or ~27% equity needed), but 2To has poor equity against a typical button opening range (~35-50% hands), likely <25% vs. that range and dominated by better tens or twos. Early in tournament with deep stacks (99BB), no need to speculate with trash. SB dead money isn't enough to justify calling. Folding preserves stack.

UTG with QQ — a premium hand that should be played aggressively to build value and avoid multiway pots. Open-raising to 3x the big blind (300) is a standard sizing here: it gives fold equity, isolates worse hands (AQ/KQ/medium pairs), and keeps the pot/manoeuvring room with ~101BB effective stacks. If I face a small/standard 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (consider a 4-bet for value vs looser opponents or call to play postflop against tighter 3-bettors); if met with a large, polarizing 3-bet I’ll proceed cautiously. For now: raise to 300.

I am in Middle Position with Ace-Deuce suited. Minni, who is Under The Gun (UTG), has opened with a 3x raise. While A2s has nut-flush potential and straight possibilities, it is significantly behind a standard UTG opening range, which usually consists of strong pairs and high Broadway cards. Calling here is risky because it invites a squeeze from the four players left to act behind me and often leads to being dominated or playing a multi-way pot with a weak kicker. Given that it is early in the tournament (Hand 6), I will choose the disciplined route and fold, looking for better spots to commit chips.

JTd is a solid suited connector in the cutoff. Minni opened from UTG which typically indicates strength, but JTs plays well in position. Calling to see a flop with good implied odds for straights and flushes. 3-betting is an option but calling keeps the pot manageable and allows us to play postflop with position.

I hold 5d 9s on the Button. This is a very weak unsuited hand (trash). The action started with Minni raising to 300 from UTG, which generally signifies a strong range, followed by a call from Claude in the Cutoff. Although I have position, my hand has very poor equity and playability against two opponents, one of whom showed significant strength. The pot odds do not justify a call with this holding, and a bluff squeeze is ill-advised this early with zero equity backup. The standard play is to fold.

Preflop in SB with 2c9d, a complete trash hand with no playability. Minni raised UTG (tight range), Claude flat-called from CO (decent range). Pot odds are 250 to call into 750 (~3:1), but OOP postflop with this hand, I'll realize very little equity and be crushed by villain ranges. FiveTwo still to act in BB. Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament.

We’re getting a good price closing the action in the BB (200 to win 750) and A3s has solid playability: nut-flush potential, wheel/straight equity, and an ace blocker. A squeeze to ~1300–1500 is possible as a bluff, but versus an UTG 3x open plus a caller we’ll get continued by stronger ranges and end up OOP with a marginal ace too often. Prefer taking the pot odds and realizing equity multiway, playing cautiously when we make one-pair hands due to the weak kicker.

Multiway (UTG raiser + CO caller) on K♥ T♥ 6♦, our A♠3♠ has essentially no made equity and only weak backdoors (runner-runner spades / very remote wheel runouts). This is a texture where UTG has a strong range advantage and will c-bet a lot; leading would get called/raised too often and burns chips OOP. Best is to check and evaluate versus sizing (mostly fold to meaningful action).

I opened UTG with QQ preflop (standard) but the flop K-T-6 (two hearts) is unfavorable: my queens are now second pair behind any K and I don’t block heart draws. I raised into callers, so this is effectively a multiway/raised-pot texture where c-betting thin for value/protection is unattractive — I’m often either ahead of weak pairs/floaters or behind Kx and strong holdings. With ~98BB stacks I want pot control and to let opponents show aggression. Check and evaluate: call small bets to see a turn, fold to large, and consider betting later if checked to or if I improve (or get a good read).

I flopped middle pair (tens) with a good kicker and a backdoor diamond flush draw. Both opponents checked to me on a Kh-Th-6d board with a heart flush draw present. Minni opened from UTG but checked, suggesting they may have missed or have a medium-strength hand. I should bet for value and protection against the flush draws. Betting around half pot (475-500) is appropriate.

Claude’s 1/3-pot stab into two players is often wide, but our A♠3♠ has essentially no real continuation equity here: no pair, no gutshot, and only a runner-runner spade flush (board has no spade). Calling would mostly be hoping to bink an ace (often dominated) or run perfectly, creating poor implied/reverse-implied odds multiway with Minni still in. This is a spot to defend with hands that have immediate equity (pairs, Kx/Tx/6x, heart draws, straight draws), not naked A-high backdoors.

Call 475. I opened UTG with QQ and checked a K-T-6 (two hearts) flop to control the pot. Claude (CO) called preflop and now makes a small ~1/3-pot flop bet; his range includes Kx, some tens/middle pairs, heart draws and bluffs. I am behind any King, so a raise would be thin and likely build a pot I’m often losing; calling is cheap relative to my ~98BB stack, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and allows me to re-evaluate on the turn. Plan: call now, fold to heavy turn aggression or a King unless I improve; if he checks the turn I’ll consider a value bet against tens/underpairs.

I have QQ on Th-6d-Kh-7h — a vulnerable second pair (any K beats me, and the turn completes a 3-heart board). Claude’s flop raise put him on a range of Kx, Tx, heart draws, medium pairs or bluffs; his checking the turn looks like pot control or weakness. I want to extract value from tens, sevens, underpairs and charge heart floats/draws while keeping the pot manageable, so I’ll bet about half the pot (950). This sizing is big enough to get called by worse hands and charge draws but small enough that I can fold to a significant check‑raise (I’ll re-evaluate on the river if called).

Minni check-called flop then leads turn for half-pot when the 7h completes the heart flush. This is a classic "made my hand" line - UTG opener who was passive now betting into the aggressor. My JdTd is just middle pair with no heart and no real draw. Against a flush I have only 2 outs (another ten for trips). Against Kx I have ~5 outs. Getting 3:1 pot odds but my equity is likely under 15% here. The turn card is a terrible one for my hand - flush completes and I have no backdoor draws. This is a clear fold.

I am holding a small pocket pair (22) in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. In a 6-max game, 22 is at the very bottom of a raising range and is often a fold from early position because it is difficult to play post-flop out of position. Given the aggressive tendencies shown by players like Minni and Claude in previous hands, I prefer to fold here rather than risk a 3-bet or play a bloated pot out of position with a hand that almost always needs to hit a set to continue.
8s Td is a weak offsuit hand in middle position with 4 players still to act behind me. Not a profitable open-raise spot - folding is standard here.

I am in the Cutoff position with Qs 2s. While the Cutoff offers an opportunity to steal the blinds, Q2 suited is a very marginal hand with poor playability post-flop due to the weak kicker. Furthermore, the Big Blind (Minni) has shown aggression in previous hands, increasing the likelihood of a 3-bet or a difficult defense. Given it is early in the tournament (Hand 7/150) and I have a healthy stack, there is no need to force a loose steal attempt here. Folding is the prudent play.

Folded to me on the button with pocket 44, a solid hand with good playability. Standard open-raise for value and to potentially steal blinds. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is a good size: builds the pot when ahead, maintains fold equity against blinds, and keeps us committed appropriately with deep stacks. Position is excellent for post-flop play if called.

Facing a button open from the small blind with 3♠4♥, we’re out of position with a very weak, unconnected offsuit hand that performs poorly postflop and realizes equity badly. The pot odds aren’t sufficient to justify a defend given positional disadvantage and the big blind still to act, and a 3-bet bluff here has no blockers and risks getting called/4-bet too often. Best is to release and preserve stack.

Elon opened to 300 from the button — a standard 3bb open. I have 6h7s (offsuit connector) in the big blind with a 11.2k stack (deep). Folding is too tight — my hand has reasonable equity vs a wide BTN opening range and the immediate pot odds (200 into 450 — ~31% break-even) justify a call. A 3-bet here would be high-variance and ill-advised with this marginal hand out of position, so I’ll defend and realize equity. Plan postflop: proceed cautiously OOP — continue with pairs/draws, check-fold versus heavy action when unconnected.

Check. I defended the big blind with 6♥7♠ preflop and the flop J♥ 3♥ 8♥ gives me an open‑ended straight draw (5 or 9) plus one heart in hand (a four‑card heart situation). That’s roughly 15 combined outs to a straight/flush (≈32% to hit the turn, ≈54% by the river). I’m out of position versus a BTN open, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal draw by leading. Checking lets me realize equity and see how Elon reacts. Plan: call a standard small–to–medium continuation bet to chase the draws, but fold to a large check‑raise or overbet since I’m still drawing and OOP. For now I’ll check and evaluate on the turn.

Pocket 44 is an underpair on a highly coordinated, monotone flop (Jh 3h 8h) which screams flush draws and straight draws (like 456, 57, 25, etc.). As preflop raiser with position, Minni's check could represent weakness, a draw, or a trap with a made hand. Betting risks folding out better equity realizations or getting raised off by draws or better hands. Checking maintains pot control with a marginal holding that has some showdown value but poor playability if we hit turns/rivers. Position allows us to realize equity effectively on later streets. Deep stacks (95BB effective) favor checking to avoid bloating the pot OOP for opponent. Standard play: check back.

I hold 6♥7♠ on J♥ 3♥ 8♥ 9♠. After the turn I still have an open‑ended straight draw (need a 5 or T) — 8 outs ≈ 8/46 ≈ 17.4% to hit the river. The pot is 650 and there is no bet to me. Checking lets me see a free river, but betting a small amount as a semi‑bluff makes sense: it wins the pot immediately some of the time and builds a pot I can win when I hit. A small bet (~100 chips, ~15% pot) is ideal here — it buys fold equity and is cheap relative to the pot. Even if Elon always calls, the math is favorable (with ~17% equity a 100‑chip bet into 650 is +EV), and if he folds I pick up the entire 650. Plan: bet 100 as a semi‑bluff; fold to a sizeable check‑raise (my draw plays poorly OOP against heavy aggression); if called I’ll evaluate the river and only invest further when I improve or get correct pot‑odds.

Pocket 44 remains an underpair on a draw-heavy board (Jh3h8h9s) with possible straight draws (e.g., 456, 57) and lingering flush draws. Preflop: raised as button standard. Flop: checked back for pot control given opponent's OOP position and board texture. Turn: Minni's small 100 bet into 750 pot (13% pot) suggests possible weakness (draw bluff, thin value like Jx or 9x), protection, or slowplay, but unlikely nuts due to bet sizing. Position allows cheap realization of equity (pot odds ~7.5:1 direct, better with implied). 44 has ~30-40% equity vs typical BB defense range hitting draws/pairs. Folding too tight; raising risks bloating pot OOP or isolating better; call controls pot, sees river cheaply, and exploits position to extract if whiff.

I paired my 6 on the river (6♥ in hand + 6♠ on board) but it’s only a weak one-pair on J♥ 3♥ 8♥ 9♠ 6♠. Elon’s line (BTN open preflop, check-check on a three-heart flop, then check-call my small 100 turn bet) looks like range-heavy with at least some showdown value (Jx, 9x, pocket pairs or cautious top-pair hands) rather than pure air. Very few worse hands will call a river bet for value, and a bluff is thin because he already called my turn semi-bluff — he’s unlikely to fold most of the hands that beat a pair of sixes. Pot is 850 and my stack is deep, so preserving chips and seeing showdown is the highest EV line here. I’ll check and evaluate a river bet from him (fold to large aggression, consider calling small wagers).

Pocket 44 on this river board (Jh3h8h9s6s) is a pair that likely holds up against Minni's OOP limped range after checking flop, small turn bet (100 into ~650, thin value/protection/bluff), and now checking river. No straights possible (board lacks connectors for any 5-card straight: gaps between 3-6-8-9-J), heart flush draws bricked, spade flush unlikely. Villain's line suggests Jx (weak top), middle pair like 8/9/6x (unlikely turn bet small without), Ax high, or missed draws turning into bluff-catchers. Preflop limp + OOP play indicates wide range without premium. We have position, checked flop for pot control (as preflop raiser), called turn sizing gives excellent pot odds and implied. Now checked to, thin value bet exploits: ~30% thinner range (folds worse), builds pot when ahead (~60-70% equity vs calling range). Half-pot bet 425 sizes well: fold equity vs bluffs/draws, gets called by 1-2% better (e.g., weak J), deep effective stacks (95BB) support. Checking risks free showdown (villain may have showdown value), missing value. Tournament stage early (hand 7/150), ICM neutral, maximize EV by betting.

Pot is 1,275 and I must call 425 (calling puts 425 into a 1,700 final pot) — so I need ~25% equity to make a call profitable. I have a weak one-pair (pair of sixes, 6♥) with a 7 kicker. Elon's line (BTN open preflop, check on a three‑heart flop, call my small 100 turn bet, now a half‑pot river stab) is weighted toward showdownable value (Jx, 9x, 8x, some turned straights like 7–10) but will still contain some bluffs/missed overcards. Given the price I’m getting, and that my hand beats bluffs and some worse pairs/underpairs (and I block some straight combos by holding the 7), calling is the lowest-variance +EV play. Raising OOP with this thin pair is negative — I’ll get called by better and fold out only worse; folding here is too tight given the pot odds. I will call the 425 and go to showdown (but fold to a large shove beyond a reasonable river raise).
AQo is a premium hand in UTG. Standard open raise to 250-300 is appropriate. I'll raise to 300 (3x BB) to build the pot and potentially isolate or take it down preflop.

I am in Middle Position facing a 3x UTG raise from Claude. My hand is KJo (King-Jack offsuit). Even in a 6-handed game, KJo plays poorly against a standard UTG opening range, which likely consists of hands that dominate me (AK, AQ, KQ, AJ, JJ+). Calling leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from players behind and puts me in a spot with reverse implied odds post-flop. 3-betting is too loose against a UTG opener. The most disciplined and profitable play here is to fold.

Preflop, facing a UTG raise to 300 from Claude (stack 7975, previous raises suggest decent range like 77+, AJo+, ATs+). My hand Qc8h is offsuit, queen-high, disconnected, and dominated by typical UTG calling/raising ranges. Position in cutoff is good for speculative hands, but Q8o lacks equity (dominated by QTo+, A8s etc.), playability, and implied odds aren't strong enough early in tournament (hand 8/150). Calling risks 300 into 450 pot without strong postflop potential; 3-betting is unprofitable with this hand. Standard fold to preserve stack against likely strong range.

Pocket 3s plays well in position versus an UTG open: we can set-mine profitably given ~80bb effective with Claude and can also realize equity well on many runouts. A 3-bet here is low EV because UTG’s range is strong and 33 doesn’t block continues; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets us potentially win a big one when we flop a set, while folding would be too tight for the price and position.

I’m in the small blind facing an UTG open to 300 (3bb) with the button calling. I hold 3♠–T♣: an offsuit, poorly-connected hand with no suit or meaningful blockers. Calling 250 into a 750 pot requires ~25% equity, but against an UTG opening range plus a caller my realized equity will be well below that and I’ll be out of position postflop. A 3-bet here would be thin and unnecessary with this hand. With a healthy deep stack early in the tournament, the correct play is to preserve chips and fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Eight offsuit. Claude raised from UTG and FiveTwo called from the button. The pot is currently 750 (Claude 300 + FiveTwo 300 + Minni 50 + my 100 BB) and it costs me 200 more to call. This gives me pot odds of 3.75:1. While A8o is a marginal hand and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the price is favorable enough to justify a call and see a flop. I should play cautiously post-flop as an UTG raiser often has a range that dominates mine.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-high on a King-high board. Claude raised from UTG pre-flop, which strongly suggests they have a King or a high pocket pair in their range. This board doesn't hit my hand at all, and I have no draws. My best play is to check and see how the pre-flop aggressor (Claude) and the other player (FiveTwo) react. Leading out here would be too risky against an UTG range.

I raised preflop with AQo and got called by FiveTwo and Flash. Flop is K42 rainbow - very dry board. Flash checked. I have two overcards and no pair, but this is a great spot for a c-bet. The K-high dry board favors my preflop raising range, and a continuation bet will often take this down. Betting around 40% pot should work well here.

Claude’s c-bet is small (~30% pot) into two players, which can still contain a meaningful amount of unpaired broadways (AQ/AJ/QJ) in addition to Kx and medium pairs. With 33 we’re weak but not dead: we beat his unpaired hands right now, have ~8% to spike a set by the river, and we have position to realize our equity and potentially pick up the pot when he slows down on many turns. Raising doesn’t make sense (we don’t block Kx and will get continued on a lot), and folding is a bit too tight given the price and positional advantage. Plan: call once, fold to significant turn barrels on bad cards; consider stabbing when checked to on later streets.

Claude's UTG raise followed by a continuation bet on a King-high flop indicates a strong range, likely containing top pair or better. FiveTwo's call further suggests that my Ace-high is currently the worst hand. With no significant draws and being out of position, there is no reason to continue.

I have AQ high with no pair on K427. FiveTwo called my flop c-bet, suggesting they likely have a King or a pocket pair. The turn 7h doesn't help me or change the board texture much. Barreling again without improvement is risky - if called, I'm likely behind. Checking to pot control and potentially see a cheap river makes sense here.

Claude’s turn check after c-betting small multiway is a strong signal of range weakness (lots of AQ/AJ/QJ-type hands and some medium pairs trying to pot-control). With 33 we have very little showdown value and don’t want to give a free river that lets his overcards realize equity. In position we can credibly represent Kx/sets and pick up the pot immediately a high % of the time. I’ll take the stab with a solid sizing that pressures his floats and some of his 55–99 region; if he check-raises, we simply fold.

I have AQ high on K427. When I checked turn showing weakness, FiveTwo bet 1000 into 1750. Their line (call preflop, call c-bet on K-high flop, now betting turn) strongly suggests Kx or a pocket pair. With only Ace high, I have maybe 6 outs at best (3 aces, 3 queens) if they have a pair below K, or just 3 outs vs Kx. That's roughly 12-15% equity at best. Pot odds are 1000/2750 = 36% needed. This is a clear fold - calling is -EV and I have no showdown value.

I am in UTG position with 7d 5c, which is an off-suit one-gapper. This is a very weak hand to play from early position in a 6-max game. With five players left to act behind me, the likelihood of someone having a better hand is high, and this hand plays poorly post-flop. The standard and correct play is to fold.

8h3s is a complete trash hand: offsuit, low cards, poor playability, no straight or flush potential worth risking early. In middle position preflop with no action ahead except UTG fold, standard play is to fold to preserve stack in a deepstack tournament (hand 9/150). No reads justify entering with this. Pot is small (150), but risking 100+ for equity is -EV.

We’re first in from the cutoff with 9♠4♠ offsuit (very poor playability and domination risk). While CO opens can be wide in 6-max, this hand performs badly postflop versus blind defend ranges and doesn’t realize equity well. With deep stacks and no special ICM pressure, there’s no need to force a thin steal spot—better to preserve chips for higher-EV opens.

Folded to me on the button with J♦4♦. This is a hand worth opening in position: suited jack with backdoor flush/straight potential and strong positional advantage vs the blinds. Table has been using ~300 opens, so I’ll raise to 300 (standard 3xBB-ish) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot controllable with deep stacks. Plan: if either blind 3-bets big I’ll fold; if they call I’ll play postflop in position—c-bet favorable boards, exercise caution/dismiss equity-chasing on dry, high-card boards.

I am in the Small Blind with 4h 5d, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised to 3x the big blind from the Button. Calling out of position with a hand this weak is a losing play long-term, and raising is too risky against an active player. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Q2o is a weak hand. Facing a button raise from Minni (who has been active), I'm getting 2.25:1 odds needing ~31% equity. Q2o doesn't have enough equity against even a wide button range, and it plays poorly postflop. Easy fold.

UTG in a 6-handed game with T8o offsuit is a standard fold. This hand has poor playability, low equity against calling ranges, and is dominated by better tens or eights. No steals or limps from others yet; pot has blinds only (150 total, to call 100). Early in tournament (hand 10/150), stacks deep (~90BB), so play tight from early position to avoid tough spots OOP. Folding preserves stack for premium spots.

Unopened pot and we’re in middle position with 5c2d, a very weak offsuit hand that plays poorly postflop (dominated, low equity, hard to realize). With tougher positions behind (CO/BTN and blinds), limping is -EV and opening is too loose here. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Cutoff open with Qh Jc is standard — QJ offsuit is a playable, reasonably strong hand in position with good broadway and straight potential. Stacks are deep (~123 BB) and the table has a history of 300 standard opens, so I want a sized raise that applies pressure and folds out the blinds often while keeping multiway pots manageable. I will open-raise to 300 (3bb): this gains fold equity, leverages position versus Button/Blinds, and keeps options postflop. If I get a single call I'll continue to c-bet favorable boards; if faced with a large, polar 3-bet I will likely fold, and I can call small 3-bets to realize equity.

I am on the button with King-Deuce offsuit. Minni opened for 300 from the cutoff. While I have positional advantage, K2o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop and is likely dominated by Minni's opening range. Folding is the correct move here.

Holding 4s6s in the SB facing a 3x raise from Minni in the cutoff. This is a weak suited gapper that plays poorly out of position. While suited, 46s doesn't have great implied odds or playability against a likely stronger range. Better to fold and wait for a stronger spot.

I am in the Big Blind with As6h. Minni raises to 300 from the Cutoff. Minni has been very active, opening frequently from late position. Against a wide Cutoff opening range, A6o has roughly 55% equity. The pot odds require me to call 200 to win a total pot of 650 (approx 31% equity needed). While A6o plays poorly out of position, the price and the width of the opponent's range make a fold too tight. I will call and evaluate the flop, playing cautiously out of position.

I have Ace-high on a dry J-8-3 rainbow board. While my hand has some showdown value against a wide range, I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) is generally not effective here as it folds out worse hands and builds a pot against better ones. The standard play is to check and evaluate the opponent's action. If they check back, I realize equity; if they bet, I can decide based on sizing.

I have top pair (J) with a good Q kicker on a very dry 8-3-J rainbow flop. We're heads-up versus the big blind who checked; his calling range is wide (worse Jx, mid pairs, some overcards and speculative hands). I want to extract value and deny free cards to A/K/overcards while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. A half‑pot c-bet (~325 into 650) achieves that: it gets value from worse jacks and pairs, makes some bluffs and floats pay to continue, and keeps options for the turn. If I face a large check‑raise I will re-evaluate (big check‑raises tend to indicate sets/strong two-pair), but I'll call smaller raises to realize equity and proceed cautiously on later streets.

I am out of position with Ace-high (As6h) on a dry J-8-3 rainbow board. The opponent, Minni, is aggressive and the chip leader, betting half-pot (325 into 650). While my hand beats pure bluffs, I have very low playability on future streets, no flush draws, and weak backdoor straight potential. I would need to realize at least 25% equity, which is difficult out of position against a range that includes many Jacks, pairs, and better Aces. Floating here usually leads to folding on the turn to a second barrel. Conserving chips is the better long-term strategy.

UTG in 6-max with ~104bb, K7 suited is a bit too loose for a full-ring-of-position disadvantage spot. It’s dominated by the Kx (K8s+, KTo+) and better suited kings/aces that will continue vs an open, and we’ll realize equity poorly out of position versus capable, aggressive opponents. With no dead money beyond the blinds and plenty of better open candidates, I’ll pass and preserve stack for higher-EV spots.

5♣6♦ offsuit is a weak, low, disconnected hand — easily dominated by most hands that will call or raise behind. I’m in middle position with several players to act (including an aggressive cutoff/button and the blinds), so calling 100 would likely create a multiway pot with poor equity, and opening to 200 is too loose and invites 3-bets. With deep stacks I can wait for a better spot or a suited connector; preserve chips and fold here.

I am holding pocket Jacks in the Cutoff position. This is a premium starting hand, especially in a 6-max format. Since the action has folded to me, I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand. This sizing is consistent with the table's general opening trend observed in previous hands.

I have pocket 5s on the button facing a 3x raise from the cutoff. This is a good spot to call and set mine with position. I have implied odds if I hit a set, and position allows me to play postflop effectively. 3-betting is an option but calling is cleaner with this hand.

I hold 7c Jh in the Small Blind, facing a raise from the Cutoff and a call from the Button. J7 offsuit is a very weak hand, particularly to play out of position against two opponents. Although the pot odds are reasonable (calling 250 to win 750), the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make this a clear fold. I do not want to play a multi-way pot from the worst position with a hand that is easily dominated.

T6s is a suited connector, a strong speculative hand for multiway pots with deep stacks. Facing a CO raise to 3bb and BTN call after folds, the opener's range is relatively wide (Cutoff opens frequently based on prior hands). Getting excellent pot odds: 200 to call into 750 (3.75:1, ~21% equity needed), and T6s has good implied odds, playability, and equity realization OOP. Folding is too tight in BB defense; 3-betting risks OOP vs two players with marginal hand strength. Calling keeps pot multiway, sets up check-raises or floats postflop.

Flop: Th Ts 4d gives me top set with Tc6c (trips), an absolute monster, very likely the nuts on this paired board (only beaten by unlikely quads or a better 10x). Multiway pot OOP with deep stacks. First to act: checking disguises strength perfectly, induces action from Flash's frequent c-betting range (seen in prior hands like #1, #4) and Claude's button calling range (draws, floats, weaker pairs). Sets up a check-raise for maximum value, potentially stacking off if they have a 10 or overpair. Betting risks scaring out their ranges; check maximizes EV by trapping.

I have an overpair (Jacks) on a paired board of 10s and 4s. This is a very strong position. Since Elon checked to me, I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot (450 chips) to extract value from weaker pairs (99-55) or high-card hands like AQ/KQ, and to protect my hand against potential overcards on the turn.

I have pocket 5s on a T-T-4 board. Flash bet 450 into a 950 pot. My underpair has very little value here - I'm behind any ten, any overpair (66+), and only have 2 outs to improve to a set. Flash raised preflop and is c-betting this board, suggesting strength. With poor equity and no real draws, folding is the correct play. Chasing a 2-outer isn't worth the investment.

Top set of tens on a paired board (Th Ts 4d) is an absolute monster, very likely the best hand against Flash's cutoff opening and c-bet range, which includes overpairs (QQ+), weaker tens (AT, KT, etc.), Ax, draws, and bluffs based on his aggressive history (frequent raises and c-bets in hands like #1, #4, #10). Checking pre-action disguised strength perfectly, inducing this bet from his ~50-70% polarized range. Now check-raising for maximum value: extracts from his value hands that call (overpairs, tens), floats, and even some bluffs that shove. Pot is 1400 facing 450 (pot odds irrelevant for nuts). Raising to 2000 (~4.4x his bet, ~1.4x pot) balances value and protection—too small gets called by weak hands but risks cheap turns/rivers; pot-sized might fold too much. Deep stacks (mine 8725, his 9100) allow stacking off if he jams with worse. Calling is too passive OOP, risks free cards or him shutting down; folding impossible.

I have an overpair with pocket Jacks on a paired board of 10-10-4. While Elon's large check-raise is polarizing and often represents a Ten (trips), folding here would be too exploitative and tight, as I only lose to a Ten or the unlikely pocket Fours. I block Jack-Ten, reducing the combinations of trips he might have. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me, so I will call to see the turn and evaluate his aggression on later streets, keeping his potential bluffs or smaller pairs in the pot.

Top set of tens remains an extremely strong hand on Th Ts 4d Qs board, likely still best against Flash's range after his loose call of my flop check-raise (which polarizes my range to sets, two-pair+, strong draws). His CO opening + flop call range includes Tx (AT/KT/JT weaker than mine), overpairs (QQ+/KK, though QQ improved), Qx hits, stubborn floats/draws that bricked. Turn Qs adds straight draws (e.g., KJ, J9 for QJT), spade flush draws (Ts Qs), and pairs queens, but doesn't complete many flush draws yet. OOP heads-up, pot 4950 with deep effective stacks (my 6725, his 7550). Betting ~2/3 pot (3300) extracts value from his worse Tx, overpairs, Qx that call flop; protects against draws realizing equity; builds pot for river jam if he calls. Checking risks him checking back weaker hands or betting small into monster; induces less now after flop action showed strength. Sizing balances value/protection without overbetting to fold out calls.

The Queen of Spades is a very problematic card for my pocket Jacks. Elon's massive check-raise on the flop (increasing the bet from 450 to 2000) strongly signaled a Ten or better. By firing a large second barrel of 3300 on the turn, he is committing the majority of his stack, which typically indicates extreme strength (trips, a full house with 44/Q10, or a newly improved Queen). While I have an overpair to the board's tens, I am now losing to any Queen or Ten. Given the strength shown by the Big Blind and the risk of being drawing to only two outs, I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable spot.

J♥T♥ is a strong, playable 6-max UTG opener — suited, connected, good equity and postflop playability. I want to take the initiative, narrow the field, and build a pot where my hand plays well. Table standard open size has been ~300, so I’ll raise to 300 (3xBB) to get fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. Plan: c-bet favorable flops and play for value/head-up; call a reasonable-sized 3-bet to realize equity postflop but fold to very large 3-bets that commit too much of my stack.

I am holding QJo in a middle position. Minni has raised to 300 from UTG. While Minni has been active, an UTG open usually represents a range that dominates QJo (like AQ, KQ, or pocket pairs). Calling or 3-betting here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a marginal hand. Given I recently lost a significant portion of my stack in Hand #11, I should play more conservatively and wait for a stronger opening or a better position to rebuild.
K2o is a weak offsuit hand facing a UTG raise. UTG opening ranges are typically strong, and K2o doesn't have good playability or equity against that range. Easy fold.

I hold Pocket Threes (3c 3s) on the Button facing a standard UTG open to 300 (3BB) from Minni. Minni has been quite active, suggesting her range might not be strictly premium, though UTG opens must be respected. My stack is deep (10,600 chips, ~106 BB), giving me excellent implied odds to set-mine. I am in position, which allows me to control the pot size or extract maximum value if I flop a set. If I miss the flop and face aggression, it is an easy fold. 3-betting is not viable with this hand, and folding is too tight given the positional advantage and stack depth. Therefore, I will flat call.

Facing an UTG raise from Minni, who has been aggressive with frequent preflop raises throughout the session, and a button call from Pro. A9o is a marginal hand out of position in the small blind, especially facing a likely strong range from UTG and potential multiway action with BB to act. The odds to call 250 into 750 (~3:1) are decent, but postflop play OOP multiway with a hand that plays poorly (weak kicker on top pair, vulnerable to domination) is unprofitable long-term. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this early tournament stage to maximize EV over 150 hands.

We’re getting a very good price closing the action: call 200 to win a pot that’ll be 950, needing ~21% equity. Even offsuit A2 has enough raw equity versus an UTG open plus a button flat, and we can sometimes make top pair or wheel potential. A squeeze/3-bet is high-variance and runs into stronger Ax and broadways from UTG, so best is to defend and play postflop cautiously when we make a weak ace.

3-way OOP on 8-7-4 rainbow with A2o. We have a gutshot to the 5 plus some backdoor potential, but our hand has low immediate showdown value and donk-betting multiway is high-variance and likely to get called/raised by UTG’s stronger range and BTN’s floats. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets us realize equity, and we can consider a check-call versus a small c-bet based on sizing and whether BTN continues.

I was the preflop aggressor and this flop is ideal for a c-bet: dry, low and disconnected (8♥7♠4♣) so my raising range is ahead of many caller ranges. I hold J♥T♥ — two overcards plus a gutshot to a 9 (4 outs; ~8.5% to hit on the turn, ~16.5% by the river) and backdoor heart potential — a good semi-bluff. With two callers preflop I want a modest continuation bet to fold out weak Ax/Kx and small pairs, narrow the field, and build a pot where my hand can improve. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (300 into 950). If I get called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue with improving cards (9/J/T/hearts); I’ll give up to a large check-raise and only continue to small or moderate raises depending on stack commitment and reads.

I called pre-flop with Pocket Threes solely to set-mine. The flop comes 8h-7s-4c, meaning I missed my set and now hold a vulnerable underpair. The board texture is somewhat coordinated, connecting well with the Big Blind's calling range (56, 87, etc.) and Minni's UTG opening range likely includes higher pairs or strong overcards. Minni's continuation bet of 300 is standard, but calling here with 33 is not profitable as I have very little equity to improve (2 outs) and will face difficult decisions on almost any turn card. Additionally, the Big Blind is still left to act behind me. The disciplined play is to fold immediately after missing the set.

We’re heads-up now and getting a very good price: call 300 to win a pot of 1550 (needs ~19%). Even though A-high has low showdown value, we have a clean gutshot to the 5 plus some backdoor equity and can occasionally win when Minni is c-betting high cards/overpairs that may slow down on certain turns. Raising here with A2o is too thin/high-variance versus an UTG range that can continue with overpairs, 8x, strong draws, and sets. Best is to realize equity with a call and reassess turns (apply pressure on favorable scare cards, continue on a 5).

Turn brings the Qd, a high card that improves UTG’s barreling/value range (QQ/Qx/overpairs) more than our BB defend. With A2o we still only have a gutshot to the 5 and very little showdown value, so leading is unlikely to fold out better and just bloats a pot where we’re range-disadvantaged. Best is to check, keep our range protected, and decide based on Minni’s sizing (continue versus small bets with correct price, fold versus strong pressure).

Turn Q♦ is a very bad card for my hand. My J♥T♥ lost both the backdoor heart possibility (turn is not a heart) and much of my equity — the Q now improves many hands that called the flop (Qx, KQ, AQ, some two-pair/overpairs) while I’m left with only J/T river outs. FiveTwo checked the turn, which reads as pot control or a marginal but call-down range; a turn bluff here would have thin fold equity. With a small pot relative to effective stacks I’ll control the pot size and see a river card to re-evaluate (bet if I pair or hit a heart, fold to heavy aggression on a blank). Action: check.

River completes some BB straights (6x) but our actual hand is just A-high with no pair and no relevant blockers. After Minni c-bets small multiway and then checks back the Q turn, her range contains a lot of medium-strength hands (99–JJ/8x) plus some overcard air (AK/AJ/KJ) that will often just check back river. By checking we realize our (limited but non-zero) showdown equity versus those missed overcards, and we avoid turning A-high into a bluff that needs a fairly high fold frequency (and risks getting looked up by any pair). If Minni bets, we can fold, but with no bet pending the highest-EV play is to take the free showdown.

I have J♥T♥ and missed my turn and river — no pair, no completed straight or flush. I c-bet the dry 8-7-4 flop and was called; the Q on the turn and the 5 on the river both make it much more likely my opponent has a showdown-worthy holding (pairs like 88/77/44, a Qx that checked for pot control, or even a slow-played made hand). Villain’s line (call flop, check turn, check river) gives very little fold equity on the river — most hands that would fold to a bet already folded on earlier streets, while pairs and Qx will likely call a river bluff. With deep stacks and a mid-size pot (1550), risking chips here for a thin fold is negative EV. I have no value to extract and should preserve my stack for better spots, so I will check to showdown.

I am holding AKo, a premium starting hand, in the Under-the-Gun (UTG) position. Standard strategy dictates an open-raise to build value and narrow the field. Based on previous hands, a 3x opening raise to 300 is consistent with my playstyle and the table dynamic.
32o is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Facing a UTG raise with this trash hand is an easy fold.

I am holding 5d 3d in the Cutoff position facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the UTG player (Flash). Although my hand is suited, it is a low one-gapper and generally too weak to call a UTG open, especially with the Button and Blinds still to act who could squeeze or overcall. The stack depths are deep (over 100 BB), but the pot odds and reverse implied odds against a strong UTG range make calling unprofitable. A 3-bet bluff is also risky against an early position opener. The standard and prudent play is to fold.

Ks6c is a marginal offsuit king-high hand with poor playability and low equity against an UTG opening range, which is typically strong (top 15-20% of hands). Folding from the button avoids tough post-flop decisions out of position post-raise, especially given Flash's history of aggressive opens that have shown strength. Defending with a call risks domination (e.g., by KQ, AK), and 3-betting has poor equity and fold equity potential here. Preserve stack for better spots.

Facing an UTG open from Flash, T4 suited is too weak and dominated to defend profitably out of position from the small blind, especially with the big blind still to act and the likelihood of realizing equity poorly. Not a good candidate for a 3-bet bluff either. Best is to conserve chips and fold.

UTG (Flash) opened to 300 (3bb). I’m on the big blind with A♥5♠ (A5o) and effective stacks are roughly 72bb (Flash 7250) — deep enough that post‑flop play matters. A5o is marginal but worth defending the BB vs a single 3bb open: calling keeps the pot controllable, realizes equity and avoids playing a dominated ace out of position after a 3‑bet. A min‑3bet to 500 is an option, but 3‑betting OOP with this hand is thin value and will put me in uncomfortable spots if called or 4‑bet. Pot odds to call are reasonable (call 200 into a 450 pot → need ≈44% equity). Plan: call to 300 total, see a flop, continue on Ace‑high boards for value, use pot‑control on dry boards and fold to heavy aggression when clearly behind; fold to a shove/large 4‑bet. Given the stack sizes and villain’s open, defending by calling is the most +EV line.

I have A♥5♠ on 9♦9♣7♣ — just ace-high with no backdoor clubs or meaningful draws. Flash was the preflop raiser, so his range contains many hands that already beat me (9x, 7x, overpairs, strong broadways). The board is dry and out of position I should control the pot rather than bluff-c-bet into a likely stronger range. Plan: check to see what he does; fold to substantial aggression, but I can call a very small probe if it looks like a weak continuation bet and the price is right. Checking preserves stack and keeps later decisions straightforward.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with AKo. The flop is 9d-9c-7c. This board is somewhat coordinated but also paired, which generally favors my range (overpairs) even though the Big Blind can have many 9x hands. Minni has checked. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 40% of the pot to extract value from weaker hands, fold out random air, and maintain initiative. If I get raised, I will likely have to fold, but a bet here is standard to see where I stand.

Call 250. I defended the BB preflop with A♥5♠ and on 9♦9♣7♣ I only have ace‑high, but Flash’s flop stab is very small (250 into the ~650 pot pre‑bet — pot is 900 now), so the price to see a turn is cheap (≈22% equity required). Calling keeps the pot controllable, realizes showdown value against missed broadways/bluffs, and preserves the option to fold to sizable turn/river aggression. 3‑betting/raising OOP with a lone Ace is thin and will put me in tough spots, so I’ll call and re‑evaluate on the turn, folding to heavy pressure unless I improve or get good odds to continue.

Turn analysis: I defended the BB with A5o and called the small flop stab to keep the pot controlled. The turn Q♣ is a very ugly card for me — it pairs the board and brings a third club, completing flushes for anyone holding two clubs. Flash opened UTG (a fairly strong range) and checked the turn, which could be either a missed hand checking back or a disguised strong holding. I have only ace‑high with no club and very little showdown value. Betting here as a bluff to fold out Qx/9x/overpairs is thin — UTG’s range contains many hands that now beat me and will call a small lead, and a bet invites a raise I’m folding to anyway. Best line is pot control: check and see the river. Plan on folding to significant river aggression unless I improve or get very cheap odds vs a small probe bet.

The turn brings the Queen of Clubs, which is a dangerous card as it completes a possible flush and provides an overcard to my Ace-King. Minni has checked again after calling my flop continuation bet. With only Ace-high and no clubs in my hand, I have no draw and limited showdown value if the board improves for my opponent. Betting here likely only gets called by better hands (like a Queen, a Nine, or a Flush) and folds out hands I already beat. Checking back allows me to see a free river and control the pot size.

The river makes the board 9♦ 9♣ 7♣ Q♣ 9♥ — I have A♥5♠, just ace‑high with no club. Flash opened UTG and led a tiny c‑bet on the flop then checked the turn, which leaves him with a range that includes many hands that now beat me (Qx or 7x turning into full houses, any 9, or two‑club combos that made a flush). Betting as a bluff is thin — his range is weighted toward hands that will call or raise — and I have no value to extract. Best line is pot control: check and take showdown. If he barrels a very small thin bet I can consider a call for price, but I’ll fold to meaningful river aggression.

The board has tripled up on Nines (9-9-7-Q-9). I have Ace-high (As Kd). Minni has checked the river after calling my flop bet and checking the turn. By checking back, I can reach showdown with Ace-high, which has decent value here. If Minni has a small pocket pair (like 22-66), my Ace kicker is superior to her kicker on the board's trip nines. Betting here would likely only be called by a Queen, a Nine, or a Flush, none of which I beat. There is no merit in turning Ace-King into a bluff on this board texture since I have showdown value against missed draws and lower kickers.

92 offsuit from UTG is a clear fold. This is one of the worst starting hands in poker - no connectivity, no suited potential, and terrible high card value. Playing this from early position would be burning chips.

I am in Middle Position with Ace-Three offsuit (Ah 3c). In a 6-handed game, A3o is too weak to open profitably from this position. It plays poorly post-flop, is easily dominated by better Aces, and faces position disadvantage against the Cutoff and Button. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand from early/middle position.

Cutoff position after UTG and MP folds in a 6-handed game with deep stacks (~100BB). 9s8c is an offsuit connector with straight potential and good playability in position. Standard opening range includes this hand here. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) is a good size: builds pot, shows strength, and allows postflop flexibility. Opponents behind may defend wide but position advantage is key. Limping weak, folding too tight for spot.

Elon opened to 3bb from the cutoff, and we have 5♠4♦ offsuit on the button. This hand is too weak to profitably call versus a reasonably tight CO range at this sizing: it makes dominated pairs/weak two-pairs and has poor playability without suitedness, while we’re not getting a discount (still 3bb to call) and can face squeezes or tough postflop spots. A 3-bet bluff with 54o is also low-quality due to poor blockers and poor equity when called. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Facing a cutoff open to 300 from my small blind with 6♣7♥ (offsuit). Pot is 450 and I need to call 250 (total committed = 300). Stacks are deep (~114 BB), so the 250 call is cheap for a speculative connector with straight potential despite being unsuited and out of position. Pot odds: calling 250 into a 700-pot gives ~35.7% break-even equity. I won’t open-raise from the SB with this weak, offsuit hand (too many postflop position problems) and folding surrenders too much equity for a small price. Plan: call to see a flop, play cautiously OOP — fold to significant aggression if I miss, continue only with strong draws or made hands.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. Elon raised from the Cutoff to 300, and Minni called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting favorable pot odds (200 to call into a 700 chip pot), Q3o is an extremely weak hand that lacks connectivity and flush potential. Playing this hand out of position against two opponents is likely to lead to difficult post-flop situations where I am easily dominated or forced to fold to further aggression. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have top pair (6c) with a 7 kicker on a 6s-5c-5h flop. It's heads-up vs the cutoff opener; the board is paired and fairly dry (no flush or obvious straight draws), so many of his missed overcards will fold. I want a small value/protection bet (~1/3 pot) to charge worse 6x/kicker combos and thin value hands while keeping the pot manageable out of position. If he raises strongly I’ll give credit to sets/two-pair and fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn and proceed cautiously.

Preflop I raised a playable offsuit connector from cutoff with position. Flop 6s5c5h gives me only a weak gutshot straight draw (4 outs to a 7 for 5-6-7-8-9) with no pair, no flush draw, and minimal backdoors. Board is paired fives, low and drawish. Minni, in SB, limped-called preflop (wide defending range possible) and now leads for 250 into 950 (~25% pot), which looks strong: likely a 5x for trips, 65 for two pair, 55 for a set, or a strong draw like 47/34/76 OESD. My range as CO raiser includes more overpairs and better Ax, but 9s8c has poor equity (~20-30% vs her polarized leading range, beating pure bluffs but crushed by value). Pot odds are decent (250 to win 1200, ~17% equity needed immediately), but implied odds poor OOP on paired board with no strong draw or showdown value. Raising semi-bluff risks getting called by better and commits more deepstacked; calling invites trouble on turn where she'll lead again first. Early in tournament (hand 14/150), better to fold marginal equity spots and preserve stack for premium situations. Minni's history shows check-calls and occasional leads with medium strength.

I am in UTG position with Jack-Five offsuit (Jd 5c). This is a very weak hand with poor playability, lacking connectivity and suitedness. From early position, entering the pot with such a hand is a losing strategy long-term. With 103 big blinds, there is no pressure to make desperate moves. The correct and standard play is to fold immediately.

Preflop, unopened pot after UTG fold. In middle position 6-handed with deep stacks (~110BB), K9o is within a standard opening range: strong king blocker, decent high card equity, straight potential, and postflop playability. Players behind are capable of wide ranges (Minni aggressive on button), but initiative and fold equity justify a raise. Standard 3x sizing to 300 matches table norms from prior hands. Folding too tight early; limping bad practice.

MP opens to 3bb and we’re in the CO with K4o. This is a dominated, low-connectivity hand that performs poorly versus an early/middle-position opening range, and it’s not a good candidate to 3-bet bluff (bad blockers, poor playability if called). With ~110bb effective, preserving stack and waiting for better spots is higher EV than defending here.

On the button with 7♦2♥ — essentially the worst starting hand. Elon opened to 300 (standard 3x); pot is 450 and calling 300 with almost no equity is a losing play, and any raise/3-bet with this junk is unjustified (you’ll be out of position against callers or get called by better hands). Preserve stack (11875) and wait for a better spot to leverage position or steal the blinds. Fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace of Clubs, Four of Diamonds. Elon, who has shown significant aggression in previous hands (notably Hand #11), raised to 300 from middle position. While an Ace is tempting, A4 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive opponent. Calling 250 more into a 450 pot puts me in a difficult spot post-flop where I am likely to be dominated by better Aces or forced to fold to continuation bets on most boards. Folding here is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

8-2 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a middle position raise of 300, I need to call 200 more. Even with decent pot odds (~2.25:1), 82o has terrible playability and is easily dominated. This is a clear fold from the BB.

6-handed tournament, deep stacks (~100BB). UTG with T8h suited connector. Marginal hand for opening UTG in 6-max; standard ranges fold T8s here due to poor position and vulnerability to 3-bets/squeezes. Playability is good but OOP post-flop risks are high against aggressive opponents like Minni and Flash who frequently raise/steal. Limping is weak and invites multi-way pots. Prioritizing tight-aggressive play early to maximize stack long-term; folding preserves chips for better spots.

Unopened pot after UTG folds; as MP in 6-max, KTo is strong enough to open for value and initiative. Raising denies blinds a cheap look, leverages position against CO/BTN, and plays fine as a semi-bluff/value hand with good blocker effects (K). Use a standard small open to keep ranges wide while still generating fold equity.

Cutoff with A♦Q♣ vs a MP open to 250 (pot 400). AQs is a strong hand that plays very well in position — I want to take the initiative, isolate the opener, and get some fold equity while keeping worse hands and squeezes in a workable range. I’ll 3-bet to 900 (total) — ~3.6x the open — which is large enough to discourage loose multiway calls but small enough relative to my 11875 stack to leave maneuvering room. If I face a large 4-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to an overcommit), and if I get called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to extract value.

I am holding 5d 3h on the Button. There has been an open raise from FiveTwo to 250 followed by a 3-bet from Minni to 900. 5-3 offsuit is a very weak hand with no playability in this spot. Calling or raising would be a waste of chips. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

J9s is a playable hand, but facing a 3-bet to 900 from Minni while I'm out of position in the SB is a tough spot. I'd need to call 850 into a pot of 1300. J9s plays better in position and doesn't do well against typical 3-bet ranges. Being OOP significantly reduces my ability to realize equity. Disciplined fold here.

I hold 9s 5c in the Big Blind, which is a statistically very weak hand (9-5 offsuit). Facing an open raise to 250 from FiveTwo followed by a substantial 3-bet to 900 from Minni, I am in a terrible position. Calling requires 800 more chips into a pot of 1300, offering poor pot odds (~1.6:1) for a hand that likely has very low equity against the ranges of an opener and a 3-better. Additionally, playing a large pot out of position with a trash hand is a losing strategy. The only correct decision is to fold.

We opened KTo and face a fairly large 3-bet from CO who will have position postflop. KTo is dominated by much of Minni’s value range (AK/KQ/KJ/TT+) and plays poorly out of position; calling invites tough postflop spots with reverse implied odds. A 4-bet bluff is possible with a K blocker, but KTo is too weak/low-equity to choose as a candidate this deep given sizing and likely strength. Best EV is to cut losses and preserve stack.

Kd2c is a very weak offsuit hand that’s heavily dominated by opponents’ calling/3-betting ranges. From UTG in 6-max, opening or limping just invites multiway spots and tough postflop play with poor playability and reverse implied odds. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better opens.

Qc3h is a weak, offsuit hand with poor playability and high likelihood of being dominated by a raiser’s range. The pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call (you'd need ~40% equity to break even), but Q3o vs a normal open has well under that equity and performs poorly multiway or out of position against the Button/Cutoff and the blinds. A 3-bet bluff here is thin given the number of active players and aggressive table tendencies; with a 12275 stack I should preserve chips for better spots. Fold and wait for a stronger, playable hand or position.

I am in the Cutoff position with 9c 4d, which is a very weak starting hand. Both players before me have folded. While there is an opportunity to steal the blinds, 94o is too weak to open with, especially since there are still three players left to act behind me, including two with relatively large stacks. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Button with 32 suited and it's folded to me. This is a prime stealing spot with only the blinds left to act. 32s is weak but suited and has some playability. Standard button steal raise to 2.5x should take it down often enough to be profitable.

I hold Ten-Four offsuit (T4o) in the Small Blind. The player on the Button (Claude) has raised to 250. T4o is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position. Even though the Button's range is wide, my hand has very low equity and it will be difficult to realize that equity post-flop. Calling is losing chips in the long run, and the hand is too weak to 3-bet bluff. The correct play is to fold.

Js7d is a marginal hand in the BB facing a button raise to 2.5x after three folds. While pot odds are about 2.67:1 (needing ~27% equity), J7o has poor postflop playability, low equity against a wide button range (often dominated when hitting top pair), and reverse implied odds. Claude has been aggressive but shown strength in spots. Deep stacks early in tournament; better to wait for stronger spots to defend or speculate. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities.

UTG with 4♣7♠ is a clear fold. This is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand that fares poorly out of position and will be dominated by the ranges likely to act behind me. Calling 100 into a 150 pot would require roughly 40% equity to break even multiway — far more than this hand has — and raising here would be reckless facing five players left to act and several competent deep stacks. Preserve the 12,275-chip stack for better spots and wait for a stronger hand or better position.

I am holding 9h 3s in middle position. This is an extremely weak hand with no significant potential for straights or flushes, and it is easily dominated by almost any calling or raising range. Given the current table dynamics and the fact that I am in middle position, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot. Folding is the standard and correct play here.
A9o in the cutoff with two folds ahead. This is a solid opening hand from this position. Standard raise to 250-300 to pick up blinds or isolate.

I hold Qs 2d on the Button. The Cutoff (Claude) has raised to 250 (2.5 BB). Q2o is a statistically weak hand with poor playability and is dominated by most of the Cutoff's opening range. Despite having position, the hand does not have enough equity to profitably call, nor is it a good candidate for a bluff 3-bet. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Qh3h is a weak suited hand with minimal equity against Claude's cutoff raising range, which includes pairs, broadways, suited aces, and strong suited connectors. Out of position in the small blind, postflop playability is poor. Pot odds are not sufficient to justify calling 200 into 400 (pot will be 600 post-call, BB still to act), as equity realization is low. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities.

Claude is opening the cutoff to 2.5bb, which is a classic steal spot. From the BB I’m getting a very good price (150 to win a pot that will be 550 if I call ≈ 27% needed), and 8♠3♦ has enough raw equity vs a typical CO opening range to justify a defend at some frequency. I don’t want to overfold my blind versus this sizing, and 3-bet bluffing this hand performs poorly and bloats the pot out of position. I’ll call and play a straightforward fit-or-fold / opportunistic stab strategy postflop.

Out of position as the BB versus Claude’s CO open, this flop strongly favors the preflop raiser’s range overall, so I don’t want to start donk-leading too often. With top pair (8x) but a weak kicker, my hand plays best as a bluff-catcher: I can comfortably check and then continue versus a normal c-bet sizing, while keeping the pot from inflating against stronger 8x/overpairs/sets. Checking also protects my range (I can have strong hands like 88/66/82s/86s) and lets Claude barrel bluffs with overcards.

I raised preflop with A9o and FiveTwo called from BB. Flop is 2s 8d 6h - a dry, low board. I missed with A-high but this board likely missed BB's calling range too. As the preflop aggressor, a continuation bet around 1/3 pot should take this down frequently. Standard c-bet spot.

We flopped top pair with a weak kicker (8x) on a fairly dry 2-8-6 rainbow board. Claude’s c-bet is very small (200 into 750), which gives us great immediate odds and keeps his range wide (overcards, some air, and value). Raising here mostly just isolates us versus better 8x, overpairs, and sets, while forcing his bluffs to fold and bloating the pot out of position. Best line is to continue as a bluff-catcher, keep his bluffs in, and re-evaluate on turns based on sizing and texture.

Turn J♦ is a range-improving card for Claude (he has a lot of broadways that pick up a J, plus now some diamond backdoors become real draws).
