With 8♠3♦ we’re still a bluff-catcher with a weak kicker: leading mostly just gets action from better hands (Jx, overpairs, better 8x, sets) while folding out the air/overcards we want to keep in. By checking we keep his range widest, allow him to continue bluffing, and we can comfortably call versus reasonable sizing and re-evaluate river.

Turn brought Jd. I have A-high with no pair and no draw. FiveTwo called my flop c-bet, showing some interest. The J could have connected with their range (Jx suited combos). With just A-high, betting again risks getting called or raised by better hands while folding out very little. Checking back for pot control and showdown value is the prudent play here.

River brings the Tc, which adds more one-pair combinations to Claude’s range (Tx, some two-pair like JT) and doesn’t meaningfully improve our 8♠3♦. After he c-bet small then checked back the J♦ turn, his range contains a lot of give-ups (AK/AQ/KQ type hands) plus some pot-control made hands (Jx/medium pairs). If we lead, we mostly get called/raised by better (Jx/Tx/overpairs/better 8x) and fold out the exact hands we want to keep in (missed overcards). Best is to check and realize our showdown value, prepared to bluff-catch versus a reasonable sizing.

River brought Tc, completing possible straights (Q9, 97). I have A-high with no pair. FiveTwo has checked three streets after calling my preflop and flop bets. Betting here serves no purpose - I can't get called by worse, and I may even lose to small pairs or better high cards. Checking back gives me a chance to win at showdown against missed draws or worse A-high.

I am Under The Gun (UTG) with Ten-Eight offsuit. This is a weak hand that does not meet the requirements for an opening raise from early position in a 6-max game. Playing such hands from UTG often leads to being out of position post-flop with a marginal holding. The optimal strategy here is to fold and wait for a stronger range.

Q9o in middle position with ~67 BBs. This is a borderline hand for opening from MP. Flash folded UTG, so I'm first to act. Q9o has some playability but not strong. I'll make a standard 2.5x open to try to take down the blinds - folding is also reasonable but I've been playing tight and can mix in some opens with suited broadway potential hands.

I hold 8h3c in the Cutoff facing a raise to 250 from Middle Position (Claude). 83o is one of the weakest starting hands in Hold'em with very poor playability and equity. There is no justification for calling or 3-betting this hand, especially with players left to act behind me. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Facing a raise to 250 from Claude in middle position into a 400 pot, with J5o offsuit on the button. This is a very weak hand with poor playability, low equity against a typical raising range, and no strong suited or connected potential. Even in position, calling risks difficult post-flop spots OOP if defended by blinds, and pot odds (400:250 ~1.6:1) don't justify it given the hand's capped range realization. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a tournament setting with deep stacks.

Calling from the small blind tends to realize poorly (we’re out of position, and the big blind can come along), and K8s is too weak to defend profitably as a flat very often. Claude’s 2.5x open looks like a standard steal/iso size and he’s not a huge stack, so a 3-bet pressures a meaningful portion of his range and can win the pot preflop frequently. Kd8d has decent playability when called and blocks some Kx continues. Plan: 3-bet and fold to a 4-bet.

I have 6♣4♠ — a weak, unsuited, unconnected hand with poor post‑flop playability and lots of reverse implied odds. Preflop the action: Claude opened to 250 and FiveTwo 3‑bet to 900, the pot is 1,250 and it costs me 800 to call (min raise to 1,550). Calling 800 into 1,250 requires ~39% equity (800/(1,250+800)≈39%), which 6‑4o does not have vs a 3‑bet/opens range — and if both players stay in my equity is much worse. I’d be out of position, likely face continuation bets, and risk getting stacked with a marginal pair or dominated draws. A squeeze/raise with this hand would be irresponsible. Fold and preserve the healthy stack for better spots.

Facing a 3-bet from SB after my MP open. Q9o is too weak to defend here - I'm out of position with a marginal hand that doesn't play well against a 3-bet range. I'd need roughly 34% equity to call and Q9o won't have that against FiveTwo's 3-bet range. Clear fold.

67o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak offsuit connector with poor playability from the earliest position. With 5 players still to act behind me, the risk of running into premium hands is high, and this hand has poor equity against typical calling/3-betting ranges. Better to wait for a stronger hand.

I am in Middle Position with King-Ten suited (KsTs). This is a strong starting hand that typically falls within the standard opening range for this position in a 6-handed game. My stack is deep (approx. 101 BB), allowing for post-flop play. Although the Small Blind (Minni) has shown aggression with 3-bets, KTs has good equity and playability (flush and straight potential). Limping is generally suboptimal, so I will enter the pot with a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind to build the pot and define ranges.

Preflop in cutoff with ATo offsuit facing a 2.5x open from middle position Pro. ATo is a strong hand with position, excellent ace blocker, and good playability. Pro has been passive postflop, frequently calling preflop but folding to cbets (e.g., Hands 10, 12). This is a standard 3-bet spot to isolate and apply pressure. 3-betting to 800 (~3.2x the open raise) is a good size on deep stacks, building the pot while denying equity to worse aces and floats.

We’re facing an MP open and a CO 3-bet, so ranges are already condensed toward strong holdings. 5♠3♦ has terrible playability (offsuit, no blockers, rarely makes strong/nutted hands) and won’t realize equity well even in position. Cold-calling 800 is too loose with poor implied odds versus a likely strong/linear 3-bet range, and 4-bet bluffing is unattractive because we block nothing (no A/K) and risk bloating a pot against strength. Best decision is to preserve stack and wait for better spots on the button.

8♣3♥ is a junk hand. Pro opened and Elon 3-bet to 800 — calling requires 750 more into a 1,200 pot, so I’d need ~38.5% equity to break even. 8-3 offsuit has effectively no chance vs a typical cutoff 3-bet range and I’ll be out of position the whole hand. A min-4-bet to 1,350 is ludicrous with this holding. Preserve my deep stack for better spots and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Seven offsuit. There has been an open raise from Pro and a 3-bet from Elon to 800 chips. This hand is significantly below the range required to call or 4-bet, especially out of position against two active opponents who have shown strength. Folding is the only viable strategic option to preserve my stack.

I opened to 2.5BB from Middle Position with KsTs, and Elon 3-bet to 8BB from the Cutoff. While KsTs is a strong suited connector, I will be out of position against a potentially strong range (likely TT+, AQ+, AJs, KQs) that dominates my hand (specifically Kx and Tx hands). Calling would require about 31% equity, which I might strictly have against a wider range, but realizing that equity out of position is difficult due to reverse implied odds on King-high boards. Given the tournament stage and stack depth, preserving chips by avoiding marginal spots out of position is the prudent play.

I am in the UTG position with AJo (Ace of Diamonds, Jack of Hearts). In a 6-max game, this is a borderline opening hand. While standard charts often suggest opening, the table dynamics observed over previous hands show frequent 3-betting from players in position (Elon, FiveTwo, Minni). Opening AJo from UTG puts me in a difficult spot if I face a 3-bet, often forcing a fold and wasting chips, or playing a bloated pot out of position with a hand that can be easily dominated. Given the aggression behind me, a disciplined fold is the best exploitative adjustment to preserve my stack for better positional spots.

T3o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability post-flop, low high-card strength, and no suited or connected potential for strong draws. In middle position with 6 players and action folding to me, folding is standard to preserve my deep stack (117 BBs) for premium hands. No need to limp or raise this trash early in the tournament (hand 21/150). Tight-aggressive strategy dictates folding here.

CO is a profitable open spot in general, but K4o is near the bottom of a CO opening range and plays poorly versus 3-bets and squeezes. With Minni on the button showing willingness to 3-bet (and both blinds able to defend), opening creates a lot of tough branches where we have to fold and burn chips. Limping is even worse with the button behind. Best stack-preserving, EV-max line is to just release.

I’m on the button with 2♠7♠ — a weak hand but suited and in prime position to attempt a steal. With everyone folded to me and both blinds having shown they defend but also play aggressively postflop, I’ll make a standard button open to apply pressure and pick up the blinds. Raise to 300 (≈3x BB): big enough to be a credible steal, small enough to avoid bloating the pot with a largely unplayable hand. Plan: fold to a substantial 3-bet (I have no equity as a 4-bet bluff), call if a light cold-call comes from the SB and play cautiously in position — c-bet small on favorable flops, check and control the pot on dry boards, and only pursue when I pick up real equity (pair, good flush/straight draw).

I am in the Small Blind with 8-6 suited. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. While 8-6 suited is a decent speculative hand, playing it out of position against an active Button raiser is generally unprofitable. The pot odds (250 to win a total pot of 450) aren't compelling enough to justify calling and playing the rest of the hand out of position, especially with the Big Blind still to act. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Holding 2c6h in the BB facing a button raise to 300. This is one of the worst starting hands in poker - offsuit, unconnected, and low cards. While I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds to call 200, this hand has terrible playability and will rarely make strong hands. Even defending the BB, this is an easy fold.

UTG with 4s9d offsuit is an extremely weak hand with no high card strength, connectivity, or suitedness. In a 6-handed game, even with deep stacks (117x BB), this is a standard fold from early position to avoid difficult post-flop situations and preserve stack early in the tournament (hand 22/150). No player tendencies from history justify entering with this trash hand.

We’re in MP in a 6-max configuration with two players still to act behind (CO and BTN) plus the blinds. K7 offsuit is too easily dominated by the Kx that continue/3-bet, and it plays poorly postflop (reverse implied odds when we make a weak king). With deep stacks and no need to thin the field here, the highest-EV line is to pass and preserve stack for better open spots (CO/BTN) or stronger holdings.

Cutoff with the best possible starting hand (A♣ A♥). Pot is only 150 and it’s cheap to build the pot and take the initiative. I want to isolate the button/blinds and avoid a multiway pot, so I’ll open-raise to a size that gets reasonable fold equity but is still standard vs this table. A 4x BB open (to 400 total) accomplishes that: it prices out speculative hands, keeps weaker ranges from calling, and sets up heads‑up play where Aces perform best. Plan if 3-bet: I’ll re-evaluate by opponent and sizing — against a small-to-medium 3-bet I’ll 4-bet or shove for value; against a very large 3-bet I may still 4-bet shove or call to get chips in later since AA is far ahead. Folding or just calling here is too passive — raising extracts value and maximizes fold equity.

I am on the button with Jack-Eight offsuit. Minni, who has been quite active in previous hands, has raised to 400 from the cutoff. While I have the benefit of position, J8o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by a standard cutoff opening range (which includes hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, and JT). Given the 4x raise size and the lack of playability of this hand post-flop, folding is the most disciplined and correct play to preserve my stack.

K8 suited in the SB facing a cutoff raise to 400. Minni has been active. K8s has decent playability with the flush draw potential. Getting about 2:1 on a call (350 to win ~700). Not strong enough to 3-bet, but worth a call to see a flop. Pro behind could squeeze but I'll take that risk.

I am in the Big Blind facing a large 4bb open from the Cutoff (Minni) and a flat call from the Small Blind (Claude). The pot is 900, and I need to call 300, giving me pot odds of 25%. My hand, K6o, is weak and easily dominated by the ranges of both opponents (e.g., K9+, KQ, KJ). Additionally, I would be playing out of position relative to the pre-flop aggressor, making it difficult to realize equity. The 4bb sizing makes the price to call unattractive for a marginal hand like K6o. Folding is the standard and prudent play here to preserve chips.

Missed this flop completely - K8c with no club draws on a 6d4h7c board. I have K-high with no pair, no flush draw, and only a weak gutshot (need a 5). This is a clear check. If Minni bets, I'll likely fold. No reason to bluff into the preflop raiser on a connected low board that could easily hit their range.

I opened from the cutoff with AA and isolated Claude in the SB preflop so I want to take the initiative and extract value. The flop (6♦4♥7♣) is very dry — no backdoor flushes and few straight completes — so my overpair is way ahead of his calling range (small pairs, some Axs and broadways). With a 900 pot I’ll make a value/protection c-bet sized to get called by worse hands but still charge any backdoor outs: bet ~1/2 pot. If he calls I’ll keep extracting on many turn cards; if he check-raises big he’s representing sets/two-pair more often and I’ll re-evaluate (but I won’t fold AA lightly — I’ll call or shove depending on sizing).

I have K-high with only a gutshot (need a 5) on a 6-4-7 board. No pair, no flush draw. Minni bet 450 into 900. Getting 3:1 but my equity is poor - just overcards and a weak gutshot (~6 outs max, realistically less since Minni could have sets, two pairs, or straights on this connected board). Not worth calling with K-high here.

6♠4♣ offsuit is too weak to open UTG in a 6-handed lineup: poor high-card strength, dominated when it makes a pair, and limited playability without suitedness. With deep-ish stacks and several aggressive opponents behind, opening invites 3-bets and tough postflop spots with little upside. Best EV is to conserve chips and wait for better holdings/positions.

I hold Tc7c in middle position with no raise ahead. Pot is 150 and it only costs 100 to see the flop. T7s is a playable, speculative hand with good postflop playability and implied odds with ~128bb effective stacks, but it’s not strong enough to open-raise from MP with four players (CO, BTN, SB, BB) left to act — I’d risk being called or squeezed. Calling keeps the pot controlled, preserves stack depth for postflop play, and lets me pursue flush/straight draws or hit top pair. Plan: call 100, evaluate on the flop; fold to large aggression when missed, and apply pressure or extract value when I connect.

I am in the Cutoff with 9s 5d, which is a very weak hand. Minni limped in middle position. Given the low strength of my hand and the fact that there are still players to act behind me (Claude, Pro, and Elon), it is best to fold and wait for a better opportunity. Limping or raising with 95 offsuit in this position is unprofitable in the long run.

AJo on the button is a strong hand. Minni limped, which is weak. I have position and should raise for value and to isolate. Standard raise to 400 (4x) is appropriate to build the pot and potentially take it down preflop.

I hold Qc 5h in the Small Blind. The action involves a limp from Middle Position and a raise to 400 from the Button. Q5 offsuit is a statistically weak hand that performs poorly in multi-way pots, especially when out of position. Facing a raise of 4bb, the cost to call is too high relative to the hand's potential and playability. The prudent play is to fold and preserve chips.

5c8d is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, no straight or flush potential. Facing a button raise to 400 after an MP limp, with pot 650 and to call 300 more (pot odds ~2.17:1 effective, but considering future action from limper Minni and multiway potential). Out of position in BB, postflop will be difficult with this hand. Player tendencies from history show Claude and Minni active/aggressive, but no need to speculate with trash. Deep stacks, fold to preserve stack for better opportunities.

I will call to 400 (put another 300 in). I already committed 100 and Claude’s raise to 400 looks like an isolation on the button — likely a fairly wide range. T7c is a playable, suited connector with good flop equity and strong implied odds with deep stacks (my stack 12,675; his 5,525). The pot is 650 and calling 300 requires ~31.6% equity (300/(650+300)), which is reasonable to see a flop with this hand. I’m out of position vs the button, so I don’t want to 3-bet light and bloat the pot OOP; folding is too tight given the cheap price. Plan: see the flop, proceed with caution — fold to significant turn/river aggression when missed, but continue/raise for value with top pair, two pair/trips, or strong draws.

Flop: Q♦ 9♣ 3♠ — I hold T♣7♣. I have no pair and only a runner-runner club flush (very low equity), so this is a marginal hand. Claude was the preflop raiser (button) and is shorter-stacked; his range connects well with a Q-high flop (Qx, KQ, broadways, some 9x/3x). He checked the flop, which can be weakness or a slowplay. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a very weak hand and face difficult decisions if I’m raised. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity for free. Plan: if Claude bets a small probe (≈≤1/3 pot, ~300–350) I can call and see a turn; fold to a large bet/raise that indicates a made hand. If he checks back I’ll take a free turn to reassess.

I have AJo on a Q93 rainbow board. I raised preflop and Minni (who limped) called. Now Minni checks to me. I have two overcards but no pair. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot (300-350) is reasonable to maintain aggression and potentially take down the pot against Minni's limping/calling range that likely missed this board. If called, I still have outs to improve with my ace-high.

Claude (button) fires a small probe bet of 325 into a 1,275 pot on Q♦9♣3♠. I hold T♣7♣ — no pair, only backdoor club/straight possibilities and 6 simple pair-outs (3 tens + 3 sevens). Calling 325 requires ≈325/(1275+325)=20.3% equity; my chance to hit at least one of those outs by the river is ≈24.1%, so this small bet is profitable to call. Claude’s sizing is consistent with a wide/turn-probing range (some Qx/9x/broads and bluffs), and I’m out of position so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand by raising. I’ll call to see a turn — fold to a large turn raise, call small turn bets (and re-evaluate if I pick up a club or pair), and only start applying pressure if I make a real hand (pair/two-pair/flush) or get a clear read on a bluff.

Board Q♦‑9♣‑3♠‑2♣, I hold T♣7♣ — I’ve turned a single‑card club draw (9 outs, ≈19.6% to hit on the river). Pot is 1,600 and Claude (button, ~5,200) bet small on the flop then checked the turn, showing pot‑control/weakness. This is a good spot to semi‑bluff OOP: a roughly half‑pot bet applies pressure to Qx/9x/medium hands that will fold and still leaves me decent equity if called. I’ll bet 800 to try to take it down now; if he raises I’ll fold (I’m drawing only). If called and I hit the river I’ll value bet; if called and I miss I’ll proceed cautiously (check/fold to significant river aggression).

I have AJo (ace high) on a Q93-2 board. Minni limp-called preflop, check-called my flop c-bet, and is now donk-betting 800 on the turn. This line typically represents strength - likely a queen or better. I have no pair, no draw, just ace-high. Getting 3:1 odds but my hand has very little equity against Minni's value-heavy range here. Even if I hit an ace, Minni could easily have Qx. Clear fold.

UTG in 6-max with A♠9♥ is marginal but playable — I want to take the initiative rather than limp and play multiway out of position. With a deep stack (~136 BB) and the table tendency to open to ~300, a 3x BB open is standard: it gives fold equity, isolates the blinds, and defines a hand range that can continue on Ace-high or favorable flops. If called I’ll proceed cautiously postflop (c-bet on Ace/fair textures, pot-control or fold to heavy resistance on bad boards). If I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold given the mediocre kicker.

Minni opened with a raise from UTG. My hand, Queen-Deuce of hearts, is a very weak suited hand that does not play well against an early position opening range, especially when out of position with several players left to act behind me. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

J2 suited is too weak to call or 3-bet against an UTG raise. Minni has been active but UTG raises typically indicate strength. J2s has poor playability and there are still 3 players behind who could squeeze. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with a suited connector (4d5d) facing a raise to 300 from Minni in UTG. Minni has been playing a very active and loose-aggressive game, opening many pots from various positions. While a UTG range is typically strong, Minni's frequency suggests a wider range than standard. We are playing 6-handed, so UTG is effectively the Lojack. With a deep stack (~97 BB) and the advantage of position, my hand has good playability and implied odds. I can check back or float flops if checked to, and if I hit my draw or hand, I can extract value from Minni's likely strong but non-nut holdings. Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to outmaneuver the opponent post-flop.

Preflop in SB with Qs4c, a trash hand with poor playability, low equity against a UTG raiser like Minni who frequently raises and shows strength. Pro has flat-called on the button, adding range considerations, but Q4o is dominated and offsuit. Pot odds are ~3:1 but not worth it OOP with no fold equity on a 3bet and deep stacks (11575). Early in tournament (hand 24/150), folding preserves stack for premium spots.

We’re facing a UTG 3bb open plus a button flat, meaning ranges are relatively strong and we’ll be out of position multiway. Although the direct price is decent (200 to win 750), 9♠3♠ is heavily dominated (by better 9x, suited connectors, and broadways) and has poor playability—most one-pair holdings will be trouble OOP, and the suited upside isn’t enough to justify the reverse-implied-odds risk versus two players. Best stack-preserving, +EV tournament decision is to release.

I opened UTG and have initiative with A♠9♥ on a dry Kc‑5s‑2c flop. This board misses a big chunk of the Button’s calling range (broadways, many small pairs and floats), so a continuation bet can win the pot immediately or narrow his range. I’ll make a modest C‑bet of 300 (~40% pot) to get folds and keep the pot manageable with a hand that has little showdown value if called. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll check the turn for pot control and fold to significant aggression (check‑raise or large shove) unless I improve (pair or backdoor help).

I hold middle pair (5s) on a K-5-2 board with a flush draw. The opponent, Minni, is playing aggressively and likely c-bets this board with a very wide range, including air (missed high cards) and flush draws, as the King favors her perceived UTG range. The bet size is relatively small (300 into 750), offering me good odds to call with a made hand in position. Folding middle pair to a single standard c-bet against an aggressive player is too weak. Raising would likely only get called by better hands (Kings, overpairs) or strong draws. I will call to keep her bluffs in the pot and re-evaluate on the turn.

I’m on the turn with A♠9♥ and no pair on Kc‑5s‑2c‑6h. I was the preflop raiser and c‑bet 300 on the flop; Pro called. Pot is 1,350 and I have very little showdown value versus his calling range (likely Kx, some pocket pairs, and strong broadways or floats). The 6♣ (6h) is a blank for my hand — a second barrel has limited fold equity against a caller and exposes me to a costly check‑raise or value bets I can’t profitably call. I want to keep the pot manageable and see a river card to re‑evaluate, while folding to significant aggression. Plan: check for pot control and fold to large turn bets; call small, non‑committal bets only when appropriate and look to realize equity or bluff on the river if the action and runout permit.

I hold a pair of fives (now third pair on the K-5-2-6 board) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3). The opponent, who opened UTG and c-bet the flop, has checked to me on the turn. Betting here accomplishes little: worse hands (like Ace-high) will likely fold, and better hands (Kings, Sixes, higher pairs) will call or raise. My hand has some showdown value against missed high cards and significant equity to improve on the river. Checking back allows me to realize this equity for free, avoid being check-raised, and keeps the pot small, which is advantageous with a marginal made hand.

I hold A♠9♥ on Kc‑5s‑2c‑6h‑Td — I missed and only have Ace‑high. I was the preflop raiser and c‑betted the flop (300); Pro called and both of us checked the turn. That passive line looks like Kx, a small/medium pair, or a float — hands that rarely fold a river bluff and that often beat my Ace‑high. The Ten on the river doesn’t credibly improve my range or give me a strong blocker, so bluffing risks a call or a raise I can’t profitably respond to. With the pot only 1,350 and deep stacks behind, pot control is the prudent play: I’ll check and preserve chips rather than fire a bluff with very little fold equity.

On the River, the board is [Kc 5s 2c 6h Td]. I hold [4d 5d] for a pair of Fives. The action went: Minni open-raised UTG, I called BTN. Flop K-5-2, she bet, I called. Turn 6, check-check. River T, she checks. Minni's line (bet flop, check turn/river) is consistent with either pot-controlling a marginal hand (weak King, mid-pair like 77-99) or giving up on a missed draw/overcards (AQ, AJ, missed clubs). My pair of Fives beats her missed overcards but loses to any King, Ten, Six, or higher pocket pair. Betting here serves no purpose: worse hands (Ace-high) will fold, and better hands (Kx, Tx, 77+) will likely call. I have sufficient showdown value to try to win against her bluffs for free. Checking back is the only logical play to realize my equity without risking more chips.

I am in the UTG (Under The Gun) position with J3 offsuit. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and is not profitable to play from early position. Standard strategy dictates folding such hands to avoid being out of position and dominated by stronger hands from the five players behind me.

65 suited is a speculative hand that plays best in late position or multiway pots. From middle position with 4 players behind me, limping invites raises and raising with this hand is marginal. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding 7s 4d in the Cutoff position. This is a 7-high off-suit hand, which is statistically one of the weaker starting hands in Texas Hold'em (bottom 10%). Although it is folded to me giving me a potential opportunity to steal the blinds, the hand has very poor playability if called, and the players in the blinds (especially Minni) have shown willingness to defend or 3-bet. There is no strategic merit in entering the pot with this holding. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Unopened pot on the button with K5o, a speculative king-high offsuit hand. Button position allows for a wide stealing range, and K5o has decent playability with position, blocker to kings, and potential for top pair. Blinds are deep-stacked but have shown loose tendencies in history (Minni aggressive preflop). Standard steal raise size of 2.5x BB to apply pressure and build pot OOP for them. Equity vs calling range sufficient in position. Limping is suboptimal; folding too tight for position.

Button opens are typically very wide 6-handed, and flat-calling Q9o from the SB is tough (domination issues, poor realization OOP) while also allowing the BB to squeeze. I prefer turning this into a light/polar 3-bet to take initiative, deny BB equity, and win the pot pre a good amount of the time. If Elon 4-bets, we simply fold; if called, we can proceed with range advantage and pressure on many flops.

I’m on the big blind with 2♦4♠ facing a button open to 250 and a small‑blind 3‑bet to 900. I must call 800 into a 1,250 pot (need ~39% equity). 2‑4 offsuit has almost no post‑flop playability, is easily dominated by both the opener’s and 3‑bettor’s ranges, and performs poorly multi‑way. With deep stacks I should preserve chips for better spots — a call is a speculative mistake and a raise/shove would be an overly thin bluff. Fold and wait for a stronger holding.

Button open-raise with K5o was standard for position and steal potential. FiveTwo's SB 3-bet to 900 (3.6x) indicates a strong polarized range: value like QQ+, AK, AQs+ and some bluffs. K5o has a king blocker but poor postflop playability offsuit with weak kicker, dominated by AK/AQ and crushed by pairs. Even with position on SB, equity ~30-35% vs value range, not justifying a call for 650 into 1900 pot with deep stacks and high SPR. 4-betting bluff risky without stronger blockers or history of opponent folding to 4-bets. Folding is GTO and preserves stack for premium spots in early tournament.
Q2o from UTG is a clear fold. This is well outside my opening range from the earliest position at a 6-handed table. No reason to play this weak hand out of position with 52BB effective.

I hold 4c Th in Middle Position. This is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand (T4 offsuit) with virtually no playability or equity. With 105 BBs, there is no pressure to play marginal hands, let alone trash hands like this. Facing a fold from UTG, my standard play is to fold immediately.

Preflop in cutoff with 9s4s, a weak suited gapped connector. No action ahead (folds from UTG and MP). Facing button and blinds behind. Deep stacks (113BB effective). Standard play is to fold this hand as it's too weak for an open-raise and limping invites multiway pots where it plays poorly postflop. Better to wait for stronger spots to build stack.

Button vs blinds with Ah9h is a clear open. We have strong blocker value (ace), good postflop playability (nut-flush potential), and the blinds will be defending/folding wide. With stacks deep enough, we want to apply pressure and realize positional advantage rather than limp. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb size to generate folds and keep ranges wide enough to outplay postflop.

I’m in the small blind with J♠5♠ facing a button open to 250. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (250 total). J5s is marginal but has suited equity and decent flop-playability; out of position vs the button I don’t want to light 3-bet with such a hand and don’t want to fold a lot of playable equity with deep stacks. The call gives ~2:1 pot odds (33% break-even equity) versus a wide button range and lets me see a flop where I can continue only with real equity (top pair, spade draws, strong combos). Plan: call to see the flop; proceed aggressively if I hit a strong draw or pair, otherwise fold to heavy action.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 6c, a strong suited connector that performs well in multi-way pots. The button (FiveTwo) opened for 250 and the Small Blind (Minni) called. I need to call 150 more into a pot that will be 750 total (600 currently). I am getting 4:1 pot odds (150 to win 600) and I am closing the action. This is a standard call to see a flop and attempt to realize equity or hit a big draw.

I have J♠5♠ and completely missed this flop (7h 7d 9d). There are no spades on board (only a very slim backdoor flush possibility) and only one overcard (J) to the 9 — my showdown equity is poor versus a button’s wide open range and the BB’s calling range. I’m out of position and multiway postflop, so barreling for fold equity is unlikely to be profitable. I’ll check and conserve chips — if I face a small probe bet I may call to see a turn (I have ~3 turn outs to a Jack and backdoor equity), but I’ll fold to sizable pressure. If checked through I’ll re-evaluate on the turn.

I have flopped trips (sevens) with a 6 kicker. The board is somewhat connected with a flush draw (diamonds) and a straight draw (9-8-x). In a three-way pot, I want to extract value while protecting my hand from draws. However, since FiveTwo was the pre-flop aggressor, it is standard to check to the raiser to see if they will continuation bet. If FiveTwo bets, I plan to check-raise to build the pot and charge any diamond draws or pocket pairs. Checking also keeps my range wider and allows opponents to bluff.

We’re the preflop raiser and have a very strong made hand on 7-7-9: nines and sevens with the A kicker. Multiway I want to bet smaller rather than polarize—this board will still connect with lots of pocket pairs, 9x, diamond draws, and random floats that can call a small c-bet. Betting also denies a free card to overcards/diamond draws. I’ll use a ~1/3 pot sizing to get value/protection while keeping my range wide; if I face a big check-raise, ranges are much more weighted to 7x/boats, so I can proceed cautiously (often call smaller raises in position, fold to large pressure).

Board: 7h 7d 9d. I hold J♠5♠ and completely missed — no pair, no diamond, only three clean outs to a Jack and minimal backdoor equity. The button's bet is tiny (250 into a 1000 pot) after two checks, so this is a cheap turn card to see. Calling 250 gives me ~20% break-even equity and preserves my stack; raising OOP with a marginal hand is a loser’s play here. Plan: call to see the turn and fold to meaningful pressure (a large raise or shove) unless I hit a Jack or pick up real additional equity on the turn. If the turn is safe (checks through or a small bet) I’ll re-evaluate for showdown or a cautious bluff-catch; if opponents show strength I’ll release.

I have flopped trips (sevens) on a wet board with flush and straight draws. After checking to the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo), who then bet 1/3rd of the pot, and seeing Minni call, I am in a perfect position to execute a check-raise. Raising here serves two purposes: first, it extracts value from hands like pocket pairs, top-pair nines, or diamond draws; second, it protects my hand by making it expensive for those draws to continue. While my kicker (six) is low, there are only two sevens left, making it less likely an opponent also holds one. If I am re-raised, I will have to proceed with caution against a better seven or a full house like nines-full-of-sevens, but a raise to 850 is a strong play to take control of the pot now.

We have A9 on 7-7-9 with two diamonds: effectively top two pair (best kicker) and well ahead of any 9x/overpairs/draws, but we’re in bad shape versus any 7x (which is a full house here). The BB’s check-raise multiway is often value-heavy, so 3-betting would mostly isolate ourselves against boats and fold out the hands we want to keep (diamond draws, worse 9x). With position and a manageable price, calling keeps bluffs in, lets Minni potentially continue with worse, and preserves our ability to fold later if the BB applies heavy pressure on bad turns (diamond/paired/board-changing cards).

I’m in the small blind with J♠5♠ on 7♥7♦9♦. Pot is 2,700 and it costs me 600 to call, so I need ~600/(2,700+600)=18.2% equity to break even. My realistic improvement chances to the river are small: 3 jacks to pair (≈12.5% to hit by river) plus a backdoor spade flush (~5.1%), roughly 17.6% combined — slightly below the pot‑odds threshold. Even if I hit a Jack I’m often behind sets or two‑pair combinations; multiway and out‑of‑position play further reduces the value of calling. Flash’s large raise to 850 is meaningful pressure (polarized to strong value or strong draws) and FiveTwo’s call indicates real equity too. Raising OOP here would be reckless. With marginal equity, multiway action and the likelihood of facing further pressure, the correct play is to conserve chips and fold.

The turn card (8d) is extremely unfavorable for my hand. It completes the diamond flush and creates straight possibilities (TJ). Additionally, if FiveTwo holds pocket 8s, they have now improved to a full house. After my flop check-raise was called, FiveTwo's range is likely narrow and strong, containing many of these hands or high diamond draws. Checking is the best course of action to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on FiveTwo's response to this dangerous card.

Turn brings the 3rd diamond and Flash (who check-raised the flop multiway) now checks. That line heavily contains hands that either just improved (two-diamond semibluffs -> flushes) or strong made hands that don’t love the flush card (7x trips, some 9x/overpairs). With A9 and no diamond we have decent showdown value but we’re not strong enough to bet/call off versus a check-raise, and betting mostly isolates us against the portion of his range that now beats us (flushes/trips) while folding out hands we already beat.

Checking back keeps the pot manageable, realizes our equity, and preserves our redraws to a full house on the river (any 9 or 7). River plan: if we improve, go for value; if we brick, bluff-catch some reasonable sizing and fold to big polar pressure.

The river completes a four-flush on the board (9d 8d 7d 4d). My hand, trips (7c 6c), has now become extremely vulnerable. While I had a strong hand on the flop, any single diamond in FiveTwo's range now beats me. FiveTwo called my flop check-raise and checked back on the turn, which could indicate a made flush that was playing cautiously or a hand with a single diamond that has now hit. There is very little value in betting here, as I am unlikely to be called by any hand that I beat, and I would be forced to fold to a significant raise. Checking is the most prudent play to attempt to reach a cheap showdown or evaluate a bet from the opponent.

River is a 4-diamond board (7h7d9d8d4d). With Ah9h we have two pair (9s and 7s) but no diamond, so we’re a pure bluff-catcher. Flash’s flop check-raise range is heavily weighted to 7x/strong 9x and diamond draws; many of the draws have now completed to a flush, and hands we beat (worse 9x/overpairs without a diamond) are unlikely to pay off on this texture after the flop action. Betting would mostly fold out worse and risk getting check-raised (forcing a fold and burning chips), while checking realizes our showdown value versus missed/non-diamond hands and keeps us from isolating ourselves against flushes/boats.

I am in UTG position with Jc 8c (Jack-Eight suited). This is a weak suited two-gapper. In a 6-handed game, the UTG opening range should be relatively tight (typically pairs, suited broadways, strong aces). J8s is well below the standard opening threshold for this position. Opening here risks domination by calling ranges and leaves me out of position for the rest of the hand. Given the table dynamics with active players behind, folding is the standard and correct play.

Preflop in middle position after UTG fold, with K6o offsuit. This is a weak hand with poor playability postflop, low kicker, offsuit, and vulnerable to domination by better kings. Deep stacks (113BB), no reason to limp or raise; standard fold to wait for better opportunities.

We’re in the CO with 54o after two folds, so opening range can be fairly wide, but this is one of the weakest connectors (offsuit, poor playability, low high-card value). The Button (Minni) has shown willingness to 3-bet (e.g., vs our opens previously), which makes stealing with marginal offsuit hands less profitable because we’ll often have to fold to pressure and bleed chips. With decent stack depth and no need to force thin edges, I’ll pass and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button with 7♦5♥ — a weak, but playable hand in position. Everyone folded to me and the pot is just the blinds (150), so open-raising to pick up the blinds or take the initiative is the right play. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the big blind) — standard sizing that maximizes fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. If I face a substantial 3-bet, I’ll fold this hand and conserve my stack; if called I’ll play postflop aggressively in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Three suited. While it's a suited hand, it's very weak and poorly connected. Minni has been an active raiser from the button throughout the tournament. Calling 250 more to play out of position against a potentially wide but stronger range is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.
9-2 offsuit is a very weak hand. Even though I'm getting decent pot odds (200 to win 450), this hand plays poorly postflop and will be dominated by Minni's raising range. Easy fold.

UTG with Th8h, a suited connector with good playability in deep-stack tournament play. Early position but fold equity likely high given history of frequent folds to UTG raises (e.g., Hand 15). Raising builds the pot and denies equity to weaker hands. Standard 3x open to 300 provides good risk-reward: risking 300 to win 150 immediately or play postflop OOP with initiative. Opponents like Minni and Flash aggressive but often fold preflop steals. T8s has multiway potential if called. Folding too tight; this is a profitable spot long-term over 150 hands.

Elon opened UTG to 3bb, which represents a relatively strong range in a 6-max lineup. With 9♢5♣ offsuit in middle position, we’re dominated too often, have poor playability, and don’t realize equity well versus an UTG range at ~110bb effective. Calling is -EV and 3-betting as a bluff is too loose given blockers and position. Best decision is to preserve stack and fold.

Hand is K♥2♥ in the cutoff facing an UTG 3x to 300. K2s is marginal vs an UTG opening range (often dominated by other Kx) but it's suited and cheap to see a flop given my deep stack (12,600). A 3-bet bluff here is thin — too many players left to act and limited fold equity — so folding would be overly nitty and 3-betting unnecessarily risky. Calling 300 preserves stack, gives me a chance to see favorable flops (king or backdoor heart/straight possibilities) and play postflop with some flexibility. Plan: call and proceed cautiously postflop — fold to large 3-bets or heavy pressure if I miss; if I hit a king or good draw, play more aggressively. Because the cost is small relative to my stack and table dynamics, a flat call is the best play.

I am on the button with Jack-Seven of spades. Elon (UTG) has raised to 3x the big blind and Minni (CO) has called. While the button offers the best post-flop position, J7s is generally too weak to call a raise from UTG, whose range should be quite strong. The presence of a caller (Minni) further suggests that I'm likely dominated or facing a strong pair. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

K6 suited in SB facing a UTG raise and one caller. Getting 4:1 pot odds (250 to win 1000). K6s has decent playability with flush potential and a broadway card. While I'll be out of position, the pot odds justify a call to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 92o, facing a raise from UTG and two callers. Although the pot odds are attractive (calling 200 to win a pot of 1000, roughly 5:1), my hand is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em. My equity against three opponents, one of whom opened UTG, is likely around 10-12%, which is below the required 16.7% pot odds. Furthermore, playing this hand out of position with poor playability and high reverse implied odds makes folding the correct decision to preserve chips.

Flopped board is Qd Qh Td - paired queens with a ten. I have K6c with no pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), and only king-high. With two opponents still in the hand (Elon who raised preflop and Minni who called), I have very little equity here. Betting as a bluff into two players on a paired board is too risky. Best to check and see a free turn card.

Preflop raiser with T8s suited connector on a Qd-Qh-Td flop, hitting middle pair (tens) with a weak 8 kicker and backdoor heart flush draw. Board is dry and paired (queens), reducing straight/flush draw equity, favoring continuation bets from my perceived strong range. Claude's SB check is weak, often indicative of speculative hands or nothing. Minni's CO call preflop is wide but vulnerable postflop. Standard c-bet for protection against gutshots (e.g., J9, 89), thin value vs worse pairs/draws, and fold equity. Half-pot sizing (600 into 1000) balances pot building with deep stacks, maintaining aggression consistent with preflop UTG open and tournament goal to maximize stack over 150 hands. Checking risks giving free cards to overcards/draws OOP-ish position.

Elon (UTG) bets 600 into a 1600 pot on Qd Qh Td. I have K♥2♥ — no pair, only K-high and a very remote backdoor heart draw (I'd need both turn and river hearts), and a single out (any K) to make one pair. Calling 600 to win ~2200 requires roughly 27% equity. Against an UTG open-raiser his c-bet range is heavily weighted toward Qx, sets/overpairs, and strong broadways that dominate or greatly outperform my hand, so my true equity is well below the needed threshold. A raise as a bluff is thin vs an UTG opener and risks a big 3-bet or getting called by better hands. With a deep stack and many better spots to take, the +EV play is to fold and conserve chips for a stronger situation.

I have K6c with no pair, no diamond draw on QQT board. Elon c-bet 600 from UTG into a paired board. I have essentially no equity - just king high. Need ~27% equity to call but likely have under 15% against his range. Clear fold.

6-max UTG, 9♦6♦ is a marginal suited one-gapper that plays poorly out of position against the likely tight/strong continuing ranges (dominated by better 9x/6x and higher suited connectors). With no dead money worth chasing and several aggressive players behind, opening it is unnecessary variance; limping is worse. Best decision is to conserve stack and wait for better UTG holdings.

Holding 2♠3♥ in middle position is a very weak, offsuit hand with poor playability out of position. This is an unopened pot (pot 150) and it would cost 100 to call — a speculative, often multiway spot where this hand has low equity and is frequently dominated. Attempting a light raise to 200 risks action from Cutoff, Button, SB and BB and is not worth bloating the pot with such a hand. With a healthy 123bb stack I’ll conserve chips and wait for a better opportunity. Fold.

I am in the Cutoff position with 3c 5h, which is a very weak starting hand. Both players before me have folded. While there is an opportunity to steal the blinds, this hand is too weak even for a speculative steal attempt. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
On the button with T6o after 3 folds. This is a good spot to steal the blinds. While T6o is weak, button position with only blinds left gives me good fold equity. Standard steal raise to 2.5x.

I hold AJo in the Small Blind, a strong starting hand that plays well against a wide Button opening range. The opponent, Claude, has been active and is playing a stack of ~45BB. While a 3-bet is a viable option to isolate and deny equity, it risks bloating the pot out of position or facing a 4-bet shove that would put me in a marginal spot. Calling allows me to keep the opponent's range wide (including many dominated hands), controls the size of the pot, and lets me leverage my post-flop skills despite being out of position. The price is good to see a flop.

82o is one of the worst hands in poker, with no high card value, no suitedness, and poor playability postflop. Facing a button raise from Claude, who often raises wide but still has a strong enough range, and a call from Pro in the small blind, my hand has negligible equity and fold equity if I shove or raise. Pot odds of calling 150 into 600 require about 20% equity, but 82o realizes far less against their combined ranges (Claude's button opening ~30-40% including broadways, suited connectors; Pro's calling range stronger). Folding preserves my deep stack (119x BB) for better spots in this multiway tournament with 6 players remaining. No reason to speculate with trash here.

I am out of position on a paired board (6c 6h Tc) with Ace-high. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser. This board texture is relatively dry, and while it hits some of the Button's range (Tx, 6x, broadways), it misses a lot of his air. Checking allows me to keep his bluffing range in the hand. If he bets, I can evaluate the sizing, but leading out (donk betting) serves little purpose as it likely folds out worse hands and builds a pot out of position with a marginal holding.

I flopped a monster - full house with sixes full of tens (T6 on a 66T board). Pro checked to me. I need to bet for value. With such a disguised hand, I want to build the pot while keeping Pro in. A standard c-bet of around half pot should look like a normal continuation bet and hopefully get called or even raised.

I hold AJo on a paired board of 6-6-T. The opponent (Claude) c-bets 300 into a pot of 600 (half pot). Given Claude's active profile and the button opening range, he likely c-bets this texture with his entire range, including many hands I beat (King-high, Queen-high, draws). Folding A-high here would be too tight and exploitable. Raising turns my showdown value into a bluff which isn't necessary. Calling allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the turn. I am getting 3:1 pot odds, and my hand is certainly good often enough to justify a call.

The turn card is the Jack of Diamonds, improving my hand to Top Pair, Top Kicker (TPTK) on a board of 6c 6h Tc Jd. This is an excellent card for me. Out of position, the standard and most effective play is to check to the aggressor. This accomplishes two things: 1) It allows the opponent to continue bluffing with air or semi-bluffs (like club draws or straight draws), and 2) It keeps the pot manageable in case he has a hidden monster (like a 6) while disguising the strength of my hand. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out his bluffs and only get action from hands that beat me or have strong equity. I will check with the intention of calling a bet or betting the river for value if it checks through.

I still have a full house (sixes full of tens) - an absolute monster. Pro has checked again. The Jack doesn't change anything for me. I need to keep extracting value. Betting around 2/3 pot to keep Pro in with any ten, jack, pocket pair, or draws. If they have a jack or ten, they might even raise.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AJo) on a board of 6-6-T-J. The opponent, Claude, has fired a second barrel of 800 into a 1200 pot. This is a polarized bet that could represent a 6, a strong Ten, a Jack, or a semi-bluff with a straight/flush draw. Given my hand strength, folding is incorrect. Raising would likely fold out his bluffs and isolate me against hands that beat me (like a random 6 or a set). The opponent has about a pot-sized bet left behind for the river. Calling is the optimal play to keep his range wide, allowing him to bluff the river or value bet a worse hand like KJs or QJs. I am well ahead of his range and getting good odds to see the river.

I hold AJo on a board of 6c 6h Tc Jd Th. I have Two Pair, Jacks and Tens, with an Ace kicker. The river brings a second Ten, double-pairing the board. This connects with any Tx the opponent might have (making a Full House) and improves overpairs (QQ+) to beat me, but I still beat missed straight/flush draws and worse Jacks (KJ, QJ). Leading out would likely only get called by better hands and fold out bluffs. Checking allows me to induce a bluff from missed draws or reach showdown cheaply against marginal hands. Given the opponent's aggressive line (double barrel), checking is standard to let him fire a third bullet if he is bluffing, or check back a worse showdown hand.

I have tens full of sixes (TTT66) - an absolute monster. The river Ten paired the board, but it actually improved my hand from sixes full of tens to tens full of sixes. Pro has been check-calling throughout, which suggests a medium-strength hand like a ten, jack, or maybe even a 6. Only JJ, TT, or 66 beat me, and those are very unlikely given my blockers and Pro's passive line. I need to value bet here - around 65% pot to get called by worse full houses (any T or 6 in their hand), trips, or stubborn two pair.

I hold AJo (Top Pair, Top Kicker) on a board of 6c 6h Tc Jd Th. The river pairs the Ten, giving me Two Pair (Jacks and Tens) with an Ace kicker. Claude has fired three barrels, a line representing extreme strength (Full House, Trips, or Overpair) or a polarized bluff. The second Ten on the board significantly reduces the combinations of value hands (Tx) Claude can have, as there are only two Tens left in the deck. Conversely, I unblock all missed flush draws (having no clubs) and straight draws (like KQ). Given Claude's aggressive history and the pot odds of ~28%, my hand is too strong to fold at the top of my range. I will call to catch his bluffs.

I’m UTG with 8♠5♥ — a very weak, offsuit hand. In early position at a 6‑handed table I need a tight opening range; calling 100 into a 150 pot would require roughly 40% equity to break even preflop, which 8‑5o doesn’t have against typical ranges. The table shows frequent 300 open-raises and aggression, so I’m likely to face a raise and be crushed or dominated postflop. With a healthy 123 BB stack and plenty of opportunities later, preserve chips and fold this marginal spot.
9h 3c is an extremely weak hand and does not warrant a call or a raise from middle position. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve the stack.

A9o in the cutoff with two folds in front. This is a solid hand to open-raise with - we have good stealing equity against the blinds and button. Standard raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to put pressure on remaining players.

I hold Kh3s on the Button. The player in the Cutoff (Claude) has raised to 250. While I have the advantage of position, K3o is a very weak hand that is typically dominated by a Cutoff opening range (often containing better Kings, Aces, and pairs). The hand plays poorly post-flop due to the weak kicker and lack of flush potential. The pot odds do not justify a call with this specific holding, and a 3-bet bluff is too loose here. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

KsQs is a strong suited broadway hand with excellent equity against a cutoff opening range, which is likely wide given the position. From the small blind, calling would put me out of position post-flop, which is suboptimal despite good pot odds (200 to call into 400). 3-betting is the best play to isolate the aggressor (Claude), build the pot with a strong hand, and take the initiative. Sizing to 800 (roughly 3x the raise) is standard and balanced, putting pressure while leaving room for further action. BB may defend, but we have good playability.

Facing a CO open and an SB 3-bet, T7o is too weak to continue profitably out of position. It’s dominated by the ranges that take this line (many Tx, 7x, broadways), plays poorly multiway, and has little implied odds. A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive here because we have no useful blockers (no A/K) and would be risking chips into a player who’s shown willingness to apply pressure. Best EV is to let it go and preserve stack.

Facing a 3-bet from Elon in the SB after my cutoff open with A9o. This is a marginal spot - A9o doesn't play well out of position against a 3-bet range. I'm getting about 3:1 odds (550 to win ~1150), but being OOP for the rest of the hand with a hand that can be dominated by better aces makes this a fold. I'll find better spots.

I am UTG (Under the Gun) with King-Deuce suited. In a 6-handed game, opening from this position requires a stronger range to account for the five players left to act behind me. King-Deuce suited is too weak for an UTG open as it is easily dominated by better Kings and has poor connectivity for straights. I will fold to maintain a tight and disciplined early position range.
72 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. In middle position with several players behind me, there's no reason to play this hand. Easy fold.

I hold Jack-Six offsuit in the Cutoff. This is a very weak hand with poor playability (unsuited, unconnected). Although it is folded to me, opening with this hand is too loose and likely -EV, especially with active players like Elon on the Button who might 3-bet. Preserving chips is the best strategy here.

Folds to the button with A2s suited, an excellent stealing spot. A2s has good playability, nut flush potential, wheel straight potential, and dominates weaker ace calls. Standard raise size of 3x BB (300) to build pot or take blinds. Deep stacks and position favor aggression.

Button open to 300 and we’re in the small blind out of position with T3o. This hand has poor playability, is dominated by a lot of Button’s continuing range (Tx, 3x with better kicker), and doesn’t realize equity well from the SB. Versus a competent/aggressive opener, defending here burns chips; better to wait for hands that can 3-bet or defend with clearer equity and connectivity.

Holding Q♣5♣ on the big blind facing a 3x button open to 300. Pot is 450 and I must call 200 (to make it 300) — calling gives me pot odds of 200/(450+200) ≈ 30.8%. Q5o is a weak, often‑dominated hand versus a BTN open and plays poorly out of position: poor kicker, no strong draws, limited blockers to opponent’s strong ranges. With deep stacks I can afford to be patient and avoid marginal OOP decisions that cost chips long‑term. A minimum reraise as a bluff is thin with this holding. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.
T2o in UTG is a clear fold. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and I'm in the worst position at the table. No reason to open this hand - I'd be dominated by almost any calling range.

I am in Middle Position with T5 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with no playability (disconnected, offsuit, low ranks). With four players left to act behind me, the probability of someone having a better hand is high, and this hand performs poorly post-flop even if I see one cheaply. There is no strategic merit in entering the pot. The correct play is to fold immediately to preserve chips.

Preflop in cutoff with Ks3s after two folds (UTG and MP). Ks3s is a suited king-high hand, playable from late position for its steal potential, flush draw equity, and domination potential against worse kings. Deep stacks allow for postflop playability. Button, SB, and BB are deep but history shows mixed aggression; raising steals dead blinds frequently and builds pot with position. Standard open-raise size of 3BB (300) given pot of 150. Folding too tight, calling/limping poor with this hand.

We’re on the button with QQ versus a cutoff open, which is a clear value 3-bet spot: we dominate Elon’s opening range, deny equity to the blinds, and build a pot in position with a premium. Given stacks ~90–120bb, a standard in-position 3-bet sizing is about 3x the raise to keep worse hands in while still applying pressure. Plan: 3-bet, then generally continue versus a 4-bet (often call; evaluate sizing/dynamics) since folding QQ at these stacks is too tight against an aggressive opener.

I’m in the small blind with Q♥6♣ facing a CO open to 300 and a BTN 3‑bet to 900. Pot is 1,350 and it costs me 850 to call. This is a multiway, out-of-position spot against two aggressive players. Calling 850 into 1,350 requires roughly 38.6% equity to break even — Q6o has very poor equity and is frequently dominated by the 3‑bettor’s and opener’s ranges. A minimum 4‑bet to 1,500 or an all‑in bluff is too committal and unjustified with this hand. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot in position with a hand that plays well postflop.

I am holding pocket sevens in the Big Blind. Elon opened for 300 from the Cutoff, and FiveTwo 3-bet to 900 from the Button. While 77 is a decent medium pair, calling a 3-bet out of position against an active player like FiveTwo is problematic. The implied odds for set-mining are marginal (around 11.6:1), and if I don't hit a set, I will be forced to play a bloated pot out of position against a range that likely dominates mine or includes overcards. Given the tournament context and the strength shown by a 3-bet over a CO open, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Preflop as cutoff raiser with Ks3s facing a 3-bet to 900 from the button (FiveTwo). Ks3s is a marginal suited king suited hand playable for position and potential, but against a button 3-bet range after isolating two folds and my raise, it faces a strong range including JJ+, AKo, AQs+, KQs. Equity is around 30-35% OOP, and postflop playability is poor out of position on a deep stack. SPR remains high (~6:1 after call), but without position or nut potential, folding preserves stack for better spots. FiveTwo shows aggression historically but exploits our wide raise range here. Standard fold.

I am holding Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o) in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Being out of position with a trash hand and a healthy stack of 72.5 BBs, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot. The only correct play is to fold immediately and conserve chips for a better opportunity.

Preflop in middle position with 5h2d, an extremely weak offsuit non-connected hand with no high cards or playability. Standard fold to avoid spew. Pot 150 (blinds only), calling 100 commits 0.8BB with trash. Preserve stack for premium hands given tournament dynamics and player tendencies to raise frequently.

We’re in the CO with Q4o in a 6-max configuration. This hand is below a profitable open-raise threshold and plays poorly postflop (domination issues, weak kicker, low connectivity). Limping is also unattractive because it invites isolation from an aggressive Button (Minni has shown willingness to 3-bet/isolate) and forces us into multiway spots out of position to the Button with a marginal holding. Given the high likelihood of facing pressure and the low realization/equity when called, the best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better steal candidates.

I’m on the button with T3s — a weak but suited hand with decent postflop play and great positional leverage. With only the blinds to act and stacks deep (≈121bb), this is a good spot to apply pressure and attempt a steal. Table tendencies show frequent 300 opens, so a standard 3x open looks natural and gives healthy fold equity against both blinds. I’ll keep plans simple: raise to 300 to take the initiative, fold to a large 3-bet, and if called proceed postflop aggressively on favorable textures (c-bet dry boards) while playing cautiously to big aggression.

I am in the Small Blind with K3o. Minni opened from the Button to 300. K3o is a weak hand to play out of position, and calling would invite the Big Blind (Claude) to either squeeze or see a flop cheaply. Given the range of a button raiser and my lack of position, K3o does not have enough equity or playability to justify a call or a 3-bet bluff in this spot. I will fold and wait for a better hand.

I have pocket 9s in the BB facing a button open to 300. This is a strong hand against a likely wide button opening range. I should 3-bet for value here - 99 plays well against button's range and I can either take down the pot now or build a pot with a solid hand. Raising to around 900 is a standard 3-bet size.

I opened to 300 on the button with T3s and Claude (BB) 3‑bet to 900, leaving 600 to call. T3s has poor equity and limited post‑flop playability versus a BB 3‑bet range that will include pairs and strong broadway hands. Although the call is only ~6bb, this is a marginal spot where I’d be playing out of my depth — my prior plan was to fold to a large 3‑bet. I’ll preserve my deep stack for better, clearer spots rather than invest another 600 into a likely dominated situation.

UTG in 6-handed play with QJo offsuit. This is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by stronger broadway hands like AKo, AQo, KQo, QJs+. Standard tight opening range from early position does not include offsuit QJ. Deep stacks (120BB effective), no prior action, better to fold and preserve stack for premium spots. Opponents like Minni and Claude are aggressive raisers, increasing risk of tough postflop spots.

K5o from middle position in a 6-max lineup is too dominated to profitably open, and limping is especially bad with aggressive players behind who can isolate/3-bet and put us in tough spots. With no dead money beyond blinds and plenty of better steal candidates, we just cut the loss and wait for a better spot.

Cutoff with Qc7d — marginal but openable from this position. I'm 118.5bb deep so I can leverage position and fold equity to steal the blinds rather than limp and concede initiative. A standard 3x open to 300 pressures the button and blinds, keeps my range wide, and matches my table image (I've opened to 300 frequently), so it won't look out of line. If I get called I'll c-bet favorable high-card/queen/ace flops and play carefully on coordinated/paired boards. If I face a sizable 3-bet (large sizing / clear strength) I'll fold; only consider continuing versus small, light 3-bets or obvious exploitative spots. Action: raise to 300.

I am on the button with Jack-Seven offsuit. Minni has raised from the cutoff to 3BB (300 chips). J7o is a very weak hand to play against a raise, even with positional advantage, as it is easily dominated and doesn't flop well. There are also two players behind me (Claude and Pro) who could potentially 3-bet or call. Given the hand's low equity and the fact that I'm facing a raise, folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

82 suited is a very weak hand, even with the suited hearts. Minni has been raising frequently, but 82s doesn't have enough equity to profitably defend here. The hand has poor high card value, limited straight potential, and while suited gives some flush potential, it's not enough to justify 250 chips. Easy fold.

Opponent Minni is in the Cutoff and has been opening very frequently (Hands 21, 22, 24, 27, 33), suggesting a wide range. I am in the Big Blind with K7o. While this is a marginal hand, I am getting excellent pot odds, needing to call 200 to win a pot of 450 (requiring ~31% equity). Against a wide late-position range, K7o has approximately 40-45% equity. Despite being out of position, the price and the opponent's aggressive tendencies make this a standard defense call.

I am out of position in the Big Blind against an aggressive Cutoff opener (Minni). The flop comes [2s 2c Th], a very dry, paired board. While I have King-high which has some showdown value against a wide range, I have no pair or strong draw. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Donk betting here serves little purpose as it likely folds out air that I beat and isolates me against better hands. I will check and evaluate based on Minni's action.

I opened to 300 preflop and was called by the BB; pot is 650 and I hold Qc7d on 2s 2c Th. I missed, but this is a dry, low-pair board that rarely connects with the caller’s range beyond tens or small pocket pairs. As the preflop aggressor a small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) is the best play — it leverages fold equity against broadways and weaker holdings while keeping the pot manageable if called. I’ll bet 225 to take it down now; if I face a sizable raise I’ll fold, and if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and only continue with improvement or a clear read.

I defended the Big Blind with K7o against an active Cutoff opener (Minni). The flop [2s 2c Th] is dry and paired. Minni c-bets 225 into 650 (approx 1/3 pot). While the price is attractive (needing ~20.5% equity), I hold only King-high with no flush or straight draws. My hand is behind all pairs and all Ax combinations in Minni's wide range, and even against pure air (like QJ), I am only slightly ahead and out of position for future streets. Without a pair or meaningful draw to improve, and facing an opponent who has range advantage on this texture, floating out of position is negative EV. Folding is the disciplined play.

6-handed UTG, Q8 suited is too loose for an opening range against a table that has shown willingness to 3-bet and apply pressure. It’s often dominated when called (Q9+, AQ/KQ), and we don’t want to build bigger pots out of position with a marginal holding. Best to pass and preserve stack for higher-EV spots.

T♠7♦ is a weak, offsuit hand with poor playability from middle against four players left to act (CO, BTN, SB, BB). Limping (call 100) invites multiway action where my kicker and straight/flush potential are often dominated; raising to steal (300) with this holding is too loose and will get me into marginal spots or 3-bet pots. Pot is small and I have deep stacks — no need to gamble out of position with a marginal hand. Fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Cutoff position with 7-5 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand that does not meet the requirements for an opening raise, even with the action folded to me. Standard strategy dictates a fold here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

KQo on the button with everyone folding to me. This is a premium stealing spot - I have a strong hand and excellent position. Standard raise to around 2.5x BB to take down the blinds or play in position against callers.

I hold 8h 3c in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand (trash). Claude raised to 250 from the Button. Although Claude is active and likely stealing, my hand has very poor playability and I would be out of position post-flop. The pot odds (needing to call 200 to win 400) are not sufficient to justify calling with this holding, and a 3-bet bluff is too risky with zero equity. Folding is the only reasonable option.

Qs9d is a marginal hand OOP vs a button raise after multiple folds. Claude's raise from the button is likely wide for a steal, given the dead money from blinds (pot 400, call 150 for ~3:1 odds). Equity vs a loose button range (~40% hands) is around 38-40%, sufficient on odds alone, and we can realize equity postflop selectively. Deep stacks (120BB), so no ICM pressure. Defending wide vs button keeps range balanced and exploits steal attempts. Calling preserves fold equity for later streets. Raising or folding too tight OOP is suboptimal here.

Flop board is 4s Tc 4d, extremely dry and paired low. Qs9d is queen-high with no pair, no draws (minor spade backdoor). Against Claude's wide button steal range (Ax, broadways, suited connectors, pairs), we have ~25-30% equity raw but poor playability OOP. First to act: donk betting air is -EV as it bloats pot and denies equity realization. Check-fold to c-bet is optimal; check-call risks stacking off OOP with marginal showdown value, and villain likely cbets 50-75% pot frequently. Preserve stack for better spots; history shows Claude aggressive but we exploit by folding weak hands post-flop OOP. Deep stacks allow patience.

I raised preflop with KQo and got called by Elon in BB. Flop is 4s Tc 4d - a paired dry board. I have two overcards but missed. This is a good c-bet spot: paired boards favor the preflop aggressor, I have position, and Elon checked. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot should take this down often, and I have equity if called with my overcards.

Qs9d is queen-high no pair, no draws on a paired 4s Tc 4d board, which is dry and favors small pairs, sets, or any ten in Claude's wide button c-bet range. Facing a small 200 bet into 750 (~27% pot) OOP after checking, check-fold is standard: equity is low (~20-30% vs polarized range including bluffs), playability poor multiway potential low but OOP vulnerable to turns/rivers. Claude's aggressive history (frequent button steals, cbets) exploited by folding marginal hands here; deep stacks (118BB) allow patience for premium spots. Calling commits to bloated pot with weak showdown value, raising air -EV. Preserve stack for 115 hands left.

UTG with K♠9♣ is a marginal, easily-dominated hand and plays poorly out of position. Many players at this table open-raise frequently (typically ~300) and postflop aggression is common, so opening or limping would frequently put me in tough spots (3-bets, multiway pots, or dominated Kx matchups). With a healthy 122bb stack there's no need to gamble from UTG — fold and wait for a better spot in position.

I am in Middle Position with a pocket pair (3d 3c). In a 6-max game, pocket pairs are strong enough to open for a raise. I want to take the initiative and either win the blinds or set myself up to win a larger pot if I flop a set. Limping is generally avoided in this format to maintain a range advantage and fold out weaker hands that might realize equity for free. A standard 3x open to 300 is appropriate here.

ATo in the cutoff facing a 3x open from Flash. This is a decent hand with good playability in position. I can call and see a flop, or 3-bet for value/isolation. Given I have position on the raiser and ATo plays well postflop, calling is the cleanest play here. 3-betting risks getting 4-bet by the players behind me.

I hold Pocket Aces (As Ad), the best starting hand, on the Button. Facing an open to 300 from Flash and a call from Claude, the pot is 750. Flat-calling would invite the blinds and result in a multi-way pot which reduces my equity realization. I must 3-bet (squeeze) for value to build the pot and isolate one opponent or take it down pre-flop. A standard squeeze sizing with one caller is roughly 3x the open plus the caller's amount (3x300 + 300 = 1200). Given my stack depth (~69bb), a raise to 1200 sets up a favorable Stack-to-Pot Ratio (~2) for post-flop play, allowing me to commit easily on safe boards.

QdTd is a suited connector with potential for straights and flushes, but facing a button 3-bet to 1200 after a middle position raise and cutoff call, it's a marginal spot. Out of position in the small blind, any continuation would likely be multi-way (BB still to act), making post-flop play difficult and unprofitable with speculative equity. Pro's button 3-bet range is strong for value or isolation, and with deep stacks (effective ~5700 with Pro), implied odds don't justify the call OOP. Folding preserves stack for better positions and stronger hands in this tournament stage (hand 36/150).

Facing a squeeze 3-bet to 12bb after an open and a call, J9o is too dominated (AJ/KJ/QJ/JT) and plays poorly out of position with low realization, especially with the original raiser/caller still to act behind me and potentially come along. The price isn’t good enough to justify the reverse-implied-odds risk, and it’s not a viable 4-bet candidate versus a 57bb button stack.

I opened with pocket threes (3d 3c) to 300, and Claude called. Pro then 3-bet to 1200 from the button. This is a large raise (4x my open). Pocket threes are primarily a set-mining hand. To profitably call for set-mining, I generally need the effective stacks to be at least 10 to 15 times the amount I have to call. Here, I need to call 900 more, and Pro's remaining stack is only 4500. This is only 5:1 implied odds, which is insufficient to justify calling with a small pair that is likely crushed by Pro's 3-betting range. Furthermore, I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Folding is the mathematically sound decision.

Pro 3-bet to 1200 from the button after Flash opened and I flatted. ATo is a marginal hand facing a 3-bet, especially since I'll be out of position against Pro postflop. The squeeze from the button could be light, but I'd need to call 900 more (about 46% of the current pot) with a hand that doesn't play well OOP. Without strong reads that Pro is squeezing wide, folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the UTG position with 8-7 offsuit. This is a weak starting hand in an early position, and I should avoid playing it to maintain my stack for more favorable spots. Folding is the standard professional play here.
QJo is a solid opening hand from middle position. Standard raise to 250 to take down blinds or build a pot with a playable hand.

I am in the Cutoff with A9o facing a 2.5x raise from Claude in Middle Position. Claude has been active and opening frequently, but A9o is a marginal hand that is often dominated by an MP opening range (which includes AT+, AJ, AQ, AK). Calling could invite squeezes from the button or blinds, and playing A9o post-flop is difficult without hitting hard. 3-betting is an option as a bluff due to the Ace blocker, but it's high variance. Given my healthy stack of ~76bb, I can wait for better spots. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

Preflop on the button facing a raise to 250 from Claude in middle position. Our hand Th2c is offsuit, disconnected, and has very poor equity against a standard MP opening range. Calling would be speculative at best with bad implied odds due to the hand's lack of playability and domination risk. No blockers for a 3-bet bluff, and with two players to act behind (SB and BB who defend wide), folding is the correct low-variance play to preserve stack early in the tournament. Pot odds don't justify a call given our ~25-30% equity vs range.

Claude opens from middle position and we’re in the small blind with 72o, a hand that plays extremely poorly out of position and is easily dominated. Calling invites the big blind to come along and puts us in tough postflop spots with very low equity; 3-bet bluffing with this holding is unnecessary and too low in playability versus a non-late-position open. Best EV is to preserve stack and fold.

Claude opened to 250 from middle position. I’m in the big blind with 3♥6♥ — a weak but suited hand with flush/straight potential. Calling 150 (total 250) is cheap given the pot (400) — ~27% immediate pot odds to see a flop — and I have postflop positional leverage with deep stacks (12k). I won’t 3‑bet light with this hand; I’ll call to see a flop and play cautiously: fold to large, continued aggression if I miss; continue or build the pot only with strong draws or made hands (two pair/trips/nut flush).

I hold 3♥6♥ on a Kh Kd 3d flop — that’s two pair (Kings and Treys). It’s a reasonably strong but vulnerable hand: any K in Claude’s MP open-raise range gives him trips and pocket 3s/33 give him a full house. The flop is very dry (no meaningful draws), so my priority is pot control and information rather than building the pot. I will check to keep the pot manageable, induce bluffs or thin value bets, and call small-to-medium C-bets to reevaluate on the turn; I will fold to large raises that strongly indicate Kx/full house/quads.

I have QJo on a KK3 board with two diamonds. No pair, just overcards. This is a great c-bet spot - the paired King board is very dry and hits my perceived range better than Minni's calling range.
