A small c-bet around 1/3 pot should get folds from hands that missed. If called or raised, I can re-evaluate on later streets.

I have 3♥6♥ on Kh Kd 3d — that’s two pair (Kings and Threes). It’s a strong but vulnerable holding: any K in Claude’s range gives trips and a preflop 33 gives a full house. Claude opened from MP preflop and now leads small (200 into a 750 pot) — a probing/thin-value sizing that often includes Kx, medium pairs, or bluffs. My priority is pot control and to extract value from worse Ks and hands that will call a small bet, not to inflate the pot and commit against trips/quads. Calling 200 keeps his weaker holdings and bluffs in, keeps the pot manageable, and lets me reevaluate on the turn (fold to heavy action if the turn strongly indicates trips/full house). Therefore I will call the 200.

Turn: I've made a full house — Kings full of Threes (K K K 3 3) with 3♥6♥. Against Claude (MP opener who c-bet the flop small) his range includes Kx, medium pairs and occasional bluffs. The only hand that beats me is the one remaining king (quads); any 3 in his hand ties. My objective is to extract value from worse hands (QQ/TT/ JJ, other pairs, some overcards that will still call a bet) while keeping the pot reasonable against the tiny quads risk. I will bet for value rather than check‑back (checking risks letting him off cheaply or giving a free card). A sizing of ~2/3 pot (~650 into 950) should coax calls from second-best hands without overcommitting if Claude has an unlikely quads/strong raise. If he raises huge or jams, I’ll re-evaluate (heavy raise likely represents the last king); if he calls or makes a small raise I’ll proceed to extract more on the river.

The board is now trip Kings (KKK3). Minni donk-bets 650 into 900. I have QJ high - no pair, no real equity. With three Kings on board, Minni could have a King (quads), a pocket pair (full house), or 33 (full house). My QJ has zero showdown value beyond the trip Kings on board. Calling here hoping to bluff river is -EV. Easy fold.

A7o from UTG is marginal but playable in 6-max. With decent stack depth (76.5bb), I can open-raise to 250 and take down blinds or play postflop with position info if called. Folding is also reasonable but I've been successfully aggressive with similar hands.

I am holding Queen-Three offsuit in middle position facing a raise from the UTG player (Claude). Q3o is a statistically very weak hand with poor playability and is significantly dominated by a standard UTG opening range. With four players left to act behind me and facing a raise, the only strategically sound decision is to fold immediately to preserve my stack.

43o is a complete trash hand with no high card value, no straight potential worth playing, and offsuit. Facing an UTG raise from Claude, who often raises with decent strength based on history. In cutoff, but this isn't a spot to call or 3-bet bluff with such a weak hand. Deep stacks, but folding preserves chips for premium spots to maximize long-term EV. No fold equity on a raise here justifies it.

Claude is opening from UTG, which should be a relatively strong range in 6-max. Q8o is dominated by a lot of his continues (AQ/KQ/QJ/QT, better broadways, medium pairs), and it doesn’t realize equity well even with position. Flatting also invites the blinds to squeeze and puts us in awkward multiway spots. A light 3-bet bluff here is too thin given poor blockers and Claude’s likely continue range. Best stack-preserving, EV-positive decision is to just let it go.

I’m in the small blind with K♣Q♥ facing an UTG open to 250 (2.5bb). UTG’s open from early position is likely a relatively strong range, and I’ll be out of position postflop. KQo offsuit is too good to fold but not strong enough to 3-bet light out of the small blind and bloat the pot OOP against multiple callers. Calling 200 more (250 total committed) keeps the pot manageable, preserves stack depth for postflop play, and lets me see a flop with a hand that plays well heads-up. If the big blind 3-bets large I will re-evaluate (and likely fold to a sizable 4-bet), but I’ll call now to see a flop and proceed based on texture and actions.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 2s, which is a very weak hand. Claude raised from UTG to 250, and Minni called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 600), playing such a marginal hand out of position against an early position raiser and a caller is generally a losing strategy. The risk of being dominated or facing significant post-flop pressure outweighs the price, so I will fold.

I’m OOP vs the UTG opener with K♣Q♥ on T♥ J♠ 5♦ — no pair, one live overcard (K) and only 4 clean outs (any A) to make the Broadway straight. The flop is fairly dry but hits a standard UTG c-betting/continuation range (T/J, A, KQ/KJ, some pairs). I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand: a c-bet bluff here will often be called by hands that currently beat me, and a raise is too ambitious OOP. I’ll check to control the pot and see Claude’s action. Plan: call a reasonable-sized c-bet (small-to-medium, e.g. ~≤1/2 pot) to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to a large raise or very large barrel. If he checks back I’ll take a free turn card and continue based on the new texture.

I missed the flop completely with A7o on Th Js 5d - just ace high. Minni checked to me. As the preflop aggressor, a c-bet here is standard to take down the pot. The board has two broadway cards which I could represent hitting. A small c-bet of around 200 (1/3 pot) should be enough to fold out weak hands while minimizing my loss if called.

I call. Holding K♣Q♥ on T♥ J♠ 5♦ I have two overcards plus strong straight equity (Aces and 9s give 8 clean outs). Claude (UTG) made a very small c‑bet (200 into ~800 ≈ 25%), so calling 200 is cheap and keeps the pot under control out of position. Pot odds: 200 to win ~1200 ⇒ ~16.7% required. Chance to hit an Ace or 9 on the turn is 8/47 ≈ 17% (≈31.5% to complete by the river), so this is a +EV call. Plan: call to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to a sizable raise unless I improve; if an A or 9 hits I'll play aggressively; if I pair K/Q I’ll proceed cautiously; if Claude checks back I take a free turn card.

Check. I’m out of position with K♣Q♥ and no made hand on T♥ J♠ 5♦ 6♠ — two clean overcards plus the straight outs (any A or 9, ~8 outs, ~31.5% to hit by the river). Claude opened UTG and fired a very small flop c‑bet, so his range contains many hands that will continue (T/J/x, pairs, some broadways) and won’t fold to a turn bluff very often. With only modest equity and OOP I want pot control and to realize equity cheaply: check to either take a free river if he checks, or to call a reasonable turn bet to see the river. I will fold to a large raise unless I improve, and if an A or 9 (or a clean pair) hits the river I’ll be prepared to play more aggressively.

Minni called my preflop raise and flop c-bet, now checking to me on the turn. I still have just ace high - no pair, no draw. The board now has a spade flush draw possibility. Double-barreling here with no equity is burning chips. If I bet and get called/raised again, I'm in a terrible spot. Check back, take a free card, and potentially show down ace high or hit an ace on the river.

Board: T♥ J♠ 5♦ 6♠ 4♥. I hold K♣Q♥ — missed. After calling a small UTG c‑bet on the flop we saw checks on both turn and river from Claude. I have no pair and no backdoor outs on the river, so I have essentially zero showdown improvement and only bluffing equity. UTG opened from early and then adopted a small c‑bet → pot control line, which is consistent with many medium‑strength value hands (T/J/x, pocket pairs) and also some thin bluffs. Betting the river would be a thin bluff that’s likely to be called by hands that beat me and fold only the very weakest hands; with Claude’s line I expect limited fold equity. Therefore the lowest variance + highest EV line is to check and take my hand to showdown rather than bluff‑shove or create a bloated pot out of position with no value. I’ll check/see showdown.

I have ace high on a Th Js 5d 6s 4h board. Minni called preflop and the flop c-bet, indicating they have some piece of this board. Betting here for value makes no sense - I'd only get called by better hands. Bluffing is also poor since Minni has shown willingness to call with their range. Check back and hope ace high is good, though it's unlikely.

I am in UTG position with QJo (Queen of Spades, Jack of Clubs). In a 6-max game, QJo is a marginal hand to open from early position, usually falling outside the standard opening range due to the risk of domination (by AQ, AJ, KQ, KJ) and the disadvantage of playing out of position post-flop. Given the table dynamics showing frequent 3-betting from players like Elon and Minni, opening this hand carries negative expected value. With a stack of ~76 BBs, I can afford to wait for better spots.

Preflop in middle position with Qc8s offsuit, a marginal hand with poor playability postflop, dominated by better queens or pairs. Pot is 150, blinds 50/100, to call 100. Folding is standard to avoid tough spots OOP multiway, preserving stack for better opportunities in tournament with 6 players remaining. Tight-aggressive strategy early on.

Unopened pot from the cutoff with JTo is a profitable open in 6-max: decent high-card value, straight potential, and plays much better heads-up than limped multiway. Raising also pressures the blinds and keeps initiative against the button. I’ll use a standard ~2.5x sizing to 250 to generate folds while not overinvesting versus potential 3-bets from the more aggressive players.

Button vs a cutoff open to 250 (2.5x) with 122 BB effective. T♥2♦ is a very marginal hand — too weak to 3‑bet for value and poorly suited for a thin bluff, and it’s easily dominated. Calling 250 keeps the pot manageable while preserving position postflop; I can fold to big aggression or continue when I actually hit (top pair or a strong draw). If SB/BB squeeze or FiveTwo 3‑bets big, I’ll fold this hand. Plan: call to see the flop in position, c‑fold to significant pressure when I miss, and play aggressively only if I connect or get a good read.

I am holding 2s 5d in the Small Blind. This is an extremely weak hand with almost no potential to win against a raise and a call. Facing a raise from FiveTwo (Cutoff) and a call from Minni (Button), my hand is an easy fold as it does not play well post-flop and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Conserving chips for better opportunities is the priority.
Ah4h is a suited ace in the BB. I'm getting great pot odds (150 to win 650) with a hand that has nut flush potential. Easy call to defend my big blind here.

I have Ah4h on a 7s5h6s board. I have a double gutshot straight draw - need a 3 for 3-4-5-6-7 or an 8 for 4-5-6-7-8 (8 outs). Also have backdoor heart flush draw. The board is draw-heavy with spade flush draw possible. With two opponents and only draws, checking to see a free turn card is the best play. If I bet and get raised, I'm in a tough spot with just ace high.

Flop is very connected (7-6-5 with a spade draw) and hits both callers’ ranges hard (pairs, 87/76/65/54, suited spades). With JTo and no spade we have only two overcards and weak backdoors, and betting into two players—especially with the button behind who can float/raise—will get called or punished too often. Best is to check back, realize our equity, and keep the pot small while we can still improve on turn/river.

Flop 7♠-5♥-6♠; holding T♥2♦ I’ve completely missed — no pair and no real draw (only a very unlikely backdoor straight). Both opponents checked; this is a poor spot to bluff multiway against two players with deep stacks because a raise or call is likely and I’ll be dominated or face tough decisions. Pot control is correct: check and see a cheap turn. If either opponent fires a meaningful bet on the turn I’ll fold (only consider calling very small bets if the price and reads justify it).

The 3h on the turn completes my straight! I now have 3-4-5-6-7 with my 4h. Plus I picked up a heart flush draw (Ah4h with 5h3h on board). This is a monster hand. I need to bet for value and protection - there are spade flush draws out there. Betting around 50-60% pot to build the pot and charge draws.

Turn lead from BB into two players on a very connected 7-6-5-3 board is heavily weighted toward made hands (pairs/two pair/straights) and strong draws. With JhTd we have only two overcards and no real river-improving draw (no flush possible with one heart in hand, and no meaningful straight outs). Calling 450 needs ~26% equity, but we’re closer to ~10–13% at best and our J/T outs are often not clean; raising is too ambitious multiway with Minni still to act. Best is to preserve stack and fold.

I’ve completely missed the turn with T♥2♦: no pair, no meaningful draws. Claude barreled the turn for 450 into a 1,250 pot (call = 450; pot odds ≈ 26.5%). I only have ~6 clean outs (any Ten or Deuce) ≈ 13% to improve on the river — far below the pot odds. Claude’s line (defend pre, check flop, bet turn) is consistent with many value hands and some semi-bluffs, so calling is a losing proposition and raising would be a thin, high-variance bluff with poor fold equity. Stick to my pre-stated plan to fold to significant turn aggression and preserve the stack for better spots.

UTG with Qs5h, a weak offsuit queen-high hand with poor playability and no straight or flush potential. Standard tight play pre-flop from early position dictates folding to avoid tough post-flop spots with a marginal hand. Deep stacks mean no need to speculate. Preserve stack for better opportunities.

UTG has folded and action is on us in middle position. 9♠4♦ offsuit is too weak to open from MP and performs poorly multiway; limping invites domination and tough postflop spots, while raising would be an unnecessary punt against still-to-act CO/BTN and the blinds. Preserve stack and wait for better opening candidates.

Folded to me in the cutoff with Qc2c (queen-deuce suited). It’s a weak Q but suited and has postflop playability; with UTG and MP folded I have position on the button and the blinds. Standard strategy is to open-steal here. A 3x BB open to 300 (small relative to my 12k stack) applies pressure, often takes the pot preflop, and keeps my range wide. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll concede (fold) — if I get single callers I’ll proceed cautiously postflop, c-betting appropriate textures and using position to realize equity.

I am holding 8h 2d on the button. This is an extremely weak hand. Minni, who has been quite active, raised to 300 from the cutoff. There is no justification for calling or raising with such a poor hand, even with positional advantage. I will fold and wait for a stronger hand to engage with.

K9 suited is a solid hand to defend from the small blind against a cutoff raise. Good playability with flush potential and decent high card strength. Calling is the most reasonable option here - folding would be too tight with a suited King, and 3-betting isn't necessary as K9s plays well postflop.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 2s facing a raise from the Cutoff (Minni) to 300 and a call from the Small Blind (Claude). The pot is 700 and I need to call 200 to see the flop, offering me pot odds of 4.5:1 (requires ~22% equity). However, 6c 2s is extremely weak with poor playability, making it very difficult to realize equity, especially in a multi-way pot where I act early post-flop relative to the pre-flop raiser. Despite the good price, the reverse implied odds and low hand strength make this a clear fold.

Flopped middle pair (9s) with a king kicker and backdoor flush draw on Qd9c5s. Against Minni's cutoff open, this is a medium-strength hand. Checking makes sense here - I can check-call if Minni c-bets, as I have showdown value but don't want to bloat the pot out of position with just second pair. If Minni checks back, I can potentially lead the turn.

I have top pair (Qc) with a very weak 2 kicker and a backdoor club possibility. I was the preflop aggressor and have position; Claude (SB) just flat-called, so his range is wide (worse Qx, 9x, small pairs, and draws). A half-pot c-bet (~350 into 700) extracts value from worse pairs, charges club/straight draws, and keeps the pot controllable given my poor kicker. If Claude calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check-raises big I’ll give strong consideration to folding because my kicker is weak and a large check-raise often signifies a strong made hand.

I have middle pair (9s) with king kicker and backdoor flush draw. Minni c-bet 350 into 650 pot - a fairly standard sizing. I'm getting good odds to call (350 into 1050 pot). Second pair with top kicker has decent showdown value against a standard c-bet range. Folding is too weak, raising would bloat pot out of position with a medium-strength hand. Call and reassess turn.

Turn 3s is essentially a blank. I have middle pair (9s) with king kicker. The board now has two spades but I have no spade draw. My hand has decent showdown value but isn't strong enough to lead and build the pot. Checking keeps pot controlled and lets me decide based on Minni's action - if they bet big I can fold, if they check I get a free river or can value bet thin.

I have top pair (Qc) with a very weak 2-kicker on Qd‑9c‑5s‑3s. Claude (SB) cold‑called my flop c‑bet, so his range is wide (worse Qx, 9x, 5x, medium pairs, floats). The 3♠ is a blank — it helps very few holdings — so betting again for value and to deny a clean river is correct. A half‑pot bet (~700 into 1400) will get called by worse Qx/9x/5x while keeping the pot controllable given my poor kicker. If Claude check‑raises or jams I’ll give strong consideration to folding (his line would represent sets/two‑pair/very strong hands). If he calls I’ll reevaluate on the river and proceed cautiously.

I have middle pair (9s) with king kicker. Minni bets 700 into 1400 pot (50% sizing). Getting 2800:700 (4:1) odds, I need ~20% equity. Second pair with top kicker has decent showdown value against Minni's range which includes Qx, draws, and bluffs. The 3s is a brick. Folding is too weak here - calling and evaluating river is the correct play. If Minni fires big on river I can fold, but I can't give up with this much equity against their betting range.

River completes a spade flush draw (6s brings three spades). I have middle pair with king kicker, no spade. Checking is optimal - I can't value bet as better hands call and worse hands fold. If Minni checks back, I likely win. If Minni bets, I need to evaluate sizing - a large bet likely means value (flush or Qx), small bet could be thin value or bluff.

I have top pair (Qc) but a very weak 2-kicker on Q♦-9♣-5♠-3♠-6♠. Claude (SB) cold‑called preflop and then called a half‑pot on the flop and a half‑pot on the turn — his range is wide (worse Qx, 9x, 5x, medium pairs and some spade draws). The river brings the third spade, so some of his calls turn into made flushes, but many of his call-down hands are still worse pairs or weak Qs that will pay a river bet. I want to extract value from those worse hands and charge missed draws while keeping the sizing small enough to fold out only very marginal hands and limit losses to flushes/two‑pair/sets. I will bet a half‑pot (1,400) to get called by second‑pair and worse Qx/9x while keeping the pot controllable. If Claude check‑raises or jams, I will fold — his line plus that action will usually represent a flush or very strong two‑pair/set.

River completed a flush with 6s (three spades). Minni triple-barreled: 350 flop, 700 turn, 1400 river. I have middle pair (9s) with king kicker, no spade. Getting 3:1 odds needing ~25% equity. However, Minni's range for triple-barreling heavily includes Qx (top pair) and flush completes. My second pair loses to all Qx, all flushes, and any two pair/sets. I only beat pure bluffs. Against a CO open range that c-bets three streets, my equity is likely below 25%. This sizing feels like value - fold is correct despite the pot odds.

6-handed UTG with AKo is a clear open for value and initiative. Limping invites multiway pots where AKo’s top-pair value is harder to realize and we lose fold equity. A standard UTG open that’s consistent with the table’s 250–300 sizing builds the pot for value while keeping our range uncapped versus potential 3-bets behind (from aggressive stacks like Minni/Elon).

Facing an UTG open to 275 from FiveTwo while I'm in middle position with 9h3s. This is a very weak, offsuit, disconnected hand with poor postflop playability and is often dominated by an UTG raising range. The pot is 425 and calling 275 would require roughly 39% equity to break even (275/(425+275) ≈ 39.3%), but 9-3o has far less equity versus a typical UTG range (well under 20%). I'm out of position and there's no compelling reason to invest chips here—I'll conserve my stack and fold.

I am holding pocket fours in the Cutoff. A player in early position (FiveTwo) has raised to 2.75x the big blind. Pocket fours are a speculative hand that plays best for set-mining. Given the stack depths (both over 80 big blinds), I have sufficient implied odds to call and try to flop a set. However, a call here might invite the players behind (Claude, Pro, Elon) to squeeze or overcall, making the hand harder to play. Folding is too tight, and raising is unnecessary against an UTG open. I will call to see a flop and check for set value, ready to fold if I miss and face significant aggression.

T9o on the button with two players in (FiveTwo raised, Flash called). Getting 2.5:1 pot odds to call 275 into 700. T9o plays well in position and has good playability postflop. Calling to see a flop in position is the best play here - hand is too weak to squeeze but too strong to fold on the button.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit (7h Td). The action started with an UTG raise to 275, followed by calls from the Cutoff and Button. The pot is currently 975, and I would need to call 225 to see the flop, offering me attractive pot odds of roughly 5.3 to 1. However, T7o is a very weak hand that plays poorly multi-way, and I would be playing the entire post-flop hand out of position against three or four opponents. The risk of domination (hitting a Ten with a worse kicker) and the difficulty of realizing equity from the Small Blind outweigh the immediate pot odds. Conserving chips is better than entering a bloated pot with a trash hand.

Facing a UTG raise to 275 from FiveTwo (10700 stack) and calls from Flash (8525) and Claude (7125), with Pro folded from SB. Pot 975, calling 175 more (total 275) into 1150 pot gives excellent ~5.6:1 direct pot odds. J9o is offsuit but has straight potential, decent playability multiway in a deepstacked early tournament spot (117BB effective). OOP postflop but implied odds justify speculative call vs loose callers (history shows frequent check-calls). Folding too tight given odds; raising light OOP multiway risks domination. Call to realize equity.

Hold top pair (jacks) with a weak 9 kicker on a coordinated flop featuring a heart flush draw (6h Th) and potential straight draws (e.g., 78, 89 for open-enders around 678TJ or similar). Multiway pot (4 players, pot 1150) as first to act OOP from BB. Deep stacks (100+BB effective). Betting risks isolation raises from better hands (AJ+, QJ, JT) or draws realizing equity; induces folds from worse pairs/draws we dominate. Checking controls pot size, realizes equity cheaply vs. callers, sets up check-raise vs. aggressive continuation bets from FiveTwo (UTG raiser, shows strength), and keeps speculative hands in vs. loose field history (frequent check-calls). Optimal balance exploits opponents' tendencies for thin value/overbetting postflop.

Multiway flop on J♣T♥6♥ is a board that connects with callers, so we can’t just range-bet tiny. That said, A♥K♣ gives us a strong semi-bluff: nut flush draw plus two overcards (and a gutshot to Q for Broadway). Betting applies pressure to hands like small pairs, weak Tx/6x, and some Kx/Qx floats, while building a pot we’re happy to win outright or continue barreling on hearts, Q, A, or K turns. I’ll use a solid ~1/2 pot sizing to maximize fold equity without overcommitting OOP.

I am holding pocket fours on a board of 6h-Jc-Th. This flop is very unfavorable for my hand as there are three overcards, and it is highly coordinated with various straight and flush draws. The initial raiser (FiveTwo) has bet 600 into a pot of 1150 into three other players, which suggests significant strength or a strong draw from an UTG range. I have missed my set, and with two players still to act behind me, calling is not viable as I have very little equity and no clear way to improve. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I flopped middle pair (tens) with an open-ended straight draw (Q or 8 completes). Board: 6h Jc Th, my hand: Ts 9c. With J-T-9, any Q or 8 gives me a straight. That's 8 straight outs plus potential two-pair/trips outs. Getting almost 3:1 pot odds (600 to win 1750), this is an easy call with my equity. Raising is possible but with Elon still to act behind, calling keeps the pot manageable while realizing my draw equity.

Facing a half-pot bet from UTG raiser FiveTwo after checking flop multiway, with Flash folding and Claude calling from the button. Hold top pair (jacks) with 9 kicker on a draw-heavy board (6h Jc Th: heart flush draw, open-enders like 78, 89 gutshot). Pot 2350, calling 600 offers ~3.8:1 direct odds (600 into 2350+600=2950), excellent for multiway pot OOP. Equity solid vs polarized range: beats bluffs/draws (Claude's frequent check-call history suggests possible 9x, Tx, flush/straight draws), some worse pairs; loses to better Js (AJ/KQ/JT), sets, two-pair but those less likely to bet/call this line. Raising risks isolation vs better hands, folding equity low OOP vs callers. Calling realizes equity cheaply deepstacked (100+BB), controls pot size, induces thin value/overbets later exploiting field tendencies (aggressive postflop, check-call heavy). Turn decisions based on further action.

Trips jacks (Jd with board Jc Jh) is a very strong hand on this 6h Jc Th Jh board, beating most of FiveTwo's and Claude's continuing ranges including Tx, Ax, Kx, lower pairs, and remaining draws (heart flush draws now with three hearts on board, straight draws like 78, 89). Vulnerable to full houses (TT, 66, possibly QJ sets but unlikely) but those are low-frequency in their ranges. Pot 2950, first to act OOP multiway (FiveTwo UTG bettor, Claude button caller with check-call history). Deep effective stacks (Claude ~65BB, others deeper). Betting ~2/3 pot (2000) extracts value from worse made hands (Tx, 9x pairs/draws) and charges draws, while protecting against runner-runner threats. Checking risks free cards to draws or getting outdrawn, and while pot control is nice, value is paramount with this monster. Avoids check-raise trap OOP vs aggressive opponents (FiveTwo history of turn bets). Exploits field tendencies for frequent check-calls postflop. Raises or calls likely from worse.

We have A♥K♣ on J♣ T♥ 6♥ J♥: nut flush draw (9 outs) plus a gutshot to Q (3 more outs, with Q♥ already included in the hearts), and some extra backdoor win chances if river checks through with A-high. Elon’s 2000 into ~4950 is a strong sizing, but we’re getting about 28–29% immediate pot odds to call, and our draw has enough equity + implied odds when we hit a heart/Q to continue. Raising would frequently get action from Jx (and sometimes induce a shove), risking our equity while not generating enough folds multiway (Claude still to act). Calling keeps dominated draws and worse one-pair hands in, and realizes our equity efficiently.

The board paired jacks (6h Jc Th Jh) with three hearts. My pair of tens is now nearly worthless - anyone with a J has me crushed. Elon bet 2000 and FiveTwo called, suggesting at least one likely has a jack or strong hand. My straight draw (Q or 8) gives me ~8 outs, but several are hearts that could complete opponent flushes. Even hitting my straight could lose to a full house. Pot odds are ~4.5:1 (need 18%), but reverse implied odds are terrible here - the paired board with flush possibilities makes this extremely dangerous. Folding to preserve stack.

River Qh completes the nut flush draw that was live since flop (6h Th), now four hearts on board, and pairs the board minimally while adding straight possibilities (e.g., Q9, but mainly flushes). Hold trips jacks (Jd with Jc Jh), strong but vulnerable to flushes, full houses (TT, 66, QQ unlikely from UTG range), and better trips/two-pair combos like JT (full house on turn). FiveTwo's line: UTG raise pre, flop c-bet multiway on wet board, called my turn value bet of 2000 (pot ~2950) heads-up after folds, showing strong range (Jx good kicker, JT top two turning FH, TT/66 sets, overpairs that improved or draws). Deep stacks (~80BB effective now). OOP first to act, pot 6950. Betting risks raise-all-in from better hands (flush/FH) with poor implied odds left and low fold equity vs calling station tendencies (history of check-calls, loose fields). Checking realizes equity vs his value range (beats worse Jx like J9-JT? JT FH beats, but AQ/KQ/AJ hit J but weak kicker), induces bluffs/margins, controls pot, and avoids spew on scary river. If bet behind/raises, fold; small bet calls possible. Optimal pot control/balance exploits opponents' passive check-call history long-term.

River Qh gives us the A-high flush (and Broadway), a very strong value hand. Elon’s turn aggression followed by a river check on a 4-heart, paired board usually caps him away from boats a decent amount and puts a lot of his range on one-heart hands (made flushes) plus some Jx/straight bluff-catchers. We should value-bet rather than check back, but avoid an overbet that folds out weaker flushes. A ~40–45% pot sizing targets calls from K-high through medium flushes and occasional Jx/straight hands, while keeping us flexible to fold if he springs a large check-raise representing a full house.

River Qh completes the nut flush draw (hearts now dominant with 6h Th Jh Qh), adding straight outs (e.g., 89TJQ broadway) and possible full houses. Hold trips jacks (Jd with Jc Jh) with weak 9s kicker, strong pre-river but now second-best to any flush, TT/66/QQ sets (FH), or JT (boat). FiveTwo's line is nutted: UTG preflop raise (tight range), flop cbet multiway on wet board (6h Jc Th), called my turn 2000 value bet heads-up (pot-committing strength, ~5100 stack left now), then donk bets 3000 (~1/3 pot) after my check on scary river. Bluff range thin (missed draws unlikely to call turn OOP vs my bet and now bet river), value-heavy (Ahx, Khx, flushes; overpairs unlikely post-turn call). Pot 9950, call 3000 (~4.3:1 odds, need ~19% equity) but <10% vs polarized nut range (beats pure air/worst pairs, loses to all value). Deep effective stacks but no implied odds left river OOP. Folding realizes no equity spew, preserves 8950 stack (~90BB) for better spots in tournament. Exploits FiveTwo's history of strong play (e.g., Hand 11 turn nuts call, Hand 26 value bets). Consistent with pre-river pot control plan vs aggressive field.

UTG in a 6-handed game with K♠9♥ — a marginal, easily-dominated offsuit king out of position. There are five players to act behind, including very deep and aggressive stacks who will 3‑bet or call wide, so postflop I’ll often be out of position and forced into tough decisions with second‑pair or dominated K‑pairs. Limping is passive and inviting multiway action; raising is spewy with little realizable equity. Prefer to conserve chips and wait for a stronger, more positionally favorable spot — fold.

I am in middle position with Q7o. This is a very weak starting hand and does not justify a raise or a call, especially with four active players remaining behind me in the hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

6h5d is a weak offsuit hand in the cutoff. With 3 players left to act behind me, this isn't profitable to open. I've seen aggressive 3-bets from FiveTwo and Elon, making speculative hands like this even less attractive. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with 7c 5s (75o), which is a very weak hand. Although the action has folded to me, offering a potential opportunity to steal the blinds, 75o is typically below the standard opening range even for the Button. My opponents in the blinds (Elon and FiveTwo) have healthy stacks, with FiveTwo being the chip leader, making them capable of defending or 3-betting. With 75 big blinds, I am not in a desperate position to force action with such low equity cards. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better spots.

Preflop in the small blind with 4d Ts offsuit, a weak hand with poor playability, low equity against a big blind defending range, and no strong stealing potential given the gaps and offsuit nature. Everyone folded to me, but completing the small blind or raising risks difficult postflop spots against FiveTwo's likely wide defense range. Pot odds are not compelling enough for a limp, and folding preserves stack for better spots in this deep-stacked tournament phase (hand 42/150). Standard discipline: fold marginal hands from SB in unopened pots.

I am in the Under The Gun (UTG) position with Queen-Deuce of diamonds. In a 6-max game, this hand is significantly below the standard opening range for early position. Opening such a weak hand would likely lead to difficult post-flop situations out of position against several active players. To preserve my stack and wait for better opportunities, I will fold.
2c 5h is a very weak hand - unsuited, disconnected low cards. From middle position with players behind me, this is a clear fold. No reason to invest chips with such poor equity.

I am in the Cutoff with Ace-Eight suited (A8d). The action has folded to me. This is a strong hand to open-raise with from late position, aiming to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. My stack is healthy (75 BB). The players in the blinds (FiveTwo and Minni) are deep-stacked and aggressive, so I expect them to defend, but A8s plays well post-flop, particularly in position. A standard raise size of 2.5BB (250) is appropriate to balance value and risk if 3-bet.

On the button with 3d6d, suited connectors that play well postflop with deep stacks and position. Pro's cutoff raise range is wide after folds, including many hands I dominate or outdraw. Excellent implied odds if we flop a draw or two pair/straight. Pot odds are favorable (calling 250 into 400, becoming 650). Prefer calling to realize equity multiway or heads-up in position rather than folding a playable hand or 3-betting a marginal bluff candidate. Blinds may defend wide, but position advantage remains strong.

We’re in the SB facing a CO open and a BTN flat. With 9♣7♦ offsuit we have poor playability and are guaranteed to be out of position in a likely multiway pot, with a decent chance the BB squeezes as well. The pot odds are tempting, but this hand realizes equity badly from the SB versus two ranges. It’s also not a good 3-bet squeeze candidate (no blockers, not suited, dominated when called). Best stack-maximizing line is to release and wait for a better spot.

Facing a cutoff open to 250 with the button calling, I'm in the big blind with 9♣5♦ — a weak, offsuit, poorly-connected hand. Calling 150 into a multiway pot out of position gives me very little equity and will leave me with difficult postflop decisions against ranges that often dominate me. A squeeze-raise to 400 is also a bad look (no blockers, likely to be 3-bet or called and played OOP). With a healthy stack and plenty of future spots, the prudent play is to fold and preserve chips.

I am out of position on the flop against the Button. The board is extremely wet: monotone spades and connected (7s 8s Ts). While I have middle pair with top kicker (Pair of 8s, Ace kicker), I have no spades in my hand. This board texture interacts very strongly with a button calling range (flushes, straights, sets, two pairs, and combo draws). Betting here turns my hand into a bluff catcher or folds out worse hands while getting action from better ones. Checking allows me to control the pot size. I intend to check-call a small bet or re-evaluate on the turn, but generally, I want to get to showdown cheaply or fold if the action gets heavy.

Flop board 7s8sTs is extremely draw-heavy with flush and straight draws galore. Pro's cutoff open and check suggests possible weakness, missed draws, or trap with strong hand, but my 3d6d has zero equity: no pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), no straight draw (gutshot unlikely), just backdoor diamond flush. With position on button heads-up, SPR ~13 (pot 650, stacks ~8k/7k), checking behind realizes no equity for free, controls pot, avoids bluffing into a range that hits this board often or calls stations. Betting air risks stack with no fold equity on this wet board; prefer seeing turn cheaply to reassess. Tournament stage favors discipline over spew.

I have turned Top Two Pair (Aces and Eights) on a board of [7s 8s Ts Ah]. The flop was checked through, which caps the villain's range, making it less likely he has a flush or a set, as those hands often bet that wet texture or raise pre-flop. The Ace on the turn favors my range as the pre-flop raiser. I need to bet for value to extract chips from worse Aces (Ax) and pairs like Tx, as well as to protect my hand against single spade draws or straight draws (J, 9, 6) that might take a free card if I check. A bet of roughly half the pot (350 into 650) balances getting called by worse hands while charging draws appropriately.

Facing a half-pot bet on the turn after both checked the very draw-heavy flop 7s8sTs. My 3d6d is complete air: no pair, no draws, no backdoors that hit. Pro's CO open, flop check could be weakness or trap, but turn bet after Ah (a reasonable card that could improve Ax hands or block some draws) suggests value or semi-bluff from range that connects better than mine. With ~7.5:1 odds (350 into 1000), but zero equity and no fold equity bluffing over him later without improving, folding preserves stack in tournament. Position doesn't help with no hand. Discipline over curiosity.

A2o from UTG is a marginal hand. While the ace has value, the deuce kicker is weak and being offsuit limits playability. From early position in a 6-handed game, I should be playing tight. This is a fold.

Current hand is KJo in Middle Position with 6 players remaining. This is a standard opening hand in a 6-max format when folded to. The stack depth is ~79BB, allowing for normal play. My recent image has been relatively solid. A raise is preferred over limping to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or play in position against the blinds. While there are aggressive big stacks behind (FiveTwo on Button), folding KJo here is too tight. A standard open size of 2.5x (250) is consistent with my previous play (e.g., Hand #20, Hand #43).

Pre-flop in cutoff facing a 2.5x raise from middle position Pro with 4d7s offsuit, a complete trash hand with poor playability, low equity, and dominated against any reasonable raising range. No suitedness, no high cards, no straight potential worth chasing. Stacks are deep but folding avoids spew and preserves stack for better spots. Button and blinds behind add multiway risk. Standard fold.

We’re on the button with T7 suited versus a 2.5bb MP open and ~75bb effective. This hand plays well in position with good playability (straight/flush potential) and decent implied odds, while a 3-bet bluff is less attractive here because we have no blockers and Pro has shown willingness to 4-bet/continue with stronger ranges. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes equity in position, and avoids getting forced into a high-variance preflop spot against the opener and possible blind squeezes.

I’m in the SB with Q♠8♥ facing a MP open to 250 and a button call (pot 650, I need to call 200). Q8o is a marginal, often-dominated hand and is very difficult to play out of position against both the opener and a caller. Calling for 200 into 650 gives ~23.5% equity breakeven, but in a multiway pot versus these ranges my practical equity and playability are poor. A 3-bet bluff isn’t attractive with callers behind and deep stacks that can call or 4-bet. Fold and wait for a better spot to put chips in.

I am in the Big Blind holding 5-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Pro raised from middle position to 2.5x, and FiveTwo called on the button. While I only need to call 150 more into a pot of 650, which gives me over 4:1 pot odds, the playability of this hand post-flop is extremely poor, especially out of position against two opponents. It is better to preserve my stack and wait for a stronger range rather than chasing with garbage hands.

I raised pre-flop with KJo and was called by the button (FiveTwo). The flop comes Ac-Ts-9d. This texture hits a flat-calling range very well (sets of 9s/Ts, AT, strong broadways like QJ, KQ, J9, T9). While I have range advantage on strong Aces (AK, AQ), my specific hand is weak (King high with a gutshot straight draw to the nuts). Being out of position against a deep-stacked opponent who has shown tendencies to be sticky (calling down in Hand #18), checking is the best option to control the pot size. I do not want to bloat the pot with a marginal draw that likely has little fold equity against a range that connects so well with this board.

We have second pair (T) on an A-high board with only a weak backdoor flush, and Pro’s range contains a lot of hands that checked to pot-control but are still ahead (JJ/QQ/KK, strong Ax traps). Betting mainly folds out hands we already beat (KQ/KJ/QJ) while getting called by better too often, and it exposes us to an awkward check-raise versus a shorter stack. In position, checking back keeps the pot manageable, realizes our showdown value, and lets us evaluate turn action/texture before committing chips.

On the turn, the board is Ac Ts 9d 9s. The pot is 650. The 9s pairs the board, which connects well with the opponent's button calling range (98s, T9s, J9s, A9). Since the opponent checked back the flop, their range is capped (unlikely to have strong Ax or sets), but they likely still have showdown value (Tx, weak Ax, or underpairs) that will not fold to a single bet. My hand is King-high with a gutshot to the nut straight (needing a Queen). Betting has little fold equity against a 9 or T, and building a pot out of position with just a gutshot against the chip leader is risky. Checking allows me to try to see a free river card to hit my straight or safe-fold if the opponent bets, minimizing losses.

Pro has checked flop and now checked turn on A-T-9-9, which caps him somewhat but still leaves plenty of hands that beat our pair of tens (Ax that’s pot-controlling, JJ/QQ/KK). If we bet, most worse hands (missed broadways, small pairs) tend to fold, while better hands continue—so we turn a decent showdown hand into a low-EV value/protection bet and open ourselves up to an awkward check-raise. In position, the best way to realize equity is to check back, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate river (often taking a showdown, occasionally bluff-catching if sizing is reasonable).

On the River, the board is Ac Ts 9d 9s 6s. I hold Kh Jd (King-high). The pot is 650. The opponent (FiveTwo) has checked back the flop and turn, indicating a capped range, likely consisting of weak Ax, Tx, small pairs, or missed straight draws (like QJ, KQ). The arrival of the third spade and the paired board makes it difficult to represent a strong hand after checking two streets. Bluffing has low success probability against a sticky chip leader who might look up a bet with a pair of Tens or even eights. Checking gives me a chance to get to showdown cheaply; I might win against a missed draw (like QJ) or chop with KQ. If the opponent bets, I will fold as I only have King-high.

We have decent showdown value with second pair (T) on A-T-9-9-6, and Pro’s triple-check line keeps in a lot of hands that either beat us (Ax, JJ/QQ/KK, better Tx) or are pure air that will usually just fold if we bet. There aren’t many realistic worse hands that can call a river bet (most underpairs and missed broadways fold), so betting turns our hand into thin/negative value and exposes us to the rare but disastrous check-raise from a slowplayed Ax/9x/flush. Best EV is to take the free showdown.

I hold Ace-King offsuit (As Kc) in the Under the Gun (UTG) position, which is a premium starting hand. Given the table dynamics and stack depths (I have ~76bb), I want to open the pot to build value and thin the field. My standard opening raise size has been 2.5bb (250 chips), which balances well with my range. I will raise to 250.

Preflop, facing a UTG raise to 250 (2.5x BB) from Pro, who has shown aggression in early position in past hands. My hand is A5o, which is offsuit with a poor kicker and dominated by any Ax or better pairs in UTG range. Middle position OOP makes calling poor for implied odds, and 3-betting is not justified without blockers or suited connectivity. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in a deep tournament with 105 hands left.

UTG opening range is relatively strong, and T5o is dominated, plays poorly multiway, and can’t realize equity well even in position. With no good 3-bet bluff properties (no blockers/connectivity) and plenty of better defend candidates, the highest-EV decision is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

Pro opened UTG to 250 (2.5x). I’m on the button with T♥2♣ — offsuit, poorly connected and frequently dominated by an UTG opening range. Calling 250 into the 400 pot would make the pot 650, so I’d need ~38% equity to break even vs Pro’s range — T2o doesn’t come close and has very poor postflop playability. There are still SB/BB to act, increasing the chance of multiway action or a squeeze. A 3-bet bluff isn’t attractive either (no useful blockers, weak hand). With a healthy 133bb stack it’s better to preserve chips and wait for a real spot to exploit position. Fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c 6s. Pro has opened with a raise to 2.5bb from UTG. This generally represents a strong range. 5-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to defend with, especially out of position against the initial raiser. Given the lack of playability and the disadvantageous position, I will fold and preserve my stack.

K4o is a weak hand to defend against an UTG raise. Pro opened from early position indicating strength. While I'm getting 2.67:1 odds, K4o plays poorly postflop and will often be dominated when I hit the king. Better to fold and wait for a stronger spot.

UTG in a 6-handed game with 3d4s offsuit is a complete trash hand with very low equity against any calling or raising range. No straight potential strong enough to warrant limping or raising from early position, and folding preserves my deep stack (86.5BB) for premium spots. Opponents like Flash, Minni, and Claude have been aggressive preflop, so no need to enter the pot voluntarily with this. Standard fold.

We’re 6-handed in middle position with 9♠2♣ offsuit (very poor high-card strength, disconnected, and heavily dominated when we make a pair). Limping would just donate EV and invite isolation; opening is unnecessary risk with little fold equity versus competent opponents behind (BTN/CO) and awkward postflop playability. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Cutoff with Ts8d (offsuit) — a marginal hand, but I have position and deep stacks (133bb). The pot is only 150 and the standard open-size at this table has been ~300; I prefer to open here to seize the initiative and pick up the blinds rather than limp and let the button act. Raise to 300 gives good fold equity against the blinds and keeps my range wide from cutoff. If I face a large 3-bet from aggressive players (Claude/Pro) I’ll fold this hand; I’ll continue post‑flop with position if called, c-betting dry boards and playing cautiously on wet boards or heavy resistance. In short: open-raise to 300 to capitalize on position and table dynamics.

I am on the button with Ace-Five suited, which is a premier hand for 3-betting as a semi-bluff. It has excellent board coverage, nut-flush potential, and an Ace blocker. Minni has been active from late positions, so a 3-bet here allows me to take control of the pot, potentially win it pre-flop, or play a bloated pot in position with a hand that flops well. I will raise to 900 to put pressure on the cutoff and the blinds.

Ac7c is a decent suited ace but facing a 3-bet to 900 from Flash after Minni's open. I'm out of position in the SB, which significantly reduces my playability. While the suited ace has some equity, playing OOP in a 3-bet pot with a marginal hand is -EV. Flash's 3-bet range from the button should be reasonably strong. Better to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 7s (unsuited connector). The action involves a raise to 300 from the Cutoff (Minni) and a 3-bet to 900 from the Button (Flash). I would need to call 800 chips into a pot of 1350. My hand is far too weak to cold call a 3-bet, especially out of position against two players. The 87o hand performs poorly in 3-bet pots and is easily dominated. The correct play is to preserve my stack of 7700 and fold.

I opened to 300 to seize the blinds, but Flash (on the button) 3-bet to 900, which puts me out of position post‑flop. T♠8♦ is a marginal, often dominated hand vs a button 3‑bet range (pocket pairs, broadways, some suited connectors) and has poor playability OOP. Pot odds to call 600 into a 1350 pot require ~31% equity — T8o rarely has that vs a 3‑betting range, especially heads‑up with aggressive post‑flop potential. A 4‑bet to the min (1500) would be too thin and a call leaves me in a difficult OOP spot. With 13000 behind and better spots to pick, folding is the least costly, highest EV choice here.

6-handed UTG, Q9 suited is comfortably within an opening range: it has good playability, can make strong top pairs and nutty draws, and benefits from taking initiative rather than limping into a multiway pot out of position. Given typical sizing in this game (250–300 opens), I’ll use a standard 2.5x open to pressure the blinds and define ranges while keeping my range balanced.

Hand: Th2h — a weak, poorly connected suited hand. Facing an UTG open to 250 from FiveTwo in a 6-max game, their opening range is relatively strong and Th2h is often dominated (Tx, A/Kx) or has poor playability postflop. Pot odds for a call are ~250/(400+250) ≈ 38%, but this hand’s equity vs a standard UTG range is well below that, and implied odds are limited because paired tens/twos are frequently second-best. There’s also the risk of a squeeze/3-bet from players behind which would put me in a difficult spot out of position. With a 130bb stack I should conserve chips for better spots in position or against weaker ranges — fold is the prudent, +EV choice here.

I am in the Cutoff with Jack-Ten offsuit. The chip leader, FiveTwo, has raised from UTG. While the raise is small (2.5x), Jack-Ten offsuit is a marginal hand that is likely dominated by a standard UTG opening range, which includes better Jacks, Tens, and big cards like AK/AQ. Additionally, there are several aggressive players behind me (Claude, Pro, and Elon) who could 3-bet, putting me in a difficult position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I want to avoid high-variance spots with mediocre hands against the table captain, folding is the most disciplined play.

65o on the button facing UTG raise. This is a weak offsuit connector that doesn't play well postflop. With two players still to act behind me (SB and BB could squeeze), and facing a UTG raise which typically represents a stronger range, folding is the disciplined play. Not worth 250 chips to speculate with this marginal holding.

I hold Jd 2c in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand (J2 offsuit). FiveTwo raised from UTG to 250, which indicates a strong range. I am out of position and my hand has poor playability and equity against an UTG opening range. Even though the price to call is relatively cheap, defending with J2o is a losing play in the long run. The correct action is to fold.

AQo is a premium hand, strong enough to 3-bet out of position against an UTG raiser. FiveTwo's UTG range is likely tight but not uncallable, and AQo has good equity and blockers. After multiple folds, it's heads-up. A min-raise to 400 is too small and invites calls; a standard 3x raise size to 750-800 builds the pot while maintaining fold equity. OOP calling risks playing postflop awkwardly with position disadvantage, so 3-betting polarizes and takes initiative. Deep stacks allow for flexibility postflop if called. Folding is too tight for AQo here.

Elon is 3-betting from the big blind to 800 versus my UTG open. The sizing is reasonable and I’m getting a good price (550 to win 1100), so I only need ~33% equity. Q9s has solid playability (can make strong draws and reasonable top pairs) and I cover Elon, giving me implied odds when I make a big hand. A 4-bet is unattractive because Q9s has poor blockers and I can’t profitably continue versus a shove given the effective stack, so the best line is to defend by calling and play postflop cautiously versus strong continued aggression.

Pre-flop I 3-bet AQo OOP vs UTG raise, isolating with a strong hand that blocks AA/AK. FiveTwo's call indicates a premium range: QQ+, AK, JJ/TT, perhaps AQ/KQ. Flop 3h-8c-9s is a good c-betting spot for my range – low, uncoordinated board that misses many overpairs but favors Ax hands and overpairs in my 3-bet range. I have ace-high showdown value and block top pairs. No draws for me, but board has some gutshots (J10, 107) possible in villain's wider UTG calling range. OOP, I need to take initiative; checking folds out equity and allows villain to bet wide. Half-pot bet ~800 sizes well for value from worse Ax/Qx, protection, and fold equity vs draws/bluffs. Deep stacks allow post-flop flexibility. Betting polarizes and continues story of strength.

We flop top pair (9) with a solid kicker (Q) plus a small backdoor heart draw. In a BB 3-bet pot this is too strong to fold to a small ~1/3-pot c-bet, and calling keeps in Elon's AK/AQ-type air and avoids inflating the pot when his value range (overpairs/sets) has us in rough shape. With the SPR already getting low, I’m happy to call and re-evaluate on the turn (continue on most bricks, be cautious versus big turn barrels on A/K/T/8/9).

Turn board brings the 4s, adding a spade flush draw and completing gutshots like 5-6 or 5-7, making the board drawier. My AQo remains ace-high with no pair, backdoor spade potential with Qs, but primarily showdown value. Villain (FiveTwo, UTG 3-bet caller) likely holds a premium like QQ+, AK, JJ-TT, sets, or two-pair that flop hit decently (88/99) and continues to call flop c-bet. Betting thin for value risks bloating pot OOP with a hand that rarely gets called by worse (Ax unlikely in their range) and folds out bluffs; overpairs may call but raise draws/pairs. Raising unnecessary without initiative to regain. Checking is best: pot control with marginal strength, induces bluffs from missed draws, check-backs from cautious strong hands, realizes equity at showdown cheaply. If bet into, small bet callable with ace-high blockers; large bet folds to better/polarized range. Maintains balance in 3-bet range (overpairs bet, this checks). Deep stacks (70bb effective) support post-turn flexibility on river.

Elon c-bet flop and then checked this low turn in a 3-bet pot at an SPR ~1.6, which often caps him toward AK/AQ (sometimes with spades), JJ/TT, and occasional slowplays. With top pair (9) and a strong kicker (Q), I want to deny equity to overcards/spade draws and get value from worse one-pair hands while keeping his range wide. A smaller turn stab accomplishes that without auto-committing me against a polar check-jam range (sets/overpairs).

Turn board 3h8c9s4s brings a second spade, giving me the Q-high flush draw (9 outs) plus ace-high top blocker and no showdown issues. Equity is excellent (~40%+ vs villain's UTG 3-bet calling range of QQ+, AK, JJ-TT, 98/87, which flop-called my c-bet: overpairs, pairs 99/88, some draws). Facing 1250 into 4550 (~28% pot), pot odds ~3.6:1 require ~22% equity, which I crush. Deep effective stacks (70bb) provide massive implied odds if flush hits (river jam potential for stack-off). OOP after check, calling controls pot size vs value, induces bluffs/checks river from cautious hands, realizes cheap showdown with ace-high if bricked. Raising semi-bluff folds out overpairs/weak pairs (bad) while risks stacks-off vs made hands (straights 65/75, sets, two-pair 98/34); villain's aggression (UTG call, flop call, turn bet) indicates strength unlikely to fold. Folding too tight for FD + A blocker. Call standard, balance 3-bet range (value raises, this calls draws/blockers). Reevaluate river: check-call/check-fold or bomb if flush.

River Td is a brick for my QsAd: no spade (missed flush draw, now pure ace-high showdown), no pair, but Ad blocks villain's top of range (AA/AK/AQ) and Qs backdoor blocker. Board 3h8c9s4sTd is draw-heavy with possible straights (e.g., 56/57/67 for 5-6-7-8-9), completed gutshots, flush draws missed, pairing nothing. Pot 5800, my stack 5775 (~pot-sized), villain FiveTwo effective 12575 but action heads-up. Villain's range (UTG open-call BB 3bet pre, flop call OOP vs c-bet, turn donk bet after my check): polarized strong - overpairs QQ+/KK (resistant to 3bet but call flop/turn), sets/two-pair (33/88/99/98/93/84/3T? unlikely), value straights (56s/67s suited connectors in UTG?), or turn semi-bluffs (spade draws like AsXs, JTs, etc.) now bricked. My ace-high has ~25-35% equity vs value range (beats AK/AQ/Ax worse pairs, some bluffs/draws), blocks nuts. OOP first to act: betting thin for value (worse Ax unlikely in range, overpairs raise/call) risks bloating pot OOP vs jams, low fold equity vs strong hands. Check best: pot control, induces bluffs from missed spades/straights (villain turn bet reps value but can barrel air), check-raises value (rare OOP first), realizes cheap showdown. If villain bets small (~1/3-1/2 pot), call favorable (odds ~3-4:1, equity sufficient + blockers); larger bet (~pot+), fold to polarized range (beats bluffs but crushed by value). Balances 3bet range (value bets river, this checks/calls). Deep stacks/tourney phase favors post-flop play over spew. Check, exploit villain's aggression range.

River goes check to me in a 3-bet pot after Elon c-bet flop, checked turn, and then check-called my 1250. That line is heavily weighted to (1) overpairs that took pot-control (TT–AA), and (2) some AK/AQ combos (often with spades) that picked up equity on the turn and continued.

On this Td river, the hands that comfortably call a bet are mostly better than Q9 (overpairs, sets, occasional T9), while the hands I beat (AK/AQ misses) usually just fold to any meaningful bet. So betting is thin and risks value-owning myself, plus invites a check-jam with his remaining ~2900 that I can’t profitably call.

Best play is to take the showdown with a medium-strength top pair and realize equity versus his missed big-card/spade holdings.

UTG in a 6‑max spot with A♦9♣ — a playable but non‑premium ace. Stacks are deep (130 BB), so I want to seize initiative and thin the field rather than limp. The table has frequent open raises and loose callers, so a standard 3x BB open is appropriate to get value from worse aces/broadways and discourage multiway pots. I’ll raise to 300 (3×BB). Plan: c‑bet most flops that favor my range, proceed cautiously on highly connected/paired boards, and fold to a large, polarized 3‑bet/4‑bet unless the sizing or opponent reads justify calling.

I am holding King-Deuce offsuit (Kc 2s) in middle position. Minni has opened from under-the-gun with a raise to 300. K2o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly against an early position opening range, which typically consists of strong pairs, big aces, and high Broadway cards. With several players still to act behind me and no significant reason to bluff or overplay this hand, folding is the standard and most profitable decision to preserve my stack.

T6o is a weak offsuit hand facing an UTG raise. Minni has been active but an UTG open typically represents strength. T6o doesn't have the suited/connected qualities to justify calling, and I'm out of position against remaining players. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with QTo. Minni, who has been playing an active and loose-aggressive strategy, opens to 300 (3BB) from UTG. While Minni's range is likely wider than a standard UTG range, QTo is a historically troublesome hand to flat call with against an early position raise due to reverse implied odds (often dominated by AQ, KQ, AJ, AT). Calling also risks squeezes from the blinds. Despite having position, the hand strength is insufficient to profitably call a 3x raise, and 3-betting is too loose here. The prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Hand is 3d2c, complete trash: offsuit, unconnected, low cards with no playability. Facing a UTG raise to 300 from Minni, who has been aggressive and often has strong ranges there based on history (e.g., frequent raises preflop that get folds or win pots). Pot 450, to call 250 (5.5:1 odds), but equity vs her range is abysmal (<20%). SB position is poor for defense. Stack 57BB, no need to speculate with garbage. Standard fold to preserve chips for better spots in tournament with 102 hands left.

Facing a 3x UTG open while out of position, 9♠3♠ is simply too far down the defending range. It’s frequently dominated (9x vs better 9x), has poor playability on many flops, and the reverse implied odds are high when we make weak pairs. Even though the direct price is decent (200 to win 650 after calling), this hand won’t realize equity well OOP versus a strong UTG range. Best decision is to preserve chips and fold.

I am in the UTG position with 6-5 offsuit. This is one of the weakest hands to be dealt in the first position of a 6-max game. Strategic standard play dictates a fold here to preserve my stack for better spots, as the probability of being outkicked or facing a 3-bet from players in better positions is very high.
93 offsuit is a very weak hand. From middle position with 4 players still to act behind me, this is a clear fold. No reason to invest chips with such poor holdings.

I am in the Cutoff with 2c4c. Although action folded to me, this hand is extremely weak (low suited gapper) and likely dominated. With three players left to act behind me, including a large chip leader in the Small Blind, the success rate of a steal is low and the hand plays poorly post-flop if called. Folding is the standard and correct play.

6-handed NLHE 50/100, button with 57BB stack. Q6o is a weak offsuit queen with poor kicker and no connectivity or suited potential. Button open-raising range is wide (40-50% in 6-max), but Q6o is below standard opens like Q9o+ due to domination risk vs better Qx and low playability OOP if defended. Folds to me from UTG, MP, CO increase steal opportunity, but blinds are deep (182BB SB FiveTwo, 130BB BB Minni who is frequently aggressive preflop from history). Limping poor as invites squeezes and denies initiative. Raising risks bloating pot OOP with marginal hand. Folding preserves stack for superior spots in tournament with 150 hands goal to maximize.

Folds to me in the SB with AKo versus only the BB left. Limping gives up initiative and lets BB realize equity too easily; AKo dominates BB’s calling range and benefits from building the pot preflop while picking up the blinds a good % of the time. I’ll use a standard SB open size that pressures the BB but still gets calls from worse aces/broadways: raise to 300 total.

FiveTwo (SB) opened to 300 and everyone else folded — I’m in the BB with 9♣8♠. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total committed). FiveTwo has been opening a wide range from the blinds and has a big stack, so I expect a fairly wide raising range. 9‑8 offsuit is marginal but has decent heads‑up playability and reasonable equity vs a wide range, and with deep stacks I can realize implied odds. A 3‑bet bluff OOP with this hand is unattractive; folding is too nitty given the pot odds (~33% break‑even). I’ll call to see a flop and proceed based on texture (fold to big C‑bets on dry boards, float or continue on coordinated boards, value bet or play for stacks if I hit).

Heads-up SB vs BB as the preflop raiser, I can apply a high c-bet frequency, but this 7-T-6 rainbow-ish connected board hits BB’s defending range reasonably well (pairs, Tx, 8-9, 9J, etc.). With AKo I still have two clean overcards plus a backdoor nut-flush draw (Ad on 7d), so a small continuation bet is ideal: it pressures BB’s pure air/overcards, denies some equity, and keeps sizing low so I can comfortably continue on A/K/diamond turns and give up on bad runouts when called.

I flopped a 10‑high straight with 9♣8♠ on 7♦‑10♠‑6♣ — essentially the nut on this texture (only another 9‑8 ties). FiveTwo (SB) fired a small bet (200) into the pot, which looks like a wide continuation bet. With a made straight and deep stacks, I want to build the pot and extract value from top pairs, single tens, and other worse hands that will call. I’ll raise to charge those hands and deny free cards that could chop or improve someone to a tie; if FiveTwo 3‑bets big I’ll re‑evaluate but I’m comfortable committing more with the nut. Raising to 800 total is a strong value raise here.

I’m getting a solid price: calling 600 more to win a pot that’ll be 2200 gives me ~27% needed. Even though this 7-T-6 board connects well with BB’s defend/check-raise range, my AKo still has meaningful equity with two overcards plus the Ad backdoor that can turn into the nut flush draw on a diamond turn. Raising here doesn’t make much sense (I don’t block their value region and I’d just get called by pairs/draws), and folding is likely too tight given the small c-bet can induce a wider check-raise. I’ll call and reassess: continue aggressively on A/K turns, continue on diamond turns (nut FD), and otherwise play cautiously versus turn barrels.

Turn brings the 2d giving me the nut flush draw (Ad) plus two overcards. After Minni’s flop check-raise, their range is still heavy on one-pair hands (Tx/7x/6x) and some draws/air that may slow down here; the check gives me a great spot to re-take initiative with a semi-bluff. Betting also denies equity to hands like 8-9/9-J and sets up strong river barrels when I hit a diamond/A/K. I’ll use a medium sizing to generate folds without over-committing; if Minni check-jams, my equity vs a value-heavy range won’t justify a call.

I have the nut straight (9c8s on 7d‑10s‑6c‑2d). FiveTwo opened wide preflop, called my flop raise and now fires a modest turn bet — their line is consistent with many second‑best hands (top pair tens, sevens, pocket sixes, maybe some overpairs or medium pairs) that will pay to see more. With essentially the absolute nuts my priority is value extraction and denying free cards that could chop or give someone a slim draw. A minimum raise to 2600 (keeping worse hands interested) maximizes EV: it charges single pairs/tens/two‑pair/set hands and still gets called by thinner holdings. If FiveTwo jams or raises large I’m prepared to commit given how unlikely I am to be behind.

Minni’s turn raise to 2600 is very small relative to the pot, giving me excellent direct odds. After my flop c-bet got check-raised and I called, my range contains strong made hands and strong draws; with AdKc specifically I now have the nut flush draw plus some additional (sometimes live) overcard equity vs Tx/7x/6x. Calling 1300 to win 7400 means I need ~18% equity, and even if I discount a couple of diamond outs when they have sets/two pair, my equity is still around that threshold and often higher. A 3-bet/jam would be higher variance and mostly gets action from the part of their range that already does well vs a draw (sets/two pair), while folding out some bluffs that I’d prefer to keep in. I’ll take the price and realize equity; on diamond rivers I can value-bet/raise, on A/K rivers I can often continue vs reasonable sizing, and on bricks I can fold to strong pressure but consider bluffing if they check.

River Jc bricks my nut flush draw and I’m at ace-high with essentially no showdown value versus Minni’s flop check-raise + turn min-raise line (they’re very often on at least a pair). The J is a good card to apply pressure: it’s an overcard to a lot of their one-pair holdings (6x/7x and even some Tx that took an aggressive line), while my line (bet/call flop, lead/call turn) credibly represents strong made hands that want to bet for value now (98 straights, sets like TT/77/66, and strong overpairs). I’ll choose a polarizing sizing that targets folds from those medium-strength hands while accepting that strong value (straights/sets/two pair) will continue. If Minni jams over this, I’ll fold.

I have 9♣8♠ for the 6–10 straight (6–7–8–9–10) — essentially the nut on this board (only a 9–8 in villain’s hand can tie). FiveTwo’s line (wide open preflop, called my flop raise, called my turn raise, now a large river raise to 5,000) is consistent with lots of second‑best value hands (sets — 66/77/TT — single tens, and rivered Jx) and occasional bluffs. The pot is 12,400; a call of 5,000 would get me ~3.48:1 on a very strong hand, but shoving to 9,450 (all‑in) extracts an extra 4,450 from any hand that will still call — and given his willingness to call multiple streets, his calling frequency here should be high. The only real downside is tying with 9‑8 (combo count is limited by my holding both a 9 and an 8), so I should maximize value. Folding is incorrect; calling leaves EV on the table. I shove all‑in to charge sets, two‑pair and single‑pair hands and to maximize chips won when I’m effectively invulnerable.

We’re facing a river 3-bet (effectively an all-in raise) after we fired a big polarized bet with A-high on a brick river. Given Minni’s line (flop check-raise, turn raise, then raise over our large river bet), their range is extremely weighted to value: 89 (made straight on the flop), sets (TT/77/66), and two-pair like JT/T7/76. The natural bluff region would be missed diamond draws, but holding the Ad significantly blocks those, while we don’t meaningfully block the main value hands (especially 89). With only A-high and poor blockers, we won’t have the ~17% equity needed to call, and calling off most of our remaining stack here is too negative-EV. Fold.
9d4c is a very weak hand - offsuit, disconnected, no high cards. From UTG position, I need to play tight and this hand doesn't meet opening standards. Easy fold.

I hold Ace-King offsuit (Ad Ks) in Middle Position, which is a premium starting hand. With approximately 76 big blinds, my goal is to build the pot for value and take the initiative. Folding or limping would be a mistake with such a strong hand. A standard open-raise size of 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is appropriate to thin the field and extract value from worse hands, consistent with my previous play.

A6o is a marginal hand offsuit with a weak kicker. Facing a middle position raise from Pro, who has shown some loose tendencies but still likely has a decent range. I'm in cutoff position, which is strong, but calling risks being dominated by better aces or facing multiway pots with button, SB, BB behind. Raising is not justified without suited or connected value. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a tournament setting where we have 100 hands left. Pot odds are decent but postflop playability is poor due to kicker and offsuit nature.

Button vs a 2.5x open, ATo suited is strong enough to 3-bet for value and initiative. In position we realize equity well, and 3-betting also denies the blinds a cheap price to come along. Pro’s stack (74bb) is deep enough to continue with worse Ax/Broadways, but if he 4-bets we can comfortably fold and avoid dominated spots. I’ll use a clean in-position sizing that pressures his open without bloating the pot too much.

Holding 3♠6♦ in the small blind facing Pro open to 250 and a Button 3‑bet to 800. This is a very weak, unsuited, unconnected hand with strong reverse-implied-odds against a 3‑betting range. Pot = 1,200 and my call is 750, so I’d need ~750/(1200+750) ≈ 38.5% equity to break even — far more than 3♠6♦ has versus a typical Button 3‑bet range (usually <<20%). I’d also be out of position postflop (and the big blind still acts after me), so postflop playability is poor. With a healthy stack I should be selective and fold marginal hands in spots like this rather than calling or 4‑betting as a bluff. Fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 5d, which is a very weak hand. Pro opened from middle position and FiveTwo 3-bet from the button. This action indicates strong ranges from my opponents. Calling would put me out of position with a marginal-at-best hand against two active players, and raising is out of the question given the strength shown by the 3-bet. Folding is the only sensible play here.

I hold Ace-King offsuit in Middle Position and faced a 3-bet to 800 from the Button (FiveTwo). FiveTwo has been active and aggressive with 3-bets in recent hands (Hands 19, 25, 32), suggesting a wider range than just premiums. While a 4-bet is a viable option to assert dominance and isolate, it risks folding out his bluffs or weaker hands that I dominate (like AQ/AJ) and isolating myself against his strongest holdings (AA/KK) or forcing a high-variance pre-flop all-in. Calling allows me to keep his range wide, keeps the pot manageable while playing out of position, and allows me to bluff-catch or extract value if I connect with the flop. The price is 550 to win a pot that will be 1750, offering good odds.

I am out of position in a 3-bet pot on a low board (3d 5h 2d). Although I have two overcards, the nut backdoor flush draw, and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4), standard strategy dictates checking to the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo). Leading out (donk betting) would likely only be called by better hands or fold out the bluffs I want to keep in the opponent's range. I will check and evaluate their action.

Pro checked to us in a 3-bet pot on 5h3d2d. This low, two-tone board connects somewhat more with the caller’s range (small pairs, suited connectors), so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a big c-bet holding only two overcards and no diamond. That said, I still have range advantage via overpairs and strong Ax, and Pro will have plenty of whiffed broadways that can fold. A small continuation bet pressures his air, denies equity to hands like KQ/QJ, and lets me play a clean fold if he check-raises (his raises are heavily weighted to sets/strong draws here).

I hold Ace-King offsuit (Ad Ks) on a board of 3d-5h-2d. I have two overcards, the nut flush blocker/backdoor draw (Ad), and a gutshot straight draw (a 4 makes a wheel). The pot is 1750, and FiveTwo bets 500 (approx. 29% pot). This small sizing often indicates a range bet or a blocker bet. Given the pot odds of calling 500 to win 2750 (requiring ~18% equity), and my robust equity from multiple draws and overcards against an aggressive opponent's wide 3-bet range, folding is incorrect. Raising turns my hand into a bluff and isolates me against better hands. Calling is the optimal play to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn card is the King of Diamonds [Kd], putting three diamonds on the board. This card improves my hand significantly, giving me Top Pair Top Kicker (Kings with an Ace) and the Nut Flush Draw (since I hold the Ace of Diamonds). While I have a very strong hand, I am out of position against the pre-flop 3-bettor who showed aggression on the flop. Leading out (donking) here is generally suboptimal as it folds out his bluffs and isolates me against his strongest hands (like made flushes or sets). By checking, I give the opponent a chance to continue bluffing or value betting worse hands (like KQ or pocket pairs). If he checks back, I am happy to see a river or bet for value then. If he bets, I have a very strong check-call or check-raise candidate given my equity (TPTK + NFD). Therefore, the standard and most profitable play is to check.

We 3-bet pre and c-bet small on a low, wet flop; Pro’s turn check keeps his range capped to a lot of one-pair hands (small/mid pairs, 5x) plus some diamond floats/slowplays. The Kd is a strong barreling card for our perceived range (AK/KK/AA with a diamond, KQ with a diamond), and it also pressures his non-diamond pairs that now hate the overcard + 3rd diamond. Even though we don’t hold a diamond (so we’re not blocking flush continues), the fold equity versus his marginal made hands is still attractive with a medium sizing, and we can comfortably fold if he check-raises (he’ll be heavily value/strong draw).

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AdKs) and the Nut Flush Draw on a board of 3d-5h-2d-Kd. FiveTwo is double-barreling for 1400 (approx. half pot). While I have a very strong hand, the board is wet (flush and straight completed). Raising here isolates me against made flushes, sets, or straights, folding out the bluffs I beat. Calling keeps the opponent's bluffing range active and allows me to realize my high equity (drawing to the nut flush if behind). With an SPR of less than 1 going into the river after calling, I can re-evaluate on the final street, but for now, calling is the highest EV play to keep the opponent's range wide.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (Ad Ks) on a board of 3d-5h-2d-Kd-7c. The river 7c is a blank that completes no new draws. I hold the Ace of Diamonds, which is a key blocker to the nut flush, making it less likely the opponent has a flush. The pot is 5,550 and I have 4,950 behind (SPR < 1). Being out of position against an aggressive opponent who has fired two barrels, leading out (donk betting) is suboptimal because it folds out their bluffs and isolates me against their strongest value range (sets, flushes, AA). Checking is the best option to induce a bluff shove from missed draws or lower pairs, or to reach showdown cheaply if the opponent checks back with a hand like QQ or JJ.

River after we’ve triple-barreled flop/turn in a 3-bet pot, Pro check-calls the turn on the 3rd diamond + K overcard and is now left with only ~2250 behind into 5550. That turn call heavily weights him toward made hands that won’t fold (flushes, sets, some Kx with a diamond, occasional straights like 64), and our AhTs has no diamond/straight blockers to improve bluff success. Because the effective shove would only be ~40% pot, it’s a very “callable” bet size and won’t generate enough folds from the portion of his range that got to this river. Best play is to give up and take the (rare) chance he has complete air.

I am in UTG position with a very weak hand, Nine of Clubs and Deuce of Clubs (9c 2c). Even though it is suited, the gap is large and the high card is low. From early position, I need a strong hand to open the betting as there are five players left to act behind me. With a healthy stack of over 100 big blinds, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with garbage hands. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Preflop, UTG (Pro) has folded. I'm in middle position with A♦T♠ offsuit, a solid opening hand with ace blocker and decent playability.
