No limpers, pot 150 (blinds), to call 100 for limp which is weak. Standard play is to raise to 3x BB (300 total) to build pot, take initiative, and narrow field. Tournament stage mid-early (hand 51/150), stacks ~57BB effective, opponents show aggression but ATo performs well vs loose calls. Folding too tight, limping bad OOP. Raise to 300.

Elon opened from middle position and we’re in the cutoff with K7o, a hand that plays poorly versus an MP opening range (dominated Kx, weak kicker, reverse implied odds). Calling needs ~40% equity and invites tough postflop spots even with position. A 3-bet bluff here isn’t attractive given sizing (risking ~900+ to win 450) and the chance Elon continues/4-bets with a 54bb stack, plus the BB can come along. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better steal/3-bet candidates.

Elon opened to 300 from middle. On the button with 8c7s (offsuit) this is a speculative but positionally strong hand — too good to fold but not strong enough to 3‑bet profitably. I’ll call 300 to keep the pot manageable, preserve stack depth, and use position to play post‑flop. A 3‑bet here could induce shoves or calls from the shorter stacks behind (Flash 8675, Claude 6275) and put me in uncomfortable spots; calling lets me realize equity and float/attack favorable flops. Post‑flop plan: continue aggressively when I pair or have strong draws, fold to heavy action if I miss and face strength.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. Elon raised from middle position to 300 and Minni called on the button. While the pot odds are roughly 4:1 to call (250 to win a total pot of 1000), J9o is a marginal hand that performs poorly out of position against two active opponents. Elon's opening range from middle position likely dominates my hand, and Minni, the chip leader, is also in the pot. Calling would likely invite the Big Blind to join as well, making it a 4-way pot where my hand has little multi-way equity. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I have 6h4h suited in the BB. Pot is 750 and I need 200 to call - getting 3.75:1 pot odds. Suited connectors play well in multiway pots, and I can potentially make straights or flushes. The price is right to see a flop.

I completely missed this flop with 6h4h. I have no pair, no straight draw, just a backdoor flush draw needing two running hearts. The board of Kh 8s Tc doesn't connect with my hand at all. It's checked to me with 0 to call - easy check to see a free card and hope for running hearts.

On the flop of K♥8♠T♣, I have second pair top kicker (pair of Tens with Ace kicker) as the preflop raiser in position against two opponents. Claude checked from the blinds, indicating possible weakness, while Minni limp-called preflop from the button, which could be a wide range including floats, draws, or weaker pairs. The board has straight draw potential (QJ, 97) and backdoor flush draws, so betting protects my hand, charges draws, and builds the pot for value from worse hands like weaker Tens, nines, eights, or ace-high floats. Half-pot bet sizes well here to get calls from worse while keeping pot manageable if raised. Pot is 950, betting 500 (about half-pot) is standard for value and balance. Folding or checking is too passive with this strong holding IP.

I call 500. On Kh‑8s‑Tc I have middle pair (8♣7♠ = pair of eights) with a weak kicker, but real showdown value against Elon's wide c‑betting range from middle. Elon opened and then fired 500 into a 1450 pot — a standard continuation bet that includes Kx, Tx, broadways and bluffs. With position on the button I prefer to keep the pot controlled and use information on the turn rather than thin‑value raising (min‑raising to 1000 would commit extra chips with a hand that is often second best and has little fold equity). Folding is too passive since I beat a lot of overcards and bluffs, and calling 500 gives reasonable pot odds (~3.9:1) to see the next card. Plan: call to evaluate the turn — if I improve (two pair/trips) extract value; if a scary K/T/overcard arrives and I face heavy aggression, fold; if checked to me on turn I’ll decide whether to barre lsmall or check based on the card and opponents’ tendencies.

I have 6h4h with a board of Kh 8s Tc. I completely missed this flop - no pair, no straight draw, just a backdoor flush draw needing two running hearts (about 4% to hit). Pot is 1950 and I need 500 to call, getting about 3.9:1 odds. But my backdoor flush draw equity is nowhere near enough to justify this call. Elon bet and Minni called - both likely have real hands or draws. Easy fold.

On the turn 6♦, the board is K♥8♠T♣6♦. I have second pair top kicker (Tens with Ace) as the preflop raiser in position against Minni, who limp-called preflop (wide range) and called my flop bet (likely pairs, draws, or floats). The 6♦ is a relatively blank card; it doesn't complete obvious straights (QJ or 97 draws from flop miss or semi-bluff less likely now) and adds minor wheel possibilities but favors my range. Pot is 1950, my stack 4925 (~49BB), Minni deep at 21k. Continuing with a half-pot bet (~1000) extracts value from worse hands like Ax-high, weaker pairs (88, 66, 99, 77), missed draws that might call or bluff-catch. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing Minni to bluff, and looks weak after flop bet. This balances my range and builds the pot with a decent showdown hand IP in a tournament spot where aggression maximizes EV.

Call 1000. I have middle pair (8s) with a weak kicker — decent showdown value vs an MP opener who barreled flop and turn. Facing 1000 into a 2950 pot gives ~3.95:1 pot odds (~20% required equity). I have roughly 9 reasonable outs to improve (2 eights, 3 sevens, 4 nines) — raw equity in the mid‑20s–30s% range and I also beat many bluffs/overcards. Elon is short‑stacked (~3.9k) so his turn pressure is polarized; raising here risks getting shoved or committing chips with a one‑pair hand that is often behind Kx/Tx. Calling keeps the pot controllable, lets me realize equity and re‑evaluate on the river (extract value if I improve, fold to heavy action or obvious value hands if I miss).

River J♦ significantly worsens my hand's relative strength. Board: K♥8♠T♣6♦J♦. I have pair of Tens with Ace kicker (second pair effectively, as Js are now on board). This card completes straights for hands like Q9 (8-9-T-Q-J straight, which had a gutshot on flop), pairs any Jx (now pair Js > Ts), and brings a second diamond potentially aiding flush draws or backdoors. Minni's range from button limp-call preflop (wide: suited connectors like Q9s/J9s, Ax, pairs, broadways) and calling flop half-pot (950 pot) and turn pot bet (1950 pot, calling 1000) is strong: likely Kx, Tx (KJ/KT/JT/T9), 88/66, or improved draws. Many of these now beat me (Ks, Js pairs/two pairs, straights). Pure bluffs unlikely after two calls OOP-ish (she's button IP actually, but still). Pot 3950, my stack 3925 (~100BB effective vs her 20k deep). Betting risks getting called by better or raised, losing my stack on a marginal hand mid-tournament (hand 51/150). Checking realizes equity safely: I beat missed draws, weaker pairs (eights/sixes), ace-high; she may check behind with similar or bet bluffs I can snap-call profitably with ace blocker. Consistent with pot control after two value bets; preserves stack for better spots vs aggressive field. No fold equity needed; showdown value present but thin value betting -EV given board.

I check to take this to showdown. I have just one pair (8♣) on K♣? (Kh 8s Tc 6d Jd) — decent showdown value versus bluffs and missed overcards but frequently behind logical double‑barrel value hands (Kx, Tx, Jx and two‑pair combos). Elon opened, c‑bet the flop and barreled the turn, then checked the river: that line is consistent with medium/strong value hands pot‑controlling or missed draws that prefer to check. Betting here risks getting called by hands that beat me while folding out only a small portion of his value range, so a river bet would be thin and negative EV. There’s no bet to call and checking preserves chips while allowing me to win when he was bluffing or show down my marginal pair when he’s weak. Action: check.

Q℃ 5♥ is a trash hand offsuit from UTG in a 6-handed game. Poor playability, low equity against ranges of callers or raisers. Opponents like Minni and FiveTwo are active, but folding preserves stack for premium spots in mid-tournament. Standard tight play maximizes long-term EV.

Unopened pot after UTG folds. From middle position in 6-handed, J5 offsuit is too weak to profitably open (dominated by most continuing ranges) and not worth limping in a tough lineup. Preserve stack and wait for better spots with clearer EV.

Cutoff with Td5d — marginal but suited and in late position with two players yet to act. The pot is 150 and calling 100 (limping) is passive and invites the Button/Blinds to raise; folding throws away position equity. I want to apply pressure and steal the blinds with a standard CO open size that matches table tendencies (most opens have been ~250–300). I’ll raise to 300 total (3x BB) to have a credible fold equity while keeping the sizing reasonable given deep stacks. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet (this hand plays poorly out of position vs strong action); if called, c-bet selectively — bet for value/semibluff when a diamond or a Ten hits, otherwise pot-control and evaluate on the turn.

Minni has been an extremely active player, opening many hands from various positions. King-Ten offsuit (KTo) is a marginal hand that is often dominated by a standard opening range. While Minni's range is likely wider than average, KTo doesn't play particularly well post-flop in a potential multi-way pot if the blinds call, nor does it stand up well to a squeeze. Given the 3x open and the presence of two active players behind me in the blinds, I will choose the disciplined route and fold, looking for a better opportunity to engage.

9h8h is a nice suited connector. Minni raised to 300 and I'm getting reasonable pot odds (250 to win 450). While I'm out of position, this hand has good playability with straight and flush potential. Minni has been active and I can realize equity postflop. Calling is better than 3-betting OOP with this speculative hand.

I am in the Big Blind with [Ac 3s]. Minni raised to 300 from the Cutoff, and Claude called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 700, and I need to call 200 to see the flop. This gives me pot odds of 4.5:1 (requiring ~22% equity). Minni has been opening a wide range from late position, and Claude's flat call likely caps his range. Although A3o plays poorly multi-way and out of position, the price is extremely good to close the action with an Ace in my hand. I have sufficient raw equity against their ranges to justify a call, intending to play fit-or-fold on the flop.

I flopped middle pair (9s) with 8h kicker on A-7-9 board. The Ace is concerning - Minni raised preflop and frequently has an Ace in their range. We're 3-way and I'm first to act. My hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value on this Ace-high board. Checking for pot control makes sense - I can call a small bet or fold to heavy action.

I have top pair with a very weak kicker (3) on a relatively dry A-9-7 rainbow board. As the Big Blind in a multi-way pot, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) is the standard line. Leading out (donking) would likely only get called by better Aces or strong draws, while folding out the hands I beat. By checking, I allow Minni to c-bet her bluffs or value hands, or give a free card to keep the pot small, which benefits my weak kicker situation. I plan to call a standard c-bet.

I opened from the cutoff and both blinds just checked the Ace‑high flop, so I’m last to act with Td5d on 7c‑9d‑Ah. This board favors callers’ ranges (Ax, pocket pairs, some 9x/7x) but both players showed weakness by checking — I can often take it down now. My hand has thin equity (three remaining Tens and three Fives = 6 pair outs) and a very slim runner‑runner diamond flush (~4%), so this is a pure semi‑bluff/continuation bet to buy the pot rather than a value bet. I’ll bet about one‑third pot (300 into 900) to apply pressure while keeping the price reasonable if I’m called. Plan: fold to a large raise (hand plays poorly vs strong action); if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and only continue aggressively if I hit a Ten or diamond or see further weakness, otherwise pot‑control and see showdown cheaply.

I have middle pair (9s) with 8 kicker on A-7-9 board. Minni's 300 bet into 900 pot is only 1/3 pot - a small sizing. I'm getting 4:1 odds to call. While the Ace is concerning for Minni's range, my pair of 9s beats all bluffs and weaker holdings. The small bet size suggests either value with an Ace or a c-bet with air. Pro is behind but if they raise I can fold. Folding middle pair to this small sizing is too tight.

I hold Top Pair with a weak kicker (Ac3s). The pot is 1500 and it costs me 300 to call, offering 5:1 pot odds. I need approximately 17% equity to continue. Minni's c-bet of 1/3 pot is a standard size that could include a wide range of value and bluffs. Claude's call from the Small Blind is concerning as it strengthens his range to likely include weaker Aces or strong 9s/draws, potentially dominating my kicker. However, folding Top Pair for this price is too tight. Being in position relative to the raiser (closing the action on this street) allows me to see the turn cheaply. I will call to realize my equity but proceed cautiously if significant aggression continues on later streets.

The 6h turn is excellent for me. I now have middle pair (9s), an open-ended straight draw (need 5 or T), AND a heart flush draw. I have roughly 15 outs to improve dramatically. With this many draws plus showdown value, I should lead here as a semi-bluff. Betting 700 into 1800 puts pressure on Minni and Pro while building the pot with my strong equity. If called, I have tons of outs. If raised, I can call profitably given my draw equity.

On the turn, the board is 7c 9d Ah 6h. The pot is 1800. Claude (SB) leads out (donk bets) for 700 into two opponents. This line—check-calling the flop and leading the turn—often indicates a hand that has improved significantly, such as two pair (A6, 76, 96), a set (66), or a straight (85), or occasionally a strong semi-bluff with a flush draw. I hold Top Pair with a very weak kicker (A3). My hand is effectively a bluff-catcher that is currently losing to almost all value hands Claude would lead with (better Aces, two pair, etc.). Furthermore, the pre-flop raiser (Minni) is still left to act behind me, creating a sandwich situation where I could be squeezed or overcalled by a better hand. Given the multi-way action, the strength represented by the donk bet, and my poor kicker, calling is negative EV. Folding is the prudent decision.

Fold. I missed the turn — with Td5d I have only 6 clean river outs (3 tens + 3 fives) ≈13% to improve. The pot is 2,500 and Claude’s turn bet is 700, so the pot odds I’m getting are ~3.57:1 (break‑even equity ≈21.9%). My raw equity (~13%) is well below what’s required, and many of my outs will still be second‑best if Claude holds Ax/9x/7x or a pair. Claude called preflop and the flop bet, then raises the turn — that line is consistent with value too often to justify calling or risking a bluff raise here. Preserve the 19,400 stack and wait for a better spot.

6-handed UTG (effectively the tightest seat). K3s is dominated by the broadway kings and strong suited kings that will continue vs an open, and it plays poorly out of position in multiway pots. Given the aggressive tendencies shown (frequent 3-bets/isolations), limping invites punishment and opening risks being 3-bet off our equity. Best is to preserve stack and wait for a stronger UTG spot.

8♦7♠ is too weak to invest out of position here. I’m in middle position in a 6-handed game facing a full-price to call 100 into a 150 pot with several aggressive players yet to act who open-raise frequently to ~300. Limping or calling will likely put me OOP in multiway pots where 87o has poor equity and is often dominated; a raise would be thin and out of my hand’s range. With a healthy 19400 stack I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am in the Cutoff with Queen-Deuce suited. Both players before me (UTG and MP) have folded. While I have the opportunity to open the pot, Q2s is significantly below the standard opening range for the Cutoff in a 6-max game. The hand has very little playability if called, and the risk of being outplayed or dominated outweighs the potential of a blind steal. I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.
62o is one of the worst hands in poker. Even on the button with good position, this hand has too little equity and playability to profitably open. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ah5s, a marginal but playable hand in a Blind vs Blind situation. The action has folded to me. My opponent in the Big Blind is Elon, who has shown a tendency to defend but also fold to post-flop aggression (e.g., Hand #43). A5o blocks strong Aces in the opponent's range and has wheel potential. While limping is a valid option to keep the pot small out of position, raising to 2.5BB (250) applies pressure to steal the dead money immediately and takes the initiative. Given the deep stacks (~77BB effective), I prefer raising to define the range and capitalize on fold equity. If I face a 3-bet, I can easily fold.

In the big blind facing a standard SB raise to 2.5BB from Pro, with Qs8c offsuit. This hand has queen-high equity, decent playability post-flop (straight/flush draws possible), and excellent pot odds: calling an additional 150 into a 350 pot requires ~30% equity, which Q8o exceeds against a typical wide SB stealing range (often 40-50% of hands). Post-flop, I have position as BB acts last. Pro has been active but not overly tight based on history. Folding is too tight in BB defense spot; 3-betting offsuit Q8o lacks strong blockers and post-flop potential if called. Calling keeps pot small, realizes equity well.

I raised pre-flop from the SB with Ah5s and was called by the BB. The flop comes Qh Th 5h. I have bottom pair and the nut flush draw. This is a very strong hand, but out of position on a monotone board, it is often best to check. By checking, I control the size of the pot and avoid facing a raise that might put me in a difficult spot despite my strong draw. I block the nut flush, which reduces the likelihood my opponent has a flush, but they often have a Queen or Ten here. Checking allows me to call a bet comfortably (check-call line) or see a free turn if they check back. Betting might fold out worse hands and only get action from better hands (like Qx or better flushes). Therefore, a check is the prudent play to realize my equity.

Top pair with Queen (Qs top pair, 8 kicker) on a highly coordinated flop Qh Th 5h, which screams draws: nut flush draw, combo draws like J9s (OESD + FD), 98s, A-high flush draws. Pro's SB open-raise pre-flop is wide (steal range), and check post-flop suggests weakness, air, or traps, but unlikely premium given check. With position, betting extracts value from worse pairs (KJ, AT, etc.), overcards, and charges draws heavily. Checking risks giving free cards to outs that beat me (flush ~35% equity if they have FD, straights). Pot 500, bet ~70% (350) builds pot while keeping fold equity vs pure bluffs and semi-bluffs. Pro's stack (9650) and history show willingness to call but not maniacal. Protects my hand and realizes equity best.

I hold the Nut Flush Draw (Ah) and bottom pair (5s) on a monotone board (Qh Th 5h). My opponent (Elon) bet 350 into a pot of 500 after my check. With the Ah, I block the nut flush, making it less likely he has the nuts, though he could still have a lower flush, a set, or top pair. My equity is very strong due to the flush draw and 5 outs to improve to two pair or trips. Raising would likely only get action from better hands or strong draws, bloating the pot out of position. Calling allows me to realize my equity cheaply, keep his bluffs in, and re-evaluate on the turn. I have ample implied odds given the stack depths.

On the turn, the board is [Qh Th 5h 9d]. I hold Ah5s, giving me bottom pair and the nut flush draw. The pot is 1200. The 9d completes possible straights (KJ, J8) and hits hands like T9 or J9. As the pre-flop aggressor who check-called the flop, leading out (donk betting) on this turn card doesn't make sense; it likely folds out bluffs I'm beating and isolates me against better hands or made straights/flushes. My goal is to realize the equity of my nut flush draw and potentially get to showdown with my pair cheaply. Checking allows me to evaluate my opponent's action. I plan to call a reasonable bet given my strong draw and pair outs.

Turn 9d completes straights like J8 and adds gutshots, while keeping heart flush draws live. Board remains highly coordinated. I hold top pair Qs with weak 8 kicker, blocking some Qx but vulnerable to better Qx (AJ/QJ/KQ), straights (J8), two-pair combos, and sets. Pro's SB preflop range is wide; checked flop (weak), called my 350 c-bet (draws/marginal made hands like Tx, 77-88), checks turn again (missed improvement or trapping, but unlikely premium given twice checking). Position allows continued aggression: betting denies equity to draws (remaining ~30% to flush), extracts from worse pairs/draws that call loosely per history, and has fold equity vs air. Checking risks free river to outs (flush ~20% equity left, runner-runner straights) and pot control suboptimal with showdown value only vs bluffs. Pot 1200, bet ~2/3 pot (800) sizes well for value/protection, <11% of my stack, commits if called but SPR ~8 post-bet leaves maneuverability. Pro's calling history (e.g., Hand 4 light calls, Hand 50) suggests thin value works. Maximizes EV in tournament.

I hold Ah5s on a board of Qh Th 5h 9d. I have bottom pair and the nut flush draw. The opponent bets 800 into a pot of 1200 (66% pot). I need approximately 28.5% equity to call directly, plus I have implied odds if I hit the nut flush against his likely value range (Two Pair, Straight, Sets, or lower flushes). Since I hold the Ah, I block the nut flush, reducing the likelihood he has a flush already. Against a range of Straights (KJ), Two Pair, or Sets, I have around 25-30% equity (9 flush outs + potential trip/two-pair outs). Raising would isolate me against hands that beat me and deny me the chance to realize my equity cheaply. Folding is too tight given the nut draw and pair. Calling is the correct play to see the river.

I have improved to the Nut Flush on the river with the Ah. The board now contains four hearts (Qh, Th, 5h, 3h), which is a drastic board texture change. Having check-called the flop and turn, leading out now would likely kill the action or only get called by strong hands, while folding out all bluffs. Given Elon's aggression on previous streets, he likely connects with this board or has been semi-bluffing with a high heart. By checking, I give him the opportunity to value bet a worse flush (like Kh or Jh) or bluff if he has a blocker. I plan to check-raise all-in if he bets to extract maximum value. If he checks back, I still win the pot, but checking is the superior play to induce a bet from an aggressive opponent on a scary board.

River 3h completes the nut flush draw (any heart pairs it), adding another low card that doesn't significantly improve straights but heightens showdown texture. I hold top pair top kicker (Qs pairing Qh, best possible kicker), blocking QQ/AQ/KQ but vulnerable to any flush (Ahxh, Khxh, Jxh suited etc., within SB wide raise range), better kickers (unlikely given triple check), sets (55/T5/33 improbable), or two-pair combos improved. Pro's line: SB min-raise pre (steal), check-call flop c-bet (weak/marginal/draws), check-call turn bet (missed 9d or floating), check river (no strong improvement). History shows Pro calls light (Hand 4 down to river light, Hand 44/50 check-call stations), suggesting showdown value vs Tx (pair tens), 99 (trips? turn paired 9s no board pair), Jx draws missed, overcards, bluffs. Betting risks: flush raises (fold equity low vs nuts), called by better (AJ/KJ?); thin value from worse unlikely post-flush card as Pro proceeds cautiously. Prior bets (350 flop +800 turn =1150 invested) extracted max from drawing/weak hands. Checking realizes EV vs improved range (beats ~40-50% including missed draws/non-nut flushes? unlikely), pot control with 6475 stack (SPR ~2.3), preserves stack for 97 hands left. No fold equity needed; position forces showdown. Best play: check, happy to showdown TP/TK.

2♦4♣ is one of the weakest hands in hold'em — offsuit, unconnected, no playability. I'm UTG (first to act), out of position against five opponents, and facing a to‑call of 100 (pot 150) with aggressive players and deep stacks behind. There's no reason to limp or invest chips here; raising would be reckless and calling will put me OOP in multiway pots where my hand performs very poorly. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Hijack position in a 6-handed game with Ace-Trey offsuit. While an Ace is always tempting to play, A3o is generally considered too weak to open from this position, especially with active players like Claude and Pro behind me. It lacks post-flop playability and is easily dominated by better Aces. I will fold and wait for a stronger opening hand.

7s5d is a weak offsuit hand. Even in late position (cutoff), this hand isn't strong enough to open-raise for a steal attempt. With a healthy stack of 72BB, no need to take unnecessary risks. Standard fold.

Action is on me on the Button after folds from UTG, Middle, and Cutoff. My hand is K9s (King of Hearts, Nine of Hearts). With blinds at 50/100, I have a stack of 11,300 (113 BBs). K9s is a strong hand to open-raise from the Button to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. It has good equity against calling ranges and flush potential. A standard open size of 2.5x (250) is appropriate to put pressure on the Small Blind (Elon) and Big Blind (FiveTwo) while offering a good risk-to-reward ratio.

Ks7h is a marginal hand at best, King-high with poor kicker, offsuit, and limited playability. Facing a button raise from Pro (who has been active), defending out of position in the SB is poor. Pot odds are about 2:1 effective (200 to call into 600 total pot after call), but postflop playability is weak against a wide button range. Standard fold to preserve stack in tournament setting with 96 hands left and decent stack (64BB). Wait for better spots.

Button opens to 2.5bb and we’re getting a good price, but T3o is near the bottom of a reasonable BB defend range versus a competent late-position raiser. It’s dominated by most of Pro’s continuing hands (Tx, broadways) and realizes equity very poorly out of position, creating lots of reverse-implied-odds spots postflop. A 3-bet bluff is also too thin with this holding (poor blockers, bad playability). Preserve stack and fold.

I am in the UTG position with 6h2h. While it is a suited hand, it is extremely weak, especially from the earliest position at a 6-max table. Playing such a low-equity hand from UTG invites getting dominated or forced to fold to later aggression. With a healthy stack of over 86 big blinds, there is no reason to risk chips on this hand. I will fold.

K8s in middle position is a borderline hand. It has some playability with flush potential and a decent high card. With the blinds at 50/100 and my stack at 72 BBs, I can afford to open-raise here. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

I hold J6 offsuit in the Cutoff, facing a standard open raise to 250 from Claude in Middle Position. J6o is a very weak hand with poor playability and is easily dominated. There are no pot odds or strategic reasons to get involved with this hand against an early/middle position opener. The standard and correct play is to fold.

87h suited connectors from the button facing a middle position raise. Excellent position to call and play post-flop speculatively. Good implied odds against deep Minni and potential multiway pot with blinds. Claude's range may be wide based on history of frequent raises. Pot odds favorable: calling 250 into 400 pot (effective odds improve multiway). Folding too tight; 3-betting not ideal with this hand as it doesn't dominate many calling ranges. Plan to control pot OOP if hit, fold if miss.

Pocket 3s plays best as a cheap set-mine. We’re getting a very good price (200 to win 650) with decent implied odds versus Claude’s open range and Elon’s cold-call range, and stacks are deep enough to get paid when we flop a set. 3-betting from the SB into an opener + caller with 33 is usually -EV (we get called too often and play a bloated pot OOP), while folding is too tight given the price.

I’m in the big blind with J♠7♣ facing a 250 MP open and two callers (SB and BTN). Calling 150 more (total 250) is a small price to see a flop with a marginal but playable hand — 3-betting OOP with J7o here is too loose and folding is unnecessarily tight given the pot odds and deep stacks. Plan: see the flop and proceed cautiously — continue only with a jack (but beware my weak kicker), a strong draw, or a favorable board texture; fold to substantial aggression if I don’t improve. Calling preserves stack flexibility and keeps weaker holdings in the pot that I can exploit postflop.

We completely missed: 33 has almost no equity on Ah-Qc-Ac, and we’re out of position in a 4-way pot. This is a very strong, range-favoring board for the preflop raiser (Claude) and also hits callers’ Ax heavily, so a lead-bluff will get called/raised too often. Best is to check and realize whatever tiny showdown value we have, then proceed very cautiously versus any significant bet.

Board A♥ Q♣ A♣ with J♠7♣ in a 4‑way pot. I have no pair and only a backdoor club runner-runner; my equity is extremely low and many opponents’ ranges (MP opener + callers) contain Ax, Qx, or strong pairs. Bluffing into multiple players is low EV and I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a naked bluff. Plan: check to control the pot and fold to meaningful aggression (I’ll only consider calling a very small probe if it’s cheap and from a single player). Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Board is Ah Qc Ac - paired aces with a queen. I have Ks8s with no connection to this board whatsoever. No pair, no flush draw (clubs on board, I have spades), no straight draw. With 3 opponents who all checked, I could c-bet but it's very risky - anyone with an ace, queen, or pocket pair is calling. My K-high has almost no equity here. Best to check and take a free card, hoping for a spade turn or king.

Held 8h7h on Ah Qc Ac flop. Only backdoor flush draw (needs two running hearts) and backdoor straight draws, no made hand or strong draws. Multiway pot (4 players) with preflop raiser Claude checking, followed by blinds. Paired aces board favors strong ace hands, reducing fold equity for a bluff. Button position allows checking for pot control and free turn card. Betting risks calling stations like Minni/FiveTwo with TP/Ace or better, while stack preservation key mid-tournament (61BB effective). Plan: check, reassess turn; fold if bet into, bet if improves.

Flop checked through 4‑way on a very range-favoring AAX texture, which usually caps everyone’s range (lots of pocket pairs/broadways that hate continuing). The Kc on the turn is a great card to apply pressure: it’s credible for us to have Ax (we can have Axs/AQo), AK, KQ, or even a club draw that picked up equity. With 33 we have essentially no showdown value, so checking is mostly giving up and hoping for a miracle; I’d rather take a controlled stab to win the 1000 pot now. I’ll use a smaller multiway sizing to keep risk down and fold cleanly versus any raise/call-heavy line.

Board: A♥ Q♣ A♣ K♣. My hand J♠7♣ gives me only a one-card club flush draw (7♣ + Q♣, A♣, K♣ = 4 clubs). I have 9 outs to hit on the river (~9/46 ≈ 19.6%). The pot is 1,450 and the turn bet is 450, so I must call 450 to contest a ~1,900 pot after my call — I need ≈23.7% equity (450/1900) to break even. My raw equity (~19.6%) is below the pot odds, and many of those outs are “dirty” — a club on the river can still lose to full houses or higher made hands (Ax, QQ, KK, A‑Q, A‑K, pocket pairs) in this multiway pot. Raising OOP here would be too loose and I have little fold equity. With a healthy stack and better, cleaner spots ahead, the correct play is to fold and preserve chips.

Turn brought Kc giving me a pair of kings, but this is a very dangerous board. We have: paired aces, three clubs (flush complete), and A-K-Q-J-T straight possible. FiveTwo's 450 bet into the pot after checking flop suggests strength - could be a flush, trip aces, or a straight. My pair of kings with 8 kicker is very vulnerable here. Even if I'm ahead now, Elon is still to act behind me. Getting ~3.2:1 pot odds but too many hands beat me (any Ace, any flush, QQ for full house, JT for straight). Fold is prudent.

Facing a 450 bet into 1450 on a turn card Kc that completes the nut club flush draw on a paired aces board (Ah Qc Ac Kc). My 87h has zero equity: no pair, no flush draw (hearts backdoor only), no straight draw. FiveTwo checked flop multiway but leads turn after folds, representing flush, full house (e.g., AKc, QKc, sets), or strong two-pair/straights. SB position often plays speculative but value-bets here. Pot odds ~3:1 but reverse implied odds terrible against range; calling risks stack to better hands while position doesn't help much OOP continuation. Consistent with pre-turn plan: fold to turn aggression. Preserve 61BB stack for better spots in tournament.
AQo is a premium hand from UTG. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x) is appropriate. Strong enough to build a pot and isolate.

I am in Middle Position with Kc Jd facing a raise to 250 (2.5 BB) from the UTG player, Claude. KJo is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by a standard UTG opening range (which includes AK, KQ, AQ, AJ, and pocket pairs). Although Claude has been active, calling here leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from the players yet to act (Cutoff, Button, Blinds) and puts me in a spot with reverse implied odds against the UTG opener. With a healthy stack of 114 BB, I do not need to take this marginal spot. Folding is the disciplined and standard play.

4d2s is an extremely weak hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suitedness. Facing an UTG raise from Claude to 250 into a 400 pot, this is a clear fold even from the cutoff. No showdown value, minimal bluff potential postflop, and risking chips with trash preserves my stack for better opportunities in a tournament setting where survival and premium hand selection maximize long-term EV.

Claude is opening UTG in a 6-max lineup, which should be relatively tight. Ks4c offsuit is dominated by his Kx/Ax and broadways, plays poorly multiway, and doesn’t realize equity well even with position because we’ll frequently be forced to fold to c-bets or face tough turn/river spots. A 3-bet bluff is too loose here (bad blocker, poor postflop playability, and invites 4-bets), and flat-calling is also -EV given domination and the chance the blinds come along/squeeze. Preserve stack for better button opportunities.

Facing an UTG open to 250 from Claude while I’m in the small blind with T♥7♠. This is a weak, easily-dominated hand with poor playability out of position. Calling 200 to see a flop would put me out of position against the opener (and give the BB a chance to come along or squeeze), and a bluff-raise is thin against an early-position raiser who will often 3-bet. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 2c. Claude, the UTG player, raised to 250. My hand is extremely weak and unlikely to play well post-flop, especially out of position against an UTG opener. While the price to call is only 150 more, 6-2 offsuit is well below the threshold for defending the Big Blind in this tournament setting. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve my stack.

I am in the UTG position with 2c 4d, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em (4-2 offsuit). Playing this hand from early position with 5 players left to act is a losing strategy due to the high probability of being dominated or facing raises. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Pocket tens is a premium hand pre-flop in middle position with no action ahead except blinds and UTG fold. Standard play is to open raise for value and to build the pot, thinning the field. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is a standard size given the tournament dynamics, player tendencies from history (frequent 250-300 opens), and my stack of ~62BB. Larger stacks behind (esp. Minni), but TT plays well multiway or heads-up. Avoid limping or min-raising as it invites multiway pots with speculative hands.

We’re in position in the cutoff with a medium pocket pair versus an MP 3bb open. 77 plays best by keeping the SPR high: we can realize equity, set-mine profitably versus Elon’s ~59bb stack, and avoid bloating a 3-bet pot where we’d often face a shove/4-bet and be forced to fold. Calling also keeps Elon’s wider range in, lets us play flops in position, and we can proceed aggressively on favorable low boards while comfortably folding to heavy heat on high/connected textures when we miss.

Button with A5♣ — suited Ace and great position. Elon opened to 300 from MP and CO called; facing a multiway pot I prefer to keep the pot controllable and realize equity in position rather than inflate it and risk a 3‑bet from the blinds or Elon. Calling 300 keeps the pot around ~1050 and lets me play postflop (look for Ax, flush/straight draws or a favorable board to apply pressure). Plan: evaluate flop with positional advantage, C‑bet/raise with top pair/nut draws, fold to heavy resistance on dry boards.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd Jh. Elon raised to 3bb from middle position, and both FiveTwo and Minni have called. I am getting excellent pot odds (250 to call into a current pot of 1050, roughly 4.2 to 1). While QJo does not play particularly well in multi-way pots and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand, the price is too attractive to fold. A squeeze raise is too risky given the number of callers and the stack sizes. I will call and look to flop two pair, a straight, or a very strong draw, while playing cautiously on one-pair boards where I might be dominated.

I have 4h6h suited in the BB. Pot is 1300, I need to call 200 more. That's 6.5:1 pot odds with 4 players already in. Suited connectors play well multiway - good implied odds if I hit a flush, straight, or two pair. Easy call to close the action.

The flop of As Kc 3d is very coordinated and favors the pre-flop raiser's range. In a 5-way pot, the likelihood that someone has an Ace or a King is extremely high. I have Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 10 for a broadway straight). Leading into four opponents on this texture is ill-advised. I will check to see how the action develops; if there is a bet and multiple callers, I will likely fold, but I'm looking for a cheap or free turn card.

Completely whiffed this AK3 board with 4h6h. I have only a backdoor flush draw and gutshot to a wheel (need a 5). No pair, no real equity. With 4 opponents in the pot on this high card board, someone likely has an A or K. Easy check - no reason to bluff into a multiway pot with this board texture.

Pocket Tens on an A♠ K♣ 3♦ flop in a 5-way pot is a tough spot. The board is very uncoordinated but hits strong ranges hard with top pair top kicker (AK), two pair, sets possible. My hand has no showdown value against likely Ax or Kx holdings from callers, especially multiway with deep stacks like Minni behind. Flash and Claude checked, but FiveTwo (CO), Minni (BTN), and later players likely to have hit this board given loose calling ranges from history. No draws really for me, just overcard vulnerability. Best play is to check, realize equity if check-folded to, or control pot. Betting risks isolation with worse and folding to raise; calling commits too much without improvement outs. Preserve stack for better spots mid-tournament.

5-way to an A-K-3 flop, our 77 is almost always behind at least one player (Ax/Kx is very prevalent in these ranges, especially the preflop raiser). With this many opponents, fold equity for a stab is low and betting mainly gets called by better hands while folding out the few worse hands we beat. Checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes our equity (including the small chance to spike a set), and lets us respond to turn action with more information.

I have top pair (A♣5♣) on a 3♦ A♠ K♣ flop in a multiway pot (Flash, Claude, Elon, FiveTwo). In position I want to extract value from worse aces, kings and medium pairs and charge any backdoor draws, but keep the pot controlled because my 5‑kicker is weak and multiple players remain. A half‑pot c‑bet (~750 into 1500) should be called by worse hands and deny free cards; I can fold to heavy aggression given my vulnerable kicker and re‑evaluate on the turn if called.

On a flop of As-Kc-3d, I am holding Qd-Jh, giving me a gutshot straight draw to Broadway. Minni has bet 750 into a pot of 1500 (3000 total after the bet). I am getting 3:1 pot odds, requiring approximately 25% equity to call. However, the probability of hitting my gutshot on the turn is only about 8.5%. This is a 5-way pot, and the board texture heavily favors the ranges of my opponents, meaning an Ace or King is almost certainly out against me. Additionally, there are three players (Claude, Elon, and FiveTwo) still left to act behind me. If any of them raise, I would be forced to fold anyway, and even if they call, I am out of position for the rest of the hand. Given the poor direct odds and the risk of being squeezed or facing further bets on later streets, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play.

Facing 750 bet into 2250 pot. I have 4h6h on AK3 board - complete miss. Only have a gutshot to a wheel (need a 5) worth ~4 outs = ~8% equity. Getting 3:1 pot odds but need ~25% to continue. With Elon and FiveTwo still to act behind me, this is a clear fold.

Pocket Tens on an A♠ K♣ 3♦ flop in a multiway pot is a disaster. The board smashes the calling ranges of FiveTwo (CO call), Minni (BTN call), Flash (SB limp-call), and Claude (BB call). Minni's 750 bet into a ~2250 pot after universal check is strong, representing AK, Ax, Kx, or better. TT has poor equity (~15-25% vs her range), no straight/flush potential, and faces reverse implied odds if I hit a set later. Flash and Claude already folded, FiveTwo behind may have hit (KQ, QJ etc.), but even heads-up vs Minni I'm crushed. Pot odds ~4:1 require ~20% equity, which I don't have reliably. Raising is a bluff with no fold equity vs her 18k stack. Best play: fold, preserve ~52BB stack for better spots mid-tournament (hand 57/150). Consistent with flop check strategy to control pot and avoid trouble.

Facing Minni’s 1/3-pot stab (750 into 2250) on A-K-3 after it checked through 4 players, our 77 is an underpair with essentially no backdoor equity and we’ll be out of position for the rest of the hand. In this multiway-to-HU line, her betting range is heavily weighted toward Ax/Kx and strong broadways; the hands we beat (random air / small pairs) are a smaller portion and we’ll have a tough time realizing equity without turning our hand into a bluff. With only ~8% to spike a set by the river and limited ability to profitably continue on most turns versus a likely barrel, this call is too thin. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

JQh suited is a strong speculative hand from UTG in 6-handed play due to its suited connector properties, offering straight and flush potential with good playability multiway or heads-up. Early position but with relatively deep stacks (58BB), raising takes initiative, builds the pot, and leverages fold equity against loose callers seen in history (many limps/check-calls). Standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate; min-raise too small. Folding too passive for tournament stack building over 150 hands.

AKo is near the top of my range and plays best as a value 3-bet here. Calling invites a multiway pot against the deep stacks behind (especially Minni/Flash), which reduces AKo’s equity realization and makes it harder to win unimproved. By 3-betting I isolate Elon’s UTG open, take initiative, and can comfortably continue versus most responses; if Elon 4-bets, we can consider stacking off given the effective ~55bb versus his stack and the fold equity generated by a jam.

Fold. I’m in the cutoff with 7♠3♦ — a very weak offsuit hand. Elon opened UTG to 300 and FiveTwo reraised to 900, which represents a strong/3-bet range; calling 900 into a 1,350 pot gives only ~40% immediate pot odds but 7-3o has essentially no equity versus that range. A min‑raise (to 1,500) or 4‑bet would be an unnecessary, high-variance bluff out of position with the button and blinds yet to act. Preserve the healthy 203bb stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

With an UTG raise from Elon and a 3-bet from FiveTwo, pocket fives are in a difficult spot. Set mining is the primary goal with this hand, but calling 900 (roughly 11% of my stack and 16% of the effective stack) doesn't offer the necessary implied odds, which usually require the stack-to-call ratio to be at least 15:1. Additionally, there is a risk that Elon 4-bets or that I am squeezed out by the blinds. Given the strength shown by an UTG open and a 3-bet, it is best to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.
I have 4s 2c - one of the worst hands in poker. Facing a raise and a 3-bet with 850 to call, this is a clear fold. No equity to justify continuing with this garbage hand against strong action.

I am in the Big Blind facing an UTG open raise to 300 and a 3-bet from Middle Position to 900. My hand, Queen-Jack offsuit (Qd Js), is significantly dominated by the likely ranges of an UTG opener and an MP 3-bettor (which typically includes AA, KK, QQ, JJ, AK, AQs). Calling cold out of position would leave me putting in nearly 10% of my stack with a hand that has poor playability and high reverse implied odds in a multi-way or heads-up 3-bet pot. The pot odds do not justify calling with a dominated offsuit broadway hand. The disciplined and correct play is to fold.

Facing a 3-bet to 900 from FiveTwo in MP after my UTG open to 300 with QJh suited. Everyone else folded, so heads-up OOP postflop. QJhh is a strong speculative hand but marginal OOP vs a 3-bet range, which likely includes QQ+, AKo/s, and some bluffs from an aggressive player like FiveTwo (history shows frequent raises/3-bets). Pot odds are favorable (600 into 1950, ~24% equity needed), but postflop playability suffers OOP on deep stacks (~55BB), and villain has position. 4-betting as bluff risks stack with mediocre blocker equity and poor postflop play if called. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament (hand 58/150), avoiding tough decisions. History shows FiveTwo capable of strong value 3-bets (e.g., Hand 49 big pot). Discipline over marginal heroism.

We’re 6-handed but still first to act (UTG) with J4o, a dominated, offsuit hand that plays poorly postflop (reverse implied odds when we make a weak jack; little connectivity to realize equity). With ~77 BB effective there’s no need to take thin, high-variance opens/limps from UTG. Best EV is to keep our range disciplined and fold.

Hand is K♦2♥ (king-deuce offsuit) in middle position with 150 in pot and 100 to call. This is a very weak, poorly playing hand: dominated by most Kx hands and with terrible kicker value postflop. There are several players left to act (cutoff, button, SB, BB) with history of calling/raising and enough stack to 3-bet — so opening or calling often gets me into marginal multiway or 3‑bet spots out of position. With a deep 203bb stack I can wait for much better spots with real equity. Fold and preserve the stack for a stronger hand/position.

I have Jack-Ten offsuit in the cutoff. With the two players before me folding, this is a standard opening raise situation to pick up the blinds or play the hand with initiative. JTo is well within my opening range for this position in a 6-handed game.
Q6o on the button facing a 3x raise from the cutoff. This is a weak hand that plays poorly postflop even with position. Not strong enough to call profitably or 3-bet as a bluff. Clean fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 7d facing a 3x raise (300) from the Cutoff (Flash). My hand is an unsuited connector, which plays poorly out of position, especially against a standard raise size. While I am getting decent pot odds, the positional disadvantage and the weakness of 67o make this a negative expected value call. The prudent play is to fold and preserve my stack of 113 BBs for better spots.

In the big blind facing an isolation raise from the cutoff after two early folds, my hand K3s is marginal at best. It's suited with a king blocker, offering some flush and straight potential, but the 3 makes postflop play difficult against a wide calling range. Pot odds are about 2.25:1 (200 to call for 650 pot post-call), but with poor equity and reverse implied odds due to the weak kicker, folding preserves my stack for better opportunities. Flash's cutoff raise suggests a wide range, but K3s doesn't play well enough multiway or heads-up. Standard fold.

Hand is T6 of clubs — suited but a weak Ten. I'm UTG with five players left to act, so out of position and very likely to face raises or multiway action that heavily dominates my hand (better Tens, broadways, suited connectors). With a healthy stack (20300) I can afford to wait for a better, more playable spot; limp-calling or opening UTG with T6s is thin + high variance. Folding preserves my stack and avoids difficult postflop decisions out of position.

I am in Middle Position with Queen-Ten offsuit. Since the first player (Minni) folded, I am the first to act. In a 6-max tournament structure, QTo is a standard opening hand from middle-to-late positions. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take initiative, define my hand, and attempt to win the blinds or play the pot with the lead if called.

I have pocket Aces - the best starting hand in poker. Flash has opened to 300 from middle position. With AA in the cutoff, I must 3-bet to build the pot and isolate. A standard 3-bet size is around 3x the open, so 900 is appropriate.

I hold Kd 2c on the button. There has been an open raise to 300 from Flash and a 3-bet to 900 from Claude. K2o is a very weak hand that cannot profitably call a 3-bet cold, nor is it a suitable candidate for a 4-bet bluff in this spot. The only correct play is to fold.

Facing a raise from Flash in middle position and a 3-bet to 900 from Claude in cutoff, with J8o offsuit in the small blind. This is a weak, offsuit hand with limited playability. Claude's 3-bet sizing suggests strength, and I'm out of position postflop against a likely strong range. Pot odds to call 850 into 1350 offer about 2.6:1, but equity realization is poor OOP with this hand. BB still to act, but folding preserves stack for better spots in this multi-way tournament dynamic. History shows aggressive play, but no need to speculate here.

We’re in the BB facing an open (300) and a 3-bet to 900 with the original raiser still behind. K2s is too weak to cold-call a 3-bet out of position: we’ll be dominated by Kx/Ax, realize equity poorly, and can get squeezed into tough spots postflop. A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive with these stack sizes (Claude can jam ~5.9k and likely won’t fold enough). Best play is to preserve chips and wait for a better defend/squeeze candidate.

I opened with a standard raise to 300 with QTo from middle position. Claude responded with a 3-bet to 900 from the cutoff. Queen-Ten offsuit is a marginal opening hand and performs poorly against a 3-bet, especially when I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Claude has shown aggression in previous hands, but even if his range is wide, QTo is often dominated by his value range (AQ, KQ, AJ, and pairs) and does not have enough equity or playability to justify calling 600 more. Folding preserves my healthy stack of over 80 big blinds for better spots.

I am in the UTG position with Ace-Eight offsuit. In a 6-max game, A8o is generally considered too weak to open from Under the Gun, as there are five players left to act who could easily have a dominating hand or re-raise me. Recent history shows Claude is willing to 3-bet (Hand 60) and Minni is playing a very large, aggressive stack. Folding here is the standard, disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities in later positions.
7h4d is a very weak offsuit hand in middle position. No reason to play this - clear fold.

I am in the Cutoff with J7o, which is a very weak disconnected off-suit hand. The action has folded to me. Despite being in late position, this hand is below the standard opening range for a Cutoff open, even when attempting to steal blinds. The risk of being called or raised by the Button or Blinds (especially the deep-stacked Big Blind) makes this a clear fold. I will wait for a better spot.

Pocket Jacks (JJ) is a premium starting hand, top 5% of hands. Folded to me on the button in a 6-handed game, this is an ideal spot to raise for value against the blinds, build the pot with a strong hand, and leverage position post-flop. Standard raise size is 3x the big blind (300) given the pot of 150 and no prior action from early positions. Minni (BB) is deep-stacked and aggressive based on history, but JJ plays well against their range. FiveTwo (SB) has a solid stack but likely wide defending range from SB. Raising isolates and applies pressure.

We’re in the small blind facing a Button min-open. From the SB we should largely play 3-bet-or-fold because calling realizes equity poorly out of position and invites the BB to come along or squeeze. Kh6d is offsuit, dominated by Elon’s continuing range (Kx, broadways, better suited hands), and it has weak playability on most flops. It’s not a good candidate to 3-bet either because we risk getting flatted by the deep BB or facing a 4-bet/jam from a 51bb opener, turning this into a costly bluff. Best is to avoid the leak and fold.

Facing a UTG+? (button) raise to 300 with Kc9d in the big blind. This is a standard button open and K9o is marginal but playable from the BB — it can flop a top pair and has decent postflop playability. The price to continue is small (to-call 200 into a ~450 pot → required equity ~31%), and stacks are deep enough to see a flop and fold to heavy action if we miss. 3-betting out of position with K9o would be overly ambitious and too often dominated; folding surrenders too much equity against a single raiser. Plan: call, see flop, proceed cautiously — c‑fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, extract value on Kx or strong draws.

I called preflop and the button checked the flop to me on 7♦ Q♦ 3♥. With K♣9♦ I have one overcard (a decent chance to make top pair) and only a runner‑runner diamond flush possibility — still a marginal hand. The button’s range includes many Qx and diamond draws, so betting here as a bluff is thin and will often get called by better hands; I prefer pot control and to realize equity. I’ll check and see how Elon reacts: call small bets to see a turn but fold to significant pressure (pot‑sized shove) unless I improve. If he checks back I get a free turn to hit a K/9 or pick a better spot to apply pressure.

Pocket Jacks remain very strong on this Qd-7d-3h flop, an overpair to the lower cards with top pair blocker (Jd blocks some QJ). The board has diamond flush draw potential, but Minni's pre-flop call from BB is wide (often speculative hands), and their check suggests weakness—no strong Qx or sets likely to check. FiveTwo folded pre, so heads-up with position. Pot 650, my stack 5125 allows a standard 60% pot c-bet (~400) for value against weaker pairs/draws and protection against diamonds/backdoors. Protects equity, builds pot with showdown value, and leverages history of Minni calling light but folding to pressure (e.g., Hands 1,10,22). Avoid check-back as it invites free cards to flush/straight outs.

Call 400. Elon opened from the button and his small flop bet (~400 into ~1050) looks like a standard continuation/probe — not a pot-sized shove — so I’ll take the cheap shot to see a turn. Pot odds are ~27.6% (400 into a ~1450 pot), and my hand (K♣9♦) has reasonable back-end equity: one overcard (three kings), three nines, plus a backdoor diamond possibility (making a flush by river only with two more diamonds). Rough math: ~24% to pair by river + ~4% to hit the backdoor flush → roughly a break-even/marginal equity vs this sizing. Raising here would be overly ambitious out of position with a marginal hand and would risk bloating the pot against an aggressive opponent with ~4.7k effective. Plan: call to see the turn; fold to significant, polarized pressure if I don’t improve; if I pair the K or pick up a strong draw (turn diamond making a clear draw) I’ll press/raise depending on sizing and opponent action.

Check. I hold K♣9♦ and missed — the turn paired the board (Qd Qh 7d 3h) so I have no made hand and my backdoor diamond draw is dead. Elon opened and c‑bet the flop, which keeps a wide range but now heavily favors Qx (which becomes trips) and other hands that will barrel turn for value. Bluffing or raising out of position here is too risky: I’d be representing a hand (a queen or a set) that he actually has a lot of, and I have very little fold equity. I’ll check to control the pot and see how he proceeds. Plan: if he fires a small turn bet I can call to see the river for cheap, but I’ll fold to large, polarized pressure since I have only king‑high; if he checks back I get a free river to try to hit a king or bluff later in a better spot.

Pocket Jacks remain an overpair on a paired queens board (Qd7d3hQh). Minni's check-call flop and check turn indicate a wide range: likely weaker pairs (88-TT), diamond flush draws (Adx, Kdx), or Qx with weak kicker. Strong Qx or QQ/set would often bet flop or turn for value. The Qh doesn't complete many new draws but keeps diamond flush draw live. With position and pot 1450, a 2/3 pot bet (~1000) extracts value from worse (e.g., 88, flush draws, Qx), protects equity, and denies free cards. My Jd blocks QJ diamond straights. Minni's history shows calling stations tendencies but folds to aggression when weak (e.g., Hands 1,10). Effective stack allows this without overcommiting.

I’m on K♣9♦ with the board 7♦ Q♦ 3♥ Q♥. I checked the turn and Elon (button opener) fired a 1,000 bet into a 2,450 pot. That bet requires ~29% equity to justify a call (1,000/(2,450+1,000) ≈ 29%). I have virtually no showdown value — only three kings can improve me to beat a Qx/trip-heavy value range — so my realistic equity vs his betting range is well under the required threshold. His line (preflop open → flop c‑bet → sizable turn barrel after the board pairs) heavily favors Qx or other value hands; raising is a poor option since I have no fold equity or strong draw and would risk getting stacked by a shove. With a big stack and no reason to chase, I fold and wait for a better spot.

Pocket 2s in UTG with 73 BBs. Small pocket pairs can be opened from UTG for set-mining value - if I hit a set I have great implied odds against deeper stacks like Minni (195 BBs). Standard 2.5x open to 250. Will fold to a 3-bet and look to hit a set on the flop.

The player 'Claude' in UTG has a demonstrated history of opening extremely wide and weak hands from early position, including 4hTc (Hand 17), Qs2d (Hand 18), and 3c8h (Hand 19). Against a standard UTG range, KJo is a fold, but against Claude's specific maniacal UTG range (likely 50%+ of hands), KJo is well ahead equity-wise and dominates many of his Kx, Jx, and trash holdings. 3-betting to isolate is a valid option but risks bloating the pot with a marginal hand or folding out the very trash I dominate. Calling allows me to play a pot in position against a weak range for a cheap price (2.5bb). I am aware of the players behind, specifically the deep-stacked Minni in the SB who might squeeze, but given the price and position, calling is a positive EV adjustment to the specific opponent.

Preflop in cutoff facing a UTG raise from Claude to 250 and a call from Pro in middle position. My hand is Tc5h, offsuit trash with poor playability, dominated equity against typical UTG ranges, and will be multiway with button, blinds to act. No fold equity worth chasing a squeeze here given stack sizes and position. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

8♦3♣ is far too weak to continue versus an UTG open and a cold-call: we’re dominated when we make a pair, have poor playability, and the pot is likely to go multiway. A squeeze here would need very high fold equity against two players (including the opener) and risks getting called in spots where we realize equity poorly. Best is to preserve stack and wait for a better spot in position.

Claude opened UTG to 250 and Pro cold‑called. I’m in the small blind with K♣7♦ — out of position to at least two opponents in a multiway pot. Calling 200 more would make the pot 850, so I’d need ~200/850 ≈ 23.5% equity to justify a call. K7o is frequently dominated by UTG/ caller ranges (better Ks, KQ, KJ, pairs, broadways) and plays poorly OOP multiway. 3‑betting here with K7o is a weak bluff: it’s out of position, will be called by better hands or punished by 4‑bets, and I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal holding. With a healthy stack I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I am holding 52o, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Claude raised from UTG and Pro called, which suggests I would be playing a multi-way pot out of position with a hand that has very poor equity and playability. Even with the pot odds offered to call the remaining 150 chips, it is strategically better to fold and preserve my stack rather than get involved in a disadvantageous spot.

Missed my set with pocket 2s on JhTd4s board. This is a coordinated board that hits many calling ranges (Jx, Tx, straight draws like KQ/Q9/89). My hand has almost no showdown value and very little equity. C-betting would be a pure bluff that folds out nothing better and gets called/raised by hands that beat me. Best to check and potentially give up or see a cheap turn.

The board is [Jh Td 4s], giving me Top Pair with a King kicker. Claude, who opened pre-flop from UTG with a very wide range, has checked to me. This check often indicates weakness (missed broadways, small pairs) or pot control, though a trap is possible. Given the connected nature of the board (straight draws for KQ, 89, Q9, plus overcards for various hands), checking back risks giving a free card that could beat me or kill my action. Claude has shown tendencies to be 'sticky' and call down with marginal hands (Hand 40), so betting for value is superior to slow-playing. I want to extract value from straight draws and worse pairs (Tx, 88, 99) while denying equity to his random air. A bet of around half-pot is appropriate.

I have pocket 2s on JhTd4s - completely missed my set. Pro bet 325 into 650 pot. I have essentially no equity here (only 2 outs) and no showdown value with two overcards on board. Calling to hit a 2 on turn/river is -EV. Easy fold.

I am holding 4d 2c in the UTG position. This is an extremely weak hand, unsuited and disconnected. Playing this hand from early position is a losing strategy in almost any circumstance, as I will be out of position against the entire table with very low equity. With a stack of 117 BBs, there is no pressure to play marginal or trash hands. The correct and standard play is to fold immediately.

Ten-Eight offsuit is a marginal hand at best, and from middle position in a 6-handed tournament with deep stacks, it lacks the strength to open or limp. It has poor playability post-flop with dominated equity against typical calling ranges and no suited/connectors value to speculate. Pro has folded UTG, but no information on others yet. Standard play is to fold and wait for better opportunities to build the stack.

We’re first in from the cutoff with J4o, which is too weak to open profitably in a 6-max lineup where the blinds (especially Claude/Flash) have shown willingness to defend and 3-bet. Limping is also dominated and invites squeezes. Best play is to pass and preserve stack for higher-EV spots.

I’m on the button with 6h7c and a very deep stack (≈195bb). Versus only the blinds I want to open for fold equity and to play postflop in position. A standard 3x BB open (to 300 total) pressures the small blind and big blind while keeping the investment small relative to my stack. Plan: if called I’ll use position to evaluate the flop; if faced with a meaningful 3‑bet I’ll usually fold this speculative hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. Minni, the chip leader, has opened from the button. This is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against an aggressive big stack. The hand does not play well post-flop, and calling would also invite the Big Blind (Claude) to squeeze or see a flop with a wider range. Given the current tournament situation and the strength of my hand, folding is the standard and most prudent play.

8c7h in BB facing a button raise to 300. Getting 450:200 pot odds (2.25:1). 87o has decent playability with straight potential. Minni is active and on the button, so their range is wide. Calling 200 to see a flop in position-disadvantage isn't great, but the pot odds make it reasonable to defend here.
Completely missed this flop with 8c7h on Kh Qc 3d. No pair, no draws. This board heavily favors the preflop raiser's range. With 8-high I have no reason to lead out - just check and fold to any bet.

Flop K♥ Q♣ 3♦ completely misses my 6♥7♣ — no pair, no meaningful draws (only a runner‑runner straight). Claude checked the flop; facing a checked board a continuation bet here is thin: his BB calling range (Kx/Qx/Ax/pairs/broadways) connects often and will call, and he has stack to raise for value. Pot is 650 and stacks are deep (me ~19,250; Claude ~6,725) — I don’t want to build a big pot with air. I’ll check to control the pot, use position to gather info, and only bluff later if he shows weakness; if he fires a sizable bet I’ll fold this hand.

Turn 6s doesn't help me - I still have 8-high with no real draws. Minni checked back the flop showing weakness, but I shouldn't try to bluff here with zero equity. If I bet and get called, I'm drawing nearly dead. Best to check and see a free river, then fold to any bet. No reason to bloat the pot with air.

Turn puts a 6 on board; I now have a very marginal pair (bottom pair with a 7 kicker). Claude is the BB who called preflop and has checked flop and turn, showing pot‑control/weakness. Pot is only 650 and Claude's effective stack (~6.7k) means I don't want to build a big pot with such a weak holding. Betting for value is thin — hands that beat me (Kx/Qx/pairs) will often call and worse hands rarely call — and a small bet simply bloats the pot against better holdings. In position I can control the size by checking and see how he reacts on the river: if he leads out strongly I can fold; if he checks back I can take a free card or make a small value/steal attempt on the river depending on the card. Therefore I check to keep the pot small and gather info.

River 5h doesn't help - still have 8-high. Minni checked back flop and turn, showing weakness, but 8-high has zero showdown value. Could attempt a bluff but Minni's passive play suggests they likely have a marginal hand (small pair, ace-high) that would call. Bluffing into a weak-made hand is usually -EV. Best to check and give up the pot if Minni bets - not worth investing more chips with complete air.

I have bottom pair (6s) with a 7 kicker on K‑Q‑3‑6‑5. Claude (BB) called my preflop open then checked flop, turn and river — a very pot‑controlling line that often contains Kx/Qx or medium pairs, and he’s got the stack to call small bets. Betting here for value is thin (few worse hands will call) and a bluff is risky because K/Q will often call a small bet and I can’t fold out many better hands. Pot is only 650 and I want to keep the pot small with this marginal holding. I’ll check and take showdown; if Claude leads out small I can call, but I’ll fold to a sizable river bet.

A3o (As3c) is a standard fold UTG in 6-handed NLHE. Weak offsuit ace with poor kicker, dominated by better aces, and doesn't play well multiway or OOP. Stacks are deep (~60BB), pot is small (150), no need to speculate. Folding preserves stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV in tournament.

We’re 6-handed and UTG has folded, so K8s is comfortably within a standard MP opening range. Limping invites multiway play against tougher ranges and forfeits initiative; raising lets us win the blinds often and realize equity with a suited, connected-ish hand when called. Given some opponents have shown willingness to 3-bet larger, I’ll use a solid but not bloated open (2.5bb) and plan to fold to significant 3-bet pressure, especially from the deeper/aggressive stacks behind.

Q♥4♣ is a weak offsuit queen with a poor kicker and very little playability. Facing a 250 open from middle position, I’m up against a range that dominates me (pairs, broadways, Axs) and I’d need ~38–39% equity to justify calling 250 into a 400 pot — Q4o rarely gets that vs an opener. Calling also leaves me out of position against Button and the blinds and invites multiway trouble. 3-betting as a bluff is unattractive because I don’t have meaningful blockers and risk a lot vs competent callers/4-bets. With a healthy 19,900 stack I’ll preserve chips for better spots and fold here.

I am on the button with KJo, a solid hand that plays well in position. FiveTwo, who has shown aggressive tendencies in previous hands (like 3-betting with 95o in Hand #50), opened for 2.5x from middle position. While KJo can be dominated by an extremely tight range, it is ahead of a standard opening range and definitely worth a call to utilize my positional advantage post-flop. 3-betting is an option, but calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to evaluate the flop before committing more chips.

J6o in the small blind facing a raise and a call. This is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position in a multiway pot. Pro can still squeeze behind me. The pot odds of 200 to win 650 aren't compelling enough with this marginal holding OOP. Easy fold.

I hold Pocket Nines [9d 9s] in the Big Blind. FiveTwo raised from Middle Position and Flash called on the Button. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot of 650) to close the action. While a 3-bet squeeze is an option to deny equity and capitalize on the dead money, it risks facing a 4-bet from the initial raiser or playing a bloated pot out of position with a hand vulnerable to overcards. Calling allows me to set-mine effectively and play a controlled pot with a hand that plays well on low textures. Given the stack depths and multi-way action, flat-calling is the most prudent play.

I hold Pocket Nines on a Ks Tc 7d board. This texture (King-Ten-Seven) connects well with the pre-flop raiser's range (FiveTwo) and the caller's range (Flash), both of whom likely have broadway cards. Being out of position in a multi-way pot with an underpair to two board cards, leading out would be ineffective. My standard line is to check to the aggressor. If faced with a bet, I will likely have to fold as my hand is weak relative to the board and the number of opponents.

We’re the preflop aggressor and this K-T-7 rainbow flop hits our range reasonably well. With K8 we have top pair but a weak kicker, and we’re out of position versus the button, so I don’t want to bloat the pot. At the same time, checking gives a free card to a lot of overcards/gutters (QJ, J9, 98) and lets worse pairs (Tx/7x) realize equity. A small c-bet targets those worse holdings, denies equity, and keeps our range protected; if we face significant raise pressure multiway, we can comfortably let this go or proceed very cautiously depending on sizing/tells.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a K-T-7 rainbow board. FiveTwo, the pre-flop aggressor, has placed a small continuation bet of about 1/3 the pot. This could be a standard continuation bet with a wide range of hands. Calling is the best option here to maintain control of the pot and use my positional advantage to see how the turn develops. Raising would likely only get called by better hands like AK or KQ, while calling keeps FiveTwo's bluffs and weaker value hands in the pot.

I hold Pocket Nines [9d 9s] on a flop of [Ks Tc 7d]. After checking, the original raiser (FiveTwo) bets 275, and the button (Flash) calls. Although the pot odds are decent (requiring ~17% equity), my hand is very weak relative to the board and the action. The board texture hits MP and BTN ranges hard (containing many Kx and Tx combos). With an underpair to two board cards, being out of position against two opponents, and having only 2 outs to improve to a set (which might not even be good), my equity is low and difficult to realize. Folding is the only profitable long-term play here.

We have top pair with a weak kicker (K8) and we’re out of position versus the button after he called our flop c-bet. That flop call weights him toward made hands (better Kx, KT/sets, Tx) plus some draws; the turn (3d) also introduces a diamond flush draw that can continue versus a second barrel. If we bet again, we rarely get raised/called by many worse hands while getting value-owned by better kings and allowing draws to continue profitably. Checking keeps the pot under control, protects our medium-strength hand, and also induces stabs from his missed floats/draws that we can bluff-catch versus reasonable sizing.

I have top pair with a King and a Jack kicker. After calling a standard continuation bet on the flop, my opponent (FiveTwo) has checked the turn. FiveTwo is an aggressive player, so a check here often indicates they are either giving up on a bluff or looking for a cheap showdown with a marginal hand like a Ten or a smaller pocket pair. While I could bet for value, checking back is a solid pot-control move with a medium-strength top pair. It protects me against a check-raise from a stronger King or a monster and allows me to either induce a bluff on the river or value-bet if checked to again.

River brings the 5d completing the diamond flush after the turn checked through. With K8 (no diamond) our hand is a medium-strength bluff-catcher: we beat his floats/missed straight draws and some Tx/7x that may check back, but we get called mostly by better Kx and all made flushes if we bet. Thin value is poor here, and a block-bet risks getting raised off our equity by flushes. Checking keeps the pot controlled and allows him to bluff missed hands; we can then respond based on sizing.

I have top pair with a King and a Jack kicker. My opponent, FiveTwo, led the flop with a small continuation bet but has since checked both the turn and the river. This sequence typically suggests either a hand with moderate showdown value (like a Ten or a mid-pocket pair) that is afraid of the King or a total bluff that has given up. While I could potentially value-bet and get called by a Ten, checking back is a more consistent strategy to realize my equity and ensure I win the pot without being exposed to a check-raise, especially since the backdoor diamond flush draw completed on the river. Given my position and the action, checking back to showdown is the most reliable path to maximizing my long-term stack.

We’re 6-handed but still first to act with five players behind. Q6 offsuit is too weak and dominated (by better queens and many suited broadways) to profitably limp or open from UTG at ~70bb effective. Folding preserves stack and avoids getting isolated or playing a multiway pot out of position with poor playability.

A♦Q♥ is a strong, standard open from middle position — vulnerable to AK but ahead of most broadway and suited connectors. The table routinely opens to ~300, stacks are deep (~199 BB), and I want initiative and fold equity. I’ll open-raise to 3x the big blind (300) to build the pot with a high-equity hand, isolate the blinds, and use a size that matches table tendencies. Postflop I’ll c-bet good textures and re-evaluate vs 3-bets (fold to very large 3-bets, call or 4-bet situationally against specific players).

I am in the Cutoff with King-Ten offsuit. Minni, who is currently the chip leader and playing very aggressively, has opened for 300 from middle position. While King-Ten is a decent hand, it is frequently dominated by an opening range, even from a loose player. Furthermore, I have an active player on the button (Claude) and the blinds yet to act, which increases the risk of being squeezed or playing a multi-way pot out of position. Given my healthy stack of 88 big blinds, there is no need to take a high-variance, marginal spot against the chip leader with a hand that does not play well post-flop when faced with resistance.

K8o on the button facing a 300 raise from Minni. Marginal hand but I have position. Minni has been active throughout the session. Calling to see a flop in position is reasonable - not strong enough to 3-bet, but playable on the button.

I hold a pair of Deuces (22) in the Small Blind. This is a classic set-mining spot. Minni raised to 300 and Claude called on the Button. I am getting direct pot odds of 3:1 (calling 250 to win 750), which is attractive. More importantly, the effective stacks are deep enough (Claude has ~64bb, Minni covers us) to offer excellent implied odds if I flop a set. The 'Call 20' rule is satisfied (effective stacks > 20 * call amount). Although I am out of position, the strategy is straightforward: fit-or-fold. I will call and look to hit a set.

Preflop in BB with 9h8h suited connectors. Pot is 1000 and I need to call 200 more, offering about 5:1 odds (20% equity required). 98s has strong implied odds potential with flush and straight draws, plays excellently multiway. Minni raised from middle position (she raises frequently), called by button Claude and SB Pro, suggesting a wide range. Calling is highly profitable due to pot odds and hand playability. Raising risks isolating with a drawing hand or getting shoved on; folding wastes equity. Standard call to see flop cheap.

I missed my set on the flop of Th 8d Tc. In a 4-way pot, my pocket pair of Deuces is extremely weak, especially on a paired board where opponents likely connect with Tens or have higher pocket pairs. My strategy pre-flop was fit-or-fold. Since I did not flop a set, I have no equity to protect or value to extract. I will check and fold to any bet.

Flop of Th8dTc gives me top two pair (tens and eights) with a 9 kicker, an extremely strong hand heads-up but still very strong multiway in a 1200-chip pot. The board is paired, reducing straight draw possibilities but opening set-mining equity. Pro checked from SB, which is weak, and we're 4-handed with Minni (frequent raiser, aggressive postflop) and Claude behind.
