Betting for value targets overpairs (QQ+, AK), weaker pairs (J9, 97 for trips/draws), and any draws. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing Minni/Claude to bet big. A 2/3 pot bet sizes well for value/call-down, protecting against villainous floats or turns that improve draws. Excellent hand to realize equity now.

I opened to 300 preflop with A♦Q♥. The flop (T♥ 8♦ T♣) pairs the board — a dangerous texture for overcards, but Elon (BB) has made a relatively small bet/raise to 800 into a ~2000 pot. With two overcards (including the A) I have decent equity vs a wide flop-raising/float range and calling gets ~28.6% pot odds (800 into a 2000 pot). This is a multiway pot with callers behind/around, so raising here would have poor fold equity and risks isolating myself against hands that already have tens or better. I’ll call to realize equity, keep weaker hands and bluffs in, and re-evaluate on the turn (if I pair my A/Q or get clear information I can bet for value; if the turn is a hostile card or heavy action continues, I can fold).

Board is Th 8d Tc - I have middle pair (8s) making two pair TT88. However, Elon bet 800 and Minni called, showing significant strength. On a paired ten board, either likely has a ten for trips, an overpair, or at minimum a better kicker with an 8. Getting 3.5:1 pot odds but I'm likely dominated. Folding is prudent.

I missed my set on the flop of Th 8d Tc. Facing a substantial bet of 800 from Elon into a 4-way pot, and a call from Minni, my pair of Deuces is virtually worthless. I am drawing to 2 outs at best, and likely drawing dead against a Ten or better full house possibilities. My plan was strictly fit-or-fold. I will fold.

Heads-up on turn with top two pair (tens and eights) on Th8dTc7s board, pot 2800. Preflop raiser Minni called my flop c-bet of 800 into ~1400, which polarizes her range to strong hands like overpairs (QQ+), Tx (T9s, suited connectors), sets (88, TT unlikely given board), or draws. The 7s turn completes J9/JT straight draws, but her MP raise range doesn't include many speculative J9 hands multiway; more likely value hands that beat my two pair are sets or better, which are rare. My hand remains very strong, ahead of most of her calling range (overpairs, mid pairs, weaker Tx). Pot odds favor betting for value against overpairs and protection vs any missed draws. She has deep stack (18800), so sizing 2/3 pot (~1900) extracts value while leaving room for river action. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or check-raise from monsters. Continue aggressing to realize equity and build pot.

Preflop I opened A♦Q♥ and called Elon's flop bet to realize equity. Turn is T♥ 8♦ T♣ 7♠ and Elon now leads for 1,900 into a 4,700 pot (I must call 1,900). Calling requires ~29% equity. My hand is just two overcards with very limited clean outs on this paired board — many of Elon's continuing hands are trips (T‑x), sets (88) or hands that turn into full houses, and an Ace or Queen on the river will often be counterfeited or still lose. Elon is relatively short and his line (c‑bet then a big turn bet) is consistent with value; while I block some AT/QT combos with my A and Q, there are still plenty of T and 8 combos that crush me. Raising would be reckless with no pair or real fold equity. With poor fold‑equity and negative expected value to continue, I fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

UTG with 6♣5♣ — a low suited connector. In early position facing five players yet to act, this hand is too speculative to open or even limp. Out of position postflop, frequently dominated by callers and vulnerable to isolation 3-bets from aggressive players at this table. Calling 100 just to see a flop puts me into multiway spots with poor playability; raising to 200 is thin and will lead to difficult postflop decisions or being exploited. Preserve the deep stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Holding Nine-Six offsuit in middle position after the first player folded. This is a very weak hand with no significant potential for straights or flushes, and it is easily dominated by any range that calls or raises behind me. With 88 big blinds and plenty of hands left in the tournament, there is no reason to play this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

4c7c in the cutoff is a marginal hand. While suited, 74s is at the bottom of reasonable opening ranges even from late position. With Pro having a large stack on the button who could 3-bet, and two players in the blinds, I don't want to build a pot with this weak holding. Better to fold and wait for a stronger spot.

I am on the Button with JTo and the action has folded to me. This is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. My hand is strong enough to open from the Button (top ~40-50% range). I will raise to 2.5bb (250), which is a standard sizing to apply pressure to the blinds while not risking too much if 3-bet.

T8o is a marginal offsuit connector in the small blind facing a button raise to 250 (2.5x). Button ranges are wide in late position, but I'm out of position post-flop, which is a significant disadvantage. Equity against a typical ~30-40% button open range is decent (~40%) but drops OOP and if BB defends (FiveTwo may squeeze or flat). Pot odds are 2:1 (call 200 into 400), but implied odds are limited with offsuit hand lacking strong nut potential or flush draws. Pro has been active in raises but shown mixed results. Standard play is to fold T8o here to avoid tough post-flop spots OOP and preserve stack for better opportunities in this long tournament (66/150 hands).

Button open to 2.5x is likely wide, and we’re getting a great price in the BB (150 to win 400 now, needing ~27% equity). While 54o is near the bottom of a defend range and has some reverse-implied-odds issues, it still realizes enough equity versus a wide BTN range, and calling keeps weaker hands in and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal offsuit hand. Plan postflop is to check a lot, continue on boards where we pick up equity (pairs/straight draws), and selectively apply pressure on favorable textures.

Heads-up BB vs BTN in a single-raised pot, this flop is one where BB can have a strong condensed value range (44/66/99, two-pairs) and BTN will c-bet a lot of overcards. With 4d5h we have a weak made hand (bottom pair) plus a gutshot to the 3, so our priority is to realize equity without inflating the pot. Checking keeps our range protected, lets BTN bluff/value-bet worse, and we can respond based on sizing (often call small bets; consider check-raising only versus frequent small c-bets when our hand functions as a semi-bluff).

I raised pre-flop from the Button with ThJs and the Big Blind defended. The flop comes 9c 4h 6s, which is a low, somewhat connected board that interacts reasonably well with the Big Blind's defending range (containing many 9x, 6x, 4x, and straight draws like 78, 57). As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage, but my specific hand (Jack-high) has merely two overcards and weak backdoor potential. C-betting here might fold out better hands like KQ or worse hands like Q8, but if I get check-raised or called, I am in a difficult spot. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hitting a Jack or Ten on the turn, or re-evaluating on later streets. It keeps the pot manageable against an opponent who might connect with this low board.

Turn brings the Tc, which is a much better card for BTN’s range (broadways/Tx) than for our BB defend range. We have a marginal showdown hand (bottom pair) with only a small improvement path, and leading risks getting raised off our equity or value-owned by Tx/9x that improved. After BTN checks back the flop, his range contains a lot of A-high and medium-strength hands that will often take a stab when checked to; checking keeps the pot manageable and lets us respond to his sizing (often continue versus small bets, fold versus big pressure).

On the turn, I have improved to top pair (Tens) with a decent kicker (Jack). The board contains possible straight draws (78 completes a straight, various other draws exist), and checking back the flop disguised my hand strength. FiveTwo has checked to me again, signaling likely weakness or a drawing hand. I should bet now to extract value from worse pairs (like 9x, 6x) and to protect my hand against free cards that could complete draws or bring overcards. A bet of around 55% of the pot is appropriate to charge draws while getting called by worse made hands.

We’re getting 300 to win 850 (need ~26% equity) after BTN checked back flop and now takes a fairly standard delayed stab on a turn card (Tc) that improves a lot of his overcard range. Our hand is weak (bottom pair), but it’s still ahead of a meaningful portion of his delayed-c-bet bluffs (A/K/Q-high, some broadways) and we also have some improvement (river 4 for trips, river 5 for two pair). Raising is unnecessary and isolates us versus stronger Tx/9x; folding is likely too tight given the price and his high-frequency stab spot. Plan: call and re-evaluate river—mostly check and fold to substantial second barrels, continue on good rivers (4/5) and vs small sizing.

River comes 8h on 9c-4h-6s-Tc-8h. We have a very weak one-pair (4s) with poor kick and no meaningful blockers. After Pro checked back flop and bet turn, his range contains plenty of Tx/9x and some delayed bluffs (A/K/Q-high), but leading doesn’t accomplish much: worse hands rarely call, and better hands (Tx/9x/overpairs/now-straights with 7x) won’t fold often enough. Betting also risks getting raised and forced to surrender extra chips. Best is to check, realize our showdown equity versus missed overcards, and allow him to bluff; if he bets big, we can comfortably fold.

I hold ThJs on a board of 9c 4h 6s Tc 8h. I turned top pair and bet for value, getting called. The river 8h is a very scary card as it puts four cards to a straight on the board (6-8-9-T). Any 7 in the opponent's hand makes a straight. The Big Blind's range defends with many hands containing a 7 (e.g., 76, 87, 97, T7, J7, A7). Additionally, two pair combos like T9 or T8 are possible. While I have top pair, it is unlikely a worse hand (like a 9 or 6) calls a value bet on this texture, and I am beat by a significant portion of the calling range. Checking back is the best option to realize my showdown value without exposing myself to a check-raise or value-owning myself.

I am in the UTG position with Queen-Eight offsuit (Qd 8s). In a 6-max tournament structure, this hand is too weak to open from early position, as it is easily dominated by better Queens and typical calling ranges from late position. With a healthy stack of 88 big blinds, there is no reason to take an unnecessary risk with a marginal hand out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
Ad3d is a suited ace - a solid open-raising hand from middle position. I'll make a standard raise to 250 to take initiative and potentially win the blinds or build a pot with position on the blinds.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kd Ks), a premium hand, in the Cutoff. Claude has opened to 250 from Middle Position. My strategy is to 3-bet for value to build the pot immediately and isolate the initial raiser, preventing a multi-way pot and charging worse hands (like lower pairs or Ax). A standard 3-bet sizing in position is roughly 3x the open. Raising to 800 (3.2x) is appropriate given the 60bb effective stack depth with Claude.

J6o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, especially facing a raise and a 3-bet from Claude and Pro, who are likely strong. On the button, but the pot odds (calling 800 into 2000) don't justify it given my low equity against their ranges. Multiple players to act behind (SB/BB), but even without, this is an easy fold to preserve stack in a tournament setting. Prioritizing stack maximization over speculative plays.

We’re in the SB facing an open (250) and a fairly sizable 3-bet to 800, with us having to act out of position and the BB still behind with a big stack. K7s is too weak to continue here: calling creates an awkward SPR and we’ll often realize equity poorly OOP versus a stronger 3-bet range; 4-bet bluffing is also unattractive because K7s has poor blockers (doesn’t reduce AK/KQ/KK/AA much) and we risk getting jammed on and burning chips. Best EV is to let it go and preserve the 64bb stack for better spots.

Fold. Facing a 3-bet to 800 from the cutoff after Claude opened to 250, my 8♣7♠ is a marginal hand and I'm out of position. The pot is 1,200 and calling 700 requires roughly 700/(1200+700) ≈ 37% equity to break even; 8-7 offsuit typically has well under that against a cutoff 3-bet range (value-heavy). If I call I’ll be OOP against a polarized/vigorous range and likely face difficult postflop decisions or a 4-bet — a light 4-bet here would be too spewy without strong blockers. I’ll preserve my healthy stack and fold to this sized 3-bet.

Pro 3-bet me to 800 after my open raise. Ad3d is a suited ace but on the weaker end for calling a 3-bet, especially out of position. I'd be committing nearly 10% of my stack preflop with a hand that won't hit hard often. Pot odds are about 3.2:1 but my hand plays poorly OOP against a 3-bet range. Disciplined fold here.

Q9o from UTG is too weak to open. This hand plays poorly from early position - it's easily dominated by better queens and doesn't have the suited/connected strength to justify opening into 5 remaining players. Standard fold.

My hand is Ten of Hearts and Deuce of Hearts (Th 2h). This is a very weak hand (T2 suited) in Middle Position. It falls well outside standard opening ranges for 6-max No-Limit Hold'em due to the large gap between the cards and low playability. There is no strategic merit in raising or limping with this holding, especially with active players yet to act. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Preflop in cutoff with A7o, unopened after UTG and MP folds. A7o is a strong enough hand from this position due to ace blocker, decent high card value, and playability postflop. Standard open-raise size of 3x the BB to 300 to build the pot, deny equity to blinds, and take initiative. Button FiveTwo is shorter but aggressive at times; SB Minni deep and calls light; BB Flash defends wide based on history. Folding or limping is too passive; this spot maximizes EV long-term.

CO opens to 3bb and we’re on the button with a suited queen. Q3s isn’t strong enough to 3-bet (poor blockers, dominated when called), but position lets us realize equity, float/bluff favorable textures, and potentially outplay postflop. Given the wide CO opening range and our positional advantage, calling is preferable to folding while keeping the pot manageable against the blinds.

Cutoff opened to 300 and Button called, pot is 750 and I need to put in 250 more (300 total). I'm in the small blind with T9o — a marginal but playable hand with good post‑flop playability and straight/paired equity, especially with deep stacks. I don't want to 3‑bet out of position into two players with a hand that plays better seeing a flop; a min‑raise would bloat the pot OOP and invite action from the big blind and button. Calling 250 (total 300) gives ~25% pot odds and preserves my stack flexibility. Plan: see the flop and proceed aggressively only with top pair, strong draws, or clear equity; otherwise be prepared to fold to heavy continuation/turn aggression.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jc 5c. Elon raised from the Cutoff to 300, and both the Button (FiveTwo) and Small Blind (Minni) called. The pot is currently 1000, and it costs me 200 more to call. This gives me pot odds of 5:1. While J5s is a marginal hand and I will be out of position for most of the hand, the price is excellent to see a flop in a multi-way pot where suited hands can realize their equity well. I will call to see if I can hit a strong hand or a draw on the flop.

Check. I’m out of position in a multiway pot (Elon opened CO, BTN and BB called) with T9o on 4c–Ks–8c. I have no pair and only a remote backdoor club/five‑card straight possibility (Tc + 4c + 8c needs both turn+river clubs to make a flush — very low equity). Multiway and facing the original raiser makes a bluff or thin c‑bet unattractive; I want to keep the pot small and see a free turn card. Plan: check and fold to significant aggression unless I pair or improve to a clear draw (or the turn is a club which changes equities and could justify a delayed semi‑bluff or small bet). Pot 1,200, my stack 18,400 — preserve chips and re‑evaluate on the turn.

I am in a four-way pot with a weak hand (Jc 5c) on a board of 4c-Ks-8c. I have a backdoor flush draw and two overcards, but no actual pair or significant draw yet. In a multi-way pot, especially when the pre-flop aggressor (Elon) is yet to act, checking is the most prudent play. I want to see how the action develops before committing any more chips.

Preflop raise with A7o from CO was standard to take initiative. Flop K s 8c 4c misses me completely - ace-high no pair, no draws, ace blocker to AK but irrelevant here. Multiway pot (4 players) with 1200 effective, board favors Kx (which callers can have), sets, flush draws. As preflop raiser first to act postflop checks, c-betting air is -EV multiway vs calling ranges including pairs/draws. Check to pot control, realize equity cheaply if improve (A or gutshots unlikely), induce bluffs/value from worse, re-eval turn in position. Opponents deep/aggressive but no reason to spew chips with nothing.

Flop checks through to us multiway on K♠-8♣-4♣. With Q♣3♣ we have a strong equity hand (flush draw) but weak showdown value if we check back and miss. Elon checking as the preflop raiser caps his range somewhat, and we can credibly represent Kx/overpairs while also denying equity to random A-high/small pairs. Multiway reduces fold equity, so I’ll use a modest sizing that can still fold out the weakest holdings and start building a pot for when we complete.

I checked the flop with T9o and now face a 500 bet into a 1,700 pot (I must call 500). I have no pair and only weak backdoor club/runner-runner straight possibilities; my only realistic immediate outs are the three 9s and three 10s (6 outs, ~24% to hit by the river), and a single pair if it comes will often still be behind in this multiway pot where the original raiser and the big blind have shown interest. Calling 500 to win ~2,200 total requires ~23% equity — my raw odds to pair are borderline, and my actual realized equity is worse because of likely Kx/overpairs/top-pair hands and possible turn aggression OOP. A raise as a bluff is risky and thin with multiple callers and I'm out of position. I preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I have a flush draw with Jc 5c on a 4c-Ks-8c board. The pot is 1700 and it costs me 500 to call FiveTwo's bet, giving me odds of 3.4:1. With a flush draw, I have roughly a 19% chance of hitting on the turn and about a 35% chance by the river. Given the multi-way nature of the pot and the fact that Elon is still left to act, calling is the most appropriate action to try and realize my equity without over-committing to a marginal draw.

Facing a 500 bet into 1700 pot (now 2200 after Flash's call) on Kc8c4c flop with Ad7h (ace-high, no pair, no draw). Preflop raise checked through to BTN FiveTwo (aggressive caller pre) who donk-bets after two checks, SB folds, BB Flash calls wide range. Multiway pot, board hits callers' ranges hard (Kx, pairs 88/44, clubs flush draws, straights). My range as CO raiser/check includes AK/AQ missed, some bluffs, but zero equity here - no fold equity to raise multiway OOP to BTN after BB call, calling builds pot OOP with showdown trash vs value/draws. Pot odds 2200:500 ~4.4:1 but <0 equity, dead money gone. Standard fold preserves stack for better spots late tournament.

I am heads-up against FiveTwo on the turn with a flush draw. The 6d is largely a blank for my hand. Since I called FiveTwo's bet on the flop, I am first to act on the turn. I will check to the aggressor to see how he reacts. Betting here doesn't make much sense as a semi-bluff given that he bet into three players on the flop, indicating strength. I'm hoping to see a free river card or get a reasonable price to continue my draw.

We’re heads-up now after our flop stab got called, and Flash checks again on a blank-ish 6♦. With Q♣3♣ we still have a strong 9-out flush draw but weak showdown value if we take the free card and miss. Flash’s flop check/call range contains a lot of 1-pair hands (Kx/8x), some 4x/pocket pairs, and club draws; many of those hands are put under real pressure by a second barrel, especially since our line credibly represents strong Kx/overpairs/sets. Because our flush won’t always be the nuts (A♣x♣ exists), I don’t want to overinflate the pot with a huge size, but I do want fold equity plus the ability to win immediately or set up profitable river decisions. A ~45% pot barrel accomplishes that while keeping our risk reasonable if we face resistance.

I have a flush draw with Jc 5c on a board of 4c-Ks-8c-6d. There are 9 clubs remaining in the deck out of 46 unseen cards, giving me a 19.6% chance of hitting my flush on the river. The pot is currently 3200 and it costs me 1000 to call, offering pot odds of 3.2:1. To justify a call on mathematical grounds alone, I would need about 23.8% equity. However, the gap between my current equity and the required odds is small enough that implied odds (the extra chips I can win from FiveTwo if I hit my flush on the river) make a call profitable. Given that FiveTwo has shown aggression by betting into three players on the flop and continuing on the turn, it is likely he has a strong hand like Kx that might pay off a river bet if the flush completes. Raising is too risky as it could force me to fold my equity against a shove, and folding is too tight given the draw and the price.

I have Jack-high with a missed club flush draw on a board of 4c-Ks-8c-6d-8s. The 8s on the river paired the board, which might change the value of certain hands, but it does not help my J-high. Throughout the hand, FiveTwo has shown significant strength by betting into multiple players on the flop and continuing his aggression on the turn. His range likely includes Kings, 8s, or possibly a slow-played set of 4s or 6s. Given that I have no showdown value, my only way to win is to bluff. However, leading into the aggressor after calling two streets usually looks like a missed draw, and it's unlikely FiveTwo will fold a King or even a weak 8 given the pot size and his remaining stack. The most prudent play is to check and concede the hand if he bets, or hope for an unlikely check-back if he was also on a draw (though his line suggests made-hand strength).

River pairs the 8 and our club draw (Qc3c) misses, so we have essentially no showdown value and should not check back. Flash’s check/call line on flop+turn is often Kx/8x/6x plus missed club draws. The 8♠ is a good bluffing card because it makes his one-pair Kx hands more uncomfortable (now losing to any 8x and some boats), while our line credibly represents value (8x for trips/boats like 86/84/66/44, or strong Kx/overpairs). I’ll use a medium pressure size (~1/2 pot) to fold out missed draws and a meaningful chunk of marginal pairs without risking my whole stack; if I face a raise, I simply fold.

On the river, the board is 4c-Ks-8c-6d-8s and I have missed my flush draw, leaving me with only Jack-high. FiveTwo has shown significant strength throughout the hand, betting into three players on the flop and continuing his aggression on both the turn and the river. His betting line is very consistent with a strong made hand, such as a King or better. I have no showdown value, and it is highly improbable that FiveTwo is bluffing with a hand worse than Jack-high after this sequence of action. While the pot odds are 3:1, I likely have near-zero equity against his range. Therefore, I will fold and preserve my remaining 5200 chips.

I am in the UTG position at a 6-handed table with KJo. This hand is on the cusp of the opening range for UTG in 6-max. While it can be opened, it often suffers from reverse implied odds against calling ranges (which include AK, KQ, AJ) and 3-bets. Looking at my history, I folded AJo from this position in Hand #21, indicating a tighter opening strategy from early position. To maintain consistency and avoid playing a marginal dominated hand out of position, I will fold.

Preflop in middle position with T3o, an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against any calling range, and no speculative value. Standard tight-aggressive strategy dictates folding to avoid spew. Pot is small (150), but risking chips with this hand is -EV long-term. Preserve stack for better spots in tournament with 81 hands remaining.

Folds to me in the cutoff and 5♠2♠ has enough playability (suited, can make strong disguised hands) to profitably steal the blinds at 6-max. I’ll open-raise with a standard sizing to apply pressure and leverage position, while planning to fold comfortably versus a 3-bet from the Button or blinds given my hand’s weak raw strength.

I’m on the button with pocket queens (Qd Qh) facing a cutoff open to 250. QQ is a premium hand and I want to take the initiative — isolate the opener and deny equity to overcards by reducing multiway action. With a deep 184 BB stack I’ll 3-bet for value and fold equity; sizing to 1000 total (~4x the open) is large enough to discourage calling by the blinds and weaker cutoffs but leaves plenty of chips for postflop play. If I face a large 4-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent, but preflop the best play is an aggressive raise to build the pot and take control.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c 5c. FiveTwo opened from the Cutoff, and Minni (the chip leader) 3-bet to 1000 from the Button. While 75s is a decent speculative hand, calling 950 more out of position against an aggressive chip leader is not profitable. The pot odds are not favorable enough to justify a call with a hand that is easily dominated and requires a very specific board to continue. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

Facing a 3-bet from Minni (1000) with 9s7s. This is a speculative suited connector that plays poorly against a 3-bet range. I'd need to call 900 into a pot of ~2300. While 97s has some playability, it's too weak to call a 3-bet out of position. Minni has been aggressive and has a large stack. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Button 3-bets our CO steal to 1000, and we’ll be out of position with 5♠2♠. This hand has very poor equity realization versus a 3-bet range (dominated by higher suited hands, struggles to make strong top pairs), and the effective SPR after calling would be low enough that we won’t get paid when we do make a wheel/flush often enough to compensate. A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive here (no blockers, high fold-frequency requirement). Best play is to cut losses and preserve stack.

T3o is a trash hand from UTG in a 6-handed game. No playable equity, dominated by any raising range. Early position requires tight play to avoid difficult post-flop spots. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. Deep stacks don't change this.

AcJs is a strong suited Broadway that plays well as an open from MP after UTG folds. Raising pressures the blinds, denies equity to the cutoff/button’s speculative hands, and lets us take initiative against aggressive opponents. I’ll use a standard 3bb size consistent with the table’s opens to reduce multiway pots while keeping my range balanced.

9♠6♥ is a very weak, low-equity hand versus a middle-position open to 300. Calling 300 into a 450 pot requires roughly 40% equity to break even (300 / (450+300)), which 9-6o cannot provide against a raiser’s range. There are still the button and blinds to act, increasing the chance of a 3-bet or multiway pot where this hand plays poorly. No reason to inflate the pot from cutoff with such a hand — fold and wait for a better, playable spot.

I am holding 8h 2h on the button. FiveTwo has opened for 300 from middle position. While I have the benefit of position, 82 suited is an extremely weak hand that is likely dominated by a standard opening range. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve my stack, there is no reason to play such a marginal hand against a show of strength from FiveTwo.

K7o is a marginal hand, especially out of position against FiveTwo's open raise with Pro still to act behind me. K7o doesn't play well out of position and I'll face difficult decisions post-flop. While the pot odds are decent at ~1.8:1, the hand is too weak to defend profitably from the SB. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Ah Td facing a 3bb open raise from FiveTwo in Middle Position. FiveTwo has been active, and a 3bb raise is a standard opening size. ATo is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind, especially given the pot odds. I need to call 200 into a pot of 450, requiring about 31% equity. Against a reasonable MP opening range, ATo has significantly more equity than that (typically 45-50%). While being out of position suggests exercising some caution, 3-betting would likely fold out worse hands and isolate me against a stronger range or force a fold to a 4-bet. Therefore, calling to see a flop and re-evaluating is the most prudent play.

I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) on a Q-5-2 two-tone board. My hand (ATo) has missed the flop, leaving me with Ace-high. Standard poker strategy dictates checking to the aggressor in this spot. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands (Queens, pocket pairs) or flush draws, while folding out worse hands that I am currently beating or chopping with (like KJ, AJ). I will check and evaluate their action; if they bet, I will decide based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the correct play to see the next card cheaply or induce a bluff.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on Qh-5h-2c, I retain a meaningful range advantage (lots of Qx, overpairs) and can profitably apply pressure with a small c-bet. Even though AcJs has no heart and is currently just two overcards, a small bet can fold out a lot of BB overcards/gutters and deny equity to hands like 66–TT without a heart. I’ll use a low-risk sizing and comfortably fold to a check-raise from Pro’s stronger Qx/sets/heart-combos. If called, I can continue on strong turns (A/K, some hearts as range barrels) and shut down on bricks as needed.

The opponent FiveTwo has made a small continuation bet of 200 chips into a pot of 650 (approximately 30% pot). This small sizing often indicates a 'range bet' or a standard stab on a relatively dry board (Q-5-2). I am getting extremely favorable pot odds of about 19% (calling 200 to win a total pot of 1050). My hand, Ace-high with the Ten of diamonds and Ace of hearts, has reasonable equity to peel one card. The Ace of hearts provides a backdoor nut flush draw, and I have 6 outs to hit a pair of Aces or Tens, which would likely be the best hand. Given the price and the possibility that the opponent is betting light or with a wide range, calling is a positive expected value play. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate, likely folding to further aggression if I do not improve.

On the Turn, the board is [5h, 2c, Qh, 9h]. The 9h puts three hearts on the board. I hold the Ah, giving me the Nut Flush Draw. I also have two overcards (Ace, Ten) that could provide pair outs, though the flush is my primary source of equity. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser who bet the flop. Leading out (donk betting) here is generally suboptimal as it denies the opponent the chance to bluff or value bet worse hands, and opens me up to a raise which would be difficult to handle with just a draw. Checking is the standard line to the aggressor. If FiveTwo bets, I have significant equity to call. If they check back, I get a free chance to hit the nut flush on the river.

Turn brings the 3rd heart, which heavily improves Pro’s flop calling range (all two-heart floats now made flushes) and also creates a lot of check-call candidates with Qx/5x that Pro has shown a willingness to continue with. With AcJs and no heart, I have no relevant blockers to the flushes I’m representing and my equity when called is very poor. This is a good spot to take the free card, keep the pot manageable versus a bigger stack, and realize my ~6-out overcard equity. If I improve on the river (A/J) and Pro checks, I can consider a thin value/protection bet; otherwise I’m mostly giving up versus meaningful river aggression.

On the River, the board is [5h 2c Qh 9h 4d]. I missed my nut flush draw and currently hold Ace-High (Ah Td). My opponent, FiveTwo, raised pre-flop, c-bet small on the flop, and then checked back the turn. The turn check-back suggests they likely do not have a flush or a very strong hand, but rather a marginal made hand (like a weak Queen, a pair of 9s, or a pocket pair) that wants to get to showdown cheaply, or a complete air/missed draw. My Ace-High has some showdown value against their missed broadway draws (like KJ, KT, JT). Betting here would turn my hand into a bluff, but it is unlikely to fold out any pair they decided to check back the turn with. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value for free if they check back, or fold comfortably if they bet, minimizing loss. Therefore, checking is the optimal play.

River checks to us on Qh-5h-2c-9h-4d after we c-bet flop and checked back the 3rd-heart turn. With AcJs and no heart, we have no flush blocker and our line doesn’t credibly represent many strong value hands that want to bet (we rarely have big flushes/sets after checking turn). Pro’s flop call range contains plenty of one-pair hands that will bluff-catch (Qx, 5x, pocket pairs with/without a heart) plus some slow-played flushes, so a river stab is likely to get called too often and has very poor equity when called. Best play is to take the free showdown with ace-high and avoid burning chips into a sticky range.

6-handed but still UTG, T8o is too loose and frequently dominated by the calling/3-betting ranges behind (AT+/KT+/QTs, better tens/eights). With several aggressive opponents left to act and decent stacks, opening or limping will often put us in tough, low-EV spots out of position. Preserve stack and wait for better UTG opens.

2-7 offsuit is the worst starting hand — no real equity and no good blockers. There are four players still to act after me (CO, Button, SB, BB), so the pot will likely become multiway or face a raise. It costs 100 to continue into a very low-expected-value situation and opening/limping out of middle with 2‑7o is a clear leak. With a healthy 18,800 stack and plenty of hands left, preserve chips for better spots. Fold.

I am in the Cutoff with Ace-Five suited. This is a standard opening hand in this position. Folding has been frequent at the table, and both FiveTwo and Minni have folded. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds, or play a strong post-flop game with a suited Ace if called by the Button or the blinds.

J7o is a weak offsuit hand. While I have position on the button, calling a 3x raise with this hand invites the blinds to come along, and I'll be playing a marginal hand potentially multiway. Not strong enough to 3-bet as a bluff, and not worth a speculative call. Fold.

I hold Pocket Threes in the Small Blind facing a standard 3x open from Flash in the Cutoff. The effective stack is Flash's 66.5 BBs, which provides sufficient implied odds to set mine (I need to call 250 to potentially win a large portion of the 6650 stack, satisfying the 'Call 20' rule). Although I am out of position, the plan is straightforward: call pre-flop and play 'fit or fold' post-flop. If I hit a set, I can play for stacks; if I miss, I can easily check-fold. 3-betting is unnecessary, and folding is too tight given the price and stack depth.

AcTc is a strong suited ace hand with excellent playability, straight potential, flush draw potential, and top pair potential. Facing a Cutoff raise from Flash (who opens wide from late position based on history) and a Small Blind call from Pro (often speculative OOP), this is a prime spot to 3-bet from the Big Blind as we close the action preflop. We have position on SB, great equity against their calling ranges (blocker to AA/KK, dominates many Ax), and deep effective stacks (74BB with Flash). 3-bet sizing around 3.5x the open raise to build pot with strong hand and apply pressure. Raising to 1000 total bet size provides fold equity while allowing postflop flexibility if called.

I opened the action with a raise to 300 from the Cutoff holding Ace-Five diamonds, a strong suited ace with good playability. The Small Blind (Pro) called, and the Big Blind (Elon) 3-bet to 1,000. This is a significant raise, but I am getting reasonable pot odds (700 to call into a pot of 1,600, not including the potential call from Pro). Most importantly, I will have absolute position on both opponents for the remainder of the hand. Ace-Five suited is an excellent hand for calling 3-bets in position because it can flop nut draws (diamonds) and wheel draws, and it provides board coverage. Given Elon's demonstrated aggression in previous hands, his 3-betting range may include some broadway hands or smaller pairs that I can outplay post-flop. I will call to realize my equity in position.

I am facing a squeeze to 1000 from the Big Blind and a call from the original raiser. I have Pocket Threes in the Small Blind. I need to call 700 more. The pot is 2300, offering roughly 3.3:1 odds. However, my primary value with this hand comes from 'set mining' (flopping three of a kind). To profitably set mine, I typically want the effective stack to be at least 15-20 times the amount I have to call. Here, the call is 700, so I would want effective stacks of around 10,500 to 14,000. The opponents (Elon and Flash) only have about 5,000 to 5,500 behind. This gives me implied odds of less than 8:1, which is insufficient to justify the call, especially since I will be out of position in a multi-way pot against strong ranges. I will likely be forced to fold on almost any flop that doesn't contain a 3. Therefore, the disciplined play is to fold.

Preflop, AcTc is a strong suited hand that 3-bets well from the BB vs CO open and SB flat. Flash's call of the 3-bet indicates strength (likely KK+, AK, some QQ/JJ, suited broadways), while Pro's fold narrows it heads-up. Flop K♦7♥3♣ is dry rainbow, somewhat favoring my perceived range (Ax, sets) but Flash likely has Kx equity often. I have Ace-high + nut club flush draw (9 clean outs with Ac blocker), ~35% equity vs typical calling range, but OOP with no pair. Betting OOP risks getting raised off equity or stacking to better hands; checking realizes equity, induces bluffs/checks from missed Kx, controls pot, and keeps fold equity for later streets if he shows weakness. Deep stacks (74BB effective preflop) support check-call/raise dynamics postflop.

On a dry King-high board (3c 7h Kd), Elon (the pre-flop 3-better) has checked to me. My hand, Ad5d, currently only has Ace-high with a backdoor nut flush draw. Elon's 3-betting range often contains many Kings (AK, KQ) or high pocket pairs (AA, KK) that might be checking to trap or to control the pot. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and keep my options open for later streets. Betting here is unlikely to fold out a better hand immediately and might provoke a check-raise from a player who has shown significant aggression in previous hands.

Preflop AcTc was a strong 3-bet candidate from BB vs CO open + SB call, and Flash's flat indicates a strong but capped range (KK+, AK/AQ, QQ/JJ, suited broadways). Flop K♦7♥3♣ missed me but gave nut club FD (9 outs + Ac blocker) and ~35% equity OOP; checking was correct to realize equity, induce bluffs from missed Kx, control pot. Turn 7♠ pairs sevens (board now K♦7♥3♣7♠), dry/paired, improving sets/trips (77/K7) but villains unlikely to have them in calling range. My NFD remains live, equity vs polarized range ~40% (flush crushes, beats bluffs/A-high, loses to sets/2pr+). OOP check-fold risks losing to free rivers; check-call vulnerable to free cards; check-raise too thin without strong read. Leading ~2/3 pot semi-bluffs: exploits Flash's weak check-back range (KQ/KT/AQ no K, floats), denies equity to his equity hands/spade draws, builds pot for flush, has fold equity vs medium strength while stacking worse (Ax) or drawing dead monsters unlikely. Effective stacks ~60BB support aggression; sizing 1500 leaves him pot odds to call lighter while committing ~25% stack, allowing postflop flexibility.

On the turn, the board has paired (7s) following a King-high flop (Kd 7h 3c). My opponent (Elon), who was the pre-flop 3-bettor, checked the flop and has now led for 1,500 into a 2,300 pot. My hand, Ace-high (Ad 5d), has no showdown value against his likely range of Kings, overpairs, or even mid-pairs (88-QQ). While Elon could be bluffing after I checked back the flop, my hand has very little equity to improve to a winner even if an Ace hits, as Elon's 3-betting range often contains AK or AA. Calling 1,500 with only Ace-high and no draw is not a profitable play. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

UTG with 4♠7♠ is a very weak, low suited hand and is dominated from early position. There are five players to act behind me and the table has been fairly aggressive (frequent raises from Elon, Pro, FiveTwo), so this spot will often face a raise or difficult multiway postflop decisions. I don’t want to limp and play out of position with a marginal hand, and there’s no justification to open-raise this thin. Fold and wait for a better spot with position or stronger holdings.

I am in middle position with Ace-Nine offsuit. After the UTG player (Minni) folded, I have a standard opening hand for this position in a 6-max tournament. I'll raise to 300, which is 3x the big blind and consistent with my previous opening sizes, to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or isolate a single opponent.
I have 4h8s offsuit in the cutoff - a very weak hand. Flash has opened to 300 from middle position. This hand has poor playability and equity against a typical opening range. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with Jh 3h. The player in Middle Position (Flash) has raised to 300 (3bb). While I have the advantage of position, J3s is a very weak hand that is often dominated by the opener's range (e.g., AJ, KJ, QJ). It does not have enough equity or playability to justify a cold call, nor is it a good candidate for a 3-bet bluff given the gap between cards and lack of high blockers. Folding is the standard play.

Preflop in the small blind facing a standard 3x raise from Flash in middle position. QJo offsuit is a marginal hand with mediocre playability out of position, especially with the big blind still to act who is likely to defend wide. Flash has shown aggression in prior hands but also folded to pressure. Pot odds are not compelling for a call (pot 450 to call 250), and calling risks multiway pots OOP. 3-betting is possible for fold equity given my stack covers Flash's 5650, but QJo is not a strong enough 3-bet candidate here compared to better broadways or suited hands. Folding preserves stack for stronger spots later in the tournament, aligning with long-term ICM and stack maximization over 150 hands.

Flash is opening from MP to 3x (300) and we’re out of position with a very marginal offsuit connector (65o). While the pot odds are decent (200 to win 450), this hand realizes equity poorly OOP versus a relatively tight MP range and can get dominated/forced into tough spots on many flops. A 3-bet bluff here is also unattractive (poor blockers, needs too much fold equity). Best is to just release and preserve the 90bb stack for better defend/3-bet candidates.

I am in the UTG position with Queen-Deuce of hearts. While suited, this hand is extremely weak for an early position opening. In a 6-max game, UTG requires a much stronger range because there are five players left to act, increasing the probability that someone holds a superior hand or will 3-bet. Given the stack sizes and the aggressive tendencies of players like Minni, folding is the most disciplined and standard play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
T8 offsuit in middle position is too weak to open. With 4 players behind including big stacks, this hand plays poorly and will often be dominated or in bad shape when called. Easy fold.

I am in the Cutoff with 7s 6c (76o). The blinds are 50/100. Action has folded to me. 76o is a very weak hand, even for a late-position steal attempt. It is dominated by many calling ranges and plays poorly post-flop. With a healthy stack of 113 BBs, there is no desperate need to force action with such marginal holdings. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand from this position.

Preflop on the button with A5o after three folds. Excellent position and stealing spot. A5o blocks strong aces, has decent playability for postflop, and blinds may fold given their tendencies. Standard button raise sizing of 3x BB to build pot or take it down immediately. Minni defends wide but with position, we can realize equity well. Raising to 300.

Elon’s button open to 3bb is a wide range, but 8♠2♦ offsuit is near the bottom of hands and plays very poorly out of position from the small blind (domination issues, weak realizability, and minimal postflop equity). Completing the extra 250 is not justified, and 3-bet bluffing this combo is also too loose given poor blockers and terrible playability when called. Best decision is to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Facing a button open to 300 while I’m in the big blind with 6♥3♦ is a clear fold. Pot is 450 and it’s 200 to call (calling would commit 200 for a total of 300 in my stack this street). 6-3 offsuit has very poor equity and is largely dominated by a button’s opening range; calling here gives me ~30.8% pot odds but my true equity vs a typical BTN range is well below that (roughly 20–25%), and I’ll be out of position facing frequent c-bets. A light 3-bet is unnecessary and poorly sized here — I can preserve 18700 chips (187 BBs) and wait for a much better spot. I fold and avoid marginal postflop situations.

K6 suited from UTG is too weak to open. While suited kings have some playability, UTG requires a tighter range. With 60BB and 5 players to act behind me, folding is the disciplined play here. Will wait for better spots.

I am in Middle Position with J7o (Jack of Clubs, Seven of Diamonds). This is a statistically weak hand that is unconnected and unsuited. With four players left to act behind me, including the button and blinds, entering the pot with this hand has a negative expected value. My stack is healthy at over 110 big blinds, so there is no need to make loose plays. The standard and correct strategic decision is to fold.

Folded to me in the cutoff with T8o, a speculative hand with straight potential and good playability. Position is excellent against the blinds. FiveTwo on button is aggressive but I have fold equity and postflop position. Minni in SB is deep and defends wide, Flash in BB shorter and more passive based on history. Standard open-raise size around 3BB to steal blinds or build pot OOP. Raising to 300 (3x) balances aggression and pot control.

Elon is opening from the cutoff and has shown willingness to raise fairly wide. On the button, QTo has enough playability and immediate pot odds to continue in position, but it’s not a great 3-bet candidate because we get dominated by a lot of his continuing range (AQ/KQ/QJ/AT+), and building a bigger pot OOP callers behind can create awkward spots. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes our positional edge, and lets us pressure many flops when he misses.

Holding 7♣3♦ — a very weak, offsuit hand with virtually no playability. Elon raised from late position and the Button called, so I’m likely facing stronger ranges and I’ll be out of position postflop to at least one player (and possibly two). Calling 250 into a 750 pot gives ~3:1 pot odds, but my hand has poor equity and limited implied odds; a raise or 3-bet here would be a marginal, high-variance play out of position. With a healthy 18,650 stack and many better spots to pick on, I’ll preserve chips and fold.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces, the best possible starting hand. Elon opened from the cutoff and FiveTwo called on the button. This is a clear 3-betting situation to build the pot and maximize value. I will raise to 1200, which is roughly 4x the initial raise, to put pressure on my opponents and potentially play a large pot with a significant advantage.

Facing a 3-bet to 1200 (900 to call into 1850 pot) from Flash in the BB after my CO open-raise to 300 and a flat-call from aggressive FiveTwo on the BTN. T8o is a marginal speculative hand with straight potential and playability, but offsuit and not premium. Flash appears more passive historically but is 3-betting after a caller, suggesting strength (top of range like QQ+, AK). FiveTwo's flat from BTN is wide, and they act after me, increasing risk of squeeze or multiway pot. Even with position, SPR postflop would be low (~4:1 after call), turning my hand into a bluff-catcher without strong equity vs 3-bet range. Calling commits ~10% stack into likely dominated spot or cooler. 4-bet risks isolation vs shorter Flash (49BB) with poor postflop playability. Best to fold, preserve stack for better spots in CO position mid-tournament (hand 74/150). Live to fight another hand.

We’ve opened/called vs Elon and now face a fairly large BB 3-bet from Flash (to 1200) with only ~49bb effective. Calling 900 more creates a low SPR spot (~1.3) where QTo is frequently dominated by Flash’s value-heavy continuing range (AQ/KQ/QJ/AT, TT+), and we won’t have much room to maneuver postflop. A 4-bet bluff/jam would need very high fold equity to be profitable, which is unlikely given his sizing and stack depth. Best play is to cut losses and preserve stack for better spots.

I am holding J2o in UTG position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em and is certainly not playable from early position given the full table left to act. With 113.5 BBs, there is absolutely no incentive to get involved with trash hands. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Preflop in middle position with 7c5s offsuit, a weak speculative hand with poor playability, low equity against typical ranges, and no suited or connected value. Standard tight strategy dictates folding trash hands from early/middle position to preserve stack and avoid difficult postflop spots, especially with 86BB deep stacks in a tournament setting. Pot is small at 150, but risking chips on this hand has negative EV long-term.

Folded to us in the cutoff, so this is a steal spot, but J2o is too far down the range: it’s heavily dominated by the blinds’ Jx/Qx/Kx defending ranges and plays poorly postflop (reverse implied odds) when called or 3-bet. With stacks ~60–90bb and competent opponents behind (Minni on BTN, Flash/Claude in blinds), we don’t need to force an open with this low-equity offsuit hand—better to preserve stack for higher EV steals and value opens.

On the button with T4o — a weak hand, but excellent position and only the blinds left to act. Pot is 150 and a small open to 3x the BB gives good fold equity versus mid stacks (Flash/Claude) while risking almost nothing relative to my 18.65k stack. I want to either steal the blinds or play a single-raised pot in position, so I’ll open to a standard 3x (300 total). Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (e.g. a shove or a sizable 3-bet well above ~700), call/counterplay if they cold-call and proceed with cautious postflop play — c-bet on dry boards, check/float or give up on highly coordinated boards unless I improve (10 or 4).

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Four of diamonds. Minni, the chip leader, has raised from the Button. While my hand is suited, the kicker is very weak, and I would be playing out of position against an aggressive opponent for the remainder of the hand. Given my current stack size and the poor positional disadvantage, folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips for better spots.
T9s is a solid suited connector getting 2.25:1 to call a button min-raise. Good implied odds for straights and flushes. Standard defend from BB.

Flop is all diamonds (5d 8d 3d) and I have Ts9s with zero diamonds. This is a terrible flop for my hand - I completely whiffed with no flush draw, no pair, and only a weak backdoor straight possibility. I should check and likely fold to any bet from Minni. No reason to put chips in here without any equity.

Heads-up on a monotone flop 5♦ 8♦ 3♦ and I hold T♦4♣. I have a strong semi-bluff: four to the diamond flush (9 outs) plus a ten that’s the highest card on the board. Pot is 650 and Claude checked after calling preflop — as the preflop aggressor a continuation bet is standard to take the pot now or build when I hit. Betting ~60% pot (400) gives fold equity versus weaker pairs and non-diamond hands while still charging his draws; if he calls I retain ~35% equity to make the flush by river and can re-evaluate the turn (raise on a diamond for value, pot-control or check back on blanks). If he check-raises big I’ll assume a made flush or very strong hand and fold unless the sizing is small enough to continue.

I have Ts9s on a monotone diamond flop (5d 8d 3d). I have zero diamonds, no pair, and only a gutshot to a 6 or 7. Even if I hit a straight, any diamond from Minni beats me. My equity is terrible here - I'm basically drawing dead or to very few clean outs. Getting 2.6:1 isn't nearly enough. Clear fold.

UTG position with Q2o is a standard fold in a 6-handed game. This hand has poor playability, low equity against the wide calling and 3-betting ranges of players behind, especially aggressive opponents like Minni and FiveTwo who frequently raise. Folding preserves stack for better spots, aligning with tight-aggressive strategy to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

We’re first in after UTG folds with a strong suited Broadway (AJs). This is well ahead of the blinds’ random holdings and plays profitably in position versus the cutoff/button if called. Limping invites multiway pots and reduces our ability to represent strong ranges on later streets, so we should open for value and initiative. Use a standard sizing that pressures the blinds without overcommitting versus potential 3-bets from deeper stacks.

Call 250. In the cutoff with K♠T♣ facing a 250 (≈2.5x) open from middle, this is a standard defend — KTo has decent equity and good post‑flop playability against a relatively wide MP opening range. Effective stacks (vs FiveTwo) are deep (~80–85 BB), so 3‑betting KTo is too thin: we’ll often be dominated by better Ks/broadways and risk a 4‑bet or a bloated pot out of position. Calling keeps the pot controllable, preserves stack depth for post‑flop play, and lets us exploit favorable flops (top pair, broadway cards, or draws). If heavy isolation or 3‑bet action comes, we can fold or re‑evaluate on the flop. Total to call: 250.

I am holding 7s 4c on the button. There has been an open-raise to 250 from FiveTwo in middle position and a call from Minni in the cutoff. 74 offsuit is an extremely weak hand with very poor playability, especially in a multi-way pot. Calling would be a waste of chips, and raising as a bluff into two opponents who have already shown interest in the pot is unnecessarily risky. The most prudent action is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I have T9o in the small blind. FiveTwo raised to 250, Minni called. I need to call 200 more to see a flop. Getting roughly 3.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 650). T9o has decent playability with straight potential. With two players already in and good pot odds, this is a reasonable spot to call and see a flop multiway.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 6h facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo, with calls from Minni and Claude (Small Blind). The pot is currently 850 chips, and I need to call 150 more. This gives me pot odds of approximately 5.6 to 1, requiring only about 15% equity to break even. My hand is a suited one-gapper, which plays well in multi-way pots due to its potential to make straights and flushes. Although I will be out of position, the price is too good to fold, and closing the action guarantees I see a flop. A squeeze is too risky against three opponents. I will call to realize my equity.

Flopped middle pair with 9c on K93 rainbow board. With 3 opponents still in, someone likely has a king. My hand has showdown value but is vulnerable. Betting here would likely only get called by better hands. Check to control pot size and see how others act - if it checks through, great. If there's significant action, I can fold cheaply.

I hold Middle Pair (Nines) with a weak kicker and a backdoor flush draw on a K-9-3 rainbow board. In a 4-way pot, my hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable to stronger Kings or better Nines. Leading out into three opponents, including the pre-flop raiser, is ill-advised as I will likely only get action from better hands. My goal is to pot control and see a cheap showdown or turn card. Therefore, checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard and most prudent play.

We opened preflop and still retain a range advantage on K-high boards (we have plenty of AK/KQ/sets), but this is a 4-way pot so we should use a small, disciplined c-bet sizing rather than blasting. AhJh has an overcard to the K (A), two overcards to most pocket pairs below K, and a backdoor nut-flush component with the Kh on board, so it’s a reasonable low-risk stab that can fold out a lot of whiffed hands (small pairs/ace-highs) and deny equity. If we get called, we can often shut down unless we improve (A/J) or pick up strong barreling cards (hearts, Q/T). If we face a raise in this multiway spot, we can comfortably fold.

I have top pair (K) with a medium kicker (T) on a fairly dry K‑9‑3 rainbow. FiveTwo’s small 350 c‑bet into a 1350 pot looks like a standard continuation from a wide MP opening range; Claude and Pro checked the flop, so raising here would bloat the pot out of position and often face callers or a 3‑bet with better Ks. Calling is the best line: I’m getting ~20% pot odds to see the turn, keep worse Kx/9x and bluffs in, and retain flexibility with deep effective stacks (~80BB). Plan: call 350, then re‑evaluate on the turn — extract value on harmless cards, pot‑control or fold to heavy aggression on scary runouts.

I have middle pair (9s) on a K93 rainbow board. FiveTwo bet 350 and Minni called. With two opponents showing interest and Pro still behind me, my middle pair is in trouble. FiveTwo was the preflop raiser and c-bet - likely has a King or overpair. Minni's call is also concerning. I'm getting ~5.8:1 pot odds (350 to win 2050), but with a vulnerable hand out of position against two players who've shown strength, folding is the disciplined play. Even if I improve to two pair, a King is very likely out there.

I hold middle pair (Nines) with a weak kicker and a backdoor flush draw on a K-9-3 rainbow board. The initial raiser (FiveTwo) bet 350 into a 4-way pot, which signals strength or a continuation bet with a strong range. Crucially, Minni called behind. This overcall significantly increases the likelihood that at least one opponent holds a King or a better Nine. I am out of position against both active players. Even though the pot odds are attractive (requiring ~17% equity), my hand is likely dominated and has very little playability on future streets multi-way. Preserving my stack is better than chasing with a marginal hand in a 'sandwiched' position.

Turn brings the Ad, which is a very good barreling card for my range (I have lots of AK/AQ/AA) and it upgrades my actual hand to top pair (A) with a solid kicker. After Minni calls a multiway flop c-bet, their range contains many Kx/9x/pocket pairs and some floats; a medium sizing should still get calls from Kx (now 2nd pair) and stubborn 9x while folding out a lot of underpairs and air that peeled once. I don’t want to check and give free cards to hands like QJ/JT/TT-QQ that can realize equity or turn my river decisions into guesswork. If I face a sizeable raise, I can comfortably fold because that line is heavily weighted to AK/two pair/sets.

Bet ~45–50% pot to extract and deny equity while keeping the pot manageable with a one-pair hand.

I have top pair (K♠) with a medium kicker on K‑9‑3‑A rainbow. FiveTwo was the preflop opener, c‑bet the flop and now makes a small/medium turn stab (800 into ~2500). That turn sizing suggests a mixed continuing range: A‑x (A9/A3/AQ/AJ/AK), some Kx, occasional sets (99/33) and bluffs. Calling 800 to win ~3300 requires ~24% equity — KTo has reasonable showdown equity vs that range and plays well in position. I want to keep worse Kx/9x and bluffs in the pot and use position to control the size on the river. Raising here is thin (I have little fold equity vs Ax and would risk bloating the pot with a marginal hand); folding is too passive given my equity and the pot odds. Plan: call the turn, evaluate the river — call small value bets and fold to large polarized overbets or jam if I improve or get a clear read.

River 2h is mostly a brick, but it does complete the wheel for 45 and improves hands like A2/A3 to two pair. After Minni calls both the flop c-bet in a multiway pot and the turn barrel on the Ad, their range is heavily weighted toward made hands (Ax, Kx that peeled, 9x, slowplays) with very little pure air left. With AJ we have a decent showdown hand but it’s not strong enough to value-bet comfortably out of position: worse hands (many Kx/9x) will often just check back and better hands (AK/AQ/two pair/sets/45) will call and can sometimes raise, putting us in a tough spot. Best line is to check and realize our equity, then decide whether to bluff-catch based on sizing if Minni bets.

I have top pair (K) with a medium kicker on K‑9‑3‑A‑2. FiveTwo was the preflop raiser, c‑bet the flop and then raised the turn after the A — that turn raise heavily favors A‑x (AK/AQ/AJ/A9/A3), some strong Kx and the occasional set. He then checked the river, which looks like pot‑control with Ax or a marginal/medium K. Betting here risks getting called by many A‑combos that actually beat me while folding out worse Kx rarely — given that, a thin value bet is likely low or negative EV. Pot is small relative to our stacks and there were effectively no river draws to bluff; the prudent line is to check back and go to showdown. If he now barrels a small amount I’ll call for showdown equity; I’ll fold to a large polarized shove.

6-handed UTG with T7o is too weak to open: it’s frequently dominated by better Tx, has poor nut potential, and plays badly out of position in multiway pots. Limping is also unattractive because we’ll realize equity poorly and face isolation. With ~105bb stacks, preserving chips and waiting for stronger open candidates is higher EV.

I have K♥9♦ in middle position with a deep 176 BB stack. UTG (FiveTwo) folded, so there are four players left to act behind me (CO Flash, BTN Claude, SB Pro, BB Elon) who have shown frequent aggression/3-betting and many standard 3x opens. Limping (calling 100) would invite isolation raises and squeezes from aggressive spots and commit me OOP with a marginal, easily dominated hand; open-raising is also unattractive because K9o doesn't fare well vs 3-bets and won't realize great equity multiway. With no clear advantage and plenty of chips to wait for a better spot, the +EV play is to fold and avoid playing a marginal hand out of position.

I am in the Cutoff with 7c 8h, a marginal hand. The action has folded to me. While 78o can sometimes be used as a late-position steal, it's generally too weak to open from the Cutoff in a 6-max game, especially with active players behind me on the Button and in the Blinds. I will fold and wait for a more favorable spot.

I'm on the button with A5o and it's folded to me. This is a prime stealing spot - only the blinds left to act. A5o is a standard open from the button with its ace-high potential and suited-type connectivity. I'll make a standard raise to 250 to pick up the blinds or play a raised pot in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 3c Tc (Ten-Three suited). The Button (Claude) has raised to 250. While Button raises are typically wide, T3s is a very weak hand to defend with, especially from out of position. I would be playing the rest of the hand with a marginal holding and without position. The Big Blind is also still left to act and could squeeze. The pot odds do not justify a call given the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage. The standard play here is to fold.

Facing a button raise to 250 from Claude after folds all around. Pot 400, to call 150 into 550 (pot odds ~3.67:1, need ~21% equity). QJo offsuit is a strong defending hand vs wide BTN range (40-50% hands), with good equity (~40%+) and playability OOP. Deep stacks allow postflop maneuvering. Folding too tight; 3-betting marginal as offsuit without strong blockers. Best to call and realize equity.

Flop completely misses my QJo: no pair, no straight draw (no gutshot even), no flush draw. Pure queen-high air OOP heads-up against button range that includes frequent Ax (beats me), broadway that missed similarly, and some draws. Preflop call was correct for pot odds and playability vs wide BTN open, but postflop dynamics favor check-fold. Betting air OOP risks getting called by better or raised; checking induces bluffs while folding to most cbets preserves stack. Opponent tendencies from history show aggression but not exploitable for hero calls. Deep stacks (83BB), no need to bluff now. Standard check-fold maximizes EV long-term.

I flopped two pair (Aces and Fives) on a 4d Ac 5s board - this is an excellent result. Elon checked to me and I need to bet for value. I want to extract value from worse aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT, etc.), pocket pairs, and draws. The board is relatively dry with minimal flush draws. I'll bet around 65% pot to build value while keeping worse hands in.

Preflop call with QJo was standard defense vs button open in BB for pot odds and playability. Flop 4d Ac 5s completely misses: no pair, no straight draw (QJ gutshot needs K for broadway, unlikely), no flush draw. Pure bluff-catcher at best, but queen-high OOP vs aggressive button c-bet range (frequent Ax, sets, draws like 23o/36o, bluffs) has poor equity (~20-25% vs polarized range) and worse playability on turn. Pot odds 900:350 (~2.6:1, needs 28%) not met; raising air OOP suicidal vs deep stack Claude who 3bets light pre (history: aggressive). Check-fold preserves stack for better spots in deep tournament (83BB), realizes EV vs history of Claude value-betting streets.

I’m UTG with K♠4♠. From first-to-act preflop I should be tight — K4s is a weak, easily-dominated K with poor postflop playability out of position. The table has shown frequent 3x opens and active players behind me, so limp-calling invites raises and multiway pots where this hand performs poorly. I’m deep-stacked (≈176 bb) and can wait for a better spot. Rather than limp or open-raise a marginal holding, I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a stronger opportunity.

I am holding 3c Jd in middle position. This is a very weak hand with no real potential for straights or flushes and poor high-card value. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve my stack, there is no reason to play this hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Q7o in the cutoff is a marginal hand. While position is decent with only 3 players left to act, Q7o plays poorly post-flop and doesn't have great equity when called. With 57 BBs, I don't need to take unnecessary risks with weak holdings. Better to wait for a stronger spot.

I hold Pocket Eights [8d 8h] on the Button. The action has folded to me. This is a strong starting hand, well above the average strength for a button opening range. My goal is to extract value from worse hands (like lower pairs or broadway cards that might call) and to deny equity to random hands in the blinds. A standard open-raise size in this tournament has been around 2.5x to 3x the big blind. I will raise to 250 (2.5 BB) to put pressure on the blinds while keeping the pot manageable if I get called. I have position on any caller, which is a significant advantage.

8c5h is a complete trash hand: low, offsuit, disconnected, no straight or flush potential worth playing. Pro's button raise suggests a wide range, but my hand has negligible equity against it (dominated often, poor post-flop playability OOP). Pot odds ~2:1 don't justify calling 200 OOP with this. Folding preserves stack for 72 more hands in tournament; discipline key to maximizing stack long-term.

Button open from Pro is likely fairly wide in 6-max, and AQs is well ahead of that range while also dominating many continues (AJ/AT/KQ/QJ). Being out of position, I prefer a value-oriented 3-bet to take initiative, deny equity to suited/gapped hands, and build a pot versus worse aces/queens that will call. If facing a large 4-bet, I can re-evaluate (often folding to tight sizing/ranges), but the preflop EV is best by pressuring the button’s open rather than flatting OOP.

I hold Pocket Eights (8d 8h) on the Button. I opened to 250, and the Big Blind (FiveTwo) has 3-bet to 900. FiveTwo has shown a tendency to 3-bet aggressively (seen in hands 19, 25, 32, 58) with this specific sizing. Folding 88 here would be too tight given my position and the likelihood that the opponent's range is wider than just premium pairs. 4-betting would likely isolate me against superior hands (99+, JJ+, AK). Calling allows me to use my position post-flop, keeps the opponent's bluffing range in the hand, and gives me a chance to hit a set or navigate a favorable board texture. The price to call is 650 into a pot of 1200, offering reasonable odds.

Flop 6-7-9 rainbow is a much better texture for the in-position caller’s 3-bet calling range (suited connectors, 88–TT, 97s/87s/76s) than for my 3-bet range. With AsQh I have two overcards and some backdoor potential, but I’m not strong enough to comfortably bet/call a raise, and a small c-bet will get called a lot by pairs and draws while folding out many hands I already beat/might bluff later. Checking keeps the pot controlled OOP, lets me realize my equity, and can induce stabs that I can call when sized reasonably, while preserving my ability to bluff on A/Q/spade turns.

I hold Pocket Eights (8d 8h) on a board of 6s 7h 9c. The pot is 1850 after I called a pre-flop 3-bet from FiveTwo. The board texture is very connected and hits both ranges, though it likely connects slightly better with a button calling range (sets, straights, two pairs) than a polarized BB 3-bet range (high pairs, AK/AQ). My hand is actually quite strong here: I have a middle pair and an open-ended straight draw (outs: 5, 10). FiveTwo checked to me. If I bet, I likely only get called by better hands (99+, overpairs) and fold out hands I am beating (AK, AQ). If I get check-raised, it puts me in a miserable spot where I might have to fold significant equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, keeps the pot manageable, and allows me to see the turn where I might improve to a straight or set. I am currently ahead of unpaired overcards and have good equity against overpairs.

Turn pairs the 9, which is a relatively good card for the preflop 3-bettor: it reduces the impact of straight/2-pair textures from the flop and lets me credibly represent overpairs (TT+) and strong 9x that would often check flop OOP. With AsQh I have little showdown value after check-check on this connected flop, so I want to take a stab now with a small sizing that pressures Pro’s unpaired broadways/ace-high floats and some weaker pairs, while keeping my risk controlled. If I face a raise, I’m folding; if called, I’ll mostly shut down unless I improve (A/Q) or get a very favorable river to continue applying pressure.

On the turn, the board is [6s 7h 9c 9d]. The pot is 1850. My opponent (FiveTwo) bets 650 (approx. 1/3 pot). I hold [8d 8h], giving me a pocket pair and an open-ended straight draw (outs: 5, 10). The paired 9 reduces the likelihood of the opponent holding a 9, though it is possible. The opponent checked the flop and is now leading small on the turn, which could be a blocker bet with a draw, a protected hand like an overpair, or Ace-high trying to steal. Given the price (650 to win 2500, roughly 4:1 odds needing ~20% equity), and the fact that I have 8 clean outs to a straight plus potential showdown value if my 8s are good against high cards, folding is too tight. Raising would likely only get action from better hands. Calling allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the river in position.

River Td is a very bad card to continue bluffing: Pro’s most natural flop/turn continues in a 3-bet pot on 6-7-9-9 include a lot of 8x (T8s/98s/87s/A8s/88) that just made a straight, plus all 9x that already had trips, and some pocket pairs that may still bluff-catch given my check-flop / small turn stab line. I don’t block the key continues (8x), and getting the needed fold frequency on this runout is unlikely. Best is to check and concede, planning to mostly check-fold versus meaningful sizing.

I hold Pocket Eights (8d 8h) and have made a Straight on a board of [6s 7h 9c 9d Td]. My straight is 6-7-8-9-T. The board is paired (9s), making Full Houses possible (TT, 99, 77, 66, T9), but the 8 is the key card for any straight on this board, meaning anyone without an 8 has no straight. My opponent 3-bet pre-flop, checked the flop, bet small on the turn, and checked the river. This line is consistent with overpairs (AA, KK, QQ, JJ), trips (A9), or possibly a trapped Full House (TT). However, the number of combos of overpairs and trips significantly exceeds the combos of Full Houses. I am likely ahead and should bet for value, targeting hands like AA, KK, QQ, and A9 that might pay off a reasonable bet. Checking back is too passive given the strength of my hand. I will bet 1250 (approx. 40% pot) to get a call from these worse hands while keeping the sizing small enough that I can fold to a shove (representing a Full House).

We’re holding only ace-high after taking a small stab on the paired 9 turn and getting called. The river Td is a very good card for Pro’s turn-calling range: it completes T8 to a straight, strengthens Tx that floated/called once, and all 9x continues still crush us. There are essentially no missed flush draws, and many of the natural straight-draw candidates (like 58/T8 on the flop) are already strong holdings, so the bluff portion after a turn call is likely too small. Even though the price is decent (1250 to win ~5650), I don’t expect Pro to be bluffing >~18% here with this line/runout. Best is to concede and preserve stack.

I am in the UTG position with Jack-Deuce of diamonds. While the hand is suited, J2s is an extremely weak hand to open from early position in a 6-max game. Opening here would likely lead to being out of position against stronger ranges from the remaining players. Conserving my stack for better opportunities is the optimal strategy.
Ah5h is a suited ace with flush potential and wheel straight possibilities. In middle position after UTG folds, this is a standard opening hand. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x) as a standard open.

I hold Pocket Tens in the Cutoff facing a standard 2.5x open from Claude in Middle Position. Claude has a stack of about 55 BB. In Hand #67, I 3-bet Claude with Kings and he folded, so he respects my aggression, but he also knows I am capable of it. With TT, I have a strong hand but one that is vulnerable to overcards and 4-bets. 3-betting risks facing a 4-bet shove from Claude (who has a re-shove stack size) or a cold 4-bet from the aggressive players behind (like Minni). Calling allows me to play a pot in position, keeping Claude's range wide and avoiding a high-variance pre-flop confrontation where I might be flipping or dominated. I can re-evaluate on the post-flop texture.

Pocket Aces is the best starting hand in NLHE, especially on the button with position on Claude's middle position raise and Pro's cutoff call. Claude's opening range from MP includes many hands AA crushes like AJs+, KQo+, pairs below AA. Pro's calling range is even wider, often speculative or medium strength. 3-betting builds a pot for value, denies equity to draws/multiway, and can isolate if called. Sizing to 900 (~3.6x the raise) is standard from position, pressuring the blinds who are OOP while keeping my stack depth flexible (83BB effective). Minni and FiveTwo may fold or defend wide but AA plays well multiway preflop. Folding or calling is -EV; flatcalling invites blinds and keeps pot small.

We’re in the small blind facing an open, a cold call, and a button 3-bet. With 3h4c (offsuit), we have terrible playability out of position, no relevant blockers for a 4-bet bluff, and we’d be going multiway often if we continue. Calling 850 to chase a low-equity hand in a bloated pot is -EV; best is to preserve stack and fold.

I fold. I’m on the big blind with J♣2♣ facing a button 3-bet to 900 after Claude opened and Pro called — a likely isolating 3-bet and potentially multiway. J2s is poorly positioned here: it’s frequently dominated by other Jx and broadway hands and has weak post‑flop playability out of position. The pot is 1,550; calling 800 requires ~34% equity just to break even, and against a 3‑bet range (with the original raiser still in) my realized equity and implied odds are slim. 4‑bet or shove with J2s would be spewy. Preserve the 17.5k stack for a better spot.

Facing Elon's 3-bet to 900 from the button. Pot is 1550, I need 650 to call (getting ~2.4:1). A5 suited has good playability - nut flush potential, wheel straight draws. Elon could be squeezing light from the button. Pro is still behind me but likely folds. With suited ace and decent pot odds, this is a call. Not strong enough to 4-bet but too good to fold given implied odds.

Facing a 3-bet squeeze to 900 from Elon on the Button and a call from the original raiser Claude, I am getting excellent pot odds to call. I need to put in 650 more to win a pot of 2200, giving me roughly 3.4:1 odds (requiring ~23% equity). Pocket Tens is well ahead of this equity requirement against reasonable ranges. While I am sandwiched between two players, the SPR will be low (around 1.7 to 2.6), which simplifies post-flop decisions. 4-betting would likely isolate me against superior hands (JJ+, AK) while folding out the bluffs I beat. Folding is too tight given the price and hand strength. Calling allows me to set-mine or play as a strong made hand on low textures.

I completely missed this flop with Ah5h on 8c-Tc-4s. No pair, no flush draw (board has clubs, I have hearts), just ace high with a gutshot to a wheel if a 2-3 comes (need both cards). I'm first to act in a 3-way pot against Elon who 3-bet preflop and Pro who called. This is a clear check - I have almost no equity and no reason to bet into two opponents with strong ranges.

I have flopped top set on a board of 8c Tc 4s with a flush draw present. The pot is significant (2850) due to the pre-flop 3-bet squeeze. The original 3-bettor, Elon, is on the button and has not acted yet on this street.
