It is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor to induce a continuation bet, which I can then check-raise or call to trap. Leading out (donk betting) might fold out Elon's bluffs or weaker hands like AK without a flush draw. Given Claude's short stack (SPR < 2), if Elon bets, action could explode, allowing me to get all the money in easily. My hand is extremely strong, so slow-playing the flop by checking is the optimal line to maximize extraction from overpairs or flush draws in Elon's range.

Flop is 8c-Tc-4s, featuring a club flush draw and possible straight draws like 6-5 or 7-5. AA is a massive overpair, ahead of villain ranges that defended a button 3-bet (Claude called from MP, Pro from CO). Both checked, which is weak but could be trapping or draws. C-betting extracts value from top pair/top kicker, sets, worse pairs (TT,88), and overcards, while denying equity to draws and protecting my hand. Sizing 2/3 pot (1900) balances value/protection without overbetting multiway; it's ~40% of Claude's remaining stack (4825), applying pressure. Checking invites free cards or bluffs but forgoes value from calling ranges. Raising not applicable as no bet to raise.

I completely whiffed this flop with Ah5h on 8c-Tc-4s. No pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), just ace high. Elon 3-bet preflop and is now c-betting 1900 into ~2850 - a strong sizing. I have virtually no equity here against his range (overpairs, Tx hands, club draws). Pro is also still to act behind me. This is a clear fold - I can't continue with just ace high and no draws.

I have flopped Top Set (TT) on a moderately wet board (8c Tc 4s) with a flush draw. After checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Elon), he has fired a large continuation bet of 1900 into a pot of 2850 (approx. 66% pot). This large sizing indicates he is either committing with an overpair (JJ-AA) or a strong draw (flush draw, likely with overcards). With the SPR being very low (less than 1), there is little room for maneuvering. While calling is a valid option to trap, the presence of the flush draw and straight connectivity (9, J, 7 runouts) makes the board dynamic. I do not hold a club, which increases the likelihood Elon has a flush draw. I want to deny equity to draws and ensure I extract maximum value from overpairs before a scare card (Club, Ace, or King) potentially kills the action or downgrades my hand strength. Given his commitment level, getting the stacks in now is the highest EV play.

Facing an all-in check-raise from Pro on a 8c-Tc-4s flop after calling my button 3-bet preflop from the cutoff. My AA is an overpair, top of range, and blocks sets or AA. Pro's range for check-calling flop then jamming: polarized to nutted hands like sets (88/TT/44 ~4% of range), combo draws (e.g., J9c, 76c, QJc ~flush+straight draws), flush draws, or bluffs (overcards, missed Ax, air). Board is draw-heavy (9 club outs, gutshots), but AA has ~82% equity vs pure flush draw, ~65% vs combo straight-flush draw, ~12% vs sets. Overall range equity ~50-60%+. Pot odds: calling 5525 into 16500 (current pot) for heads-up all-in pot of 22025, requiring ~25% equity - crushing value. Folding AA here is massively -EV; Pro's history includes light calls (e.g., Hand 78 river bluff-catch) but aggression here likely thin value or semi-bluff. Position irrelevant post-all-in. Stack commitment: all-in either way, call to realize equity.

QJo in UTG with 48BB stack. Decent holding for a 5-handed game. Standard open raise to 250 is appropriate - hand is playable but not strong enough to get too aggressive with. Looking to pick up blinds or play post-flop if called.

I am in UTG+1 with Q9o, facing a raise to 250 from Claude in UTG. Q9o is a weak off-suit hand that is frequently dominated by an UTG opening range (which typically includes AQ, KQ, QJs, 99+). Calling here would be a loose play with negative expected value due to reverse implied odds and position. As the chip leader, there is no need to bleed chips in marginal spots. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Claude’s 2.5x open is coming from a relatively short/medium stack and he’s shown a willingness to open/pressure fairly wide across prior hands. On the button I’m getting a good price (250 to win 400 now, with strong positional advantage) and 87 can realize equity well when it flops straights/two pair. I’m not interested in turning 78o into a 3-bet bluff versus a stack that can 4-bet jam ~45bb, and I’m comfortable calling and playing a small pot in position (folding if the blinds put in a large squeeze).

I have A♠ K♣ in the small blind facing an UTG raise to 250 in a 5-handed game. This is one of my best non-pair hands and, short-handed, Claude's opening range is relatively wide — I want to take the initiative, build value, and isolate. A 3-bet to 1000 total applies pressure to the button and big blind, gives good fold equity, and still leaves me plenty of stack to play postflop (and to maneuver versus 4-bets). If called I'll continue aggressively on favorable runouts; I'll re-evaluate if I face a very large 4-bet/shove.

I am in the Big Blind with T4 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude opened from UTG, and Minni has 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1000. The action is heavy, and my hand has no potential to compete against the ranges of an early position raiser and a 3-better, especially when I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

QJo facing a 3-bet to 1000 after my UTG open and FiveTwo's call. Minni has been aggressive throughout (lots of opens/3-bets), but this is still a significant 3-bet. QJo plays poorly OOP against a 3-betting range. With ~46BB effective, calling 750 more commits me to a difficult spot post-flop. Pot odds of ~2.1:1 aren't great for a hand that often makes second-best pairs. Better to fold and preserve chips for stronger spots.

Facing Minni’s squeeze to 1000 after my cold call, this is a much tighter/stronger range than a single raise (SB squeeze vs UTG open + call). With 87o I’m too often dominated, and the low-ish SPR if I call (~2350 pot with ~7950 behind) makes it hard to realize equity—I'll face frequent c-bets and be forced to fold my misses, while my hits are often marginal. No good 4-bet options with this hand, so the highest-EV play is to preserve my stack and fold.

I am in UTG position (first to act) in a 5-handed game with Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo). Although the table is short-handed, QTo is a marginal hand to open from the earliest position. It is easily dominated by hands like AQ, KQ, and AJ, and playing it out of position can be difficult. With a healthy stack of over 220 big blinds, I do not need to take unnecessary risks with weak offsuit broadway cards from early position. The disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot or better position.

We’re first in after UTG folds (effectively early position 4-handed). 8♠2♥ offsuit has very poor playability, is dominated when it makes top pair, and doesn’t realize equity well out of position versus the button/ blinds. With ~87bb, there’s no need to leak chips limping or opening this hand; better to keep a tight UTG+1 range and wait for stronger, more profitable spots.

I'm on the button with A♠2♦ (As2d). Only the blinds remain and the pot is 150 with 100 to call — this is a good spot to attempt a steal and play the flop in position. I will open-raise to 300 (total). This size (≈3x BB) is large enough to discourage cheap calls and isolate one opponent, yet small relative to my 18,100 stack. The Ace is a useful blocker to strong A-x hands. Plan: if I'm met with a large 3-bet I will fold A2o; if called I'll use positional advantage to continue with top-pair/ace-high strategies or apply pressure on favorable boards.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 3d. Minni has raised to 3x from the Button. While the hand is suited, it is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Given the stack sizes and the fact that Claude is still to act in the Big Blind, calling here often leads to difficult post-flop situations or being squeezed out pre-flop. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

T6o is a weak hand to defend from the BB against a button raise. While I'm getting about 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450), T6o plays poorly out of position against Minni who has been very aggressive. The hand lacks connectivity and I'll often be dominated or making second-best hands. Better to save chips and wait for a better spot.

5-handed UTG with no antes, A4o is a marginal offsuit ace that plays poorly multiway and gets dominated when we make top pair. With aggressive/stack-leveraged opponents behind (notably Pro and Minni) who can 3-bet and apply pressure, opening risks losing chips in low-realization spots. Best is to pass and preserve our 87bb stack for stronger opens.

A7s in UTG+1 at a 5-handed table is worth an open — suited Ace has good playability and fold equity. I want to take the initiative and avoid multiway pots, so I’ll open-raise to a standard ~3x the big blind (300). Size is big enough to get weaker hands and limpers to fold, but small enough that I can continue postflop comfortably with my 18k stack. Plan: if called, c-bet on A or highly favorable boards; play cautiously on dry boards and fold to large resistance. If facing a large 3-bet (heavy sizing or from the very deep Pro), I’ll lean to folding; consider calling smaller 3-bets from the button/SB to see a flop because of suit and position.

I am holding Jack-Deuce offsuit on the button. This is one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni has opened from UTG+1 (effectively the cutoff in this 5-handed game) to 3x the big blind. There is no strategic reason to call or 3-bet with this hand, as it has poor playability and is significantly behind even a wide opening range. Folding is the standard and correct move to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

T7o is a weak offsuit hand, and I'm out of position against Minni who has been raising frequently. Facing a 3x raise with this marginal holding OOP, folding is the disciplined play. The pot odds aren't good enough to justify calling with this hand that will be difficult to play postflop.

I am in the Big Blind with J2s facing a raise to 300 from Minni (UTG+1). The pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, requiring about 31% equity to call. However, J2s is a very weak hand that suffers heavily from reverse implied odds (dominated pairs and flushes) and poor playability, especially when playing out of position against a tighter early-position range. I previously folded J3s (Hand 72) in the same position against a likely wider Button raise. Since Minni's range is stronger than a Button opening range, and J2s is technically worse than or equal to J3s, folding is the correct disciplined decision to preserve chips.

UTG with Td6s (off-suit) in a 5-handed game: this is a marginal, easily dominated hand with poor post‑flop playability. The pot is just the blinds (150) and calling 100 to limp leaves me out of position vs four opponents; raising to the min (200) to open is thin and exposes me to 3-bets from deep, aggressive stacks (Pro 21875, Flash 6550, etc.). Prefer to preserve a healthy 18400 stack and wait for a better spot in position with a stronger hand. Fold.

I am holding Ten-Deuce offsuit (Tc 2h) in UTG+1. This is a very weak hand that lacks both high-card strength and connectivity. Given that there are still three players to act behind me and Minni has already folded, there is no reason to enter the pot with such a poor hand. Folding is the optimal play to preserve my stack.

Pocket tens on the button is a premium hand. With Minni and Flash folding, I'm in excellent position to raise. Standard 2.5x raise to build the pot and take initiative. With 44BB effective, I want value from this strong pair.

I am in the Small Blind with A3o facing a raise from the Button (Claude). Claude has about 42 BBs and I am the chip leader with over 200 BBs. A3o is a marginal hand that is difficult to play out of position, often dominated by better Aces or pairs. While the pot odds are tempting, the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make folding the most prudent long-term play, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

We’re in the big blind with AKo versus a button open from Claude, whose range is wide in 5-handed play and whose stack (4175) is only ~42 BB. Calling invites the small blind to come along and puts us out of position in a bloated multiway pot; AKo plays best as a value 3-bet that pressures his opens and sets up an easy call if he jams. With his stack depth, a sizing around 3.5–4x keeps worse hands (AQ, AJ, KQ, suited broadways, medium pairs) in while still creating strong fold equity versus his weakest steals. If Claude 4-bet jams, we call profitably given his likely reshove range and the dead money.

Facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo with pocket tens on the button. TT is a strong hand but with ~42BB effective, 4-bet shoving risks getting called only by better (JJ+, AK). Calling is reasonable - I have position and a premium pair that plays well postflop. The 650 call into 1200 pot gives me good odds to see a flop and re-evaluate.

Heads-up 3-bet pot, we’re the preflop aggressor and this 9-7-3 rainbow is relatively dry. Claude’s 3-bet calling range contains a lot of unpaired broadways (AQ/AJ/KQ/QJ, etc.) that should fold to a continuation bet, while his continuing range is mostly pairs/9x. With an SPR ~1.4, we don’t need a big sizing to generate folds and we keep our risk controlled—if he jams over this, his range is usually pair-heavy and we can comfortably fold AK-high. If called, we can still apply pressure on many turns (A/K/Q/J/T) and occasionally improve to top pair.

Excellent flop for my pocket tens - I have an overpair on a dry 973 rainbow board. FiveTwo 3-bet preflop (likely JJ+, AK, AQ) and is c-betting small (600 into ~1850). On this board, AK/AQ missed completely and I'm ahead of most of their range except JJ+. I should raise for value and protection against overcards. Raising to ~1700 puts pressure on overcards while building the pot when I'm ahead.

Claude’s flop click-raise to 1700 in a 3-bet pot comes with an SPR that’s already very low (he has only ~1825 behind). If we just call, we’ll be out of position in a pot where he can comfortably jam most turns, forcing us to fold a huge portion of the time when we miss.

With AKo we have solid equity versus the part of his range that does this with one-pair hands (9x, 88–QQ) plus we retain some fold equity versus his occasional raise-bluffs/unpaired broadways. The best way to realize our equity and capitalize on the dead money is to put his remaining stack at risk now.

So we raise to 3525 total, which effectively shoves Claude in (he can only call off his last 1825).

I have TT on a 973 rainbow board - an overpair on a very dry texture. FiveTwo 3-bet preflop and now re-raised my flop raise. This is a strong line suggesting JJ+ or possibly AK making a move. However, the math is compelling: pot is 7075 and I need to call only 1825 (my remaining stack) - getting almost 4:1 odds. I need ~20% equity to call profitably. Against JJ+ I have ~8-10% equity, but if FiveTwo ever does this with AK/AQ (which missed), I'm way ahead. Given the pot odds and that I'm already pot-committed, calling is correct. Even if I'm often behind, folding here with these odds would be too tight.

Holding 8-7 offsuit under the gun (UTG) in a 5-handed game is a clear fold. This hand is too weak to open from early position, as it has poor playability and is likely to be dominated by the ranges of the players behind me, particularly the aggressive blinds.
Pocket Jacks is a premium hand in UTG+1. Standard open raise to build the pot and thin the field. With 89BB effective, I'll raise to 250 (2.5x) to set up nicely for postflop play.

I am on the Button with Queen-Five offsuit (Q5o), facing a standard open raise to 250 from Claude in the Cutoff/UTG+1 position. Q5o is a very weak hand that is easily dominated by the opener's range (KQ, AQ, QJ, pairs). Despite having the advantage of position, the hand strength is insufficient to justify a call or a 3-bet bluff, especially with active players in the blinds. The prudent play is to fold.

AdKc is a premium that plays best as an isolation/value 3-bet from the small blind. Flat-calling invites the big blind in and leaves us out of position in a multiway pot, while also missing the chance to win preflop. With ~42bb, a 3-bet to a size that pressures Claude’s opening range sets up a comfortable stack-to-pot ratio where we can commit on many flops and are happy to get it in if Claude 4-bet jams.

Facing a large 3-bet to 900 from the small blind after Claude’s open, A♥3♣ is too weak to continue. I’m out of position and this hand will be dominated by the SB/raiser’s Ax and broadway-heavy 3‑bet ranges; calling 800 into a 1,250 pot with a weak kicker is a negative‑EV, multiway spot. A 4‑bet would be a wildly thin/isolation play and a shove is unnecessary risk with a poor kicker. Preserve a healthy deep stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

JJ is a premium hand facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo who is short-stacked (3375 chips). FiveTwo has been aggressive with 3-bets throughout. With JJ, I have a strong hand that plays well against their likely 3-betting range. Given FiveTwo's short stack, a 4-bet here essentially pot-commits them. If I 4-bet to ~2200, they'll have ~1175 left with a pot of ~3500, making them pot-committed. I'm happy to get it in with JJ against a short stack's range.

Claude has 4-bet to 2200 after my 3-bet, and with my remaining stack size this is a classic low-SPR spot: if I call 1300, the pot becomes huge and I’d have only ~2075 behind (SPR < 0.5), meaning I’m essentially committed anyway but out of position and allowing him to realize equity with hands that might otherwise fold. AdKc is near the top of my range, blocks AA/KK, and plays best as a 5-bet jam here to maximize fold equity versus any 4-bet bluffs/light value and to get it in versus his value range.

FiveTwo has 5-bet to 3500 with only 775 chips remaining behind. They're essentially committed. With JJ, I'm getting excellent pot odds (5800 pot, 1300 to call). Even against a tight 5-bet range of AA/KK/QQ/AK, I have enough equity and pot odds to continue. Since they only have 775 left, I should just put them all-in now rather than letting them see a free flop. I'll raise to put maximum pressure.

We’ve already put in 3500 and have only 775 behind facing Claude’s 4275. Calling puts us all-in for 775 more into a pot of 7875 (and 8650 after we call), giving us enormous pot odds (we need ~9% equity). Even versus the absolute worst case (AA/KK), AdKc has far more than that, and versus any realistic 6-bet range (QQ+/AK with some bluffs), this is a mandatory stack-off. Folding here would be a major EV mistake given the price and our hand strength/blockers.

I am in UTG position (first to act) in a 4-handed game with 7-4 offsuit. This is a very weak hand with no playability, disconnected and unsuited. With ~218 BB deep, there is no incentive to get involved with trash hands from early position. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I’m on the button with 6h5c (marginal but playable in position). UTG folded, pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop. With 183bb effective and only the blinds left, I want to take the initiative and apply pressure — a standard 2.5–3x open is appropriate to generate fold equity and give me position post‑flop. Min-raising to 200 is too small; I raise to 300 to discourage loose defenses and keep the pot manageable. If I face a big 3‑bet I can fold; if called I’ll play cautiously and use position to realize equity.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 2c, which is a very weak hand. Minni raised from the Button, and while ranges are wider in a 4-handed game, 82s is not strong enough to defend out of position, especially with a short stack relative to the table. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chips for a better spot.

Ac6c is a suited ace in the BB facing a button raise. Good playability with flush potential and the ability to flop top pair. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). This is a standard defend against a button open - calling is the right play.

Flop is 2d7cJd. I have Ac6c - just ace high with a backdoor club flush draw. I completely missed this flop. I'm out of position against the preflop raiser. Standard play is to check and see what Minni does. I can potentially check-call a small bet with my overcard and backdoor equity, or fold to heavy aggression.

I was the preflop aggressor from the button and Claude (BB) just flat-called. My hand (6♥5♣) has no pair and essentially no realistic draws on 2♦-7♣-J♦, so this is a pure bluff candidate. As the raiser I have range advantage on this flop and can take the pot down with a small continuation bet. Claude has shown a tendency to call fairly wide preflop and check the flop, so I size to about a third of the pot to maximize fold equity vs air/overcards while keeping the pot controllable if called. Plan: bet 225 to try to fold out missed broadways and small pairs; fold to a significant raise. If called I will proceed cautiously — check the turn on non‑improving cards and only continue with real equity or a clear bluff target on favorable runouts.

I have Ac6c on 2d7cJd - complete miss with just ace high and a backdoor club flush draw. Minni bet 225 into 650 (small ~1/3 pot bet). Pot odds are 225 to win 875, about 3.9:1 needing ~20% equity. I have 6 outs to an ace (~12% to hit turn), backdoor flush draw, and position to check-fold the turn if I miss. The small bet size makes this a reasonable float/peel, though I'm weak. Calling is marginally +EV given the price.

Turn brought the 4d, putting three diamonds on board. I have Ac6c - just ace high with no flush draw (my backdoor clubs are dead). Board is 2d7cJd4d. I'm first to act with 0 to call. With just ace high and no real equity, I should check. If Minni bets again, I'll likely fold as I have no showdown value and no draws. Checking is the only sensible play here.

I have 6♥5♣ with no pair. I was the preflop/ flop aggressor and Claude check‑called my c‑bet on 2♦‑7♣‑J♦. The turn 4♦ doesn't improve my hand — it actually makes the board more dangerous (three diamonds) but gives me 8 outs to a straight (3s and 8s) ~17% to hit the river. Claude's passive line (check‑call on flop, check on turn) looks like a marginal made hand or a medium pair rather than a two‑diamond flush (which would likely have bet/raised to protect). Betting again here would be a thin second‑barrel that bloats the pot out of position and can be crushed or check‑raised. I prefer pot control and to realize my equity: check the turn, see the river, and only continue aggressively if I hit or face a river spot where a bluff is clearly credible. If Claude bets the river, I'll call small bets with my 8 outs but fold to large, value‑heavy river bets.

River brought the Ah giving me top pair with weak kicker (A6). Minni checked back the turn showing weakness after their flop c-bet. My options: 1) Bet for thin value - but I only get called by better aces, two pair, or sets. Worse hands fold. 2) Check - allows Minni to potentially bluff with missed draws (many diamonds out there that bricked) or check behind with worse hands like pocket pairs or a jack. Given Minni's turn check showed weakness, checking to induce and showdown is the better line.

Check. My 6♥5♣ missed the river — the A♥ actually improves many hands in Claude’s check‑call flop range (pairs and broadways) and makes a thin bluff unlikely to succeed. Claude’s line (call flop, check turn) looks like medium/weak made hands rather than a two‑diamond flush, so betting here would bloat the pot out of position with almost no equity. I’ll take the safer line and check to showdown. If Claude now leads a very small probe (tiny fraction of the pot) I may call as a bluff‑catch, but I will fold to any meaningful value bet — this spot is not profitable for an aggressive bluff.

Open‑raise to take the initiative. In 4‑handed play A♥3♥ is playable—suited ace with good postflop equity and blockers to strong Ax. Limping is too passive; a standard 3x BB open (300) applies pressure, isolates the button and gives us fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. If I face a large 3‑bet from Pro or Claude I will fold; I may call a small 3‑bet depending on size and position to realize equity.

I am on the button with 4h 3d, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni has raised from UTG. Given the strength of my hand relative to an UTG opening range, there is no reason to continue. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my chips.
63o is a trash hand, especially facing an UTG raise. Out of position with no playability, this is an easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Js 8h. Minni raises to 300 from UTG (4-handed). The pot offers reasonable odds (calling 200 to win 450, roughly 3.25:1), but J8 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against the second-largest stack. It is dominated by many hands in Minni's opening range (better Jacks, pocket pairs, high cards). Despite the attractive price, the reverse implied odds and lack of playability make this a fold.

I am in the UTG position with Ks4s, which is a marginal suited King. Given the current 4-handed table dynamics and my stack size of 6,500 chips (65 big blinds), I have several options. While suited Kings often have some value in late position, opening UTG (which is effectively the Hijack/Cutoff in a 4-handed game) requires a slightly tighter range. However, looking at the previous hands, there has been a significant amount of aggressive 3-betting and 4-betting, particularly involving Claude and FiveTwo (who is no longer in this hand description, but the remaining players like Minni and Pro have shown activity). Folding is the safest play to preserve my stack for better spots, especially since I'm the shortest stack at the table and don't want to get involved in a pot out of position against deep stacks.
QTsuited on the button is a strong hand to open. With only the blinds left to act, I should raise to steal or build a pot with position. Standard button open to 250.

I hold 9h 4d in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand (unconnected, off-suit, low ranks). Facing a raise from the Button (Claude) to 250, I am out of position and the hand has very little playability or equity. Even though the raise size is standard, defending with 94o is not profitable. Folding is the only reasonable option.

Facing a button open to 250 (2.5bb) from Claude; I’m in the big blind with K♥3♥ and need to call 150. K3s is marginal but suited and has decent implied odds vs a wide button opening range. Pot odds are attractive (150 to win 400 -> ~27% break-even), and with deep stacks I can realize equity on favorable flops. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with a weak K (often dominated) — that’s high variance and unnecessary here. Plan: defend by calling to see the flop; proceed aggressively if I hit a King or a heart/strong draw, otherwise play cautiously and fold to large sustained pressure on dry boards.

Flop: A♥ 2♣ 8♦, I hold K♥3♥ — no pair, only a very weak backdoor heart (needs both turn and river). I defended preflop as a price call, but leading out OOP on an ace-high board with a marginal K is high-variance and usually dominated by Claude's button range (lots of A-x, better Kx). Check to keep the pot small and let the button c-bet; plan is to fold to meaningful continuation bets and only continue with a clear improvement or very cheap probing bet on later streets if Claude shows weakness.

I have QTcc on an Ah 2c 8d board - completely missed. However, I have position and the preflop lead. A small c-bet here can represent the Ace and fold out hands like small pairs or worse high cards. Minni has shown tendencies to check-call then lead on later streets with made hands. A ~1/3 pot c-bet is efficient - if called, I can reassess on the turn with potential backdoor club draws.

Claude opened from the button and then fired a very small c-bet (200 into a 750 pot) on A♥ 2♣ 8♦. I hold K♥3♥ — no pair, only a backdoor (runner-runner) heart draw and the chance to hit a King. I’m likely behind to most of Claude’s A‑x and better Kx combos, but the bet is small so the pot odds to call are attractive (~21%). I’ll call the 200 to see the turn, keeping the pot manageable and allowing me to fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve (pair a King or pick up a meaningful heart/card or get a free card if he checks). If Claude shows continued strength on the turn I will release this marginal hand.

Board A♥ 2♣ 8♦ 9♣, I hold K♥3♥ — no pair and my backdoor heart flush chance is gone. Claude opened from the button and made a small c‑bet on the flop, so his range is heavy in A‑x, better Kx and small pairs; I’m OOP with marginal showdown value. Pot is already 950 and there’s no bet to call on the turn, so I’ll control the pot and check to see his action. I’ll fold to significant turn aggression (I have too few clean outs) and only continue if I pair a King, see a cheap river, or get a clear read to bluff.

Turn brought the 9c, giving me a flush draw with QcTc (9 outs) plus a gutshot to a straight (J gives me QJT98 - 3 more outs). I now have ~12 outs for a strong hand on the river (~24% equity). With position and the betting lead, a semi-bluff bet makes sense - I can continue representing the Ace while building the pot with my improved equity. If called, I have decent odds to hit. Betting around half pot (450-500) keeps pressure on.

Fold. I have K♥3♥ with no pair and no realistic draw on A♥ 2♣ 8♦ 9♣. Claude opened BTN, c‑bet the flop and now fires a turn bet — his line is weighted toward A‑x and other value hands that dominate me. Pot odds to call 475 into a 1425 pot require ~25% equity; my clean outs are ~3 kings + 3 threes = 6 outs → 6/46 ≈ 13%, well below break‑even (and some outs are dead when Claude holds Kx/A3). A raise as a bluff lacks reliable fold equity on an ace‑high board and risks committing chips with no equity. Stick to the earlier plan: fold to significant turn aggression and preserve the stack for better spots.
3h8h from UTG is too weak to open. Suited connectors/gappers need to be stronger from early position. Easy fold.

I hold 8s 6c on the Button. This is a weak off-suit one-gapper. Although I am in position and the chip leader with over 200 BBs, the hand is too weak to profitably open-raise, especially with active opponents in the blinds who are also relatively deep (the shortest stack is 64 BBs). There is no need to force action with trash hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

We’re in the small blind with Q♠T♣ and the button and UTG have folded — just the big blind (Flash, 6.4k) to act. Q-T offsuit is a reasonable hand to open heads‑up; I want fold equity and to isolate the BB rather than check/complete and play OOP for free. I’ll take the initiative with a standard 3xBB-sized open (300 total) — large enough to discourage marginal calls but small enough not to overcommit out of position. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll play the flop with position considerations in mind.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd 2d. Minni (the Small Blind) has raised to 300. It costs me 200 more to call into a pot of 400, meaning I need about 33% equity to continue. Suited hands like this perform reasonably well against a wide Small Blind opening range, and I will have the advantage of being in position for the remainder of the hand. Given the stack depths and the pot odds, calling is a standard defensive play.

I was the preflop aggressor with Q♠T♣ and Flash (BB) just called — I should continue on this dry, low flop to take advantage of position and fold equity. Board (9♣ 2♣ 4♠) is uncoordinated except for two clubs; I have no pair but two overcards and a backdoor club/straight possibility. Flash defended widely preflop, so a standard small-to-medium c-bet will fold out weaker holdings (air, Jx, low pairs) while keeping club draws and medium pairs in the pot. I’ll size to ~40% pot (250) to maintain pressure without bloating the pot out of position. Plan: if called, reassess on the turn for pairs or improved draws; fold to a large check‑raise since I have no made hand.

On the flop of 9c 2c 4s, I have bottom pair (Deuces) with a Queen kicker. Minni has made a continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 600. The pot odds are approximately 3.4 to 1, requiring me to have roughly 23% equity to call. Given that Minni is betting from the Small Blind after an open, her range is quite wide and includes many overcards and flush draws that I am currently beating. With position and a pair, folding to a single small bet would be overly weak. I will call to see how the turn develops and potentially reach a cheap showdown or take the pot if she checks.

I have Q♠T♣ on 9♣‑2♣‑4♠‑5♠. I have two overcards but no realistic multi‑card draw on the turn — with one card to come my only clean outs are pairing the Q or T (~6 outs, ~12–13%). Flash defended the flop wide, so his calling range contains lots of marginal pairs and club draws that can fold. I’m in position, so I should use fold equity and a small-to-medium turn barrel to pick up the pot now or keep it manageable if called. I’ll bet ~40% of the pot (450) — big enough to fold weak pairs and pure air, small enough to avoid bloating a pot where I have no made hand. If he raises I’ll give up; if he calls I’ll reassess on the river and proceed cautiously.

On the turn of 9c 2c 4s 5s, I still hold bottom pair with a Queen kicker. Minni's bet of 450 into the 1100 pot (roughly 41% of the pot) provides me with pot odds of approximately 3.4 to 1. In a Small Blind vs. Big Blind scenario, Minni's range is extremely wide, encompassing many semi-bluffs like club flush draws, straight draws (such as 67 or 78), and various overcards. The 5s is relatively neutral, though it does pair A5 or 56. Given that I am in position, I have the advantage of seeing her action on the river before making my final decision. Calling here is a standard defense to see if she slows down on the river or if I can reach a relatively inexpensive showdown against her missed draws. I will fold to a significant third barrel on most river cards unless I improve to trips or two pair.

I missed my hand (Q♠T♣) — board 9♣-2♣-4♠-5♠-6♥ has no pair for me and my backdoor club/straight chances are gone. Flash cold‑called my flop and turn bets, so his continuing range is heavy with one‑pair hands (9x/5x/4x/2x), some made 6s on the river, and a few 3x combos that just made a 2‑6 straight — plus some missed club/overcard junk. To make a river bluff profitable I’d need him to fold a large fraction (e.g. >33% to a ~1k bet), but given he’s been calling my pressure that’s unlikely. There’s no value to extract from Q‑high, so the +EV play is to check and take showdown (or fold to a sizable river shove if he leads).

The board texture (9c 2c 4s 5s 6h) has become quite connected, with various straight possibilities (any 3 or 78). Minni, after betting both the flop and turn, has checked the river. This usually signals either a hand with moderate showdown value (like a pair of 9s or 4s) that is now afraid of the straight/two-pair cards, or a total air/missed draw (like clubs) that is giving up. My bottom pair of Deuces beats her total bluffs and missed club draws, but loses to any higher pair (4s, 5s, 6s, 9s) or overcards that paired up. Since I have some showdown value, checking back is the most prudent play. Betting as a bluff is unlikely to force a fold from a 9 or a better pair, and I'd rather take my chances at winning the pot for free than risk more chips when I might already have the best hand against her missed draws.

Current scenario: 4-handed game, I am UTG (Cutoff equivalent) with a deep stack of ~217 BBs. Hand: Qd Jd (Queen-Jack suited). Strategic assessment: QJs is a premium drawing hand and a strong top-pair maker that benefits significantly from fold equity and initiative. In a 4-handed format, opening ranges are wider, and this hand is well within the top 15-20% of hands usually opened from this position. My standard opening size throughout this tournament has been 2.5 BB (250 chips), which balances pot control with fold equity. Raising allows me to define opponents' ranges and potentially take down the blinds uncontested or play the hand in position against the blinds if called.

Button with A5s — good postflop playability (nut-flush & wheel potential) and an Ace blocker. Pro opened UTG to 250, so his range is fairly strong; with a deep 164BB stack I prefer to keep the pot controllable and use position. A tiny raise to 400 would be non-credible and can induce 4-bets or isolate me out of position against better Aces. I’ll call 250 to see a flop in position, plan to apply pressure on Ace or strong-draw flops, and fold to heavy aggression on dry, unfavorable boards.

I am in the Small Blind with Qs9s, which is a strong enough hand to continue against a standard open and a flat-call. Pro opened from UTG to 2.5x, and Minni called on the button. The pot is offering me 2.25:1 odds to call (200 to win 450 + 200). Calling allows me to realize the equity of my suited semi-connector in a multi-way pot while keeping the pot size manageable, given that I am out of position against both active players.

I have Ac5c in the BB facing a raise to 250 that's been called by two players. I need to call 150 into a pot of 850, getting nearly 6:1 odds. A5 suited plays well multiway with flush and wheel straight potential. Easy call.

On a paired board (Th Tc 8s) in a four-way pot, I am out of position. My hand, Qs 9s, has some potential with a gutshot to a straight (needing a Jack) and a backdoor flush draw, but it is not strong enough to lead out into three opponents, including the pre-flop aggressor. Checking is the most prudent play to see how the action develops and to determine if I can profitably continue to the turn.

I have Ac5c on a 8s Th Tc board - just ace high with a backdoor club flush draw. The board is paired with tens, and I completely missed. Flash checked, and I'm in position. Betting here would be a pure bluff into a multiway pot where Pro (UTG raiser) likely has connected with this board. With ace-high I have some showdown value and a backdoor flush draw. Best to check and see a free turn card - if a club comes I can reassess.

In a 4-way pot, the board [8s Th Tc] interacts heavily with the calling ranges of the blinds and the button, which often contain Tx, 9x, Jx, or pocket pairs. As the pre-flop aggressor, while I have range advantage in terms of overpairs, the probability of one of three opponents holding a Ten or an Eight is significant. My hand (QJs) has decent equity with two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9), but betting into three players is risky and unlikely to generate enough folds to be profitable immediately. Checking allows me to realize my equity, see a free turn potentially, and avoid getting check-raised or bloating the pot with a marginal drawing hand.

Flop is 8♠ T♥ T♣; I hold A♥5♥ so I have no pair — just Ace-high with a very slim backdoor heart-run possibility (need both turn+river hearts). This is a multi‑way pot (UTG open + two callers) and UTG's check suggests either a missed overcard or a slowplay, so many continuing ranges have at least some showdown value (pairs, small sets, or calling hands). With marginal showdown equity and deep stacks, I don't want to build a pot by barreling as a bluff into three opponents. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity in position, and keep options open: if the turn brings an Ace or a favorable card (or everyone checks), I can apply pressure or value bet; if I face significant aggression on this street I’ll fold this air hand to avoid committing chips without a made hand.

On the turn, the board is 10h-10c-8s-Kc. I have Qs-9s, giving me a gutshot straight draw (a Jack provides a straight). Since the flop was checked around by all four players, it is unlikely anyone is holding a Ten, though someone could be trapping. The King is a scare card that likely improves someone's range, particularly the pre-flop raiser (Pro) or the callers. As I am first to act and only have a draw with limited showdown value, checking is the most appropriate move. This allows me to see how the other three players react to the King and potentially see a river card cheaply or for free.

Turn brought Kc giving me the nut flush draw with Ac5c. Board is 8s Th Tc Kc - paired tens with 2 clubs. Everyone has checked to me. I have 9 outs to the nut flush (~18% equity). However, the paired board is concerning - if I bet and get called/raised, I could be drawing dead to a full house. With a free card available and good implied odds if I hit the nut flush on the river, checking is the correct play. Take the free card and evaluate on the river.

The hand is QdJd. The board is 8s Th Tc Kc. Pot is 1000. It is a 4-way pot where everyone checked the flop. The turn brings a King. I have an open-ended straight draw (needs a 9 or A) and Queen-high. The King hits my UTG opening range (AK, KQ, KJ), but also hits opponents' ranges who might have floated or checked a King. Betting into three opponents with just a draw in a multi-way pot is risky, as I have little fold equity against a Ten or a King, and the pot is small. Checking allows me to realize my equity for free (if the button checks) or decide on the river. I want to see the river as cheaply as possible to hit my straight.

Multi-way pot (Pro UTG + Flash + Claude) and I hold only Ace-high (A♥5♥) with no paired or plausible draw — the turn K♣ kills my backdoor heart-runner possibility. Pro opened UTG so his checking twice can be either a slowplay or a medium/strong made hand; with three players to act a bet here has poor fold equity and I don’t want to inflate the pot with air. Pot control and equity realization in position is the best line: check behind to see a free river and re-evaluate. If I face sizable aggression on the river I’ll fold given my lack of showdown-worthy hand; if the river brings an A or a very harmless card and everyone checks, I can consider a small value/steal attempt or a showdown. Therefore I’ll check to keep the pot small and preserve stack depth for better spots.

On the river, the board completes a broadway-heavy runout (8s-Th-Tc-Kc-Js). I have Qs-9s, which gives me a King-high straight (9-10-J-Q-K). While this is a very strong hand, there are several concerns: first, the board is paired (Tens), so any player holding a Ten has a full house; second, any player with AQ has the nut straight (Ace-high). Given that the flop and turn were checked around by all four players, the range of my opponents is somewhat capped, but slow-playing a Ten or AQ is possible in a multi-way pot. By checking first to act, I can evaluate the strength of my opponents based on their betting behavior. If the action is checked through again, I likely have the best hand. If there is a bet, I can decide whether to call or raise depending on the sizing and who makes the bet, as I avoid the risk of betting into a superior hand while still allowing for potential value-catching or inducing bluffs.

River is Js, I missed my flush draw completely with Ac5c. I have ace-high on a board of 8s Th Tc Kc Js - paired tens with K-J on board. Everyone has checked to me. My ace-high has almost no showdown value and little fold equity bluffing into 3 opponents who checked through. Someone likely has at least a pair or is trapping with a ten. Check and accept the loss rather than spewing chips.

I hold QdJd in a 4-way pot. The board is 8s Th Tc Kc Js. Action checked to me on the river after checking through flop and turn. I have Two Pair, Tens and Jacks, with a Queen kicker (best hand T-T-K-Q-J). While I have showdown value, the board is dangerous. I lose to any King (making Kings and Tens), any Ten (Trips), and straights (Q9 or AQ). Given the passive action, opponents likely have weak holdings, but betting is unlikely to get called by worse (8x or underpairs fold) while better hands (Kx, Tx) will call. Checking minimizes loss against better hands and allows me to see a showdown.

Multiway pot (UTG open + two callers) on a paired, connected board (8♠ T♥ T♣ K♣ J♠). I hold A♥5♥ — only Ace-high with no completed flush or pair; turn killed my backdoor heart. Everyone checked down, which means low fold equity and many continuing ranges have at least showdown value (pairs, Kx, Jx, possibly a slow-played T). Pot is small (1,000) vs deep stacks, so risking chips with pure air is unnecessary. Check and take the free showdown; fold to any sizable river bet if faced.

Short-handed (4 players) and very deep (≈162bb) — I can open a fairly wide range from UTG. Qc9d is marginal but has good playability and a queen-blocker, so I prefer to take the initiative rather than limp. A standard 3x open (300 total) pressures the blinds, charges callers, and lets me play position/control postflop. If I face a large 3-bet I will fold; vs a small 3-bet or single call I will proceed with standard c-bet strategy on favorable textures and play cautiously multiway.

I am on the Button with 76o, which is a very weak hand. Minni has opened for 300 from UTG. While the table is short-handed (4 players), calling a raise with 76o into two remaining players in the blinds is generally unprofitable. I am currently the shortest stack at the table with over 80 big blinds, so there is no immediate pressure to defend with subpar hands. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

K5o is a weak hand facing an UTG raise from Minni. UTG raises typically represent strong ranges, and K5o is easily dominated by better kings while lacking good playability. The pot odds (~1.8:1) aren't enough to justify calling with this marginal holding. Fold is the disciplined play.

Minni has been opening frequently (raising to 300) from various positions, indicating a wider range than a standard UTG opener. Held [Jd Kc] in the Big Blind, facing a standard 3x raise. KJo is a strong broadway hand that plays well as a defend, especially getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win 450). 3-betting out of position might isolate me against a range that dominates me (AK, KQ, AJ) or fold out hands I dominate. Calling allows me to keep her range wide and realize equity post-flop. I have a significant chip lead over the table average, so preserving stack while defending blinds with decent holdings is a sound strategy.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a rainbow board of J-8-6. This is a strong hand, but I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni). Donk betting doesn't accomplish much as it folds out her bluffs and likely only gets action from better hands or strong draws. The standard line is to check to the aggressor to allow her to make a continuation bet (c-bet). If she bets, I have an easy call to keep her range wide. If she checks back, I can lead the turn. Therefore, I will check.

I raised preflop and got one caller (Pro). The flop J♠ 8♦ 6♥ is dry and favorable for a continuation bet — few strong made hands and lots of turn-overcards. Q♣9♦ has no pair but the Q-blocker reduces QJ combos; betting now uses fold equity to pick up the pot against many hands that called preflop (broadways, weak pairs, suited connectors). I want to keep the bet small (~1/3 pot) to maximize folds while preserving stack flexibility deep-stacked. Plan: bet 225 into 650; fold or give up if faced with a significant check-raise, and if called re-evaluate on the turn (continue only if I pair or the turn is favourable, otherwise control the pot or let it go).

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (King) on a J-8-6 rainbow board. The opponent, who opened UTG, has made a small continuation bet of 225 into a pot of 650. This small sizing is typical for a wide range of hands, including weaker pairs, draws, and air, as well as monsters. Raising here would likely fold out the bluffs I'm beating while isolating me against hands that dominate me (AJ, AA, KK, Sets). My hand is too strong to fold but plays best as a bluff-catcher out of position. I will call to keep the opponent's range wide and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the Turn, the board pairs with the Six of Clubs [8d 6h Js 6c]. This card is relatively safe for my Top Pair (Jacks) with a King kicker, as it is unlikely the UTG opener has a Six in their range (except perhaps pocket Sixes or A6s, though A6s is loose for UTG). My hand remains strong relative to Minni's likely range. I am out of position. Leading out here (donking) would likely fold out her bluffs and only get action from hands that beat me (AJ, Overpairs, Sets/Full Houses). Checking is the standard play to induce further bluffs or value bets from worse hands, or to get to a cheaper showdown. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor.

I check. I c-bet the dry J-8-6 flop with Q♣9♦ and got one call from the big blind. The turn (6♣) pairs the board and I still have no pair or meaningful redraw. That card reduces my bluff equity and meaningfully improves caller’s range (6x, sets, two-pair, or hands with a jack or a mid pair). Betting here would often get called by hands that beat me and I don’t want to inflate the pot with no showdown value. Checking controls the pot, preserves my deep stack, and lets me see if Pro shows weakness. My plan: check back and either take the pot on the river if he checks, or fold to meaningful turn aggression (I’ll fold to a substantial probe bet — roughly >~1/3 pot — and only consider calling very small, obvious bluff-probing bets if my read is that Pro is bluff-heavy). If I see a favorable river (I pair or a clear fold-inducing card), I’ll re-evaluate then.

I hold Top Pair (Jacks) with a King kicker, and the board has run out double-paired (8-8-6-6-J), giving me Jacks and Eights with a King kicker. The opponent checked back the turn, which caps her range; she likely does not have a strong hand like an overpair, a set turned full house, or strong AJ, as those would typically bet the turn for value or protection. Her range likely consists of weaker Jacks (QJ, JT), mid-pairs (99, TT), or Ace-high hands. Betting the river is unlikely to get called by worse hands (except perhaps a stubborn pocket pair) and isolates me against better hands. Checking allows me to induce bluffs from missed draws or Ace-highs, and comfortably get to showdown to realize my equity against her marginal made hands. I plan to call a reasonable bet or check-down.

Board is double-paired (8♦ 6♥ J♠ 6♣ 8♥). I hold Q♣9♦ — no pair and only a Q-kicker against a board two-pair. Pro called my preflop open and my small c-bet on the flop, then checked turn and river; that line is consistent with either a weak holding that never improved or a slowplayed strong hand (any 8, any 6, JJ, or pocket pairs that make a full house/quads). Betting here has very low fold equity (many of Pro’s calling hands will either have a better kicker or a full house) and I have virtually no value to extract. With deep stacks and lots of hands that beat me or beat my kicker, the +EV play is to check to showdown rather than bluff or inflate the pot. I check and will fold to a meaningful river raise (per my earlier plan).

In a 4-handed game, UTG is effectively the Cutoff position. Queen-Ten offsuit is a marginal hand that sits at the very bottom of an opening range. Considering the aggressive tendencies of Claude and Minni shown in previous hands, opening such a weak-ranking offsuit hand risks being forced to fold to a 3-bet or playing a difficult pot out of position. I will fold to maintain a tight and solid image.

I have 64o on the button - a weak hand, but I'm in position with only the blinds to act after Flash folded. This is a standard button steal spot. I'll raise to 250 to try to take down the blinds. If I get 3-bet, I can fold easily.

I am in the Small Blind with [8s, 9h], facing a 2.5x raise (250) from Claude on the Button. Although I have a healthy chip stack (22,000), 89o is a marginal hand to play out of position. The pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600 if BB folds) require about 33% equity realization, which is difficult to achieve OOP without initiative. Additionally, flat-calling invites the Big Blind (Minni) to squeeze or come along, making the spot harder. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here to avoid bleeding chips in low-EV situations.

Claude opened to 250 from the button; I’m in the big blind with J♦3♦. Calling costs 150 into a 400 pot (~27% break-even) so it’s cheap to defend. J3s has some playability (backdoor flush, occasional top-pair), but it’s too weak and too often dominated to 3-bet out of position — and I don’t want to fold a reasonable defending hand for only 150. Plan: call to see the flop, continue with equity (flush or a jack) or proceed cautiously and fold to heavy action on dry boards.

I have J♦3♦ on a Q♠ 8♥ 2♦ flop — a 10‑out diamond flush draw (~38% to hit by the river) but no pair. I’m out of position (BB vs button raiser) and Claude opened preflop, so his range is wide and he’ll c-bet fairly often on this dry board. Betting out OOP with only a draw bloats the pot when I’m likely behind to Qx or pair-heavy hands. I’ll check to control pot size and induce a continuation bet. If Claude fires a small‑to‑medium c‑bet I’ll call and realize my equity (the price is usually correct), if he checks back I get a free turn to improve, and if he raises big I can fold and preserve a deep stack for better spots.

I raised preflop with 64o as a button steal and got called by Minni in the BB. Flop is Q82 rainbow - I've completely whiffed with 6-high. Minni checked to me. While I have the betting lead, this board likely connects with Minni's calling range (Qx hands, pocket pairs). With zero equity and no draws, a c-bet risks getting called or raised with nothing. Better to check back and see a free turn card.

Turn (Ts) kills my backdoor diamond plan — with J♦3♦ and the flop containing 2♦ I needed two more diamonds to make a flush, and there’s only one card left. I currently have only J‑high and very little equity (≈3/46 ≈ 6.5% to pair the J on the river). Claude checked the flop, which can be either weakness or a controlled strong hand, so bluffing out of position into an unopened pot with no real outs is thin. Pot is 550 and my stack is deep; no reason to bloat the pot with a hand that can’t improve except by pairing. I’ll check and take a free look at the river; plan is to fold to any meaningful bet on the turn and re-evaluate only if Claude checks back.

Turn brought Ts, adding a spade flush draw to the board. I still have complete air with 64o - no pair, no draws. Minni checked again which could indicate weakness or a trap. With 6-high and no equity, there's no reason to bluff into a pot where Minni has shown willingness to call preflop and could easily have a queen, ten, eight, or a draw. Best to check back and see a free river.

I have J♦3♦ and the river completes to 8♥ 2♦ Q♠ T♠ 7♥. Claude checked flop and turn after raising preflop, so his range is wide and likely contains many hands that beat a lone Jack (Qx, Tx, 8x, pocket pairs, KJ, etc.). I have no real showdown value or meaningful blockers and a bluff OOP into a raiser who has shown passivity on earlier streets is thin — many of his hands will call a river bet. The pot is small (550) relative to stacks, so risking chips here with essentially air is negative EV. I’ll check back and preserve my stack rather than bluff.

River completed with 7h. I have 6-high - complete air with no showdown value. Minni has checked all three streets, suggesting either a weak hand or a trap. The pot is only 550. While I can't win at showdown, bluffing here against a player who has shown passive tendencies risks getting picked off by any pair. The pot is small enough that taking the loss is acceptable rather than committing more chips with nothing.
J5s from UTG in a 4-handed game is too weak to open. While suited, it lacks connectivity and the jack isn't strong enough to compensate. With 137 BB, I can wait for better spots.

I am on the Button with 5d 3c, which is an extremely weak off-suit hand. Although I have the chip lead and position, this hand is far below the threshold for a profitable open-raise, even in a 4-handed game. Opening here would be unnecessary spewing. The correct play is to fold.

I’m in the small blind with Kc6h heads-up vs the big blind (Flash). This is a marginal hand that’s often dominated by common calling ranges, but vs a single opponent it has enough playability to try and steal the blind. Raising to the minimum (to 200 total) applies pressure, avoids seeing flops out of position for free with a dominated Kx, and keeps the investment small. If Flash 3-bets I can fold and preserve most of my stack; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously OOP (look to C-bet favorable flops, fold to heavy resistance on dry boards unless I improve).

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 2d, one of the weakest possible starting hands. Minni, in the Small Blind, has made a minimum raise to 200. While the pot odds are 4:1 and I would have position post-flop, 42o has extremely poor playability and equity, even against a wide Small Blind opening range. At this stage of the tournament with a stack of 81.5 big blinds, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand and risk losing more chips post-flop.

I am in UTG position with King-Three offsuit (K3o), which is a very weak hand. With three players left to act behind me and 50/100 blinds, opening with this hand is not profitable. My stack is healthy (chip leader), so there is no need to take unnecessary risks with garbage hands from early position. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Button with J♥8♣ — a marginal but playable hand in position. Pot is 150 and calling 100 is passive; opening to ~3x BB (300 total) takes the initiative, applies pressure to the blinds and preserves plenty of stack for post‑flop play. Flash (SB) and Claude (BB) defend fairly wide, so 300 is large enough to fold out the weakest ranges but small enough not to overcommit with a hand that plays best in position. Plan: if called I'll C‑bet favorable high‑card or dry flops and play cautiously on coordinated/flushy boards; fold to a large, committed 3‑bet (or respond based on sizing/reads), consider calling a small 3‑bet to use positional advantage. Raising maximizes expected value from this spot while keeping postflop options open.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Deuce of diamonds (T2s). Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. While the hand is suited, it is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position, especially against an active player like Minni who has a significant stack. With 81 big blinds, I have plenty of room to wait for a stronger opening or a better spot to defend. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.
J8o in BB facing a min-raise from button. Getting 2.25:1 odds (200 to win 450). Minni has been active with raises. J8o is marginal but defendable at this price from the BB. I'll call and see a flop.
Flop is 2c Ts 6c. I have J8o - complete whiff. No pair, no flush draw (no clubs), no straight draw. I'm first to act with J-high. Obvious check - no value in betting air here.

I raised preflop and have position with Jh8c on a dry, low flop (2c‑Ts‑6c). This is a classic continuation‑bet spot — I have no pair but I have the initiative and a very small backdoor club; the flop is unlikely to have hit Claude’s calling range cleanly. I’ll bet for fold equity and to deny free cards: ~½ pot is large enough to fold many unpaired overcards and small pairs but small enough to continue post‑call. If Claude folds I win the pot; if he calls I can re‑evaluate on the turn and fold to a big raise (Jh8c has poor showdown value vs heavy action).
Complete miss on 2c Ts 6c. I have J8o with no pair, no flush draw (no clubs), and no straight draw. Getting ~3:1 odds but I have virtually zero equity here. Clear fold.

Qc3c (queen-three suited) from UTG in a 4‑hand game is a marginal, easily-dominated holding despite the suit. Acting first and then playing out of position against the Button, SB and a deep BB who 3-bets sometimes would put me into difficult postflop spots. A min-open invites 3-bets and a call is cheap but likely leaves me multiway and OOP with poor equity. Prefer to preserve my sizable stack and wait for a better spot to attack the blinds or isolate. Fold preflop.

I am on the Button with an Ace-rag (Ac 4h) in a 4-handed game. Since the action folded to me, this is a standard opening spot to attack the blinds and use my positional advantage. My stack is roughly 81 big blinds, which is enough to play post-flop, but I am currently the shortest stack at the table, so I want to be proactive in building my chips. I will raise to 300, which has been a consistent opening size at this table.

Q9o in the SB facing a button raise of 3x. This is a marginal hand but playable against a likely wide button stealing range. Getting decent pot odds (450:250). Calling to see a flop is reasonable - can flop top pair or a draw. 3-betting would be too aggressive with this holding out of position.

I am in the Big Blind holding 4d 2h, which is statistically one of the worst hands in Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button (Flash) to 300 and a call from the Small Blind (Claude), the pot is 700 and I need to call 200. The pot odds are roughly 4.5 to 1 (requiring ~22% equity). However, 4-2 offsuit performs very poorly multi-way, has terrible playability (making very few strong hands), and suffers from position disadvantage against the raiser. Even with the good price, calling here is negative EV. Preserving chips is the correct play.

Missed the flop completely with Q9o on 3h Jd 6s. No pair, no draw - just Q-high. Out of position against the preflop raiser. Betting as a bluff here is risky since Flash likely has many hands that connect with this board or can call with overcards. Best to check and potentially give up if faced with aggression.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the flop of J-6-3 is quite dry, which generally favors my opening range over the Small Blind's calling range. Since Claude checked, I will lead out with a standard continuation bet of about 43% of the pot. This sizing is enough to fold out random overcards or weak draws that missed this board, while allowing me to keep the pot manageable if Claude has a Jack or a pocket pair and decides to continue.

Q-high on a J-6-3 board with no draws. Flash c-bet 300 into 700 pot. I have zero showdown value and no equity to continue - no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw. Out of position makes floating very unprofitable. Easy fold.

I am UTG with 6d 3c, which is an extremely weak starting hand. In a 4-handed game, while opening ranges are wider, 6-3 offsuit is well outside any reasonable range. With a stack of 85 BBs, there is no reason to play this hand and I should wait for better opportunities.

On the button with T2o, only blinds left to act. This is a decent steal spot. T2o is weak but position advantage and fold equity make a standard raise profitable here. I'll raise to 250 to try to take down the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Q8o, facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (Claude). This is a standard fold in most equilibrium strategies. Q8o is easily dominated by the opener's calling range (KQ, QJ, QT, A8) and plays poorly out of position. Although we are deep-stacked (over 200 BBs), defending here is likely -EV due to the positional disadvantage and hand weakness. I will fold and preserve chips.

Facing a button open to 250 with Qc3s in the big blind. Q3o is a weak, out-of-position hand — 3-betting to the min of 400 would be too loose and costly. Calling 150 more (250 total) is cheap versus my 16,350 stack and gives ~27% immediate pot odds; Claude has been opening frequently from the button so defending some portion of the range is correct. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to strong continuation pressure on dry boards unless I hit a queen/3 or develop good draws; only continue aggressively when I have real equity or a clear fold equity line.

Dry, disconnected flop (Th 4h 2s) against a frequent button opener. Qc3s has no pair and no realistic draw (backdoor straight/flush is essentially impossible here), so I should not voluntarily build the pot. I defended preflop because the price was cheap and Claude opens a wide range, but postflop the correct line is to check and fold to meaningful continuation pressure. Plan: check now to keep the pot small; if Claude checks back I see a cheap turn, if he makes a very small c-bet (~≤1/3 pot, ~≤180–200) I may call once for pot odds and to realise equity, but I will fold to medium/large c-bets (~>1/2 pot) because I have no value or draw to continue.

Flopped two pair (tens and deuces) on Th4h2s. This is a strong hand that I need to bet for value and protection. There's a heart flush draw on board, so I want to charge any draws. Minni checked, so I'll bet around 65% pot to extract value and deny equity to flush draws.

Fold. I defended preflop because the price was cheap and Claude opens wide, but this flop (Th 4h 2s) is very dry and Qc3s has no pair and essentially no real draws. I only have ~6 outs to hit a pair (≈24% to improve by the river), while Claude’s c‑bet of 350 into a 900 pot (~39% of the pot) gives me only ~28% immediate pot odds (350/1250). That makes a call roughly -EV; even if I hit I’ll be out of position and often facing further aggression. I would consider calling a much smaller c‑bet (~≤1/3 pot), but at this size I fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

K9o in UTG at a 4-handed table. This is a marginal opening hand from early position. While K9o has some playability, it's easily dominated by better kings and can get into tricky spots postflop. With a healthy stack of 134.5bb, I'll take a standard open here at 2.5x to put pressure on the blinds.

I am on the Button facing a raise to 250 from Claude in UTG. My hand is 6c 3c, which is a very weak suited gapper. Against a UTG opening range, this hand has very poor equity and playability. Despite my chip lead and position, there is no incentive to get involved in this pot with such a weak holding. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Claude opened UTG to 250 — an early-position raise that represents a relatively tight/strong range. My hand 9♣7♠ is a weak, offsuit connector that is often dominated and has poor equity versus an UTG range. Calling 200 into a 400 pot would leave me out of position against the opener (and the BB can still act), making postflop decisions difficult and low-expected-value. A min 3-bet to 400 would be an over-aggro bluff here with poor blockers and high risk of a 4-bet or being called by better hands. With a healthy stack and many better spots ahead, folding and preserving chips is the correct play.

In a 4-handed game, Claude's open from UTG (which is effectively the cutoff) represents a relatively strong range. Queen-Five offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop, especially when out of position. Although the price to call is only 150 chips to win a pot of 400, defending with such a weak hand often leads to difficult situations on later streets. With a stack of 84 big blinds, there is no need to take a marginal or negative expectation spot here. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
I am in UTG position with 9d 2h, which is a very weak hand (disconnected, low off-suit cards). With 4 players left, playing garbage hands from early position is not profitable. I should fold.

Button with K♣8♠ — marginal but strong enough to open in position against only the blinds. I want to apply pressure and leverage position, so I’ll raise rather than limp/call. A standard 3x BB sizing (~300) preserves fold equity against the SB/BB and keeps the pot controllable with deep stacks (I have ~162bb). Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call or continue postflop against single calls and use my position to c-bet favorable boards or pot-control on scary texture. Calling 100 is too passive; raising to 300 is the best exploitative line here.

I am in the Small Blind with a premium hand, Ace-Queen of spades. Minni, on the button, has been opening frequently with a wide range of hands. A 3-bet is the standard and most profitable play here to take the initiative, isolate the raiser, and potentially win the pot pre-flop. Given Minni's 300 open, a raise to 1000 (roughly 3.3x) is an appropriate size to put pressure on the button and discourage the Big Blind from entering the pot lightly.

T6o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet. Flash 3-bet to 1000 from the SB after Minni opened. I'd need to call 900 to see a flop with a hand that plays poorly out of position against a strong range. Easy fold.

I opened to 300 in position with Kc8s but Flash’s sizable 3-bet to 1000 is a clear polarizing/strong action from the SB. The pot is 1400 and calling 700 would require ~33% equity to break even (700/2100). K8o is frequently dominated by a typical 3‑bet range (AK, AQ, KQ, TT+ and strong suited broadways) and won’t realize enough equity postflop against that range. 4‑betting as a bluff to 1700+ is too thin and would commit too many chips with a marginal hand. Given the stack depths and my earlier plan to fold to a large 3‑bet, the +EV line here is to fold and preserve my deep stack for better spots.

I'm UTG in a 4-handed pot with 7d7h and a deep stack (~158bb). In short-handed play pocket sevens are strong enough to open — they play well heads-up and have set value if called. I want to build the pot and isolate the blinds while denying free cards, so I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x the BB. This sizing (300 total) is big enough to fold out the weakest blinds sometimes but keeps worse hands and speculative calls in. Plan: proceed cautiously postflop when I miss; if I flop a set I’ll extract maximum value. Versus a large 3-bet from a deep-stacked reg (Pro/Claude), I’ll respect the strength and likely fold; I may call small 3-bets for set-mining depending on opponent and implied odds.

I am on the button with Ten-Four of clubs. Minni has opened from UTG (4-handed) to 300. While I have the advantage of position, Ten-Four suited is a very weak hand to defend against a standard 3x raise from an active player. My stack is currently the smallest at the table (8800), and I want to look for higher-equity spots to commit chips. Calling here would likely lead to a difficult post-flop situation against a range that dominates me. I will fold and wait for a better hand.

Kh8h is a playable suited hand. Facing an UTG raise of 300, I'm getting decent odds to call 250 into a pot of 450. Suited kings have flush potential and can flop top pair. While I'll be out of position, the hand has enough playability to see a flop. Folding seems too tight, and 3-betting without a stronger hand against UTG is risky.

I am in the Big Blind with T3s facing a 3bb raise from Minni (UTG) and a call from Claude (SB). The pot size is 700, and I need to call 200 more, giving me direct pot odds of 3.5:1 (or roughly 22% equity required). While T3s is a weak hand, it is suited, and the price is excellent to close the action in a multi-way pot. My stack depth (largest at table) allows me to peel and look for flush draws or two pair+ on the flop. I will call to defend my blind.

I flopped top pair with K8s on Kd4h7c. I have the best hand currently most of the time - Minni raised UTG but could have many hands that missed this board. Betting for value and protection makes sense. I want to charge draws (56, 68 for straights) and get value from worse kings or medium pairs. A ~half pot bet of 400 is appropriate.

I hold Ten-Three suited (Diamonds) on a King-Seven-Four rainbow board containing one diamond (Kd). This gives me only a backdoor flush draw and no immediate straight draws or pairs. Facing a lead bet (donk bet) of 400 into a pot of 900 from the Small Blind, and with the original UTG raiser still to act behind me, my hand has very little equity and poor playability. Floating with just backdoors and Ten-high is not profitable in this spot.

I flopped a set of 7s on K♦ 4♥ 7♣ — one of the strongest hands possible on this dry board. Claude’s small bet of 400 into a 1,300 pot is a probe-sized bet that represents a wide, medium-strength range (Kx, small pairs, occasional bluffs). I want to extract value and deny free cards, so I’ll raise rather than just calling. I size to 1,600 total on the flop: big enough to build the pot (and charge top-pair/overcards) but not so large as to fold out most Kx and weaker pairs that pay off. If Claude jams or puts in a large 3-bet I’ll strongly favor calling — my set dominates most of his betting/calling range. If he calls my raise, I’ll continue to value-bet on safe turns and re-evaluate on scary cards (another K or an A).

Minni opened UTG and check-raised my 400 bet to 1600 - a 4x raise. This is a strong line representing better kings (AK, KQ, KJ), sets (77, 44), or two pair. My K8 is a weak top pair that's dominated by most value hands in Minni's range. While I have backdoor hearts, calling 1200 OOP against a polarized range is -EV. Minni has shown aggression throughout this tournament and this sizing indicates strength.

I am dealt 9-2 offsuit in the UTG position with four players remaining. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em and offers virtually no potential for profit. Given I am the short stack at the table with 88 big blinds, there is no strategic reason to open-raise or enter the pot with such a hand. The optimal play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I have A9o on the button with only the blinds left to act. This is a standard open-raise spot - A9 is well within my button raising range. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take it down preflop or play in position postflop.

I hold Q7o in the Small Blind facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (Claude). Although the pot odds are tempting, Q7o plays very poorly out of position and is often dominated by the opener's range (e.g., KQ, QJ, A7). My equity realization will be low, and I risk losing a larger pot if I hit a pair that is outkicked. With a comfortable stack, there is no need to get involved in a marginal spot. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I have QhJs (QJ offsuit) in the big blind facing Claude's button open to 250 (2.5x). Pot is 400 and it costs me 150 to call (total commit on this street will be 250). Calling is the right play: QJ has good equity vs a button opening range and is playable postflop, and the call gives ~27% immediate pot‑odds (150 into a 550 pot). I'm out of position, stacks are deep (~16.7k vs 12.6k) so I don't want to inflate the pot with a marginal 3‑bet — a min‑3bet to 400 is too small to fold him out and a larger 3‑bet commits me OOP with a hand better used to see flops. Plan: see the flop, continue when I make a pair or a strong draw, and fold to heavy, non‑favorable pressure on dry boards.

Check. On 8h-8d-Ks my QhJs has no pair and is dominated by any King and most A/K or Kx hands Claude opened with. He opens wide from the button and will c-bet a lot, so I prefer pot control and to see his action rather than lead OOP with a marginal hand. Checking lets me fold to heavy pressure, call small continuation bets to realize equity vs bluffs, or take the pot down if he checks back. With deep stacks I avoid bloating the pot with only QJ.

Flop is K88 rainbow. I have A9o - just ace high. Minni called preflop and checked. This paired board favors my range as the preflop aggressor. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot makes sense to deny equity from hands like QJ, QT, JT and potentially get folds from small pairs. My ace high could even be best sometimes. Betting ~175-200.

Board: 8h 8d Ks. I have Qh Js — no pair and dominated by any K and many broadways, but Claude bet tiny (175 into a 725 pot ~24% bet). Calling 175 gets me a final pot of ~900 so I need only ~19–20% equity to break even. Versus a wide button c-bet range I have roughly that equity with two overcards and backdoor possibilities, so a call is a cheap way to realize equity and keep weaker hands/bluffs in. Raising OOP with QJ is thin: I have little fold equity against an aggressive button and risk getting 3‑bet or committed out of position with a marginal holding. Plan: call the 175, fold to heavy turn/river pressure unless I improve to a pair or a clear draw; if I pair a Q or J I will proceed for value.

Turn pairs the board into trip eights (8h 8d 8s K). I hold Qh Js — no pair and no meaningful equity except ~13% to hit a full house on the river (6 outs). This is heads‑up vs Claude (he opened preflop and c‑bet the flop small, then checked the turn). While some of his calling range can be folded, any King in his hand is already a full house and any eight in hand is quads — hands I cannot beat. Betting here is a thin bluff into the nuts with limited fold equity and the risk of getting check‑raised or called by better hands. I’ll take the conservative / exploitative line: check and see the river. If Claude shows aggression on the river I can fold; if he checks again I can consider a small river bluff or a showdown line depending on the card.

Turn brings a third 8, making quad 8s on the board with a King. I have A9 (ace high). Minni called my flop c-bet and checked turn. With trip 8s on board, any pocket pair = full house, any King = full house. My ace high has showdown value against floats/bluffs but gets crushed by anything that called flop for value. No reason to bet - just check back and see a free river. If I'm ahead of bluff-catchers, I can potentially win at showdown.

Check. I missed my 6 outs on the river (3s) and Q♠/J♠ has no pair — I cannot beat any Kx (those now make a full house), any 8 (quads), or a paired hand that turns into a full house. Claude’s line (button open, small flop c‑bet, turn check) is consistent with both marginal hands and slowplays, so my fold equity bluffing here is very low. Betting risks getting called by better (and losing a big chunk of my deep stack) for little expected gain. With nothing to call, the +EV line is pot control: check to showdown and fold to a large river raise if one appears.

River completes with quad 8s on board. Everyone has quads - the winner is determined by the 5th card kicker. I have Ace-high, the best possible kicker. Minni checked to me. This is a clear value bet spot - I beat any hand without an Ace (Kings, Queens, pocket pairs, etc). I should bet small to get called by worse kickers. ~1/3 pot around 300.

Board: 8h 8d Ks 8s 3s. I hold Qh Js — no pair and essentially zero chance vs any Kx (kings become a full house on the turn) or any 8 (quads). Claude’s line (button open, small flop c‑bet, turn check, small 300 river bet into ~900) is consistent with both strong value (Kx / 8x) and thin value / bluffs. The river bet is small (~33% pot), so calling 300 gives me pot odds of ~20% (300 to win ~1500) and is a cheap way to catch bluffs or weaker showdown hands. I have no realistic raise/bluff equity OOP and raising would be reckless with nothing to fold out. I’ll take the low‑variance line and call the 300 to see showdown; fold to a large re‑raise but not here.

3d5d from UTG is a weak suited connector. Even 4-handed, this isn't strong enough to open from early position. The gap between 3 and 5 makes straight potential limited. Folding is the standard play here.
