I hold Pocket Tens (Th Ts) on the Button, which is a premium starting hand. With the UTG player folding, I have an excellent opportunity to raise for value and potentially steal the blinds or play a pot in position. My stack of 21,500 is the largest, giving me leverage. A standard raise size of 250 (2.5 BB) is appropriate to build the pot while not committing too many chips if 3-bet heavily, although I would likely continue against the blinds. Limping is passive and allows the blinds to realize equity for free, which is not ideal for TT.

Holding 2♥5♣ in the small blind facing a clean button open to 250 (I must call 200). This is a very weak, unplayable hand — almost always dominated by a standard BTN raising range and has poor flop playability and blocker value. Calling 200 into a 400 pot requires ~33% equity to break even; 2-5 offsuit has far less vs a BTN open and I'd be out of position on every post‑flop street. With a healthy stack there’s no reason to manufacture action with this junk — preserve chips for better spots (squeezes, 3‑bets, or playable postflop hands). Fold.

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a very strong hand against a standard Button opening range. Pro, the chip leader, has raised to 250. To capitalize on my hand strength and take the initiative, I will 3-bet to 800. This puts pressure on the Button's wide steal range and allows me to win the pot immediately or play a bloated pot with a range advantage if called.

I hold Pocket Tens (Th Ts) on the Button. After opening to 250, the Big Blind (Flash) 3-bets to 800. TT is a strong hand that is well ahead of a typical BB 3-betting range which includes bluffs, suited connectors, and pairs 77+. However, 4-betting would likely isolate me against a range that dominates me (JJ+, QQ+, AK) and fold out the hands I beat. By calling, I keep the opponent's range wide, play a medium-sized pot in position, and can evaluate the texture of the flop. The price to call is 550 to win a pot of 1100, offering good odds. I will call.

The flop is low and disconnected (2s-6c-5h), which generally favors my 3-betting range of overpairs more than the Button's calling range, although he could have pocket pairs. I have two overcards and a backdoor straight draw. I will place a continuation bet of 600 (roughly 36% of the pot) to maintain the initiative I established pre-flop, represent a strong hand like an overpair, and fold out his missed high cards like KQ or QJ.

I hold Pocket Tens on a low, dry flop of 2s 6c 5h in a 3-bet pot. My opponent, the Big Blind, c-bets 600 into a pot of 1650 (approx 1/3 pot). My hand is an overpair to the board, putting me ahead of my opponent's missed high cards (AK, AQ) and lower pairs (77-99), though behind higher pairs (JJ+). Raising here would likely fold out the bluffs I beat and isolate me against superior hands. Calling allows me to keep my opponent's range wide, utilizing my position to re-evaluate on the turn. The price is good, and the board texture is safe enough to flat call.

The turn 7c is a card that significantly connects the board and is likely better for the caller's range than my 3-betting range. Having missed the board entirely with AhJs, and considering the potential for Pro to have improved to two pair, a set, or a straight draw, I will check. This allows me to control the pot size and see how Pro reacts. If he checks behind, my Ace-high might still be good or I can re-evaluate on the river. If he bets, I will likely fold as I have very little equity against a range that calls a flop bet on this board texture.

On the turn, the board is 2s 6c 5h 7c. The opponent, who 3-bet pre-flop and c-bet the flop, has checked. This check often signifies pot control with a hand like a higher overpair (JJ-QQ) fearful of the wet board, or a give-up with missed overcards (AK/AQ). My hand (TT) is a marginal made hand in this spot: I am ahead of missed overcards and draws but behind higher pairs and sets. Betting here isolates me against a stronger range (calls from JJ+, sets, strong draws) and folds out the hands I beat (AK, AQ). Checking back allows me to exercise pot control, realize my equity without facing a check-raise, and potentially induce bluffs on the river or catch a cheaper showdown. Given the opponent's stack size (SPR ~2), there is no need to bloat the pot with a one-pair hand.

The board has completed as 2s-6c-5h-7c-Tc. I have Ace-high (Ah Js). After 3-betting pre-flop and continuation betting the flop, I checked the turn after the board became significantly more coordinated with the 7c. My opponent (Pro) also checked behind on the turn, which suggests he might have a marginal made hand (like a weak pair) or high cards that he is looking to take to a cheap showdown. My Ace-high has some showdown value against his missed draws or lower high cards (like KQ or QJ), but it is unlikely to fold out a better hand with a bet, nor is it likely to be called by a worse hand if I bet. Therefore, checking to realize my showdown equity is the most logical and conservative play here. If Pro bets, I will likely have to fold, as he would rarely bluff in this spot after I have shown weakness on two streets.

I hold a set of Tens on a river board of 2s 6c 5h 7c Tc. The river card improved my hand significantly from an overpair to a set. My opponent, Flash, 3-bet pre-flop and c-bet the flop, but then checked the turn and river. This line strongly suggests he is potting controlling with an overpair (JJ-AA) or has given up with missed overcards (AK/AQ). While a flush or straight is possible on this board, Flash's passive play on the turn and river makes these strong hands less likely, as he would typically bet them for value. I have a clear value bet here targeting his overpairs. Checking back would miss too much value. I will bet approximately 40% of the pot (1200 into 2850), a size that is small enough to induce a 'crying call' from hands like JJ, QQ, KK, or AA, but large enough to extract meaningful chips. If he check-raises, I will re-evaluate, but for now, betting for value is the optimal play.

After 3-betting pre-flop and continuation betting the flop with Ace-high (Ah Js), the board texture became very coordinated (2s-6c-5h-7c-Tc). While Pro checked back the turn, which usually indicates a marginal hand or a surrender, his river bet of 1200 into 4050 is problematic for my hand. My Ace-high is a weak bluff-catcher here because almost all of the hands Pro would call a 3-bet and a flop c-bet with have either improved to a pair or a straight (like 89, 45, or 78) or were already pocket pairs (88, 99) that beat me. There are very few 'total air' hands in his range that wouldn't have at least a small pair by the river. Given the pot odds and the board runout, I don't believe my Ace-high is good 23% of the time. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I hold AQs in UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is a premium hand that sits near the top of my range. With a deep stack of over 200 BBs, I want to build the pot and take the initiative. Opening for a standard raise size of 2.5x (250 chips) is appropriate to thin the field and extract value from worse hands that might call, such as weaker Aces, Kings, or suited connectors.

Pro opened UTG to 250. Q♠2♣ is a very weak, oft-dominated hand versus an UTG open. Calling 250 into a 400 pot (pot becomes 650) requires ~38–39% equity to break even. Versus a typical UTG opening range (and with SB/BB still to act who can call or 3‑bet), Q2o has significantly less than that and will be difficult to play postflop. Stack is deep enough to wait for better spots on the button with real playability (suited connectors, broadways, pairs). Fold and preserve stack.

I am holding 82o in the small blind facing an open raise from the table chip leader. This is one of the weakest possible hands in poker and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack depths and the strength of the opening range from UTG (even in a 4-handed game), there is no reason to defend this hand or attempt a bluff. Folding is the only viable move here.

Pocket 6s in the BB facing a UTG raise of 250. I need 150 to call into a 400 pot. With deep stacks (both over 130bb), I have excellent implied odds to set mine. UTG raises typically show strength, but pocket pairs play well in set-mining spots. If I hit my set ~12% of the time, I can win a big pot. Calling is the standard play here.

Missed my set on Tc 3s Ah board. The Ace is a terrible card for my pocket 6s - Pro opened UTG and likely has many Ax hands in his range. I have no showdown value here. Check and fold to any bet is the standard line when set mining fails on a bad board.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (AQ on A-T-3 rainbow). This is a very strong hand on a dry board where I have a significant range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. While checking back is a valid option to trap or induce bluffs, betting allows me to extract immediate value from weaker Aces (A2-A9) and Ten-X hands that likely call a small bet. A small c-bet size (around 35-40% of the pot) is sufficient to deny equity to random broadway straight draws (like KJ, QJ) and get value from worse made hands without forcing folds from the hands I want to target.

Missed my set on Tc 3s Ah. Pro's UTG range crushes this Ace-high board with hands like AK, AQ, AJ, ATs. My pocket 6s have almost no equity (~8%) and no draws. The 200 bet into 500 pot gives me 3.5:1 but I need much better odds to continue with just 2 outs. Set mining failed, time to fold.

UTG with J♠2♠ in a 4‑handed game: this is a marginal, easily dominated hand with very little post‑flop playability out of position against the Button, SB and BB. The pot is just the blinds (150) and the cost to see a flop is 100. Raising UTG with J2s is too loose and will often get called or squeezed by deeper stacks (Pro/Claude) putting me in tough spots. Limping/calling 100 also yields poor implied odds and multiway action where this hand performs poorly. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot in position or with a stronger holding.

I am on the button with Ac6c in a 4-handed game. This is a strong starting hand for this position. After UTG (Minni) folded, I should definitely open for a raise to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately. A standard raise size for me has been 300, which is 3x the big blind, and I will continue with that sizing here to put pressure on the blinds (Claude and Pro).

As7s is a decent suited ace against a button raise. I have position disadvantage but the hand has good playability with nut flush potential. Calling 250 into a pot of 450 gives me decent odds to see a flop. 3-betting is an option but calling seems more standard here given my position.

I am in the Big Blind with J6s. The Button raised to 300 and the Small Blind called. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 700, giving me very favorable pot odds of 4.5 to 1 (requiring ~22% equity). While I will be out of position to the initial raiser post-flop, J6s plays reasonably well in multi-way pots due to its flush and straight potential. Given the price and closing the action, calling is the positive EV decision.

Flopped middle pair (7s) with ace kicker on 7h Qc 6d. Board is relatively dry. With two opponents behind, including the pre-flop aggressor (Flash), checking is standard. My hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to build a big pot. I'll check and evaluate based on their actions.

I hold bottom pair (6s) on a Q-7-6 rainbow board in a multi-way pot. Being in the Big Blind and sandwiched between the Small Blind and the Button (pre-flop aggressor), leading out is not optimal as it likely folds out worse hands and gets action from better ones (Qx, 7x, overpairs). The standard line is to check to the pre-flop raiser to see their action. I have some showdown value and potential to improve to two pair or trips, but I want to keep the pot manageable.

I have bottom pair (sixes) with an Ace kicker on a board of Qh-7h-6d. After my pre-flop raise, both Claude (SB) and Pro (BB) called. On the flop, both opponents have checked to me. While I could bet for protection or to represent a Queen, my hand has decent showdown value and some potential to improve (an Ace for top pair, a six for trips, or clubs for a backdoor flush draw). By checking back, I control the size of the pot and get to see a free turn card, which is a safer play in this 3-way pot against deep stacks.

Turn brought 3d, creating a two-diamond board. I have middle pair with ace kicker. Everyone checked the flop, so Flash likely doesn't have a strong queen. Betting small here for value and protection makes sense - I can extract value from weaker pairs (6x) and prevent free cards for straight draws (54, 85) and the diamond draw. A ~40% pot bet balances value and pot control.

I hold bottom pair (6s) on a turn board of [7h Qc 6d 3d]. The action checked through on the flop. On the turn, the Small Blind (Claude) leads out for 375 into a pot of 900. While the price is good (requiring ~23% equity), I am in a multi-way pot sandwiched between the bettor and the Button (Flash), who is yet to act. The Button checked back the flop, indicating weakness or a trap, but the SB's lead into two opponents usually signifies at least a marginal made hand (like a 7, pocket pair, or weak Q) or a strong draw. My hand is vulnerable to many river cards (overcards, diamonds), and I am out of position. Calling with bottom pair weak kicker in a multi-way pot is often a leak, and I risk being squeezed by the Button or facing a difficult decision on the river. Folding is the prudent, low-variance play to preserve chips.

I have bottom pair (sixes) with an Ace kicker on a board of Qh-7h-6d-3d. After the flop was checked around, Claude led out for 375 into a 900 pot (roughly 42% pot). This sizing and the turn lead often represent a 'probe' bet with a wide range, including medium pairs (like a 7), straight draws (89, 54), or even total air trying to take down the pot. Given my position and the fact that I have a pair and an overcard, I have enough equity and pot odds (needing ~23%) to justify a call. Folding would be too exploitable here, and raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me.

River paired the board with 3s, giving me two pair (7s and 3s) with ace kicker. Flash called my turn bet - they likely have a marginal queen, small pocket pair, or missed draw. Value betting is thin here: better hands (Qx, 77, 66) call, worse hands mostly fold. Checking allows me to win at showdown against missed draws and potentially induce a bluff. If Flash has a queen, they'll likely check back. Best to check and realize my showdown value.

On the river, the board pairs the three (7h Qc 6d 3d 3s). I have bottom pair (sixes) with an Ace kicker. Claude, who led the turn for a small bet of 375, has now checked to me on the river. This check often indicates that the opponent either has a marginal hand they want to take to showdown (like a 7 or a mid-pocket pair) or they were bluffing on the turn and are now giving up. Since my hand has significant showdown value against missed draws (like 89, 54, or diamond draws) but is not strong enough to bet for value (as I would likely only be called by a Queen or a better pair), the best course of action is to check back and see the showdown.

I am in the UTG position with K5o. This is generally a weak hand to open with in a four-handed game, especially from early position. My stack size is approximately 67 big blinds, which is healthy but the smallest at the table. Given the aggressiveness of Claude and Minni shown in previous hands, opening with a marginal hand like K5o from UTG is likely to lead to difficult post-flop situations or being outplayed by their wider ranges. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Q5o on the button with only blinds left. This is a standard button steal spot. While Q5o is marginal, position gives me an edge and I can profitably open this hand to steal the blinds. Raising to 250 (2.5x) as a standard open.

I hold 3d 2h in the Small Blind, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a standard raise from the Button (Claude), I am out of position and holding a hand with very poor playability and equity. Although I have the chip lead, playing this hand would be a negative EV move. The correct action is to fold immediately.

I’m in the big blind (100) facing Claude’s button open to 250 (to call 150). I have 9♠4♠ — a weak but suited holding. Heads-up Claude’s opening range is wide, and with deep stacks the hand has reasonable implied equity and backdoor potential. I don’t want to 3-bet a pure bluff with no meaningful blockers, and folding too often to his standard opens surrenders too much value. Calling 150 (total commit 250) gives ~27% pot odds and lets me realize equity; I’ll proceed cautiously postflop (check/control OOP, fold to heavy aggression unless I pair or hit strong draws).

Flop J♦-4♥-T♥ and I hold 9♠4♠ — second pair (a weak 4) with a poor kicker and no real nut draw. Claude opened wide from the button, so his range contains a lot of missed overcards, but the two hearts and J/T on board make my hand vulnerable. Out of position I prioritize pot control and allowing bluffs, so I’ll check: if Claude bets a small-to-medium c-bet (roughly ≤1/2 pot) I’ll call to realize equity; I’ll fold or strongly re-evaluate to very large raises that polarize to sets/top pair. If he checks back I keep the pot small and reassess on the turn.

Flop is Jd 4h Th - two hearts and connected. I have Q5o with no hearts, giving me only a gutshot (need K for straight) and an overcard. This is a poor spot to c-bet: coordinated board, no backdoor flush draw, and Minni has shown they'll check-raise or lead with strong hands. Checking back to take a free card and potentially improve on the turn is the better play here.

Board J♦-4♥-T♥-2♦; I have 9♠4♠ — second pair with a very weak kicker and I'm out of position. Claude opened wide preflop then checked the flop and the turn, which signals a wide, often missed or medium-strength range (overcards, small pairs, some slowplays). With a marginal made hand I want pot control and to avoid getting raised off a better hand, so I will check. Checking keeps the pot small, lets me call a reasonable river bet for showdown value, and can induce bluffs from missed broadway cards. I will call modest river bets for thin value but fold to large, polarized river aggression that represents two pair/sets/top pair with a better kicker.

Turn 2d is a blank that doesn't change much. I still have Q-high with just a gutshot to K (4 outs). Minni checked again - they could have a weak J/T, a draw, or be trapping. Betting here with no equity besides a gutshot against a player who's been sticky is -EV. Taking a free card to see if I hit my K on the river is the best line.

I rivered trips (4♠ plus board 4♥4♦). Claude checked flop and turn and has shown a fairly passive line — likely a wide, often-missed opening range (overcards, single J/T pair, small pairs). With the pot at 550 this is an easy value spot: many hands in his range (Jx, Tx, 9x, lower pairs, K/A high) will call a modest river bet but would fold to a very large polarizing bet. There is a small risk he holds a full house (JJ, TT, or 22) that crushes me, but those combos are relatively rare. I’ll bet about half-pot to extract value while keeping fold equity vs bluffs and avoiding bloating the pot vs the small full-house combos; if he check-raises large I’ll strongly consider he has a full house and fold. Bet 300.

River paired the 4s and completed backdoor diamonds. Minni leads 300 into 550 (small bet). I have Q-high with no showdown value - can't beat any pair. Minni's small bet is likely thin value with a J, T, or even a 4. Calling with Q-high makes no sense as I lose to any made hand. Bluff-raising is too risky against Minni who has shown stickiness. Easy fold.

K2 suited in UTG - marginal hand but the suited diamond combination gives some playability. I've been opening somewhat wide and have a healthy stack at 140BB. A standard 2.5x open is reasonable here, though this is on the weaker end of my opening range from UTG.

I hold Ace-Five offsuit on the Button facing a standard 2.5bb raise from Claude in the UTG position. While 4-handed play widens ranges, A5o remains a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by an UTG opening range (better Aces, pairs). Calling is unattractive as it invites the blinds to enter and leaves me with a hand that plays poorly post-flop. A 3-bet bluff is an option due to the Ace blocker, but unnecessary given my comfortable chip lead and the risk of bloating a pot against a solid stack. The most prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude opened UTG to 250 (2.5x). I have A♣ T♦ in the small blind — a playable hand but often dominated by stronger Aces and vulnerable out of position. The call is cheap relative to my 16,325 stack (200 more to see the flop, 250 total committed), and keeps the pot manageable OOP against a likely strong UTG range. I don’t want to 3-bet light here and face a 4-bet or play a big pot OOP with A‑10o. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to a large 3‑bet, and proceed postflop for value or fold depending on flop texture and opponent aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with 4-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Claude raised from UTG and Minni called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 4:1 odds to call the extra 150 chips, 4-2 offsuit performs very poorly in multi-way pots and is extremely difficult to play out of position. Given that I want to preserve my stack for better spots in this tournament, folding is the most prudent play.

I have A♣T♦ on Tc Jd Ts — trips (three tens) with an ace kicker. This is an extremely strong hand on a static board: few hands beat me right now (main threats are pocket Jacks giving a full house or the last Ten making quads on a later street), and there are essentially no straight/flush draws to be afraid of. Claude opened UTG so his range contains many hands that will call a flop bet (overpairs, broadways, single J hands, some A‑X and occasional T combos). Best play is to get value now and deny a free card that could pair a J/T on later streets, so I will bet for value and to build the pot. I size to ~2/3 pot (400) to charge weaker pairs and broadways to call but still keep worse hands in. If Claude raises big I will strongly suspect a full house and re‑evaluate (likely fold to an overcommitting raise); if he just calls I will continue extracting on safe turns.

I have K2 suited but completely missed this Tc-Jd-Ts board. I have no pair, just king high with only one backdoor diamond. Minni's donk bet of 400 into 600 suggests they have something - could be a ten, jack, or pocket pair. With no equity besides a weak backdoor flush draw and no showdown value, continuing here is unprofitable.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with pocket Sevens (7s 7c). This is a strong starting hand in a short-handed game, suitable for opening the pot to steal the blinds or play for set value. With a deep stack of over 200 big blinds, I want to build the pot. My standard opening size throughout the tournament has been 2.5x (250 chips), which balances well with my bluffs and stronger value hands. Therefore, I will raise to 250.

6♣4♥ is a weak, poorly-connected offsuit hand and is heavily dominated by a UTG open in a 4-handed table. Although I'm on the button (position is good), calling 250 into a 400 pot with this rag will put me in tough postflop spots and has little equity or implied odds; a 3-bet bluff is also risky with two players behind who can wake up with strong hands or 4‑bet. Preserve the deep stack for better spots — fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 3c, which is a very weak hand. Pro has raised from UTG (in a 4-handed game), which typically indicates a range much stronger than my holdings. Calling out of position with such a weak hand is a negative expected value play. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Facing a min-raise from UTG (Pro) with 7d5d suited in the BB. Getting ~2.67:1 pot odds (150 to win 400). Suited connectors have decent playability - can flop flush draws, straight draws, or two pair. The price is cheap enough to see a flop, though I'm out of position against what's typically a strong UTG range. Call and play fit-or-fold post-flop.

Flopped bottom pair with 5d on a A-Q-5 board. Pro opened UTG which is a strong range that connects well with this board (AK, AQ, AJ, KK, QQ all likely). My pair of 5s with a 7 kicker is very weak here - I'm behind to any Ace, Queen, or overpair. Best to check and see what Pro does. If they c-bet, I'll likely have to fold as I have minimal equity against their range.

I hold pocket sevens on an A-Q-5 rainbow board. As the pre-flop aggressor from early position, this board texture heavily favors my perceived range (containing strong Aces, sets, and broadways). However, my specific hand (77) is currently weak relative to the board; it is well behind any Ace or Queen that Claude might have in his defending range. C-betting would essentially turn my hand into a bluff, as worse hands (lower pairs) might fold, but better hands (Ax, Qx) will certainly call. Since the board is dry and static, there are few draws to protect against. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, potentially induce bluffs on later streets from missed hands, or get to a cheap showdown against lower pocket pairs. I decide to check behind to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

Pro checked back the flop on A-Q-5, showing weakness. With an UTG range, they likely would have bet AK, AQ, or big pairs. Their check suggests a medium pocket pair or weak hand. Turn 8c changes nothing. I have bottom pair with weak kicker - some showdown value but can't stand much action. Checking to see a free river is optimal here.

The turn brings the 8c, making the board 5s Qd Ah 8c. Claude checks again. My hand (77) remains a marginal showdown hand. The 8c is a brick for most draws but connects with some of Claude's defending range (like 87s, 98s). Betting here accomplishes little: worse hands like lower pocket pairs or air will fold, while better hands like any 8x, Qx, or Ax will call. By checking, I keep the pot small, maintain my showdown value against his missed hands, and see a free river card. I am essentially pot-controlling with a mid-strength hand on a board that favors the defender's range more than mine if I show weakness, but checking back flop signaled I likely don't have a strong Ace. I will check back to realize equity.

Pro checked back flop and turn on A-Q-5-8 board after UTG open. Two checks indicate weakness - likely a medium pocket pair (99-JJ) or missed hand (KJ, KT). My bottom pair of 5s has some showdown value. Betting for value is risky as I only get called by better. Checking to showdown is optimal - I might win against their missed hands but can't extract value from worse.

I hold a pair of Sevens on a board of 5s-Qd-Ah-8c-2h. The action has been checked through on the flop and turn. My hand (77) is ahead of Claude's potential lower pairs (like 66, 44, 33) and any 5x holdings (since 77 > 55), as well as all unpaired hands (King-high, etc.). However, if I bet, I likely fold out all the hands I beat while only getting called by hands that beat me (like an 8x, Qx, or weak Ax that trapped). Claude's triple check indicates significant weakness, likely a low pair or missed connectors. Since there is little value to be extracted from worse hands and no reason to turn my hand into a bluff (as I have decent showdown value against his weak range), checking back to realize my equity and see the showdown is the optimal play.

UTG with K♣5♦ (offsuit) in a 4‑handed game — first to act and out of position versus the button, SB and BB. This hand is weak and frequently dominated by other Kx hands; it has poor postflop playability. Calling 100 to play OOP multiway is a negative‑EV line, and opening/raising with K5o is too loose — it will often run into 3‑bets or better holdings. I’ll fold, preserve my deep 167BB stack, and wait for a much stronger spot to exploit position or isolated heads‑up situations.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit (J2o). This is a very weak starting hand. Although it is folded to me, and I could theoretically attempt a steal, my opponents in the blinds (Claude and Pro) have shown themselves to be active and willing to defend. With a stack of 65 big blinds, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with such a low-equity hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up against the big blind with Q3o. This is a marginal hand but playable in position vs just the BB. With only 50 more to call into a 150 pot, I'm getting 3:1 odds. Completing is reasonable to see a cheap flop rather than folding equity or bloating the pot with a weak holding against Pro's large stack.

I am in the Big Blind with J3o. The action folded to the Small Blind who completed (limped). I have the option to check or raise. With a weak off-suit hand like J3, there is little value in raising to build a pot, and isolation raises are risky if the opponent is trapping or sticky. Checking allows me to see a free flop in position. I will check and evaluate the board texture.

Flopped top pair with Q3 on Qd5s4c. Heads up against Pro who just checked preflop from BB, so his range is wide. My queen is likely good here. Should bet for value and protection - a small bet around 1/3 pot to get called by worse hands and deny equity to draws/overcards.

In a limped blind-versus-blind pot, I hold Jack-high (J3o) on a Q-5-4 rainbow board. I missed the flop completely with no straight or flush draws. My opponent, acting from the Small Blind, leads out for half the pot. Given my hand has very low equity and virtually no playability on future streets, and I have no invested interest beyond my checked big blind, there is no strategic value in floating or raising. Folding is the correct decision to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with 4h 3h. While these are suited connectors, they are at the very bottom of the rank and generally unsuitable for an opening raise from this position, especially given that I am the shortest stack at the table. To maintain my stack for higher equity opportunities in the remaining 43 hands, folding is the most disciplined play here.

I have 3d5d on the button with only the blinds left to act. This is a marginal hand, but being on the button gives me excellent position. 35s has some playability with straight and flush potential. With a healthy stack of 136bb, I can make a standard steal attempt here - if I get 3-bet I can fold easily.

I am in the Small Blind with K6o, facing a standard open raise from the Button (Claude). While I have the chip lead and the raise size is small, K6o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. It is often dominated by better Kings in the opponent's range, and realizing equity will be difficult. I have better spots to play given my stack advantage and do not need to defend this marginal holding. Folding is the prudent long-term play here.

Claude (button) opened to 250. I’m in the big blind with Qc8d — a marginal but defendable hand vs a button open. Stacks are deep (~166BB), so I don’t want to 3‑bet out of position with a hand that’s often dominated. Calling to see a flop is standard: it costs me 150 more (250 total) to realize equity, keep the pot controllable, and play postflop. Plan: proceed cautiously out of position — continue with top pair or strong draws, concede to heavy aggression on unfavorable boards.

Check. I have Qc8d on a 9d–Ad–Js flop — no pair, only a backdoor diamond and very little showdown value. Claude opened from the button preflop, so his range contains a lot of Ax and Jx combos that beat me. Out of position with deep stacks I want pot control and to avoid building a pot with a hand that is usually behind. Checking keeps the pot small, lets me fold to a c‑bet, and allows me to re‑evaluate on the turn if I pick up equity or get a very cheap card.

I flopped a flush draw with 3d5d on 9d Ad Js board. I have 9 outs to the flush. Minni checked to me, so I should c-bet as a semi-bluff. Standard sizing around 1/3-1/2 pot works well - I can continue building the pot when I hit, and fold equity helps when I miss. Betting 275.

Fold. I’m out of position with Qc8d on A♦–9♦–J♠: no pair, only a runner‑runner diamond backdoor (very low probability) and almost no showdown value vs a button open/c‑bet range that contains lots of Ax and Jx combos that dominate me. Calling 275 into a 825 pot (to win ~1100) requires ~25% equity; my realistic equity vs Claude’s continuing range is well below that. A check‑raise bluff here is too risky OOP with deep stacks (he can call or 3‑bet with many continuing hands). I’ll preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.
8c4s is a very weak hand - offsuit, disconnected, no high cards. From UTG position, I need to play tight. This is an easy fold.

Current hand is J9o on the Button in a 4-handed game. While the Button is a strong position to steal the blinds from, J9o is a marginal hand that often suffers from reverse implied odds, especially against a large stack in the Small Blind (Minni) who has shown aggression, and a short stack in the Big Blind (Flash) who might shove. With a comfortable chip lead, there is no need to force action with weak-marginal holdings. Folding preserves chips for higher equity spots.

Heads-up in the small blind with K♠ T♦ — this is a standard open-raise spot. I don't want to limp OOP; KTo is strong enough heads-up to take the initiative. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) to deny correct pot odds to speculative hands, thin the field, and build fold equity against Flash (who has ~64BB). If called I’ll c-bet favorable textures and play cautiously out of position; if faced with a large 3-bet I’ll fold KTo. Raise to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Five offsuit. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Although I have position on Minni post-flop, Q5o is a very weak hand that does not play well even with the positional advantage. Minni has been quite active, but a 3x raise usually warrants a stronger defense. Given that I am the shortest stack at the table, I should avoid marginal spots and preserve my chips for better opportunities.

Playing 4-handed, UTG acts as the Cutoff. While Q9s is a reasonable hand to open from the Cutoff in many 4-handed games, the player on the Button (Minni) has been quite aggressive recently (raising frequently). Opening a marginal hand like Q9s from UTG risks getting 3-bet or playing a bloated pot out of position against an aggressive opponent with a hand that often suffers from kicker trouble (dominated by AQ, KQ, QJ, etc.). Given my comfortable chip lead, there is no need to force marginal spots. Folding is the low-variance play to preserve the stack.

I have 3♦4♦ on the button with deep stacks (≈165bb). In a four-handed game this is a playable, speculative suited connector with excellent positional and implied-odds value against the blinds. Open-raising is standard here to (1) steal the blinds, (2) take the initiative postflop, and (3) deny comfortable odds to the small blind. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) — big enough to generate fold equity and keep worse hands in, but not so big that I never realize equity if called. Plan: c-bet and apply pressure on flops that favour my range (low, connected, or with diamond backdoors); if I face a large 3-bet (size that commits a lot of stack) or an isolated shove from the SB/BB I’ll fold; I’ll consider calling a small 3-bet (600–900) given deep stacks to realize straight/flush equity.

I am in the Small Blind with Ks7s, which is a decent suited king. Minni has opened to 3 big blinds from the Button. While my hand has some playability, 3-betting against a deep-stacked aggressive player like Minni is risky, and calling out of position is often suboptimal. However, with the suited connectivity and king high, I have enough equity to see a flop. Folding is too tight, and raising might bloat a pot out of position. I will call to see if I can hit the board or a flush draw.

J7o is a marginal hand, but I'm getting 3.5:1 pot odds (200 to win 700) in a multiway pot. With position on Flash and the opportunity to see a flop cheaply, this is a reasonable call. If I miss the flop, I can easily fold to aggression.

On a board of 8c Ts Qh, I have missed completely with Ks7s, holding only King-high and a backdoor spade draw. This board is very coordinated and likely connects well with the ranges of both the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) and the Big Blind (Claude), who often defend with connectors and broadway cards. As the first to act, leading into two opponents on this texture is ill-advised without a strong hand or a significant draw. I will check to see how the other players react to the board. If there is significant betting, I will likely fold as I have very little equity.

I have J7o on a Q-T-8 board with a gutshot straight draw (need a 9 for Q-J-T-9-8). No pair, weak draw. Flash checked, and Minni (the preflop aggressor) is behind me. With only 4 outs and no made hand, checking is the best option to see a free card. No reason to bluff into the preflop raiser multiway.

I opened preflop from the button with 3♦4♦ and both blinds called. The flop (8♣ 10♠ Q♥) is a dry, rainbow texture that missed my hand but is one where the preflop raiser can credibly represent top pair/overcards. Both opponents checked to me, so a small continuation bet maximizes fold equity while minimizing commitment with air. I’ll make a 1/3-pot c-bet (300) to take the initiative: it folds out many unpaired hands and weaker holdings, keeps weaker pairs guessing, and keeps the pot manageable if called. Plan: fold to a strong raise, and if called proceed cautiously on the turn (check blanks, continue with appropriate double-barrel on favorable runouts).

The flop 8c-Ts-Qh is highly coordinated and likely connects well with both the pre-flop aggressor's range (Minni) and the Big Blind's defending range (Claude). With only King-high and no direct straight or flush draws, I have extremely low equity in this three-way pot. Calling a bet out of position here would be a mistake, especially with another player still to act behind me. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I have J7o with a gutshot straight draw (need a 9 for Q-J-T-9-8). Getting 4:1 pot odds (300 to win 1200) but a gutshot is roughly 11:1 against. No implied odds guarantee since Minni could have a strong hand. With only 4 outs and no pair, this is a fold. The pot odds don't justify calling with just a gutshot, especially against an aggressive player like Minni who's been betting strong throughout.

UTG with 8♣9♥ (offsuit connector) in a 4-handed game — marginal but playable as an open when ranges widen. Table open sizing has commonly been ~300, so I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) to take the initiative, avoid giving the big blind a cheap price, and keep pot control. Plan: c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously — fold to large 3-bets since 8-9o is often dominated, consider calling only small 3-bets depending on opponent and pot odds.

I am on the button with Ace-Deuce suited (Ah 2h), which is a strong speculative hand in a 4-handed game. Minni opened for 300 from UTG. While a UTG raise generally shows strength, in a short-handed game, ranges are wider. I have a stack of 6125 chips, giving me plenty of playability. Calling here allows me to see a flop in position, keeping the pot manageable while retaining the potential to hit a nut flush draw or a straight. Folding is too tight given the hand's potential and my position, and a 3-bet might unnecessarily bloat the pot against a larger stack early in the hand.

AJo is a strong hand in the small blind. Minni raised from UTG and Flash called. While I could 3-bet to isolate, AJo plays reasonably well multiway and I'm out of position. Calling 250 more to see a flop with two high cards that can make top pair strong kicker is the right play here. A 3-bet risks getting 4-bet by Minni's UTG range which could have me dominated.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s 6d. Minni raised to 300 (3bb) from UTG, and there are two callers (Flash on Button, Claude in Small Blind). The pot is currently 1000 chips, and I need to call 200 to see the flop. This gives me excellent pot odds of 5:1 (requiring ~16.7% equity). Although 56o is a weak hand and I will be out of position in a 4-way pot, the price is incredibly cheap, and my hand has some connectivity for straights. In previous hands (e.g., Hand #4 with Q2o, Hand #102 with J6o), I have defended the Big Blind with weak holdings given good prices. With my deep stack (22,950), investing 200 chips is a low-risk play with potential upside if I flop well.

Flopped board is Q-Q-3 and I have AJo - complete miss. I have ace high with no draw. Checking is mandatory here - I can't value bet, and bluffing into 3 opponents on a paired board is terrible. I'll check and likely fold to any bet.

I defended the Big Blind with 5s 6d getting excellent odds in a 4-way pot. The flop comes Q-Q-3, which completely misses my hand. I have no pair and no meaningful draw. With three opponents remaining, including the pre-flop raiser, it is extremely likely someone has a Queen or a better pair. I have no reason to put chips in the pot. My plan is to check and fold to any aggression.

I opened to 300 with 8♣9♥ and got three callers — the flop is 3♥ Q♣ Q♠. I have missed completely (only a useless backdoor club possibility with 8♣) and this paired board heavily favors calling ranges (Qx, pocket pairs). With multiple opponents out of position, a continuation bet has poor fold equity and would bloat a pot I have almost no equity in. I’ll check for pot control: plan to fold to a large bet, consider calling a small c-bet to float only if it’s heads-up and the price is right, and if checked through take a free turn to reassess/bluff on very favorable cards.

On a board of 3h Qc Qs, I have Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. After the pre-flop raiser (Minni) and both blinds check, the most logical play is to check behind. Betting into three opponents on a paired board is risky, as someone could easily be slow-playing a Queen or have a pocket pair. Checking allows me to preserve my stack and potentially realize my equity or showdown value on later streets if the action remains passive.

Turn Ks gives me a gutshot to Broadway (need T), but I still have no made hand - just ace high. Everyone checked the flop which suggests weakness, but betting into 3 opponents with just a 4-outer gutshot is too thin. The Ks could have connected with someone's range. I'll check and see a free river if possible, or fold to significant action.

I hold 5s 6d on a board of 3h Qc Qs Ks. My hand has zero showdown value (5-high) and no draws. The flop checked through, but the King on the turn significantly favors my opponents' ranges (UTG raiser and callers often have Kx broadway hands like AK, KQ, KJ). Bluffing into three opponents on this texture is extremely low-percentage and risky given I have no equity. The correct play is to check and fold to any bet.

I missed completely (8♣9♥) — board is 3♥ Q♣ Q♠ K♠. This is a four‑way pot and opponents’ calling ranges are heavily weighted toward Qx/pocket pairs and Kx (KQ two‑pair) so I have almost no showdown equity and very little fold equity. Betting here would bloat the pot with air and be easily called or raised. I check for pot control and take a free river; plan is to fold to any meaningful bet on the turn and only continue to the river if checked through or faced with a tiny bet that offers correct pot odds to call.

We are in a four-way pot on a board of 3h-Qc-Qs-Ks. Everyone checked the flop, and everyone has now checked to me on the turn. The absence of a bet on the flop suggests it is unlikely anyone has a Queen, and the fact that the pre-flop raiser (Minni) and the blinds checked the King on the turn suggests they likely don't have a King either. However, with three opponents, a bluff is risky. Ace-high has significant showdown value in this spot, and by checking, I can see a free river card and keep the pot small while maintaining my stack size in this high-roller tournament.

River 9d completes the board: 3h Qc Qs Ks 9d. I have ace-high with AJo. Everyone checked flop and turn, indicating likely weakness across the board - probably no one has a Queen or King. My ace-high could actually be good at showdown. Betting for thin value is risky since anyone calling likely beats me, and I could get check-raised. The optimal play is to check and see if ace-high takes it down. If someone bets, I'll fold since my hand can't beat much that would bet for value.

I hold 5s 6d on a board of 3h Qc Qs Ks 9d. The action has checked through on both the flop and turn in this 4-way pot. I have absolutely no showdown value with 5-high. While the passive action suggests weakness, bluffing into three opponents on the river is risky; one of them likely holds Ace-high, a small pocket pair, or a weak pair (like a 3 or 9) that has enough showdown value to call a bluff, or could even be trapping with a Queen or King. Given the multi-way nature and my lack of blockers, the most prudent play is to give up and preserve my stack.

I improved to a pair of 9s on the river (8♣9♥ → pair of 9s), but this is a four-way hand on a very Q/K‑heavy board (3♥ Q♣ Q♠ K♠ 9♦). Opponents’ calling ranges from the flop are weighted toward Qx/trips, Kx and other made hands that crush my second‑pair. Everyone checked down — I have almost no thin value to extract and very little fold equity to bluff. Betting risks being called or raised by better hands; checking takes the free showdown and preserves stack. I’ll check.

The board is 3h-Qc-Qs-Ks-9d. Every player, including the pre-flop raiser Minni, has checked the flop, turn, and river. This passive behavior across all streets strongly suggests that no one has a Queen (which would have made trips) or likely even a King. While any pocket pair or a King in the hole would beat my Ace-high (which currently plays as a pair of Queens from the board with Ace-King-9 kickers), Ace-high holds significant showdown value in a four-way checked-down pot. Betting as a bluff is unnecessary and risky, as a small pocket pair or a weak King might still call. Checking allows me to reach showdown for free and potentially win against other non-paired hands or missed draws.

I am holding 3c 5d UTG in a 4-handed game. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands and does not warrant a raise or a call, especially from early position with the smallest stack at the table (though still 58 BBs deep). I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

Pocket 7s on the button is a solid hand. With Flash folding UTG and only the blinds left to act, I have excellent position. I should raise to try to take down the blinds or play postflop in position. Standard raise to 250.

I am in the Small Blind with J6s facing a Button raise from Claude to 250. Although the price to call is attractive (200 into a pot of 400), playing J6s out of position against a competent opener and with the Big Blind still to act is a losing strategy long-term. J6s is easily dominated and difficult to play post-flop, especially with deep stacks (130bb+ effective). A fold is the prudent and disciplined decision here to preserve my stack lead.

Claude opened to 250 from the button; I’m in the big blind (100 already posted) and need to call 150 more. Pot is 400 and stacks are deep (Minni ~17,975 vs Claude ~13,100). Q5o is marginal but heads-up vs a button open I should defend fairly wide. Folding is too tight given the pot odds; 3-betting OOP with Q5o is unnecessary and cedes initiative or bloats the pot with a weak hand. Calling keeps the pot under control, lets me realize equity and see a flop to make a postflop decision based on texture and Claude’s tendencies. I will call to see the flop.

I have Q♣?wait Q♥5♣ on T♦9♥2♣ — I completely missed the board. With one heart in hand and only one on the flop a flush is impossible; my only realistic outs are pairing the Q or the 5 (~6 outs, ~25% to improve by river). Claude opened wide from the button and will c-bet frequently, but this is a very dry board and I want to keep the pot small with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot and see how he reacts: call small continuation bets (roughly ≤ ~1/2 pot) to realize equity, fold to large/committed barrels. If he checks back I’ll take a free turn card and re-evaluate.

I have pocket 7s on a T-9-2 rainbow board. Minni checked to me. I have an underpair but it's still a decent holding. The board is relatively dry with no flush draws. I should c-bet for value and protection - my 77 beats any ace-high or lower pocket pairs, and I want to charge draws like J8 or QJ. A ~50% pot bet of around 275 is appropriate.

Claude opened wide from the button and fires a half‑pot c‑bet (275). I hold Q♥5♣ on T♦9♥2♣ — a marginal hand with ~6 outs (~24% to hit by the river). Facing a ~1/2 pot bet from a frequent c‑bettor, I want to realize equity and keep the pot reasonable; folding is too tight and a raise would be trying to build a pot with only drawing/overcard equity. I’ll call to see a turn and re‑evaluate (plan to fold to heavy turn aggression that shuts down my outs). Commit additional 275 (total committed this hand = 375).

Board: T♦ 9♥ 2♣ 2♦, my hand Q♥5♣. I have ~6 clean outs (three Qs, three 5s) but no current showdown value. Pot is 1,100 and stacks are deep. Claude opened pre and c-bet the flop, but on this paired turn I want to control the pot — bluffing or betting OOP with just overcard/draw equity is spotty and vulnerable to a check‑raise or call from better pairs. Checking keeps the pot small and lets me realize equity on the river. Plan: check and take a free card; if Claude bets the river I’ll evaluate bet size and his tendencies (call small bets that offer correct pot odds if I have a chance to hit, fold to large river aggression unless I pair).

Turn brought a deuce, pairing the board. My 77 is still an underpair to T and 9. Minni called my flop c-bet, indicating some connection to the board - likely a T, 9, or a draw. The paired board doesn't really help me. Checking here for pot control makes sense - if I bet and get called/raised, I'm likely behind. I can potentially catch a showdown cheaply.

River: board Td 9h 2c 2d 3h, I hold Qh5c — no pair, no straight or flush, just Q-high. Pot is 1,100 and Claude opened wide pre and c-bet the flop then checked the paired turn. That line is consistent with a wide, often missed range or medium pair pot‑controlling; either way I have almost no showdown value and almost no fold equity as a river bluffer. Betting here would be a thin bluff that will be called by any T/9/2/3 or many hands that have some showdown value, and I don’t block many of his value combos. Best play is to keep the pot small and check for showdown. If Claude bets a very small probe I’ll consider a call only with a strong read that he’s bluffing; otherwise I’ll fold to meaningful river aggression. Therefore: check.

River completes with 3h. Board is Td-9h-2c-2d-3h. I have 77, an underpair to both the T and 9. Minni has checked all three streets after calling my flop c-bet. This passive line suggests showdown value - likely a T, 9, or possibly a small pair. Betting here accomplishes little: worse hands won't call, and better hands won't fold. My 77 has some showdown value and might beat missed draws or smaller pairs. Check back for a free showdown.
KJo is a solid opening hand from UTG. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to build the pot and take initiative.

I am holding Queen-Deuce offsuit on the button. Claude has opened with a raise from UTG to 250. This is a very weak hand that is easily dominated by an UTG opening range (which typically includes strong Queens, Aces, and pairs). Despite being the chip leader, there is no strategic value in playing this hand against an early position raiser. The correct play is to fold and preserve chips.

2♦8♦ is a very weak hand. Claude opened to 250 (2.5x) from early position with both the button and the big blind yet to act. From the small blind I’d be out of position postflop and likely in a multiway pot — calling 200 more to see a flop with this garbage is negative EV. A light 3‑bet bluff is risky here (can be squeezed or 4‑bet) and unnecessary given my healthy stack. Preserve chips for better spots and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 6s. Claude has raised to 250 from the UTG position (4-handed). To call, I need to commit 150 more into a pot of 400, giving me roughly 2.6:1 odds. While the price is relatively cheap, 96o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against an active opener like Claude who often follows up on post-flop streets. Given that I have a mid-sized stack and we are at hand 112 of 150, I prefer to wait for better opportunities rather than defending with a marginal hand that is likely dominated or will be difficult to play on most board textures.

I am in the UTG position (4-handed) with 7d 9h, a weak off-suit one-gapper. With 4 players left, UTG is effectively the Cutoff, but 97o is still well below the standard opening range. I have a strong chip lead (22,700) and do not need to take unnecessary risks with trash hands out of position. Folding is the disciplined and correct play here.

I'm on the button with 4h6s — a weak hand but excellent positional leverage against only the blinds. With deep stacks (175bb) and just the SB/BB to beat, an open-raise is the most profitable line to pick up the blinds and avoid a limp-induced multiway pot. I choose a standard button open to 300 (3x BB) — big enough to generate fold equity versus the blinds but small enough not to overcommit a marginal hand. Postflop plan: if I take it down preflop great; if called, proceed cautiously — c-bet small on dry flops, fold to sizable aggression when I haven't connected, and fold to a 3-bet unless I get good implied odds or a strong draw.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd 3s. Minni, who is the chip leader among the active players on this street, has raised to 3BB from the Button. My hand is very weak and does not play well out of position against a Button range. Given the stage of the tournament and my stack size of 56.75BB, there is no reason to defend or contest this pot with such a sub-marginal hand. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I have pocket Aces - the best starting hand in poker. Minni raised from the button to 300. This is a clear 3-bet for value. I want to build a pot and get more money in while I have the best hand. A standard 3-bet size would be around 3x their raise, so 900-1000.

I opened to 300 on the button with 4♥6♠ as a steal. SB folded and Claude 3‑bet to 950, leaving me to call 650 more (pot 1,300). With stacks deep (172bb vs 131bb) Claude’s sizing represents a fairly strong/polarized 3‑bet range and my hand has very poor equity and playability (unsuited, no real backdoor prospects). Calling requires ~33% equity to break even but I’m well below that versus a typical 3‑bet range; 4‑betting as a bluff to 1,600 is thin and easy to fold to further pressure. Preserve the stack for better spots — fold.

UTG with Q♦5♥ — a weak offsuit queen in early position. Out of position versus the button and blinds and easily dominated by typical calling/raising ranges. Calling 100 or opening (which would invite 3-bets) is unnecessary with deep stacks and 4 players remaining. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

I am on the button with 7s 4d, which is a very weak hand. Although I am in a late position and the action has folded to me, this hand does not have enough equity to justify a raise or a call, especially with two large stacks remaining in the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to increase my stack.
Heads up vs the BB with J4s after folds. This is a standard steal spot from SB - suited jack is easily good enough to raise here. Opening to 250 to pressure Pro and take down the blinds.

In a Blind vs Blind scenario, Claude (SB) raises to 2.5BB. I hold Ace-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a very strong hand relative to a typical SB opening range. While a 3-bet for value is a viable option to extract value from worse Ax or Kx hands, calling is often superior with deep stacks (140BB effective). Calling keeps Claude's range wide, including all his bluffs, and allows me to utilize my positional advantage post-flop. I avoid isolating myself against his top-tier hands and keep the pot manageable while holding a hand that dominates his range. I will call the raise.

Flopped top pair with Js on a 3s8dJd board. Good spot to c-bet for value and protection against the diamond draw. Pro called from BB so could have a wide range. Betting ~50% pot to get value from worse pairs and charge draws.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind and am facing a continuation bet of 250 (half-pot) from the Small Blind on a [3s 8d Jd] board. This is a standard Blind vs Blind scenario where ranges are wide. I have two clean overcards and reasonable showdown value with Ace-high. My opponent, Claude, has shown a tendency to fold to raises on the flop in previous hands, but AQ is strong enough to call and keep his bluffs in the hand rather than isolating myself against his value range. I have position and can re-evaluate on the turn, potentially realizing my equity if I hit an Ace or Queen, or if the action checks down. Folding is too weak given my hand strength relative to a wide SB range.

Turn brought 3d making three diamonds on board. I have top pair weak kicker but the flush completing card is concerning. Pro called flop, could have diamond draw that just got there, or middle pair, or better Jx. Checking for pot control makes sense - I can call a reasonable bet or catch bluffs, and avoid bloating pot when behind to flushes.

On the turn, the board is [3s 8d Jd 3d]. The 3d pairs the board and brings a third diamond, making flushes and trips possible. Claude checked to me. I hold AcQh, giving me Ace-high with a decent kicker. On this wet and paired board, my hand has some showdown value against missed straight draws or lower high cards, but unlikely has value to bet for extraction. Betting would likely fold out hands I beat and get called by hands that beat me (flushes, trips, pairs). Checking back allows me to realize my equity, control the pot size, and potentially get to showdown cheaply or catch an Ace/Queen on the river.

River 6s completes no draws. I have top pair weak kicker. Pro checked turn behind me, suggesting they likely don't have a flush. However, with J4s I have minimal showdown value improvement from betting - better hands call, worse hands fold. Check to realize my equity at showdown.

On the river, the board is [3s 8d Jd 3d 6s]. The pot is 1000. Claude checked to me again. My hand is AcQh (Ace-high). Claude's line of betting the flop and checking the turn and river suggests he likely has either a weak pair that is trying to get to showdown cheaply (like 8x, 6x, or a low pocket pair), a missed draw (like T9), or potentially a weaker Ace/King high. My Ace-high has decent showdown value in this Blind vs Blind spot. Betting would likely only fold out hands I currently beat (like King-high or missed draws) and get called by better hands (any pair). There is little value in bluffing as I don't credibly represent the flush after checking the turn, and I have enough showdown value to win against his bluffs. Therefore, checking back to realize my equity is the best play.

In a 4-handed game, the UTG position is effectively the Cutoff. While the range for opening expands as the table shrinks, A6o is a marginal hand that often struggles post-flop, especially out of position. Given that I am currently the shortest stack at the table (approx. 56BB) and the remaining players (Claude and Minni) have shown significant aggression in recent hands, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a stronger opening opportunity rather than risking chips in a potentially dominated spot.

On the button with 9s7c, Flash folded UTG. This is a good stealing spot with only the blinds left. While 97o isn't strong, position advantage and fold equity make a standard button raise profitable here. I'll open to 2.5x.

I hold Ten-Five offsuit in the Small Blind, facing a standard 2.5x raise from the Button (Claude). This is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position. Although Claude is active, T5o is not strong enough to defend, and the Big Blind is still to act behind me. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

3♥8♣ is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with almost no postflop playability or blocker value. Claude opened to 250 from the button and the SB folded, so I’m heads-up out of position — calling 150 into a 400 pot (~27% immediate pot odds) is tempting but this hand has poor equity vs a standard BTN opening range and will struggle to realize equity OOP. A 3‑bet bluff here is thin: stacks are deep enough that Claude will often continue or 4‑bet, and 3‑8 has no good blockers. Best to conserve chips and fold — pick better spots to defend or attack.
QTo from UTG in a 4-handed game is a reasonable open. Standard 2.5x raise to 250.

I am on the Button with King-Five offsuit. The UTG player (Claude) has raised to 250. Although Claude has been very active recently, opening the last several hands, K5o is a very weak hand that performs poorly against a UTG opening range, often being dominated by better Kings. While I have the chip lead and position, there is no need to enter a pot with a marginal holding against an early position raise. The disciplined play is to fold.

Facing an UTG open to 250 from the small blind with J♥T♦. With deep stacks (~170bb) JTo is worth defending — it has decent equity and post‑flop playability, especially if the BB (Flash) comes along. A 3‑bet here is marginal: re‑raising to the min (400) would put me out of position with a hand that struggles OOP and would likely get called by better hands, so fold equity is limited. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity and play the flop carefully: fold to strong continuation pressure when I miss, but float/continue with pair or strong draws. I will call (put 250 total into the pot).

I am in the Big Blind holding 6d 4d. Claude (UTG) raised to 250 and Minni (Small Blind) called. The pot is currently 600 chips, and it costs me only 150 more to call. This gives me pot odds of 4:1. 6d 4d is a decent speculative suited hand that plays well in multi-way pots. Given the excellent price and my stack size of over 50 big blinds, calling to see a flop is the most profitable play here.

Check. The flop K♥ 9♣ 9♠ is very unfriendly to J♥T♦ — I have no pair and almost no realizable equity. Claude opened UTG so his range contains many Kx and pocket pairs that beat me, and I’m out of position. I want to keep the pot small and avoid bluffing OOP on a paired board that typically hits the opener’s range. If opponents check through I can see a free turn; if I face a small C‑bet I can call once for backdoor options (and because I block some heart combos), but I’ll fold to significant pressure. Betting as a bluff here is high-variance and likely to get called by better hands, so checking is the prudent line.

On a flop of Kh 9c 9s, I have zero current value and very little potential equity with 6d 4d. The board is paired and contains a King, which heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's (Claude's) range. Since Minni has already checked, the standard play is to check to the aggressor and see how the action unfolds. I have no reason to lead out or bluff into two opponents on this texture.

I have QTo on K99 board. Both opponents checked to me. I have minimal showdown value - just overcards to the 9s and a gutshot. A small c-bet (around 1/3 pot) makes sense here to pick up the pot when they show weakness by checking. If called, I can shut down or continue with the gutshot draw.

Claude opened UTG so his range is heavy for Kx and pocket pairs; this K♥ 9♣ 9♠ flop misses J♥T♦ — I have no pair and no realistic flush (I only have one heart). My only useful equity is a Q (4 outs) which completes a 9‑10‑J‑Q‑K straight (≈8.5% to hit on the turn, ≈16% by the river). Claude's bet is small (~¼ pot), so calling 250 into a ~1000 pot gives ~16.7% break‑even — the call is marginal but justified given the straight outs plus occasional showdown value if J/T pair up and the cheap price to see the turn. Plan: call the flop to see a turn; if I don't improve and Claude puts meaningful pressure on the turn I'll fold. I will not raise or lead — raising OOP on a paired board with no made hand is too risky.

On a flop of Kh 9c 9s, I have no pair, no draw, and zero equity with 6d 4d. The board favors the UTG raiser, Claude, who has followed up with a continuation bet. Furthermore, Minni has called that bet, which indicates some level of strength (likely a King, a nine, or a pocket pair). With two opponents showing interest in the pot and my hand having no potential to improve to the best hand, folding is the only correct decision.

I already called the small flop bet to see the turn. The 6♣ on the turn doesn’t help J♥T♦ — I have no made hand and only a single-card straight draw (four Q outs, ~8–9% to hit the river) plus weak pair-outs that rarely win vs Claude’s likely range. Claude checked the turn, which looks like pot control or marginal strength (Kx, underpair or a pocket pair). I’m out of position and the pot (1,250) is still small relative to stacks, so betting as an OOP bluff is high-variance and will often be called by better hands. I’ll check to control the pot and realize my equity. Plan: fold to meaningful river pressure if I miss; if a Queen hits I can extract value or call a reasonable bet depending on sizing.

Minni called my flop c-bet on K99, showing some strength. The 6c turn doesn't help me - I still have just queen high with a gutshot to the straight (need a J). Betting again into a caller without any real equity is burning money. Check back and see a free river where I could hit Q, T, or J to improve.

Check. I have Jh Td and missed — no pair and my only realistic straight outs (queens) didn’t hit. I called the small flop bet to see a turn and checked the 6♣ to control the pot; the river 8♣ doesn’t improve me and actually brings three clubs on board (9♣–6♣–8♣), which gives a flush to anyone holding two clubs. Claude’s line (UTG open, small flop bet, then check) looks like Kx or a pocket pair — hands that will call a river bluff too often. A bluff here would need an unrealistically high fold frequency to be +EV (e.g., a half‑pot bluff would need >30% folds). Out of position with deep stacks, I should avoid bloating the pot on a very thin bluff. I’ll check and plan to fold if Claude fires a value-sized river bet.

River brings 8c completing three clubs. I have Qh Tc - just queen high with no pair and no flush. Minni called my flop c-bet showing interest, then checked turn and river. My hand has zero showdown value, but bluffing here is risky - Minni has shown they'll call with medium strength hands. The flush completing could be a scare card, but if Minni had a flush draw that hit they'd likely bet. If they have a king, pocket pair, or trips they're not folding. Check back and accept the loss rather than burn chips on a bluff against a calling station.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with J7s. While my chip lead allows for some aggression, J7s is a marginal hand that is typically outside the standard opening range for this position, even short-handed. It is easily dominated by hands that call (like KJ, QJ, AJ) and plays poorly out of position if called by the Button. With an aggressive player on the Button (Minni), opening this hand risks getting 3-bet or floated in a spot where I have a weak range. Preserving my chip lead by avoiding marginal spots out of position is prudent.

On the button with K♦J♦ — a strong, suited broadway in position with deep stacks (166 BB). There's only the blinds in front (to call 100), so this is an ideal spot to seize initiative, apply pressure, and build a pot with a hand that plays well postflop. I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x BB (300) to get fold equity against the blinds and isolate heads-up or take the lead in position. If I face a 3-bet I can re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent tendencies, but 300 is the right size to balance value and fold equity here.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-5 offsuit, a very weak hand. The Button (Minni) has opened with a standard raise to 300. Calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand against a player who likely has a wider but stronger range than my current hand. Given my stack size of approximately 54 BB and the low equity of 7-5 offsuit, there is no reason to defend or bluff here. Folding is the standard and most profitable play.

J4o is a weak holding in the BB facing a button raise. I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450), but I'll be out of position postflop with a hand that doesn't play well. Minni has been active, but J4o is too weak to defend profitably here - I'll often make weak top pairs or nothing at all. Fold and wait for a better spot.

UTG in a 4-handed game with 6♦3♥ — a very weak, offsuit, non-connected hand with poor playability and high chance of being dominated. I’d be out of position against the Button, SB and BB and could easily face raises or pressure postflop with virtually no fold equity or equity to improve. Calling 100 or attempting a limp invites aggression; a raise is unwarranted with this rag. With a deep stack but no reason to spew chips, I fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 7-6 offsuit. While this is a marginal hand that can occasionally be used for a steal in a 4-handed game, the players in the blinds, Claude and Pro, have significantly larger stacks and have shown a willingness to defend and play aggressively pre-flop. Given the current tournament situation and my position as the shortest stack at this small table, I will choose to fold this weak hand and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

I have 6h3c, a weak offsuit hand in the SB heads-up against the BB. Only need to call 50 more to see a flop. With such a weak hand, folding is fine but completing for 50 to see a cheap flop is reasonable given pot odds (50 to win 150). Raising as a steal with this garbage isn't worth it against Pro's big stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd Ts. The Small Blind (Claude) has limped in. My hand is a decent unsuited connector that plays well in position post-flop. While raising is an option to attack the limp, JTo is often dominated by limp-calling ranges (KJ, QJ, AJ) or limp-traps. Checking allows me to see a free flop with position and keeps the pot manageable, leveraging my skill advantage and stack depth post-flop without inflating the pot pre-flop with a marginal holding.
Completely whiffed this flop with 6h3c - no pair, no draw. K-high board favors BB's range. No reason to bluff into Pro here with zero equity. Check and give up.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd Ts in a limped pot against the Small Blind. The flop is 8s Kd 2d. The opponent checked to me. My hand has very little immediate value (Jack high) and only backdoor straight/flush possibilities. The board texture connects reasonably well with a limping range (which often contains random Kings or small pairs). Betting here would likely only fold out worse hands (like Queen high) and get called by better hands (Kings, pairs, flush draws). Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hit a pair on the turn, or re-evaluate on later streets with position. There is no need to inflate the pot with a marginal holding.

I have 6h3c on 8s Kd 2d 7s. Still no pair, no meaningful draw. Both players checked the flop, indicating weak hands. The 7s doesn't help me at all. I could try a small bluff but Pro has a huge stack and this limped pot isn't worth risking chips. Better to check and see a free river - I could spike a 6 or 3 for a small pair.

I hold JdTs on a board of 8s Kd 2d 7s. The pot is 200. Action has gone check-check on the flop, and the Small Blind checks to me again on the turn. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9) and Jack-high. My opponent's line suggests weakness, but in a limped Blind-vs-Blind pot, he could easily have a weak pair (like a 2, 7, or 8) or Ace-high that he is looking to check-call. Betting here has little utility as I unlikely fold out better hands (pairs) and I bloat the pot with a weak holding. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, trying to hit my straight on the river, or get to showdown cheaply if my Jack-high happens to be good against missed draws.

River with 6h3c on 8s Kd 2d 7s Ks - complete air. Both players checked through flop and turn indicating weak hands. The pot is tiny (200) and bluffing here makes no sense - Pro likely has some showdown value given his checking pattern, and I have zero fold equity against anything that beats 6-high. Just check and accept losing at showdown.

On the river, the board is 8s Kd 2d 7s Ks. The pot is very small (200) after checking down all streets. My hand is Jack-high. The opponent (Claude) in the Small Blind has shown weakness by checking three streets, but his range likely consists of weak showdown value (small pairs like 2x, 7x, 8x or Ace-high) that might call a bluff in such a small pot, or missed draws that I beat or chop with. Representing the King or flush is possible, but the reward (200 chips) does not justify the risk of being looked up by a curiosity call. Checking back is the lowest variance play to get to showdown cheaply.

I am holding T5o in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is a very weak hand with poor playability and is easily dominated. Given my current stack size and the strength of the opponents, there is no reason to enter the pot with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

On the button with 8Ts after UTG fold. This is a decent spot to steal with a marginal hand. T8o has some playability and I have position on both blinds. Standard button raise to 250 to put pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 92o, a very weak hand. The Button (Claude) has raised to 2.5 BB. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call, playing 92o out of position is historically a losing play due to poor equity realization and domination issues. There is no strategic merit to calling or 3-betting this hand against a standard button open.

I’m on the big blind with 3h9h — a weak, low suited hand. Claude opened to 250; the pot is 400 and it costs me 150 to call, so I need ~27.3% equity to break even (150/(400+150)). Versus a button opening range this hand is often dominated and plays very poorly out of position postflop. Raising is not reasonable with this holding and calling would put me in difficult spots against an aggressive opponent. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left to play, I’ll fold and wait for a better, positional spot.

A4o from UTG in a 4-handed game. This is a borderline hand but playable with a raise at this stack depth. With 30 hands left, I should continue my aggressive opening strategy with suited/ace hands. Standard 2.5x open.

I am on the Button with Q7 offsuit. The UTG player (Claude) has raised to 250. Although Claude has been observed opening very wide ranges from UTG (showing hands like T5o, Q2o, K5o recently), Q7o is still a structurally poor hand to play against a raise. It is often dominated, plays poorly post-flop, and calling invites the blinds into the pot. A 3-bet bluff is risky without better blockers or playability. Given my healthy stack size, I can afford to wait for a better spot to exploit Claude's loose opening frequencies. The prudent play is to fold.

Claude opened UTG to 250. I’m in the small blind with 6♦2♠ — one of the weakest hands in poker. To call I must put in 200 into a 400 pot (pot will be 600), so I’m getting ~33% pot odds, but versus an UTG opening range my raw equity with 6-2o is negligible (single digits to low teens) and I’ll be out of position postflop. 3-betting light is an option in some spots, but not with this hand — it’s not profitable and risks a larger commit against a likely stronger range. With deep stacks but no reason to gamble with garbage, the +EV play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs7s. Claude raised to 250 from UTG (which is the cutoff in this 4-handed game). Pro and Minni have folded. The pot is currently 400 and it costs me 150 more to call, giving me pot odds of 2.66:1. Given that Claude has been opening frequently and I have a suited hand with some high-card value, calling to see a flop is a standard defensive play. My stack is the shortest at the table, but I still have over 50 big blinds, so I don't need to jam or fold here.

On the flop of 9h 2d Ts, I have overcards with Qs and some backdoor potential with a spade draw. The board texture is somewhat connected but doesn't hit a standard UTG opening range extremely hard, though it does contain a Ten. I am out of position against Claude. Given that the pot is small and I have no direct pair or strong draw, checking is the most standard and prudent play to see how Claude reacts and potentially see a free turn or evaluate a bet.

Flop is 9h 2d Ts - I completely missed with A4o. Flash checked to me. I have the initiative and a dry-ish board. A small c-bet (about 1/3 pot) can take this down against Flash's weaker range that might have called with suited connectors or smaller pairs. If I get raised, I can easily fold.

On a flop of 9h-2d-Ts, I have missed completely with Qs7s, holding only Queen-high and a backdoor spade flush draw. Claude, who opened from UTG/CO in this 4-handed game, has bet 175 into a pot of 550. While the price is relatively cheap (requiring roughly 19.4% equity), I am out of position against an aggressive opponent. Without a direct draw or a pair, and given my status as the short stack (though still comfortable at 50BB), I prefer to fold and preserve chips rather than peel with a marginal holding that will likely face another bet on the turn.

Playing 4-handed, I am in the UTG (Cutoff) position with A7o. While this hand is sometimes opened from the Cutoff, the effective stacks are very deep (I have 222BB, Minni on the Button has 166BB, Flash in SB has 50BB). In deep-stacked play, unsuited weak Aces suffer significantly from reverse implied odds; if I hit an Ace, I am often dominated by better Aces (A8-AK) that opponents will play, making it difficult to extract value or comfortably call down. Minni on the Button has been active, and opening invites a 3-bet or a flat call where I would be out of position with a marginal hand. Given my chip lead, I prefer to avoid high-variance, low-edge spots. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I'm on the button with K♥T♦ — a playable, positionally advantaged hand. The pot is small (150) and the blinds are the only opponents left; this is a spot to apply pressure and take the initiative. Calling 100 is passive and invites multiway action; a min-raise to 200 is too small and gives good pot odds to callers/3-bettors. I want a clean heads-up or controllable pot with position, so I open to ~3x the BB (300 total).
