This sizing targets folding weak holdings and small pairs, isolates the short-stacked SB (Flash ~50BB) and gives me favorable postflop options. If Flash shoves or Claude 3-bets big, I will respect the strength and fold KTo to large, polarized 3-bets; versus a small 3-bet I may call and play postflop carefully.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Six offsuit, a weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. Calling out of position with a marginal hand is generally unprofitable, especially with a active player in the Big Blind yet to act. Given my stack size is the shortest at the table (approximately 50 BBs), I should prioritize fold equity and stronger starting hands. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chips for a more advantageous situation.

9h5s is a weak hand. Facing a button min-raise from Minni who has been active, I'm getting 200 to win 450 (about 2.25:1). Even with decent pot odds, 95o plays poorly out of position and struggles to realize equity. Fold is the disciplined play here.

Pocket tens (Ts Td) UTG in a 4-player game is a strong open-hand — I want initiative and to narrow the field. Table tendencies show standard opens around 3x BB; with a deep 16,750 stack I can comfortably build a pot and play postflop. I’ll open to 3x the BB (300) to fold out weaker hands and isolate heads-up where TT plays well. If I face a small-to-medium 3-bet I’ll usually call and evaluate the flop; versus a large 3-bet I can consider a 4-bet or fold depending on the opponent and sizing. Standard, balanced play here is to raise to 300.

I have Kd Qd on the button, which is a very strong hand in a 4-handed game. Minni has been opening frequently from UTG (which is effectively the cutoff in this setup). By 3-betting to 900, I take the initiative, put pressure on the opener, and leverage my positional advantage while potentially thinning the field by forcing the blinds to fold marginal hands.
J8o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet. I'm out of position in the small blind with a hand that doesn't play well against Flash's 3-bet range. Easy fold - no need to defend here with this holding.

I hold 4d 2s in the Big Blind, which is a very weak hand (garbage). The action involves an UTG open to 300 followed by a 3-bet from the Button to 900. Calling 800 more chips into a 3-bet pot with 42 offsuit is mathematically unsound as I likely have very low equity against their ranges and will be out of position. The correct play is to fold immediately to preserve my stack.

Flash (BTN) 3-bet to 900 after my UTG open to 300. TT is a strong hand here and I don’t want to be left OOP playing a bloated pot vs an aggressive, relatively short 41bb opponent. I want to isolate and seize initiative — a 4-bet sized to ~3x the 3-bet is standard and gives good fold equity vs a wide 3-bet range while still being comfortable to call a shove. If Flash folds I take down the pot; if he calls we proceed postflop with a strong pocket pair; if he jams I’ll call because TT has good equity vs his likely shove range. Raise to 2700 total.

I have Kd Qd on the button and 3-bet Minni's opening raise to 900 to take initiative. However, Minni has responded with a large 4-bet to 2,700. This is a significant show of strength in a 4-max game. While Kd Qd is strong, it is often dominated by a typical 4-betting range (JJ+, AK). My remaining stack is 4,175, and calling 1,800 more would leave me with only 1,475 in a pot of over 5,500, making me effectively pot-committed on the flop with very little fold equity. Given the 4-bet size and the fact that I still have over 30 big blinds if I fold, I choose to let go of this hand rather than risk my tournament life as a likely underdog.

In a 4-handed game, Queen-Six offsuit is a very weak hand to open from under-the-gun (UTG). My stack is currently the shortest at the table with approximately 42 big blinds. Opening this hand would likely lead to difficult post-flop situations against larger stacks who have position on me. There is no reason to risk chips with a sub-standard hand when I can wait for a better spot to utilize my stack effectively. Folding is the most prudent professional play here.

6c8c suited connector on the button is a solid open. I'm in position with decent playability - can make straights and flushes. Flash folded so I just need to get through the blinds. Standard 2.5x open.

I hold King-Ten offsuit (KTo) in the Small Blind facing a 2.5x raise from Claude on the Button. While KTo has decent high-card value, it plays poorly out of position, especially when unsuited. It is often dominated by the opener's value range (KQ, KJ, AK, AT) and faces the threat of a squeeze from the Big Blind. Although I am the chip leader and have a deep stack, playing marginal offsuit broadways out of position is a spot with high reverse implied odds. Defending here is generally slightly negative EV or at best break-even compared to folding. I previously defended KJo in a similar spot (Hand #62), but KTo is weaker. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my chip lead and avoid unnecessary variance.

Claude opened to 250 from the button; I’m in the big blind with Qc9d. To call 150 into the 400 pot requires ~27% equity — Q9o has playable equity heads-up against a likely wide BTN open and the stacks are very deep (17700), giving good implied odds. Q9o is not a strong 3-bet candidate here (out of position postflop and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand), so I’ll defend by calling and evaluate the flop. Plan: proceed for value if I pair the queen or pick up strong draws; otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to heavy aggression.

I defended preflop with Qc9d against a wide BTN open because the pot odds and implied odds justify calling. The flop (2d-7c-4s) is extremely dry: I have no pair and no realistic draw (only one diamond on board, so a flush is impossible). Out of position I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand or bluff-raise; checking keeps the pot small and lets Claude take the initiative. Plan: check and evaluate — call small c-bets to realize equity and fold to heavy aggression; only start building the pot if I pair the Queen or pick up a genuine draw.

Dry board (2d7c4s) that doesn't connect well with BB calling ranges. I have a gutshot (need a 5) and backdoor flush draw in clubs. Standard c-bet spot as preflop aggressor with position. Small sizing around 1/3 pot should get folds often enough to be profitable.

Call. Claude's flop bet (175 into a 725 pot) is very small on an extremely dry 2d-7c-4s board. I have no pair or meaningful draws, but the pot odds/implied odds justify seeing another card — I need ~19% equity to break even (175 to win ~900) and Q9o has roughly mid-20s% to pair by the river. I'm out of position and don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal bluff/raise, so I’ll call to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn: fold to heavy turn aggression, or start extracting value if I pair the Q or 9.

I turned top pair (Qc9d on 2d-7c-4s-Qs) heads-up versus a wide BTN open. Claude c‑bet very small on the flop, so his range is wide (many Kx/Qx/mid pairs and bluffs). This is a textbook spot to start extracting value — I beat worse queens, many KQ/QJ combos and a bunch of pocket pairs that will call a reasonable bet, and I also want to deny a free river card to any backdoor combos. I’ll lead for about half the pot to get called by worse while keeping the pot manageable if he shows strength. If Claude check‑raises large I’ll heavily discount my hand (likely set/two‑pair or a better queen) and re‑evaluate (fold to an extreme shove, call a small/standard check‑raise depending on sizing).

Minni check-called flop then leads 450 on the Queen turn. This line often indicates they either hit the Queen or had a medium hand they're now protecting. I only have a gutshot (4 outs, ~8% equity) with 8-high - no showdown value. Pot odds are 3:1 requiring ~25% equity. Clear fold.
5h9h from UTG is too weak to open. Suited one-gapper has some playability but not enough equity or strength to open from early position in a 4-handed game. Folding is standard here.

I am on the Button with Q7s, which is a standard opening hand in a 4-handed game due to position and suited potential. I am the chip leader, allowing me to apply pressure. The UTG player folded. I will raise to 2.5BB (250) to attempt to steal the blinds. If the aggressive Small Blind (Minni) 3-bets or the Short Stack Big Blind (Flash) shoves, I can easily fold without significant damage to my stack.

Facing a button open to 250 from the small blind with K♦6♠ (stack 18,225). In 3-handed play the button's opening range is wide, and Kx has decent defensive value, so I prefer to defend and see a flop rather than fold a marginal but live hand. I don't want to 3-bet out of position with a weak kicker — that would commit chips and create a tough post-flop spot, plus the big blind (short ~4k) could shove and isolate. I'll call the additional 200 (250 total committed including my SB) to see a flop, and plan to proceed cautiously: fold to substantial 3-bets/shoves, continue only with a King or strong draw, and control the pot OOP otherwise.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven of hearts, a decent suited gapper that plays well post-flop. The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5x, and the Small Blind (Minni) has called. I need to call 150 chips into a pot that will be 750, giving me 5:1 odds (requiring 16.7% equity). Given the price and the fact that I am closing the action pre-flop, this is a standard defend. While I am the shortest stack, I still have over 40 big blinds, so I have enough maneuverability to play post-flop rather than needing to jam or fold immediately.

Board is Q♠ 9♣ 5♠; I hold K♦6♠ — no pair, just king-high and a single spade (only a runner‑runner flush draw, ~4% to make). There are two opponents (Button opened, BB called), so c-betting OOP into multiple players is low EV: many hands in their ranges (Qx, 9x, spade draws, broadways) either already beat me or call/raise profitably. My preflop plan was to control the pot OOP with a marginal K and that still applies — I don’t want to bloat a multiway pot with no made hand. I’ll check to keep the pot small and see how my opponents act. If I face a small probe bet I may call to realize equity versus pure bluffs, but I will fold to significant pressure or an all‑in from the short stack unless I improve. If checked around I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and only invest further if I pair the King or pick up a workable draw.

I have a very weak hand (Ten-high) on a board of Queen-Nine-Five. I have no direct draws, only very thin backdoor possibilities. Since the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) is yet to act and the Small Blind (Minni) has already checked, checking is the most prudent play to see how the street develops without committing more chips with a marginal holding.

I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a weak kicker (7) in position in a 3-way pot. The board texture [9c, Qs, 5s] contains a flush draw and some straight connectivity, making it somewhat 'wet'. While checking back for pot control is a valid option with a weak kicker to avoid bloating the pot against better Queens, betting is preferred here to deny free equity to overcards (Kings, Aces) and to charge flush/straight draws and worse pairs (9x, 5x, pocket pairs). Given the stack depths and the fact that both opponents checked, a small c-bet (around 40% of the pot) serves to extract value from draws while keeping the price cheap enough to fold comfortably if check-raised by the aggressive small blind.

Board: Q♠ 9♣ 5♠; I hold K♦6♠ — no pair, only a very weak backdoor spade possibility. Pro (button) fires a small probe bet 325 into a 1075 pot. Folding would be too nitty given the cheap price and the c-bet size; raising OOP with K6 is a poor high-variance bluff and will often get me in trouble. Calling gives me correct pot odds (~19%) to continue vs a wide c-betting range, keeps the pot controlled OOP, and lets me fold to meaningful turn aggression unless I improve. If the short stack (Flash) shoves I’ll fold this hand (or fold to heavy action) unless I pick up a King or a real draw. Plan: call 325 now and re‑evaluate on the turn — look to fold to strong pressure, continue only with a pair or a credible draw, and avoid bloating the pot with K-high.

I have Ten-high on a Queen-Nine-Five board with no direct draws and no pairs. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has bet and the Small Blind (Minni) has called. Given that I am facing two opponents who have both shown interest in the pot and I have essentially no equity, folding is the only viable option to preserve my stack.

Board Q♠-9♣-5♠-A♣, I hold K♦6♠: no pair and no realistic draw. Pot is 1,400 while stacks (~17.7k vs 21.5k) are large relative to the pot — I don't want to bloat the pot OOP with nothing. Pro's preflop open + flop c‑bet is consistent with many Qx/9x/Ax combos that the A‑turn helps, so my fold equity is very low and a bluff is unlikely to succeed. Best line is pot control: check and fold to meaningful turn/river aggression, re‑evaluate only if I pick up a King or see a clear bluffing frequency from Pro.

On the turn, the Ace of Clubs arrives, making the board [9c, Qs, 5s, Ac]. This is a significant scare card. My hand (Top Pair, weak kicker) has downgraded to Second Pair. The Ace hits many hands in the Small Blind's flat-calling range (such as Ax suited, A9, A5, or floaty broadways like AJ/AT). Betting here achieves little: better hands (Ax, Qx with better kicker) will call or raise, and worse hands (9x, 5x, pocket pairs) will likely fold. While draws (flush draws, straight draws) might call, the risk of getting check-raised or facing a difficult river decision is high. Checking back is the standard play for pot control, allowing me to realize my equity and potentially catch a bluff on the river or get to showdown cheaply.

I check. I have K♦6♠ with no pair and no real draw on a Q♠-9♣-5♠-A♣-T♣ board. Pro’s line (preflop open, small flop c-bet, turn check) is consistent with both medium/strong value hands and some bluffs — I have very poor blockers and little fold equity on the river, so a bluff is low EV. Pot control is best: keep the pot 1,400 small and take it down if he checks; if he bets river I’ll fold to meaningful aggression.

I hold second pair (Queens) with a weak kicker on a board that has developed significantly: [9c, Qs, 5s, Ac, Tc]. The river brings a third club and completes several straight possibilities (KJ, J8). My opponent, Minni, has checked to me on the river after check-calling the flop and checking the turn. While she could have missed spade draws or a lower pair (like 9x) which I beat, her range also includes many hands that beat me, such as any Ace (Top Pair), better Queens, two pair (QT), straights, or flushes. Betting here achieves nothing: worse hands will fold, and better hands will call. I have sufficient showdown value to win against her bluffs or missed draws, but my hand is too weak to value bet. Checking back is the optimal play to realize my equity without risking more chips.

I am in UTG (first to act in a 4-handed game) with [7c 8h], an off-suit connector. While 4-handed play requires opening a wider range, 78o is generally below the threshold for a profitable open raise from the first position, even with my chip lead. The hand lacks flush potential and dominates very few hands that would call. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Button with 5♦6♦ — a playable suited connector in position. Only the blinds are left (SB short ~38bb, BB deep ~154bb), so opening is standard: we want to apply pressure, isolate the SB and play the hand in position. I’ll open-raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) — large enough to pick up many blind-only pots and still keep postflop playability. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet from Claude, call or proceed postflop versus a single call and c-bet favorable textures; if Flash (SB) shoves I’ll evaluate the shove size/reads but won’t auto-call large all-ins without equity confirmation.

I am currently the short stack with approximately 38.75 big blinds. In a 4-handed game, Ace-Deuce suited (A2s) is a strong speculative hand, especially from the Small Blind. Minni, on the Button, has been very active, and a raise to 300 is a standard opening. Calling here would put me out of position against both Minni and the Big Blind (Claude) and would allow Claude a cheap entry into the pot. Given the tournament progress (Hand 125 of 150), I need to look for opportunities to accumulate chips. 3-betting A2s is a classic move because the Ace blocks some of the opponent's strongest continuing ranges (like AA, AK, AQ) and the hand has excellent playability on many flops. I will raise to 1,000 to exert pressure on Minni and potentially win the pot pre-flop, or set up a post-flop situation where I have the initiative.

Q7o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet. Flash is short-stacked (2925) and committed over 1/3 of their stack with this 3-bet, indicating significant strength. Q7o plays poorly against a 3-betting range and has poor playability postflop. Easy fold.

I opened to 300 on the button with 5♦6♦ and Flash (SB, ~29bb) 3‑bet to 1000. Pot is 1,400 and I must call 700 more (total committed 1,000). With a call the pot becomes ~2,100, so I’m getting ~3:1 odds — I need ≈33% equity to break even. 5♦6♦s is a good in‑position, playable hand vs a likely wide/polar SB 3‑bet range (and it realizes equity well postflop: straights/flushes, good blockers and implied odds). 4‑betting or folding here are both suboptimal — 4‑betting is too spewy with a speculative hand, and folding surrenders profitable pot odds and position. I’ll call the 700 (total 1,000) to see a flop in position and then proceed based on texture/reads: continue vs favorable flops, fold to extreme aggression when I miss, and only call any shove if pot odds and his shove range justify it.

After 3-betting pre-flop, I find myself in a low SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) situation of less than 1. The flop (Kc-Tc-6h) contains two high cards that frequently hit a 3-bet calling range. While I represent strong hands like AK or KK, Minni's call pre-flop suggests she has a hand she's willing to play. If I continuation-bet here, I am essentially committing most of my remaining stack. By checking, I can gauge Minni's strength. If she checks back, I get a free turn to potentially hit an Ace or a spade draw; if she bets, I can decide whether to fold and preserve my remaining 19 big blinds for a more advantageous shove later, given the tournament is nearing its end.

I flopped a marginal but playable hand — middle pair (6♦) with 5♦6♦ on Kc Tc 6h. Flash (SB) 3‑bet preflop and is short (~29bb) and checked the flop, so his range is fairly wide/polarized (some K/T combos, pocket pairs, and lots of bluffs/draws). The board has two clubs, so there are real club draws that need to be charged and denied equity. With position and a pair I should continue — checking allows free cards that help his draws. I’ll c‑bet for value and protection: bet ~1/2 pot (1,000). That size extracts from worse pairs and Ax, charges club draws, and gives fold equity vs pure overcards. If he jams I’ll re‑evaluate — I’ll call only when his shove range is weak/draw heavy or the pot odds justify it; if he just calls I’ll play the turn cautiously (fold to big aggression if a club or a K/T pairs the board).

In this hand, I 3-bet pre-flop as a semi-bluff with As2s to exert pressure on the active button raiser. The flop of Kc-Tc-6h is quite unfavorable for my specific hand, as it lacks spades for a flush draw and contains two high cards that connect well with a 3-bet calling range (Kx, Tx, QJ, and club draws). After I checked to gauge Minni's strength, she bet 1,000 into a pot of approximately 2,100. Calling here would commit more than a third of my remaining stack with only Ace-high and very few ways to improve. Given that there are only 25 hands left in the tournament and I still have nearly 30 big blinds, it is more prudent to fold now and preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation rather than chasing a small chance of winning a high-variance pot.

4-handed table and I'm UTG with T9 suited hearts — a hand that plays very well short-handed and deep-stacked. Limping is too passive; I want the initiative and fold equity (button is short ~29BB and can be pressured). Standard 3x open here is appropriate: it keeps the pot build under control versus deep SB/BB, denies free cards, and lets me play postflop as the aggressor. I will raise to 300. Plan: if the short-stacked button jams I will strongly consider/call (T9s has reasonable equity vs typical shove ranges and pot odds are attractive); if Claude or Pro 3-bet large and non-committal I will fold and avoid marginal, out-of-position spots; if called I will c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously versus heavy resistance.

I am on the button with a stack of approximately 29BB. Minni, who has been opening very aggressively from all positions, has raised to 3x. A4o is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by her value range and plays poorly post-flop with a weak kicker. Given that I have the shortest stack at the table and both players in the blinds have massive stacks that could potentially squeeze, I will choose to fold and preserve my chips for a more advantageous situation or a better hand to commit with.
J8o is a weak hand facing an UTG open from Minni. Out of position with poor playability. Standard fold here - we don't have the odds or hand strength to continue profitably.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s5s, a suited connector, facing a 3x raise from Minni in UTG. We are very deep-stacked (over 180 BBs), which increases the value of suited connectors due to implied odds. Although I will be out of position, the price to call (200 chips) is small relative to the potential payout if I hit a strong draw or made hand. I am getting roughly 3.25:1 odds on a call. Folding is reasonable given the UTG range, but defending is standard with this hand class deep-stacked.

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw (5-6-7-8) on a board of Qc-7h-8c. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni from UTG), the standard play is to check to the raiser. This allows me to see how they proceed. If they bet, I have a decision between calling (getting good implied odds with the draw) or check-raising as a semi-bluff. Leading out (donk betting) is generally not optimal here as it denies me the information of their action and folds out their bluffs that I beat while getting called by better hands.

Flop: Qc 7h 8c and I hold Th9h — an open‑ended straight draw (J or 6 completes, 8 outs, ~31–32% to hit by river) with a backdoor heart possibility. I was the preflop aggressor and I have position on Pro (BB), who called preflop with a fairly wide range. This is a textbook semi‑bluff: I get fold equity against many Qx/overcards/small pairs and still have strong equity if called. Pot is 650; a bet of 225 (~35% pot) gives the caller break‑even equity of ~20.5% (225 / 1100), which is well below my equity. I want to apply pressure while keeping the pot controllable deep‑stacked. Plan: bet 225 as a c‑bet; if called I'll continue for value when I hit (J/6 or hearts) and often check‑behind blanks to control the pot; fold to a large turn raise unless I improve.

I hold 5s6s on a board of Qc-7h-8c, giving me an open-ended straight draw (outs: 4, 9). The pot is 875 and I need to call 225, offering me nearly 3.9:1 pot odds. With 8 outs, I have approximately 32% equity to make my straight by the river (about 17% on the next card), which easily justifies the call (requiring ~20% equity). The opponent's bet is small (about 1/3 pot), giving me a great price to see the turn. Raising is a viable semi-bluff but unnecessary given the excellent price to call.

On the Turn, the board is Qc 7h 8c Jc. The Jack of Clubs is a significant card: it completes the club flush draw and connects various straight possibilities. I still hold 5s 6s, giving me an open-ended straight draw (needing a 4 or 9), but my equity has degraded. A flush is now possible, and my straight outs might be tainted (if the 9 or 4 is a club) or beaten by higher straights (T9 makes a higher straight). Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) who likely has a strong range that connects well with this board (sets, flushes, top pair), leading out is not profitable. The standard play is to check to the raiser and evaluate their action. I hope to see a free river card.

On the turn I’ve just completed the straight. With Th9h on Qc‑7h‑8c‑Jc I have 8‑9‑10‑J‑Q (the best possible straight except for a club flush). The only realistic hands that now beat me are hands with two clubs (they already have a club flush). Pro called my flop c‑bet and checked the Jc — that line is consistent with many one‑pair hands, turned pairs, single‑club hands, or marginal draws rather than frequent slow‑played two‑club flushes. I should be extracting value and charging draws while keeping the sizing reasonable to avoid overcommitting to the rare club‑flush raiser. I will bet for value (~2/3 pot) to get calls from Qx, Jx, 10x/9x, one‑club combos and small pockets, and to deny free rivers. If I face a large check‑raise or shove I’ll give credit to the flush and fold; I’ll call modest resistance and re‑evaluate on the river if called.

I hold 5s6s on a board of Qc-7h-8c-Jc. The turn card Jc is very bad for my hand. It completes the flush draw (three clubs on board) and connects many higher straights (T9 is now the nuts). While I still have an open-ended straight draw (needing a 4 or 9), my outs are 'tainted'. If a club hits, I lose to a flush. If a 9 hits, I make the low end of a straight (5-6-7-8-9) while any Ten makes a higher straight (8-9-T-J-Q). If a 4 hits, I also have the low end if the opponent holds T9. Minni raised UTG and is double-barreling, representing a strong range that likely includes sets, flushes, or higher straights/draws. I need 28% equity to call (700 into 2500), but with potential reverse implied odds and only 1 river card to come, my effective equity is likely far lower than the raw probability of hitting my cards. Folding is the disciplined choice here.

I am UTG with Q5s and a stack of 2925 (roughly 29 BB). In a 4-max game, Q5s is generally too weak to open from the first position, especially since the remaining three players (Claude, Pro, and Minni) all have significantly larger stacks and have shown a willingness to be active. With only 23 hands remaining in the tournament, I need to look for better spots to commit my chips than a marginal suited hand out of position.

Pocket 6s on the button is a standard opening hand. Flash folded, so I only have the blinds to get through. I'll raise to 250 as a standard button open - I have position and a decent pocket pair that plays well post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Kd 7h facing a Button raise to 2.5bb from Claude. We are 4-handed. Flash (UTG) is the short stack with ~30bb, while I have ~223bb relative to the big blind? No, stacks are 2925 (Flash), 15100 (Claude), 19275 (Minni), 22300 (Pro). Blinds are 50/100. So Flash has ~29bb, Claude ~151bb, Minni ~193bb, Pro ~223bb. Everyone is deep except Flash who is getting shorter but not critical yet. Actually, checking the blinds, 50/100 means Flash has 29bb. With K7o, I am out of position against an aggressive opener. Given the stack depths and ICM implications of preserving my chip lead while a short stack exists (though not critically short), playing a marginal hand like K7o out of position is not ideal. A fold is the standard disciplined play here to avoid bleeding chips in a low-edge spot.

Heads-up from the big blind with A9o facing Claude's standard button open to 250. I only need 150 to call into a 400 pot — good pot odds and A9o is a clear defend in this spot. I prefer to keep the pot controlled and see a flop rather than make a light 3-bet; postflop I'll evaluate flop texture (look to c-bet/raise on Ace-high boards, pot-control or fold on dry boards when checked to, and proceed cautiously OOP vs heavy action). Call and play the flop.

I have A♥9♠ on 8♠ J♥ 9♥ — a pair of nines with the Ace blocker. This is a vulnerable, medium-strength hand out of position on a fairly wet, two‑heart/connected flop where Claude (BTN) will c‑bet a wide range. I want to keep the pot under control and let him c‑bet bluffs and weaker holdings so I can call and extract value rather than bloat the pot OOP. Checking induces his frequency of continuation bets and keeps options open: I’ll call small–medium c‑bets, fold to a large raise, and re‑evaluate on the turn (bet for value/raise if I improve, or pot‑control/fold if action looks strong). With pot 550 and a healthy stack, preserving fold equity and avoiding big confrontations with just second pair is the right line.

I have pocket 6s on a coordinated 8-J-9 board with two hearts. My pair is an underpair to the entire board with no real draws. This is a bad texture for my hand - there are straight draws, flush draws, and any J, 9, or 8 beats me. Minni called preflop and checked, but this board connects well with BB calling ranges. C-betting here with a weak underpair on such a wet board is unlikely to get better hands to fold and just bloats the pot when I'm behind. Checking back for pot control is the best play - I can see a cheap turn and reassess.

I have A♥9♠ on 8♠ J♥ 9♥ 3♦ — second pair with the best possible kicker but a vulnerable hand on a two‑heart, connected board. Claude opened BTN preflop and checked the flop, which is consistent with a wide checking range (weak Jx/9x, medium pairs, or air) or a slowplayed top pair; either way I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with just second pair. Betting here would commit me to larger action and allow Claude to raise with Jx or strong draws; checking preserves pot control, keeps worse hands and bluffs in his range, and lets me call reasonable river bets. Plan: check the turn, call small–medium bets on the river for value (and showdown) but fold to large pressure/raises that represent two‑pair or better. For now pot control is optimal — check.

Turn is a brick 3d. I still have an underpair (66) to the entire J-9-8 board. Minni checked again - could be weak or trapping. This board is too coordinated with potential straights and flush draws. Betting risks getting check-raised or called by better hands. Checking keeps the pot small and lets me see a free river card. Pot control is the right approach with this marginal holding.

Board: 8♠ J♥ 9♥ 3♦ 2♠. I hold A♥9♠ — second pair with the best kicker. Claude opened from the button and checked flop and turn, which is consistent with a wide, often-marginal checking range (air, missed overcards, small pairs, Jx or occasional slow-played strong hands). With the river checked to me a small thin value bet could get called by worse (missed overcards, weak pairs) but also runs the risk of being raised by hands that beat me (Jx, two-pair) — and I don’t want to commit chips with a marginal holding OOP. Pot is only 550 relative to our stacks, so the upside of betting is limited while the downside (facing a raise or calling down a better hand) is meaningful. I’ll take the low-variance line and check to showdown; if Claude bets I’ll call small/medium-sized bets for value but fold to large river aggression per my prior plan.

River is another brick (2s). I have pocket 6s which is an underpair to the J-9-8 board. Minni has checked all four streets. My hand has marginal showdown value - I beat missed draws and ace-high but lose to any pair of 8s or better. Betting here would be a mistake as I'd only get called by better hands. Checking to showdown gives me a chance to win against Minni's missed draws or weaker holdings.

A7o in UTG at a 4-handed table. This is a playable hand - Ace-high with reasonable kicker. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is appropriate. Not strong enough to raise larger, but definitely worth opening.

I am on the Button with Ah Js (AJo). The tournament is 4-handed, so the UTG player (Claude) is effectively opening from the Cutoff. Claude raises to 250 (2.5 BB). AJo is a strong hand that is well ahead of a typical 4-handed opening range. However, 3-betting often folds out the weaker hands in Claude's range (dominated Aces, weaker broadways) that I want to keep in, while isolating me against hands that dominate me (AQ, AK, big pairs). By flat calling, I utilize my position to navigate post-flop, keep the pot manageable, and allow Claude to continue with a wider range. My stack is deep (223 BB), allowing plenty of room for post-flop play.

Facing an UTG open to 250 with the button calling, A♣7♣ in the small blind is too good to fold but marginal for a 3-bet in this spot. Calling (putting a total of 250 in) keeps the pot controlled, takes advantage of the hand's nut-Ace and club-flush potential, and gives correct pot odds (200 into a 650 pot is cheap) to see a flop. A 3-bet would risk isolating myself against the button and invite a shove from the short-stacked big blind (2825), which I don't want OOP with A7s. Plan: call, evaluate the flop—bet/raise for value on Ace/club/strong texture, exercise pot control and fold to heavy aggression on dry, hostile boards.

I am holding 94o in the big blind. While the pot odds are mathematically enticing (calling 150 to win a total pot of 1000, roughly 6.6:1), the hand is exceptionally weak and plays poorly in a four-way pot, especially out of position against three opponents. With a stack of approximately 28 big blinds, I should prioritize preserving my chips for more favorable spots rather than calling with trash that is easily dominated and has very little equity realization post-flop.

Check. With A♣7♣ I have only an overcard + a backdoor club draw (needs runner‑runner) — no pair. The flop Q♣ J♥ T♦ is very connected and likely hits Claude’s UTG raising range and the button’s call range (broadways, two‑pairs/sets, or completed straights). OOP and in a multiway pot I want pot control and to realize equity cheaply: check to see how the raiser and caller proceed. Plan: call a small continuation bet to try to hit my Ace or a club, but fold to a large bet or any raise/multi‑street aggression.

Board is J-T-Q which is very connected. I have ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (need a King for the nut straight). This board texture hits many calling ranges - any broadway card connects here. With just A-high and a gutshot, a c-bet is risky as I'll often get called or raised by better hands. Better to check and take a free card to potentially hit my straight.

The flop falls Jack of Hearts, Ten of Diamonds, Queen of Clubs. This is an extremely wet, coordinated board that hits both the UTG opening range (AK, AQ, QQ, JJ, TT, KQ) and the SB calling range (KJ, QJ, QT, JT, K9) very hard. 

I hold Ace-Jack, giving me middle pair (Jacks) and an open-ended straight draw to the nuts (any King completes the A-K-Q-J-T straight). While I have decent equity, my hand is currently vulnerable and likely behind a Queen or better. 

Both opponents have checked to me. Claude checking as the pre-flop aggressor on this texture suggests he might be exercising pot control with a hand like AA/KK/AQ or trapping, but potentially giving up with underpairs. Betting here risks getting check-raised, which would force me to make a tough decision with a hand that has excellent draw potential but marginal current showdown value. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see a turn card, and potentially hit my straight or improve to two pair/trips while keeping the pot manageable. Position is a key advantage here; I will take the free card.

Check. I have A♣7♣ and after the turn (J♦) I’ve completely missed — the backdoor club draw is dead and I’m just Ace‑high. The board Q♣ J♥ T♦ J♦ is extremely connected and hits Claude’s UTG raising range and Pro’s calling range hard (Broadways, straights, two‑pair/set combos). I’m out of position and multiway, so pot control is the priority: keep the pot small and fold to meaningful aggression. If I face only a very small probe bet (a thin blocking bet under ~1/3 pot) I may consider a call to see the river, but I’ll fold to larger bets. If both opponents check to the river I’ll reevaluate — only a river Ace (or a surprisingly harmless card and a bluff opportunity) changes my plan.

Turn paired the board with Jd. I still have just A-high with a gutshot to the nut straight (need K). Board is now J-T-Q-J. Both opponents checked, which could indicate weakness or slowplaying trips. With only A-high and 4 outs to the nuts, I should check and take a free river card rather than bluff into a scary paired board.

I have improved to Trip Jacks (Ah Js) on a board of [Jh Td Qc Jd]. The turn card Jd pairs the board, bringing full house possibilities (QQ, TT, JT, QJ) and flush draws (diamonds), while straights (AK, K9) are also present. However, both opponents have checked to me on the turn after checking the flop. This passive play on such a dynamic board suggests they likely do not have the nuts (Straight/Boat) and are more likely holding marginal hands (Qx, Tx, underpairs) or draws. I need to bet to extract value from worse hands like AQ, KQ, or sticky pairs, and to deny equity to flush/straight draws. Checking back risks giving a free card on a board where many river cards could kill my action or beat me. A bet of 400 (roughly half pot) is substantial enough to charge draws but allows me to fold comfortably if I face a check-raise, which would represent a monster.

I have A♣7♣ and have missed every meaningful draw — the backdoor club is dead after the J♦ turn. This is a three‑way pot (UTG Claude + Button Pro) on a very connected board Q♣–J♥–T♦–J♦ that heavily favors UTG’s UTG raising range and the button’s calling/float range. Pro’s turn bet (400 into 1250) is small but is consistent with value (Jx, Qx, T combos, or straights) and there are many combos that completely crush my Ace‑high. I’d only be a marginal bluff‑catcher calling 400 (pot odds ≈24%) and with two opponents left my actual showdown equity is well below that. Raising OOP as a bluff into multiple players is reckless with no fold equity and no backdoor outs. Fold — preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Pro bet 400 into 850 on a paired J-T-Q-J board. I have A-high with a gutshot (need K for nut straight). Pot odds are ~24% (400 to win 1250), but my gutshot gives me only ~8.5% equity. A-high has almost no showdown value on this board texture. Pro's bet could be value from trips, two pair, or even a made straight. Without the right odds and with such weak equity, folding is correct.

I am holding 4d 2s in UTG, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (unsuited, unconnected, low ranks). With a healthy stack of 22,900 chips and the blinds at 50/100, there is absolutely no incentive to play this hand from early position. I have three opponents left to act, and opening here would simply be bleeding chips. I will fold immediately.

Button with A♠3♦ in a 4-handed spot — good opportunity to take the initiative. Table standard open is ~3x BB; raising to 300 (3x) gives me fold equity against the big blind, applies pressure to the short-stacked SB (Flash ~27.8BB) and lets me play postflop in position vs Claude. Size is big enough to punish limp/call but not so big that I overcommit. Plan: open to 300; if Claude 3-bets big (large sizing/900+), I’ll fold A3o; if Flash (SB) shoves his ~27BB I’ll evaluate based on his tendencies but will be inclined to call more often than fold because his shove range here should be wide. Postflop I’ll use position to continue with top-pairs/strong draws and pick good spots to fold marginal hands.

Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak hand to play from the Small Blind, especially against an open raise from the Button. My stack is relatively short (under 30 BBs), and I need to conserve chips for better spots rather than defending with junk out of position.

A5o in the BB facing a button raise to 300. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (need to call 200 into 450). Ace-high has reasonable equity against a button opening range, and the pot odds justify a call to see a flop. Not strong enough to 3-bet but too good to fold with this price.

Flop is Jh Tc Qc - a very connected board. I have A5o which gives me ace high and a gutshot straight draw (need a K for Broadway). This is a marginal hand on this texture. I should check - I don't have enough to bet for value, and checking allows me to see what Minni does. If checked through, I might improve; if bet into, I can decide based on sizing whether my gutshot odds justify a call.

Board Qc-Tc-Jh is very connected and heavily intersects the big blind’s calling range (Qx/Jx/Tx, Kx, suited connectors, club draws). With A♠3♦ I have only Ace-high and a tiny backdoor broadway draw (need a K — 4 outs, ~8.5% on the turn, ~16.5% by river). Versus a single caller who defended preflop, a continuation bet here would be a bluff-heavy line with limited fold equity and poor equity if called. In position I prefer pot control: check to see Claude’s action, call small single-barrels to realize my fold-to-bluff + improvement equity, and only invest more if I improve (K or A) or get a very strong read/sizing that indicates a bluff. Stack/pot context (stack 19,125 vs his 14,550, pot 650) makes preserving chips and letting him bluff or show weakness more profitable than barreling a marginal c-bet.

Turn brings 3c completing a 4-flush on board. I have Ah5s with no clubs - zero flush equity. My only draw is the gutshot to Broadway (need K, ~8%). Minni checked flop, which could mean they're trapping or have a weak hand. With 4 clubs out there, anyone with a single club has a flush. No reason to bet into this - checking keeps pot small and I can reevaluate on river.

I just paired my 3 on the turn (As3d → board Jh Tc Qc 3c). That’s an improvement, but it’s still a very marginal hand on a highly connected board: straights (K9, AK, 98, etc.) and a backdoor/now-made club flush (board has three clubs) are in Claude’s calling range. Claude has been passive (call preflop, check flop, check turn) so he can easily have a medium/strong made hand or a slow-played draw/flush. Betting here would be thin value and exposes me to check-raises and getting stacked when I’m behind; a bet will also fold out hands I currently beat and keep worse hands from bluffing rivers, so it’s not high EV. In position I prefer pot control with this marginal pair: check and see the river, then decide based on the card and Claude’s action. If I improve (two pair/trips) I’ll value bet; if the river is a blank and he bets small I’ll often call; if he puts in a large bet I’ll fold. Action: check to control the pot and realize equity from my pair while keeping future options flexible.

River is 2h on a 4-club board (Jh Tc Qc 3c 2h). I have Ah5s - ace high with no club. Both of us checked flop and turn showing weakness. My hand has no showdown value - any single club beats me, any pair beats me. Bluffing into a 4-flush board is risky as Minni likely has at least one club in their button opening range. Check and concede the pot if they have anything.

I have A♠3♦ on Jh Tc Qc 3c 2h. I improved to a single three on the turn, but this is an extremely wet, connected board with many hands that comfortably beat me: straights (AK, K9, 98, 89 etc.), two-pair/sets and club flushes (players holding two clubs). Claude’s line (call pre, check/call flop, check/call turn, check river) is passive and consistent with medium-to-strong made hands or a straightforward calling range that contains a lot of hands ahead of my pair. Betting for thin value is ill-advised — I’ll get called by better hands and often fold to a raise; bluffing is also poor because many of his holdings will just call. With position and a marginal showdown catcher, I should control the pot and see a showdown. Action: check now; plan to call a small river bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot) but fold to larger overbets.

Short-handed (4 players) and deep-stacked (~198 BB), so open-raising a wider UTG range is appropriate. Q♠T♣ is borderline but has good postflop playability (broadway/straight potential) and can be raised to seize initiative and isolate the short-stacked Button. Table tendencies show standard opens around 3x BB, so sizing to 300 is consistent and gives fold equity while keeping pot manageable. If we face a large 3-bet or shove from a deep stack, we’ll fold; versus a short-stack shove we’ll evaluate based on shove range and pot odds. Raise to 300 to open the action.

I am holding 8d 9c on the button with a stack of 2775 chips (approx. 28 BB). Minni has opened from UTG (effectively the cutoff in this 4-handed game) to 300. 98 offsuit is a weak hand to play against an opening raise, even with the benefit of position. Given my stack size relative to the table, I should look for better spots to commit chips or find a double-up opportunity. Folding here is the standard disciplined play.

8-9 offsuit is a marginal hand to defend from the small blind against an UTG raise. I'll be out of position postflop, and 89o doesn't have great implied odds without being suited. Minni has been active but UTG raises typically represent stronger ranges. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Current Hand 130/150. 4-handed play. Minni (UTG/CO) raises to 300. I am in the Big Blind with Ks8c. The pot is 450 and I need to call 200. This gives me pot odds of roughly 3.25 to 1 (needing ~30.7% equity). In a 4-handed game, the UTG open range is reasonably wide (effectively Cutoff), and K8o has sufficient equity (~40% against a standard wide 4-handed opening range) to defend. The cost is negligible relative to my stack (22,800). I am closing the action and can realize equity or fold on unfavorable flops.

I missed the flop completely with King-high and no immediate draws on a Jh 2s 7h board. The pre-flop aggressor (Minni) has range advantage here. I will check to the raiser and likely fold to a continuation bet.

I opened to 300 preflop and got one caller from the BB (heads-up). Flop J♥ 2♠ 7♥ is fairly dry and not very connected; I hold Q♠T♣ — no pair, just one overcard (Q) and only very remote backdoor outs. As the preflop aggressor I should typically continuation-bet to take advantage of fold equity and deny free cards. The BB (Pro) has been a fairly passive check/caller type, so I expect more calls than folds; therefore I prefer a modest c-bet to keep the pot manageable while still applying pressure. Betting 250 into the 650 pot (~38% pot) gives good fold equity and allows me to fold to a large raise (my hand has limited showdown value) or proceed cautiously if called and re-evaluate on the turn.

On a Flop of Jh 2s 7h, I hold Ks 8c (King-high). I have no pair and no immediate draw (not even a strong backdoor flush draw). The opponent (Minni) c-bets 250 into a pot of 650. While the price is cheap (calling 250 to win 900 requires ~22% equity), my hand likely has less equity than that against a standard range that includes top pairs, pocket pairs, and flush draws. Being out of position with a weak hand makes it difficult to realize any equity I might have. Folding is the standard and disciplined play to preserve my stack.

K7o is a weak hand to open from UTG, even in a 4-handed game. With a stack of approximately 28 big blinds, I have enough breathing room to wait for a better hand or a more advantageous position before committing chips to the pot. Folding is the most prudent strategy here to conserve my stack for a more promising opportunity in the remaining 20 hands.
KQs on the button is a premium hand. With Flash folding UTG, I have excellent position and should raise for value. Standard button open to 3x BB.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 6d, facing a raise to 300 from the Button (Claude). My hand is an unsuited connector, which has limited playability, especially out of position. Although the raise size is standard (3x), calling would require putting in 250 more chips into a pot of 450, offering roughly 1.8:1 odds immediately, but likely enticing the Big Blind to come along or squeeze. Playing 56o out of position against an aggressive Button opener is generally a negative expected value play due to the lack of flush potential and the difficulty of realizing equity. Given the stack depths (I have over 200 BBs), I can wait for better spots to contest pots. Folding is the standard and prudent decision here.

Pocket 4s in the big blind facing a button open to 300. Call costs 200 more (300 total) into a 450 pot — cheap relative to my deep 200bb stack and Claude's ~142bb. This is a classic set-mining spot: 11.8% to hit a set on the flop, ~19.1% by the river, and the implied odds with deep stacks make a flat call profitable more often than a small 3-bet. I’m out of position vs the button, so I don’t want to inflate the pot without a stronger hand. Plan: see the flop and proceed — if I hit a set or strong two pair I’ll build the pot; if I miss and face heavy aggression on bad boards I will often fold to conserve chips.

I called preflop as a set-mining call with pocket 4s and missed this flop (5♣ 6♠ 9♦). I only have two outs to a set (~8–9% to hit by river) and I'm out of position versus the preflop raiser (Claude). With deep stacks my priority is pot control and preserving equity — betting OOP here is a thin bluff that can be raised or called by many better hands. I'll check to see the turn for free. Plan: if I hit a 4 I will start building the pot; if Claude checks behind I’ll reevaluate on the turn; if he c-bets a small sizing (≈≤½ pot) I’ll usually call to see the turn but fold to large pressure on later streets when I miss.

I raised preflop with KQs and completely whiffed this low 5-6-9 rainbow flop. However, as the preflop aggressor in position, a continuation bet is standard here. The board is dry and doesn't connect well with typical BB calling ranges. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot should fold out hands like Ax high and weak floats while keeping my bluff cheap.

I called preflop as classic set-mining with pocket 4s and missed this flop (5♣6♠9♦). Claude's c-bet is small (225 into the ~650 pot, pot now 875), so calling 225 is a cheap way to see the turn with deep stacks. Purely as a draw I only have ~8–9% to hit a set by the river, but my pocket pair still has showdown value vs a lot of missed c-bet hands (broadways/overcards) and calling controls the pot out of position. Plan: check–call to realize equity, fold to large turn/river pressure if I don't improve, and start building the pot or raise if I hit a 4 or get a strong read of weakness.

Turn pairs the board and gives me trips (4h4c on 5c6s9d4d). This is a very strong hand versus Claude's wide BTN open/c-bet range, so I want to start building the pot and charge worse pairs and one-pair hands that called the flop (9x, 6x, 5x, Kx/Qx, medium pairs) while also denying a free river to potential diamond backdoor draws. There are some straights that now beat me (notably 7+8, 3+7, 2+3 combos) but those are relatively rare vs a button opener and I can react if I face big aggression. Claude's flop c-bet was small and he checked the turn (pot = 1100, action to me) — that's a good spot to bet for value rather than check and allow him to realize equity or check-raise me on the river.

Sizing: I choose about half the pot (~550). That's large enough to get called by worse made hands and charge draws, but small enough to keep worse hands and some bluffs in his calling range. Plan: if called, continue extracting on the river (size relative to river texture); if I face a big check-raise (pot-sized or shove) I will strongly consider that Claude has a straight or the one remaining 4 and will re-evaluate (likely fold to an unrealistic shove, call smaller raises and proceed carefully).

I have KQhh with no pair, no draw on a 5-6-9-4 board with two diamonds. Minni check-called flop then led turn for 550 - this is typically a strong line indicating they've connected with this low board (likely a 9, two pair, or straight draw that picked up equity with the 4). I have zero showdown value, no backdoor draws, and just 6 outs to top pair which may not even be good. Folding is clearly correct here.

J9o from UTG is a marginal hand. While it has some playability, opening from early position with this hand invites 3-bets from the remaining players. With healthy stacks behind me and only 18 hands left in the tournament, I should be more selective with my opens from UTG. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am on the Button with Ten-Five offsuit (T5o), which is a very weak hand. The player in the Big Blind (Flash) is short-stacked (around 26 BBs) and might be looking for spots to shove, making a steal attempt risky. The Small Blind (Minni) is deep-stacked and aggressive. Given the lack of strength in my hand and the risk of facing a re-raise from the short stack or a call from the aggressive big stack, the most prudent play is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm in the small blind with K♥2♥ and only the big blind (Flash) to act. This is a classic steal/isolate spot: K2s has useful blockers to kings and backdoor heart flush potential, and with the button folded I want to apply pressure. Flash is a relatively short stack (~26 BB) and will defend wide or shove, so a minimum raise to 200 (2x) buys fold equity while keeping my commitment reasonable. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I proceed OOP and play cautiously — c-bet favorable textures, give up to heavy resistance if I miss; if he 3-bets shove I’ll evaluate his range but am willing to call given the pot odds and his tendency to defend wide. Raising to 200 maximizes my fold equity and EV in this spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 6s, a decent suited gapper. The Small Blind (Minni) has raised to 200, which is a min-raise. I only need to call 100 more chips into a pot that will be 400 total. This gives me 4:1 pot odds, requiring only 20% equity to justify a call. 8s 6s typically has well over 35-40% equity against a wide Small Blind opening range. Given my relatively short stack of 2675 (26.75 BB), I want to see a flop and potentially realize my equity or outplay the opponent post-flop, rather than folding or turning this hand into a 3-bet bluff immediately.

The flop (4♠ 3♠ A♥) completely misses my K♥2♥ — I have no pair and only a very slim backdoor heart flush (need two hearts on turn+river). This texture heavily favors the big blind’s calling range (many A-x, small pairs, suited connectors and spade draws), and Flash is short (~25bb) and likely to either call down with A/x or shove to apply pressure. Betting here would either get called by hands that beat me or induce a shove that puts me to a difficult fold/call decision with very poor equity. With a big stack advantage I should avoid bloating the pot OOP with air. Checking controls the pot, allows me to fold to aggression, and keeps the option to bluff-turn when I pick up equity or see weakness. Plan: check — fold to a large shove or sizable bet; consider a small call only vs a tiny probe to realize equity and potentially fire a later barrel if I improve or sense weakness.

On the flop of 4s 3s Ah, I have flopped a flush draw with 8s 6s. The Small Blind (Minni) checked to me. While I could bet to try and take the pot down immediately, checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and see the turn. Given my relatively short stack (around 25 BB), I want to avoid getting check-raised off my draw. If a spade hits the turn, I'll be in a strong position to bet for value or semi-bluff if the board gets more coordinated. Checking back keeps the pot manageable and ensures I see at least one more card.

I missed the flop and the turn (4♠ 3♠ A♥ J♦) doesn’t improve K♥2♥ — my flush backdoors are dead after the non-heart turn and I only have king/two high with a single backdoor pair-out at the river. Flash (BB) is short (~25bb) and his continuing range includes lots of A-x, small pairs and spade draws; he’s also likely to shove or call wide. Betting OOP here will either get called by better hands or induce a shove that I have very poor equity against. I’ll control the pot and keep my options open: check the turn, fold to significant aggression, and only call a very small probe to realize my slim river equity. Re-evaluate on the river if I pair up or face clear weakness.

I currently hold a flush draw (9 outs) on an Ace-high board. The Small Blind (Minni) has checked to me for the second time, which strongly suggests she does not have an Ace or a strong pair. While I could attempt to bluff here, checking back is the most prudent play. It guarantees that I see the river card for free, allowing me to fully realize my equity without the risk of being check-raised off my draw. If I hit my spade on the river, I can value bet; if I miss, I can re-evaluate based on the river card and her action.

I missed this hand completely (K♥2♥) — river (4♠ 3♠ A♥ J♦ T♠) didn't pair me and actually brings a third spade to the board, making completed flushes possible. Flash (BB) defended wide preflop and is short (~25bb), so his continuing range includes A‑x, small pairs and spade draws that now often have made hands. Both players checked every street, so there's no clear weakness to exploit and any bet is likely to be called by better or induce a shove I cannot profitably call. Pot is only 400 — not worth bloating OOP with air. I’ll check to avoid losing more to a value hand or being forced into a costly call; plan to fold to meaningful river aggression and take the free showdown if checked to.

I have completed an 8-high flush on the river with the 10 of spades appearing (board: 4s-3s-Ah-Jd-Ts; my hand: 6s-8s). Minni raised from the Small Blind pre-flop but has checked on every street (flop, turn, and river). This passive line strongly suggests she does not have a strong hand like an Ace or a high flush, and she is likely in 'check-fold' or 'check-call' mode with a weak pair or high cards. I must bet for value to extract chips from her potential calling range (like Ax or Jx). A bet of 250 into the 400 pot is a solid value-targeting size.

The river bet is a small probe (250 into a 650 pot) but I have nothing — K♥2♥ is just king-high and the board now has three spades and an A. Flash defended wide preflop and checked down, so his continuing range heavily contains A‑x, small/mid pairs, and spade holdings that now often have made hands. Calling 250 to win a 1,150 total pot requires ~21.7% equity; versus Flash’s likely range my showdown equity with K‑2 is well below that. A raise is a thin, high-variance bluff that will fold out only the weakest holdings and will get called or shoved by hands that beat me (A‑x, pairs, flushes). I’m deep enough to preserve chips and don’t need to gamble here — fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG position with [8c 3h], which is an extremely weak hand (unsuited, unconnected, low ranks). With 8-3 offsuit, I have very little equity against any reasonable range and poor playability post-flop. Being first to act with three players behind me, including aggressive opponents and a short stack who might shove, the only strategically sound decision is to fold immediately to conserve chips.

I’m on the button with A♣2♦ — a hand with the nut-A potential and good positional leverage. The table’s standard open size has been around 300 from the button, and with 205bb effective for me I want to apply pressure on the blinds while keeping post‑flop maneuverability. Raising to 300 (≈3x BB) achieves fold equity vs the blinds (especially the short SB at ~29bb) and reduces multiway pots. If the SB shoves I’ll evaluate pot odds and the shove range (likely wide) — A2o has reasonable equity vs a wide short‑stack shove and is often a call; if the BB 3‑bets large I can fold without losing much. Raising to 300 is the standard, +EV aggressive play here.

I am in the Small Blind with K5o. Minni, who has been quite active and currently holds a massive stack, has raised to 3x from the Button. My stack is relatively short at 2925 (29 BBs). Calling K5o out of position against a big stack is generally a losing play, and 3-betting light here is risky given my stack size and the fact that Claude is still to act in the Big Blind. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for a better spot later in the tournament.

Q5 suited in the BB facing a standard open from Minni. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). Suited queen has decent playability with flush potential. Minni has been opening frequently. This is a reasonable defend spot closing the action.

Flop is 6h9cKc. I have Qh5h - very weak holding on this board. No pair, no flush draw (board has clubs, I have hearts), only a backdoor heart flush draw. The king on board hits Minni's opening range frequently. With essentially no equity and being out of position, checking is clearly the right play. If Minni bets, I'll need to fold as I have almost no equity to continue.

I opened to 300 from the button and Claude (BB) just checked the K♣9♣6♥ flop. I have A♣2♦ — no pair but an overcard + the A♣ gives me runner‑runner nut‑club potential and some extra equity. Claude’s check after calling preflop indicates a fairly wide, often‑missed range. With position I want to apply pressure and fold out lots of unpaired hands while charging club/straight draws. A c‑bet of 300 (~45% of the 650 pot) achieves that: it’s large enough to force folds from Qx, Jx and small pairs, but keeps the pot manageable if called. Plan: call down or barre lightly if I pick up equity (A or club); fold to a large raise since I currently only have Ace‑high.

I have Qh5h on a 6h9cKc board. No pair, no direct flush draw (board has clubs, I have hearts), only a backdoor heart flush draw. Minni bets 300 into 650. I need ~24% equity to call. With just Q-high and a backdoor draw, I have almost no equity against Minni's range which connects well with this K-high board. Easy fold.

UTG in a 4-handed game with 7♥6♠ offsuit — a marginal, easily dominated hand. I’m out of position to three opponents (button, SB, BB), and the minimum open to 200 is small and will invite callers, squeezes or a shove from the short stack. Multiway pots OOP with 76o have low equity and high post‑flop difficulty, and with a large deep stack there’s no reason to spew chips with a weak holding. Fold and wait for a better spot or position to apply pressure.

I am on the button with 7s 4h, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have the opportunity to open-raise as a steal attempt, my stack is relatively short (under 30 BBs) compared to the blinds who have much larger stacks. 74 offsuit has very little playability post-flop, and risking chips on a low-equity bluff here is not strategic given the remaining hands in the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit my chips.

Heads up vs the big blind with J6o. This is a marginal hand but playable in a steal situation. I'll raise to try to take down the pot preflop or at least have the initiative if called. A standard raise to 250 applies pressure on Pro.

In the Big Blind facing a Small Blind open to 2.5bb. The pot odds are favorable (calling 150 into a pot of 350, requiring ~30% equity). King-Five offsuit is a reasonable defense hand in a Blind vs Blind scenario, especially since I will have position on the raiser post-flop. Folding would be too tight, and raising is unnecessary with this specific hand strength. I choose to call to see a flop in position.

Flopped completely nothing with J6 on a 5-5-2 board. However, this paired low board is excellent for a continuation bet - it likely missed Pro's range as well, and as the preflop aggressor I should have perceived range advantage. A small c-bet of around 1/3 pot should take this down often enough to be profitable.

I hold K5 on a 5-2-5 board, giving me trips with a strong kicker. This is a monster hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario. My opponent betting small (175 into 500) suggests a standard continuation bet, likely with a wide range including air, pocket pairs, or flush draws. Raising here would likely fold out his bluffs, which constitute a significant part of his range, while only getting action from hands that have good equity or are already strong. By calling, I keep his range wide, allowing him to potentially bluff on the turn or improve to a second-best hand (like hitting a pair with his hole cards) that can pay me off later. I have position and cover him, so playing a trapped pot is advantageous.

Turn brought 4d, completing no draws for me. I have jack high with no equity. Pro called my flop c-bet which shows some interest. Barreling again into a player who has shown strength would be risky - if I bet and get raised or called, I'm in a terrible spot. Checking allows me to control pot size and potentially check-fold if Pro bets, or see a free river if Pro checks behind.

I hold trip Fives with a King kicker (5h Ks) on a board of 5d-2s-5s-4d. This is a very strong hand. The pot is 850. Claude checked the turn. While the board has two flush draws (spades and diamonds) and some straight potential, betting here likely folds out all of Claude's air, which makes up a large portion of his blind-vs-blind range. By checking back, I under-represent my hand strength, potentially inducing him to bluff on the river or value bet a worse hand if he improves (e.g., hitting a pair). Given I am the chip leader and have position, I can afford to give a free card to try and extract a bet on the river rather than forcing a fold now. My hand is strong enough to catch bluffs on almost any river card.

River brought Th. I have J6 high - complete air. Pro checked behind on turn showing weakness, likely holding a marginal hand like Ace-high or a small pair. My check-check line doesn't tell a coherent bluff story for a river bet, and Pro could easily hero-call with any pair given the pot size. Checking and showing down is the best option - I might even beat some Ace-high that also missed.

I hold trip Fives with a King kicker on a board of 5-2-5-4-T. The river Ten likely improves some of Claude's range (hands like AT, KT, QT, JT, T9) that might now call a value bet. Claude checked the turn and river, indicating he likely doesn't have a monster (like a full house) and missed his flush draws. However, he might have a pocket pair or hit the Ten and decide to check-call. Checking back guarantees the win but misses value. A bet of around half-pot (450 into 850) targets his one-pair hands and might induce a call from Ace-high looking to bluff-catch on a board with missed draws. It is unlikely I am beat here, so a value bet is the optimal play.

Pro checked turn and now bets 450 into 1300 on the river. I have J6 high - complete air. The small bet sizing (about 1/3 pot) typically indicates value wanting a call. I lose to any pair, any ten, any 5, and most Ace/King high hands. The pot odds are about 25% (450 to win 1750), but J-high simply cannot beat Pro's value range and likely loses to many of their bluffs too. Easy fold.

I am currently the short stack with approximately 29 big blinds. We are approaching the final hands of the tournament (Hand 135/150). Holding Nine of Spades and Seven of Diamonds (97o) Under the Gun (UTG) in a 4-handed game is equivalent to being in the Cutoff. However, 97o is a very weak starting hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given my stack size relative to the others, I need to be more selective with my entries to ensure I don't bleed chips before finding a premium hand to double up with. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

J6o on the button with Flash folding. While not a premium hand, I have position and only the blinds to get through. A standard button steal raise is appropriate here - if I get 3-bet I can fold, but often I'll take down the blinds. Raising to 250 is a standard 2.5x open.

Claude opens from the Button to 250. As the Small Blind with J8s, I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win potentially 550+ if BB calls). My hand has reasonable playability (suited, connector gap) against a wide Button opening range. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, the cost is small relative to my stack (22,925), and folding suited broadway/connector type hands to a single raise from the button is generally too tight. I will call to see a flop.

Facing a standard button open to 250 with the small blind calling, I’m in the big blind with 7♥9♦ offsuit. The pot is 600 and it costs me 150 more to see the flop (150/(600+150) ≈ 20% pot‑odds). This is a multiway, out‑of‑position spot where 7‑9o has poor equity and is often dominated; it’s not a hand I want to 3‑bet with either (little fold equity and weak playability). With deep stacks I can wait for a better spot — fold and preserve the stack.

I hold Jc8c on a flop of 2d-9c-Td. I have flopped an open-ended straight draw (needing a 7 or Q) and a backdoor flush draw. This is a strong draw with 8 clean outs for the straight. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Claude on the Button), the standard line is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) is possible but checking keeps his bluffing range wide and allows me to evaluate his action. If he bets, I can comfortably call or consider a check-raise depending on sizing. If he checks, I take a free card to try and realize my equity.

Flop came 2d 9c Td. I have J6o - just jack-high with a gutshot to a straight (need Q). Pro checked to me. As the preflop aggressor with position, a small c-bet is standard here. This board has some connectivity that could hit Pro's calling range, but I can represent overpairs or top pair. A small bet around 1/3 pot should get folds from unpaired hands while keeping my risk low when called.

I hold Jc8c on a 2d-9c-Td flop, giving me an open-ended straight draw (needs a 7 or Q) and a backdoor flush draw. The pot is 600, and the opponent has bet 200 (1/3 pot). I need to call 200 to win a total pot of 1000, offering me 5:1 odds (or 16.7% equity required to break even on a pure call). With an OESD, I have 8 outs, giving me approximately 17% equity to hit on the turn and roughly 31% by the river. Since my direct equity is close to the pot odds and I have implied odds if I hit my straight, calling is clearly profitable. Raising is an option but risks blowing me off my equity if the opponent jams or 3-bets, and the small bet size makes calling very attractive to keep the opponent's bluffing range in.

I have turned a straight (8-9-T-J-Q) with my Jc8c on the 2d-9c-Td-Qc board. I hold the second nuts (only losing to K-J). As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking is standard to allow the pre-flop aggressor (Claude) to continue betting with his value hands (sets, two pairs, top pair) or semi-bluffs. The board is very coordinated, so leading might scare off weaker hands or isolate me against better ones. I will check with the intention of check-raising or calling depending on the bet size.

Turn brought the Qc giving me a gutshot straight draw (need K for KQJT9). I only have J-high currently. Pro has checked twice, showing weakness or potentially trapping. With just 4 outs to the straight and no real made hand, betting here as a bluff is risky - if called, I'm drawing thin. Better to take the free card and potentially bluff or hit the river. Check back for pot control.

I hold a Queen-high straight (8-9-T-J-Q) on a board of [2d 9c Td Qc 5d]. While my hand is strong, the river card (5d) completes the flush draw, and any King makes a higher straight. My opponent checked back the turn, which is consistent with pot-controlling a one-pair hand (like Qx or Tx) or taking a free card with a flush draw. Since the flush draw arrived and a higher straight is possible, leading out is risky; better hands call/raise, and worse hands likely fold on this scary board. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply against his one-pair hands or potentially induce a bluff from missed draws (like clubs). I will check and evaluate his action.

River is 5d completing three diamonds on board. I have J-high with no diamond. Pro has check-called flop and checked turn/river - showing a weak/passive line but still has something to call flop with. My J-high beats only complete air/missed draws. Betting as a bluff is risky since Pro already showed interest by calling flop - they likely have at least a small pair. Check back and hope to win against missed straight draws.

QhTh is a solid suited broadway hand in UTG. Good equity with straight and flush potential. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is appropriate here - consistent with my table image and gives me fold equity while building the pot with a playable hand.

I am on the Button with Jc 8h. Claude, the UTG player (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed), has opened to 250. J8 offsuit is a weak hand that is dominated by most of an opening range (e.g., AJ, KJ, QJ, 88+). Although I am the chip leader and have position, there is no incentive to play a pot with a hand this weak against an UTG raise, especially with a short stack in the Big Blind who could squeeze. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

2♠8♣ is one of the weakest starting hands and I’m out of position in the small blind facing an UTG open to 250. Calling 200 to see a flop would put me OOP in a bloated pot and requires ~33% equity just to break even — something 2-8o doesn’t offer versus an UTG raising range. A 3-bet bluff here is high-risk: Claude opens from early position (likely a strong-ish range) and the big blind is short and could shove or call, creating messy multiway scenarios. With a healthy stack (20,700) I should conserve chips for better spots. Fold and wait for a playable hand.

I am in the Big Blind holding 2h 5c, one of the weakest hands in poker. Claude, who is in the UTG position (equivalent to the Cutoff in a 4-handed game), has raised to 250. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (150 more to win a pot of 400), this hand has very little equity, poor playability, and no connectivity. My stack size is relatively short (28 BB), and I should look for better spots to commit chips rather than defending with such a marginal hand against an opening raise.

I am in the UTG position in a 4-handed game with [5d 4c], which is a very weak unsuited connector. While playing 4-handed requires wider ranges, 54o is at the bottom of playable hands and generally a fold from the first position. Additionally, the Small Blind (Flash) is short-stacked (approx 27 BB) and might shove over an open, and the Button (Minni) has a large stack that covers me, making it dangerous to play out of position. Given my healthy stack size relative to the blinds and the late stage of the tournament (Hand 137/150), there is no need to take unnecessary risks with such a weak holding. A fold is the standard and correct play here.

2♣5♥ is one of the weakest hands. Calling 100 into a 150 pot gives me a pot of 250 and requires ~40% equity to break even — far more than this hand can achieve, especially multiway. A min-raise as a bluff is too speculative here: Flash (SB, ~28bb) can defend or shove and Claude (BB) defends fairly often, so I’d face uncomfortable decisions postflop with no playability. I’m deep-stacked enough to wait for a better spot on the button; fold and preserve the stack.

I am in the Small Blind with QsTs, which is a premium broadway hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario. Since the action folded to me, I have the opportunity to take control of the pot. Raising is the standard play here to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot with initiative against Claude. Given my stack size of approximately 28 big blinds, a standard 3x raise is appropriate.

QTo in the BB facing a 300 raise from Flash (short stack with 2525). I need to call 200 into a 400 pot - getting 2:1. QTo is a solid hand to defend with these pot odds. Flash is short-stacked so a 3-bet might induce a shove, and I'm not sure I want to play for stacks. Calling is the standard play here.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board has a King, which favors my range over the Big Blind's calling range. I hold QsTs, giving me a gutshot straight draw to a Jack and a backdoor spade flush draw. A continuation bet here puts pressure on Claude's middle pairs and air, and allows me to take control of the pot. I will bet 250 into the 600 pot to maintain initiative.

I have QTo on a K87 board - completely missed. No pair, no straight draw, just two overcards that are dominated by the King on board. Flash bet 250 into 600 pot. I have no equity worth continuing with here. Easy fold.

4-handed, UTG with QcTs — a reasonable opening hand in short-handed play. Stacks are deep (207BB) so postflop playability and fold equity matter. I want to apply pressure, isolate the short BTN (Flash, ~31BB) and avoid giving cheap flops to the blinds. Standard 3xBB sizing (300) accomplishes that: builds the pot when I'm likely ahead of callers, uses blockers (Q and T) and preserves maneuverability postflop. If I face a shove from the short stack I can commit with good equity; versus a larger 3-bet I can re-evaluate. Open to 300.

I am on the button with a medium pocket pair (7d 7h) and a stack of 3,125 chips (roughly 31 big blinds). Minni, who has been an active player, raised to 300 from the UTG position in this 4-handed game. While a 3-bet is an option to try and take the pot immediately, calling allows me to play a pot in position and see a flop. Given my stack size relative to the others, I want to keep the pot manageable unless I flop a set. I will call the 300 to see the flop.

8c9d is a marginal hand facing an UTG raise and a caller. I'm out of position in the SB with a non-suited connector. While I'm getting 3:1 odds, 89o plays poorly multiway and OOP. Pro could still squeeze behind me. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Big Blind with [Ad 4d]. Minni raised to 300 from UTG, and Flash called on the Button. The pot is offering me excellent odds (calling 200 to win 750, or 3.75:1 direct odds, plus implies odds). A4s is a strong candidate for defending the Big Blind as it retains equity well against strong ranges and can make nut flushes or straights. Given Minni's UTG open indicates a stronger range and I am out of position, a 3-bet squeeze is risky and likely unnecessary. Calling closes the action and allows me to see a flop cheaply with a playable hand.

I have bottom pair (4s) with an Ace kicker on a [6d 8s 4c] flop. I am out of position against the UTG opener (Minni) and the Button caller (Flash). Leading out (donking) into two opponents on a board that likely connects well with the Button's calling range and leaves me vulnerable to the UTG's overpairs is unwise. My hand has some showdown value and backdoor potential (flush and straight), but it functions best as a bluff-catcher or a check-call candidate for small bets. I will check to the aggressor to see how the action develops.

I was the preflop aggressor and this flop (6♦ 8♠ 4♣) is very dry with almost no realistic draws. My Q♣T♠ has two overcards and a weak backdoor straight possibility but no pair or flush draw, so this is a textbook spot to continuation-bet to pick up the pot. The pot is 950 — I’ll size a c-bet to ~50% (475) to apply pressure on single-pair and overcard hands while keeping the pot controllable against the deep BB and the short-ish BTN. Plan: bet 475; if I face a large raise I’ll fold QTs (unless it’s a small raise where pot odds and reads justify calling), if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously when I don’t improve.
