I am holding pocket sevens on a 6-8-4 board, giving me an overpair to the bottom two cards and an open-ended straight draw (any 5 or 9). Minni's bet of 475 into a 950 pot is a standard continuation bet that could represent anything from overcards to a middle pair or a draw. While a raise is an option to exert pressure, calling in position is a more flexible play. This keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see a turn card. With ten outs to a straight or a set, and the possibility that my sevens are currently the best hand, I have significant equity. If the turn is favorable, I can then look to commit more chips.

On a flop of [6d 8s 4c], I hold bottom pair (4s) with an Ace kicker. The UTG player (Minni) c-bets 475 into a pot of 900, showing strength or a semi-bluff. Crucially, the Button (Flash), who is short-stacked, calls the bet. Flash's call indicates they likely have a piece of the board (8x, 6x, or pocket pair) or a strong draw, and they are essentially committed. Being out of position against two opponents who have shown interest in the pot puts me in a very weak spot. My bottom pair is likely behind, and my outs (Aces or Fours) could be dominated or give opponents better hands (e.g., if Minni has A8). Despite getting decent pot odds (~20%), the reverse implied odds and low equity realization make this a fold.

I turned top pair (T♠) with a Q♣ kicker after leading the hand. Flash is short (~23.5BB) and called my preflop open and flop c‑bet on a very dry 6♦‑8♠‑4♣ board, so his continuing range is wide (small pocket pairs, underpairs, broadways and floats). This is a clear value-bet spot: extract from worse one‑pair hands/floaters, deny a free river and pressure the short stack. I’ll size ~50% pot to accomplish that — big enough to get value and charge draws but not so large that an all‑in shove forces an easy fold. If he jams I’m prepared to call given the pot odds and my equity.

I am facing a second barrel from Minni on a board that has become increasingly dangerous. The Ten of Clubs on the turn is an overcard to my pocket sevens and likely hits a significant portion of an UTG raising range (like AT, KT, QT, or pocket Tens). While I have an open-ended straight draw (any 5 or 9 gives me a straight) and two remaining 7s for a set, providing about 10 outs (approx. 21.7% equity), the pot odds are not quite favorable enough to justify a call. I need roughly 25.6% equity to break even on the call alone (1000 to win 3900). Given my relatively short stack of 2,350 chips (23.5 BB), calling here would leave me with only 1,350 chips (13.5 BB), severely limiting my future options if I miss. With only 12 hands remaining in the tournament, it is more prudent to preserve my stack and look for a better spot to commit my chips, such as a pre-flop shove, rather than chasing a marginal draw against an aggressive opponent.

I am UTG with Td 4c, which is a very weak starting hand. Given my relatively short stack of 23.5 big blinds compared to the other players, I need to be selective about the hands I play as the tournament nears its end. There is no reason to enter the pot with such a weak hand from out of position.

3h6s is a very weak hand, even on the button. With only 12 hands remaining and a solid stack of 12,600, there's no need to open junk offsuit hands. Both blinds have significantly larger stacks and could easily 3-bet me. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s 3s, a very weak hand. The action has folded to me. My opponent in the Big Blind (Minni) is the other chip leader with a deep stack. Playing a weak hand out of position against a competent deep-stacked opponent is not profitable. Folding preserves my chip lead without unnecessary risk.
4-handed with Ah2d in UTG. Ace-high is playable in a short-handed game. Standard open raise to 250 to build the pot with my ace and apply pressure. If I get 3-bet, I can fold easily.

I am on the Button with Queen-Seven offsuit (Qc 7d). The player in the Under-the-Gun (UTG) position, Claude, has raised to 250. Q7o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by a UTG opening range (which typically includes AQ, KQ, QJ, pocket pairs, etc.). Given the stage of the tournament (Hand 140/150) and my healthy stack size, there is no reason to speculate with a marginal hand against an early position raiser. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I’m in the small blind with 7♣8♠ facing an UTG open to 250. This is a marginal, unsuited connector to defend out of position — calling 200 more will likely create a multiway pot (Flash in the BB is short but can still call) where my hand fares poorly. Pot odds for a cold-call are ~2:1 (need ~33% equity vs one opponent and more vs two), and versus an UTG opening range my equity with 7♣8♠ is well below that in a multiway spot. A light 3-bet to 400 is too loose here (I’d be out of position against the raiser and may still get action from the BB). With a healthy stack and lots of hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better, positionally-favored spot is the correct play — fold.

In Hand 140 of 150, I am the big blind with a stack of 2250 (22.5 BB). Claude raised to 250 from UTG. My hand, Jc 7h, is quite weak and does not play well out of position against a range that is typically tighter coming from UTG. Although the price to call is relatively small (150 more into a pot of 400), my short stack and the late stage of the tournament make it more prudent to wait for a better hand to commit my chips. Preserving my stack for the final few hands is more important than defending with a marginal, off-suit hand like J7.

I am in UTG position with 9d 3c, which is a very weak hand (9-3 offsuit). With three players left to act behind me, including a short stack in the Small Blind who might shove, and a solid stack on the Button, there is no strategic merit in playing this hand. Even though I am the chip leader, opening with trash from UTG is unnecessary and -EV. I will fold.

I have 6♦3♥ on the button — a very weak offsuit hand but great position. Pot is only 150 and the to-call is 100, so I can try to steal the blinds with a standard button open. Flash (SB) is short (≈22BB) and likely to fold or shove; Claude (BB) defends frequently but a modest-sized open will pick up fold equity and keep the pot manageable if called. I raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) to apply pressure and isolate; I’m prepared to fold to a shove or a sizable 3‑bet given how weak my hand is. If called, I’ll play cautiously in position and fold to significant aggression on unfavorable flops.

I am currently the short stack with 22 big blinds (2200 chips) and there are only 10 hands remaining in the tournament. Minni, the chip leader, has raised from the button, which is a common late-position steal attempt. [Qh, Ts] is a strong enough hand to defend my small blind against a wide button range. While a 3-bet shove is an option, 22 BBs is slightly deep for a pure shove with this hand. Calling allows me to see a flop and potentially double up if I hit a strong piece of the board, while preserving fold equity for future hands if the flop is unfavorable.

I have A3o in the BB facing a button raise from Minni to 300 and a call from Flash. I need 200 more to call into a pot of 700, giving me 3.5:1 odds. A3o is a reasonable defending hand from the BB at this price - I have an ace and decent implied odds if I hit. Folding would be too tight, and 3-betting with A3o isn't profitable. Calling is the standard play here.

On a board of Jd-7s-2c, my Qh-Ts has no direct connection other than two overcards to the lower board cards. As the short stack (19.5 BB) out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) and the big blind (Claude), checking is the most disciplined play. I want to see how the other players react to this dry board before committing any more of my limited chips. If it checks around, I get a free turn card to potentially improve to a top pair or a draw.

Flopped nothing on J72 rainbow - just ace high with a backdoor wheel draw. Flash checked, Minni (the preflop aggressor) is still behind me. No reason to bet here with no pair and no draw. Check and see what Minni does.

I raised preflop with position and picked up the pot heads-up odds, but on the flop (2♣ J♦ 7♠) I have 6♦3♥ — no pair, no relevant draw and very little showdown equity. The flop is relatively dry but there are many hands in Claude’s calling range that contain a Jack or a 7 (or at least an overcard) and he’s shown a tendency to defend and check‑call. Flash (SB) is short and might fold to pressure, but with two opponents a continuation bet here is low‑EV — I’m unlikely to get both to fold and risk creating a bloated pot with essentially zero equity. I’ll check to control the pot, see a free turn, and fold to meaningful aggression. If both opponents check again I can consider barreling a safe turn card, but for now pot control is the correct line.

The turn 5s is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. After the flop checked through, it is likely that neither Minni nor Claude has a strong hand. However, my Qh-Ts still only has overcard value. As the short stack with under 20 BB, I want to avoid bloating the pot with a pure bluff out of position. I will check again to try and see a free river card, which could give me the best hand if a Queen or Ten appears.

Turn brought 5s giving me a gutshot wheel draw (need a 4) along with ace high. Board now has two spades. Flash checked, Minni behind me. With just ace high and a weak gutshot, checking is correct - no reason to bet into two opponents with no made hand. If it checks through I might win at showdown, and I can fold to significant aggression.

I hold 6♦3♥ on 2♣ J♦ 7♠ 5♠ — no pair, no backdoor draw and essentially zero showdown equity. Pot is 900 and both opponents have checked the flop and turn. Flash is short (~1,950) and may fold to pressure, but Claude (~12,450) is a frequent defender who will call small bluffs. Betting into two opponents here is low EV: I’ll likely get called and be committed to a bloated pot with no real chance to improve. I checked the flop to control size and will do the same on the turn to preserve my stack and keep options for the river. Plan: fold to any meaningful turn shove/bet; if both check to the river I’ll re-evaluate and only bluff a very favorable scare card. Optimal line now is pot control — check.

The board has been checked through on both the flop and the turn, suggesting that no player has a significant piece of this dry board. However, with three players still in the hand, my Queen-high is unlikely to be the best hand at showdown if I check, but it also lacks enough strength to value bet. As the short stack, I must be careful not to waste chips on a pure bluff into two opponents, especially since a small pair or even Ace-high will likely call a river bet in a checked-down pot. Checking is the most prudent play to see if I can win at showdown for free, while being prepared to fold if there is any significant betting action.

The 4d on the river completed my wheel straight (A-2-3-4-5)! I now have the nuts. Flash checked, pot is 900. I should value bet here. Flash has only 1950 behind, Minni has a big stack. I'll bet around 2/3 pot to extract value from any Jack or pair that might call.

Board: 2♣ J♦ 7♠ 5♠ 4♦. I hold 6♦3♥ — no pair, no meaningful blocker. Pot is 1500 and Claude leads for 600. Calling 600 to win a total pot of 2700 requires ≈22% equity; my actual showdown equity versus Claude’s river-betting range (Jx, 7x, 5x, pocket pairs and some thin value) is essentially zero. Flash (SB, ~1,950) already checked and could still act behind if I call or raise, so calling risks getting a second caller. Raising to the minimum (1200 total) as a bluff is very thin — I have no good blockers and Claude is a frequent caller who will call small bluffs; Flash could also shove and put me in a horrible spot. I planned to check for pot control and fold to meaningful river aggression; Claude’s river stab is exactly that. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

The board has been checked through on the flop and turn, indicating significant weakness from all players. On the river, the board is J-7-2-5-4. Claude, in the Big Blind, has now bet 600 into a 900 pot. While this could be a 'steal' attempt given the previous checks, my Queen-high (Qh-Ts) is extremely weak. I lose to any pair (even a pair of 2s or 4s) and I lose to any Ace-high. Since I am the short stack with only 10 hands remaining in the tournament, I cannot justify calling 30% of my stack with a hand that loses to nearly all of Claude's potential bluffing range (like Ace-high) and all of his value range. Folding is the most disciplined move to preserve my remaining 19.5 big blinds.

4-handed table — ranges should be wider than a full ring, but UTG is still a relatively early seat. J♠9♦ is marginal from UTG but has decent playability (two-way straight potential and a jack blocker). I want to take the initiative and deny the blinds good odds to see cheap flops, so I’ll open to a standard 3x BB sizing (300). 3x accomplishes: (1) isolates the short-stacked Button, (2) folds out weaker live hands, and (3) gives us a manageable pot with position/discretion postflop. If I face a shove/3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (vs a tight shove I’ll fold; vs a very wide shove I may call), but as the opener this is the right aggressive, exploitative line to maximize expected value here.

I am currently the short stack with approximately 19.5 big blinds. Hand 142 out of 150 means the tournament is nearing its end. Minni has opened from UTG (in this 4-handed game, essentially the cutoff position) for 300. While King-Four suited on the button is a tempting hand to play in position, calling here commits about 15% of my stack. Given that Minni's opening range from UTG is likely to dominate my weak kicker and I'm looking for a better spot to double up or pick up blinds (ideally with an Ace or a pocket pair), folding is the most prudent play to preserve my remaining chips for the final few hands.

Holding 3c7h - a very weak offsuit hand. Facing a UTG raise from Minni who has been aggressive. This hand has poor playability and low equity against a raising range. Not worth 250 chips to defend with such a marginal holding. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind facing a standard opening raise to 300 from Minni (UTG). The pot is 450 (300 + 50 SB + 100 BB), and I need to call 200. This offers me pot odds of roughly 30.7%. My hand, Jd7d, is a suited gapper with speculative potential. Against a typical UTG opening range, this hand has approximately 33-35% equity, which technically clears the direct pot odds hurdle, although being out of position (OOP) against the other big stack makes realizing that equity harder. However, we are very deep-stacked (over 200 BBs effective), which increases the implied odds if I flop a strong draw or two pair. Looking at my history (e.g., Hand #24, Hand #34), I have consistently defended the Big Blind against Minni's 300 chip raises with similar speculative holdings (suited connectors, small pairs, offsuit kings). The cost to call (200 chips) is less than 1% of my stack, making this a low-risk defense to potentially crack a strong hand. I will call and evaluate the flop, planning to check-fold if I miss.

I have flopped middle pair (sevens) and a gutshot straight draw on a wet Ten-Eight-Seven board. Being out of position against the other big stack (Minni), who raised pre-flop from UTG, caution is required. This board texture hits a standard UTG opening range quite hard (sets, overpairs, top pair, straight draws). Leading out would likely only get called by better hands or strong draws. Checking is the standard line to control the pot size and evaluate Minni's action. If Minni bets, I will evaluate based on sizing; if Minni checks, I get a free turn to potentially improve my hand.

I have J♠9♦ on T♣7♠8♣ — that’s already a made straight (7-8-9-10-J). Against the BB’s call preflop range this is very strong: almost nothing currently beats me except Q9 combinations that make a Q-high straight, and a runner-runner/club-runout for someone holding two clubs. Pro out of the BB has been a fairly sticky caller, so I should extract value and charge club/straight-draw combos rather than slowplay. I’ll bet for value and to deny correct odds to two-club combos — roughly 2/3 pot is appropriate here to get called by worse (pairs, single-card straight/flush draws, broadways) while still leaving room to navigate turn decisions. If Pro check-raises big I’ll re-evaluate (it would often indicate the rare Q9 or a very polarized line), but against a simple call I plan to continue extracting on later streets unless a dangerous card (Q or a club) lands.

I hold middle pair and a gutshot straight draw (need a 9) on a coordinated T-8-7 board. I am getting favorable pot odds (calling 400 to win 1450, approx 27% required equity) and the stacks are very deep, providing high implied odds if I hit my straight or trips. While Minni's UTG range connects well with this board, folding a pair plus a draw for one bet is too weak. I will call to see the turn, planning to re-evaluate if facing significant aggression on later streets without improvement.

On the turn, the board is [Tc 7s 8c Qd]. I hold [7d Jd], giving me third pair and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Nine). The Queen is an overcard that likely connects well with Minni's UTG opening range (hands like AQ, KQ, QJs, QT, or even J9 for the straight). As the Big Blind out of position against the only other deep stack in the tournament (Minni has ~20k, I have ~22k), I must be cautious. Leading out would not achieve much as better hands call and worse hands fold. My goal is to control the pot size and hopefully see a cheap river or showdown. Therefore, I will check to the aggressor.

Turn Qd is a dangerous card — on the flop I had the nut straight (7-8-9-10-J), but the Q gives any opponent holding a 9 a Q-high straight that now beats me. Pro’s line (BB call preflop, flop check-call of my 400) is sticky and contains a decent number of single-9 combos; his passive check on the turn looks like a classic slowplay/check-to-induced-bet line if he turned that higher straight. Betting here risks getting check‑raised into a large pot by the rare but crushing 9x hands (or bloated by a raise from a made Qx), so pot‑control is correct. I’ll check and keep the pot small; on the river I’ll re-evaluate — value-bet blanks, be cautious vs heavy action, fold to large aggression if the river completes obvious beaters (9 or club/flush), and call reasonable bets from hands I beat (Qx, single pairs, draws).

The river brings the Queen of Clubs, pairing the board and completing a possible flush draw. The final board is [Tc 7s 8c Qd Qc]. My hand [7d Jd] is now Two Pair (Queens and Sevens), but this is a very weak holding given the board texture; any opponent holding an 8, T, Q, overpair, or flush beats me. Minni checked back the turn, suggesting they might not have a strong Queen or Straight, but they could easily have a marginal made hand like Tx or 8x that still beats my 7s. Betting here has no value as worse hands (like missed AK) will fold and better hands will call. Being out of position against the other big stack, I will check with the intention of giving up if facing a significant bet, or hoping to see a cheap showdown against Ace-high.

Check. I currently hold the nut-turned straight (7-8-9-10-J) but the board pairing Qd/Qc on turn/river creates a new, narrow class of hands that now beat me (full houses/quads — e.g. QQ, Q10, TT, 77, 88, or pocket Qs). Pro’s line (BB call pre, check-call my 400 on the flop, then passive checks on turn and river) is very passive and doesn’t look like the line of someone with a full house/quads. Betting here would extract from some worse pairs but also opens me to being check‑raised by the rare crushing hands and unnecessarily bloats the pot when my hand only loses to a small subset. With pot control and a high showdown equity, the +EV line is to check and take it to showdown. If Pro bets a small amount I will call for value; I will respect a large raise and fold to avoid committing with a hand that can be crushed.

I am UTG with King-Deuce offsuit (K2o) and a stack of 1950 (19.5 BB). In a 4-handed game, K2o is a very weak starting hand, especially from the first position to act. The three opponents remaining behind me (Claude, Pro, and Minni) all have significantly larger stacks, which gives them the leverage to either call or 3-bet me frequently. Given that there are only 8 hands left in the tournament and my stack is relatively healthy enough to wait for a better spot, folding this hand is the standard and most prudent play to avoid unnecessary chip loss.

6c2d is one of the worst hands in poker. Even on the button with only blinds to act, this hand has virtually no playability postflop. With 13,300 chips and only 8 hands remaining, there's no need to take unnecessary risks with garbage. Folding and waiting for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 4h, a very weak offsuit hand. The action has folded to me. The Big Blind (Minni) has a stack size very similar to mine, and we are both the chip leaders. Playing a trash hand out of position against a competent big stack is not profitable, even for just 50 chips to call. The goal is to preserve my stack lead. Folding is the correct decision.

Q6 suited from UTG is a marginal hand. While it has some suited value, from early position it's too weak to open. Poor connectivity, and will often be dominated by better queens. With 7 hands left in the tournament and a healthy 133BB stack, no need to take unnecessary risks with a weak holding from the worst position.

I am on the Button with Ac2c (Ace-Deuce suited). UTG has folded. I have a deep stack (224BB), while the Big Blind is relatively short (18.5BB). A2s is a standard opening hand from the Button due to its blocker potential, flush capabilities, and the positional advantage. I will open-raise to 2.5BB (250) to apply pressure on the blinds and attempt to take the pot down pre-flop or play comfortably in position.

I have 6♦4♥ in the small blind facing a button open to 250 (2.5x). This is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand that performs poorly out of position. The pot is 400 and calling 200 to see a flop requires roughly 33% equity (200/(400+200)). Versus a typical button opening range my hand’s equity is well below that and is frequently dominated; 3-betting with this holding is also unjustified. With a short stack (Flash) behind there’s a chance of getting squeezed or forced into tough post‑flop spots. Fold and preserve the healthy stack for better spots.

With a stack of 1,850 chips (18.5 BB) and holding a very weak hand like T3o in the Big Blind, I need to be selective about which hands I use to defend or shove. Pro (the chip leader) raised from the Button, which is a wide range, but T3o is too weak to call out of position, even getting a decent price (150 to call into a pot of 400). Since there are only a few hands left in the tournament, I should preserve my stack for a better spot to double up rather than bleeding chips with garbage hands.

I am in the UTG position in a 4-handed game with Queen-Seven suited (Qc 7c). While suited queens have some playability, Q7s is generally below the standard opening threshold from the first position, even in a short-handed game. With a short-stacked player in the Small Blind who might be looking for a shove spot and a large stack on the Button who can apply pressure, opening this marginal hand carries unnecessary risk. Being the chip leader (marginally), preserving my stack is preferable to opening loose hands out of position. I will fold.

I’m on the button with Tc5d (marginal unsuited) facing only the blinds. With a deep effective stack but a relatively short SB (18bb) and a mid/deep BB, this is a standard steal opportunity — folding loses position and calling gives a multiway flop with a weak hand. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to apply pressure and take the initiative; this size is standard at the table, gives good fold equity, and keeps pot control if called. If I face a strong 3-bet or an SB shove I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to large aggression from deep stacks, maybe call a short-stack shove only with a read), but plan to play postflop in position and c-bet favorable flops.

I am currently the short stack with 1,800 chips (18 BB) at Hand 145/150. Minni, the chip leader on the button, has opened for 300. In a 4-max game with 18 BBs, Ace-Eight offsuit is a strong enough hand to three-bet shove from the small blind against a button open. Minni has been opening very wide, and a shove exerts maximum pressure on their range. If Minni folds, I increase my stack by 25% (450 chips) without a showdown. If called, I likely have around 40-50% equity against a calling range, providing a good opportunity to double up and climb the leaderboard before the tournament ends in 5 hands. Calling and playing out of position with a shallow stack is suboptimal.

Flash has 3-bet to 1800 with only 50 chips behind - essentially all-in. A6o is a marginal hand against a 3-bet range. I need to call 1700 into a 2200 pot (getting ~1.3:1 odds, needing ~43% equity). Against Flash's committed 3-bet range (likely strong hands like JJ+, AK, AQ), A6o only has about 25-30% equity. I'm likely dominated by better Ax hands. Not getting the right price to call.

Flash (SB) has effectively shoved to 1,800 (only ~50 behind) after my 300 open — this is an isolate situation heads-up. I need to put in 1,500 more (1800 total). Pot odds are favorable (I’m getting roughly the price to call vs an all-in), and against a short-stack shove the SB’s range will be wide enough that T♣5♦ has reasonable showdown equity. A tiny reraise to 1,850 gains nothing (he’s all-in/near-all-in) and folding is too conservative here given the price and position. I’ll call the 1,500 (1800 total) to see the showdown.

I have only 50 chips remaining in my stack after my large pre-flop raise which was called by Minni. The pot is currently 3,700 chips. With Ace-high on a board of King-Jack-Seven, I have very little equity if I'm behind, but I am committed to the pot regardless of the board texture. Since I am first to act on the flop and have less than one big blind remaining, I must put my last chips into the pot. There is no strategic value in checking, as any bet from Minni would force me to call with my last 50 chips anyway.

I already committed 1,800 chips preflop to call Flash's near-all-in. He posts a tiny 50 into a 3,750 pot on a Jc-Kd-7h board — the price to see the turn is negligible (~1.3%). With T♣5♦ I have no pair and little fold equity, so folding now would be overly conservative given the pot odds and my committed chips. Raising is pointless here (Flash is effectively short/all-in and I have a weak hand). I’ll call the 50 to see the turn (total committed = 1,850).

I have 2♠8♦ — one of the worst starting hands (unsuited, unconnected). I'm UTG in a 4‑handed game, out of position to the Button/SB/BB, and the table has shown frequent preflop aggression (many opens/3‑bets). Calling 100 or opening with this hand is unjustified: it will be dominated often and has negligible postflop playability or implied odds. With a healthy stack I should preserve chips for better spots. Fold.

I am on the button with King-Six suited in a 4-handed game. With only 5 hands remaining in the tournament and a stack of 38 big blinds, I need to look for opportunities to pick up blinds and antes or play pots in position. K6s is a standard opening hand from the button. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to pressure the blinds.

Holding 9d4d in the SB facing a button raise to 300. This is a weak suited hand that doesn't play well out of position. Flash has a short stack (3500) so could be stealing, but 94s is too weak to defend profitably here. Need to call 250 into 450 pot, but even with decent pot odds, this hand has poor playability OOP. Easy fold.

The tournament is in its final stages (Hand 146/150), and I hold the chip lead. I am facing a 3BB raise from the Button (Flash), who is the short stack with about 35BB. My hand, King-Five of Clubs, is a suited King, which is a standard defense from the Big Blind against a Button open due to good playability and equity against a wide range. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win 450), requiring only about 31% equity, which K5s easily meets. Calling allows me to realize equity without bloating the pot out of position against a range that includes strong hands, while keeping Flash's range wide.

I have flopped two pair (Kings and Fives) on a board of Ks-9s-5d. This is a very strong hand, well ahead of my opponent's range, which includes many one-pair hands, flush draws, and pure bluffs. My opponent, Flash, is the pre-flop aggressor and is currently the short stack. As the out-of-position player, the standard and most effective play is to check to the raiser. This allows Flash to make a continuation bet with his strong hands (which I crush), his draws (which I want to charge but can check-raise or call), and his bluffs (which would fold to a lead). Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from hands that beat me or strong draws, while folding out his air range that might bluff. Given the board texture has a flush draw, I will likely check-raise if he bets to build the pot and protect against draws, or check-call to trap if the bet sizing suggests he is weak, but the first step is to check.

I have top pair with a King on a King-high board. After Pro checked to me, a continuation bet is standard to extract value from weaker pairs like 9x or 5x, as well as various draws like the spade flush draw or straight draws. A bet of 350 into the 650 pot (roughly 54%) is a solid sizing to protect my hand while keeping the pot manageable for my 35 BB stack.

I have flopped two pair (Kings and Fives) on a board with a flush draw. My opponent is the short stack and has made a continuation bet. With a strong but vulnerable hand on a wet board, and a low Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR), my goal is to build the pot immediately to extract value from Top Pair (Kx) and charge flush draws. A check-raise here is the most effective line to deny equity and commit the opponent's stack. Raising to 1100 sets up a turn shove or forces a decision for his remaining chips.

I have top pair on a King-high board in a 4-handed game, which is a strong position. Pro's check-raise from the Big Blind is significant and could represent a set (99, 55), two-pair (K9), or a strong draw like a nut flush draw (AsXs). However, with my stack at 31 big blinds and only five hands remaining in the tournament, I cannot easily fold top pair to a single raise on the flop. A shove would be overplaying my hand given the weak kicker (6), as I would mostly be called by better Kings or hands that have me crushed. Calling the 750 (to match the 1100 raise) allows me to see a turn card and re-evaluate. If the turn is a non-spade blank, I can decide whether to commit the rest of my stack based on his turn action. Folding here would be too exploitable against an aggressive chip leader who likely uses his stack to pressure opponents.

Current situation: Hand 146/150. I hold Two Pair (Kings and Fives) on a wet board (Ks-9s-5d-Td). The pot is 2,850 and my opponent, Flash, has only 2,400 chips remaining, creating an SPR of less than 1. Flash called my check-raise on the flop, indicating a strong range likely consisting of Top Pair (AK, KQ, KJ), strong draws, or perhaps a set/two pair. The turn Ten brings a second flush draw and completes a straight for QJ, but I remain ahead of most of his range (AK, KQ, KJ, flush draws). Checking here is risky as it allows draws to realize equity for free on a board where many river cards are bad for me. Furthermore, Flash might check back made hands like KQ/KJ for pot control. Betting puts his tournament life at risk and extracts maximum value from worse hands while denying equity to draws. Given the low SPR, shoving (betting his effective stack) is the optimal play to maximize my expected value and stack size.

On the turn, the Ten of Diamonds is a highly problematic card for my hand. While I have top pair with King-Six, this board has become extremely coordinated. The Ten completes a straight for Queen-Jack and creates two-pair possibilities for hands like King-Ten or Ten-Nine, which the Big Blind could certainly have in their range. Pro's line of check-raising the flop and then leading for a full-stack shove on the turn represents extreme strength and is rarely a total bluff. Even if Pro is semi-bluffing with a nut flush draw (spades), they still have significant equity, and my weak kicker (six) means I lose to every other King in their value range. Given the high probability that I am either far behind or drawing dead, and with the tournament nearing its final hands, I will fold to preserve my remaining 24 big blinds and look for a more favorable spot to commit my chips.

I am currently the short stack with 2400 chips (24 BB) and there are only four hands remaining in the tournament. King-Jack suited is a very strong hand in a 4-handed game and is well within my opening range from under-the-gun. My goal is to accumulate chips to improve my final standing. I will open with my standard 3x raise to 300.

K4o is a weak hand facing an UTG raise. Flash has only 2100 chips remaining, so they're committed to their raise. K4o plays poorly post-flop and isn't strong enough to call or 3-bet against UTG range. With only 4 hands left and a healthy stack of 13150, no need to take marginal spots. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 6h. The UTG player (Flash) has raised to 300. While I am the chip leader with a deep stack, Flash is playing a short stack of about 21 big blinds. Speculative hands like suited one-gappers rely heavily on implied odds to be profitable, but Flash's short stack limits my potential winnings to about 8:1, which is insufficient for this hand type, especially considering I would be playing out of position against a UTG opening range. Preserving my chip lead against Minni is a priority, and avoiding marginal spots out of position is the correct strategic adjustment.

I'm in the big blind with J♥6♣ facing a standard 3bb open to 300 from a short-stacked opener (Flash ~2,100). The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 more to call (300 total). Min re-raise would be to 500. J6o is a marginal hand — too weak to 3-bet light here because a raise will often induce a shove from his ~21bb stack and I'd be forced to call a shove with a mediocre hand. Folding is too nitty versus a single raiser. Calling keeps the pot manageable, gives me decent immediate pot odds (450:200 ≈ 2.25:1, needing ~31% equity) and lets me realize equity and use postflop skill heads-up. I'll call to see a flop and proceed cautiously.

Dry, low rainbow flop 7♠-3♦-2♣; I hold J♥6♣ — no pair, no meaningful backdoor draw and very little showdown value. Flash is a short-stacked UTG opener who will c-bet wide but is also capable of shoving or betting polarised ranges. I want pot control and information: checking keeps the pot small, lets him bluff/c-bet so I can call small bets and fold to large aggression, and avoids building a pot OOP with a marginal hand. Plan: check — call small continuation bets (roughly ≤~1/2 pot) and fold to big bets/shoves; if he checks back I take a free card and reevaluate on the turn.

The flop is extremely dry (7-3-2 rainbow) and should favor my opening range from UTG much more than Minni's defending range in the Big Blind. Since Minni checked, I will place a standard continuation bet of approximately 40% of the pot to take down the 650 chips. I have two overcards and a backdoor club draw for equity if the bet is called.

I’m OOP in the BB with J♥6♣ on a dry 7♠-3♦-2♣ flop — no pair, no meaningful draw and limited showdown value. Flash (UTG, short stack ~1.85k) opened and now c-bet 250 into a 900 pot (~28% pot). This is a small c-bet against a wide, short-stacked opener, so calling is the +EV line: I get to see a turn for a relatively small price and the pot odds are strong (250 to win 1,150 → ~21.7% break-even). Raising would be reckless (I have little fold equity and he can shove), and folding to such a small barrel is too nitty. Plan: call the 250, keep the pot controlled, and fold to a significant raise/shove; if he checks the turn I can take a free card and re-evaluate on any improvement or further aggression.

Turn: 7♠-3♦-2♣-T♠; I hold J♥6♣ with no pair and virtually no draw. Pot is 1,150 and Flash is a short-stacked UTG opener (~1,850) who c-bet small on the flop then checked the turn — a sign of weakness or pot control. Betting here would gain some fold equity but risks getting shoved by a short stack and unnecessarily bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Best EV line is pot control: check back, take a free river, then call small river bets or fold to large aggression. Action: check to preserve stack and keep options on the river.

The turn Ten of Spades is a somewhat neutral card, though it potentially improves some of Minni's calling range (like AT, JT, or T9). I still have two overcards with my King-Jack, giving me 6 outs to a likely best hand. However, my stack is quite short (18.5 BB remaining), and I need to be careful about committing more chips to a hand without a made pair. By checking behind, I take a free card to try and hit a King or a Jack on the river and avoid getting check-raised off my equity. This preserves my stack for the final three hands of the tournament while still giving me a chance to win the pot if the river is favorable or if King-high holds up against a missed draw.

Board: 7♠-3♦-2♣-T♠-Q♥. I hold J♥6♣ — no pair, negligible showdown value. Villain (Flash) is a short-stacked UTG opener who c-bet the flop small then checked the turn: that line screams wide/weak range or a missed c-bet. Checking behind guarantees a losing showdown most of the time; a small river probe can often pick up the pot versus his missed hands. I want fold equity while minimizing downside if he jams. Betting ~1/3–40% pot (400 into 1,150) is a credible steal: it folds lots of air/ace-highs and small underpairs, but if he raises all-in I’ll fold (I have almost no equity vs his continuing range). If he calls a small bet I can live with losing a modest amount — if he folds I scoop the full pot. Therefore I make a small bluff: bet 400 and fold to a shove.

On a board of 7-3-2-T-Q, my King-high (Kc Jc) has almost no showdown value. Minni called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, which suggests she has a piece of the board (like a 7 or a pocket pair) or an Ace-high float. When I checked the turn, I signaled weakness, and her lead for 400 on the river is a small bet that could be a thin value bet with a marginal pair or a blocking bet. However, I lose to any pair and any Ace-high. Given that I have a very short stack and there are only three hands remaining in the tournament, making a hero call with King-high is too risky. It is better to fold and preserve my 18.5 BB for a better spot in the final few hands.

KJs is a strong hand from UTG - suited broadway cards with good playability. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x) is appropriate. With 3 hands left, I should be playing premium hands like this aggressively.

I am on the Button with 7s 8h, an unsuited connector. Claude has opened from UTG to 250 (2.5 BB). An UTG opening range is typically strong (pairs, big aces, broadways), against which 87o has very poor equity (~30-35%). With only 3 hands left in the tournament and a large stack, there is no incentive to speculate with a weak hand against an early position raiser, especially with players still behind in the blinds. The standard and prudent play is to fold.

Claude opened UTG to 250 (2.5x BB) — an early-position raise that represents a fairly strong range. I hold T9o (Td9c): a marginal, speculative hand that plays much better in position. If I call I’ll be out of position on every post‑flop street and likely face either a CB from Claude or a troublesome shove/call from the very short-stacked BB (Flash, 1,750) — both scenarios are undesirable. 3-betting as a bluff from the SB here is also unattractive: I’m OOP, the opener is in position postflop relative to the button (who already folded), and Claude has shown aggression and willingness to inflate pots. The extra 200 to continue isn’t worth the poor postflop dynamics and flip equity against an UTG range, so I’ll preserve my deep stack for a better spot and fold.

With only three hands remaining in the tournament and my stack sitting at 1,750 chips (17.5 BB), I am significantly shorter than my three opponents. Claude has been playing very aggressively, raising frequently from all positions. While A3o is a reasonable hand to defend the Big Blind with for a small amount (150 to call into a 400 pot), doing so out of position with a short stack often leads to difficult post-flop scenarios where I'll be forced to check-fold and lose more of my remaining equity. Shoving here is also risky as Claude's UTG range is likely stronger than my A3o, and he has the stack size to call me comfortably. Given that I will be in the Small Blind and on the Button in the final two hands, I'll fold here to preserve my stack and look for a more favorable situation or position to commit my chips.

I am in UTG position with 7s 3d, which is an extremely weak hand (7-3 offsuit). With only 2 hands remaining in the tournament (Hand 149/150) and holding the chip lead, there is absolutely no strategic merit to playing this hand. It has negative expected value to open or call. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately to conserve chips.

I’m on the button with J-To (decent hand in position) and both blinds are behind: Flash is short (~17bb) and Claude is a big but active stack. With the table near the end and a big effective stack, I want to take initiative and try to steal the blinds while preserving fold equity. A raise to 400 is a standard, assertive sizing (4x the BB) — large enough to fold out a lot of BB defense and force the short SB into a tough decision (he may shove, which I can call with decent equity), but not so large that I overcommit out of position. Plan: call a short-stack shove (wide shove range = acceptable equity), but fold to a sizable 3-bet from Claude and proceed postflop aggressively if called.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit, a premium hand, and have a short stack of 1,700 chips (17 big blinds) with only two hands remaining in the tournament. Minni, as the chip leader on the button, is likely opening a wide range of hands. Given my stack size and the strength of my hand, a three-bet shove is the most mathematically sound play to either take down the pot immediately or double up and maximize my final tournament standing.

I have 8d4s, a very weak hand. Facing a 3-bet to 1700 from Flash (who is essentially all-in with only 50 behind), I need to call 1600. This is a clear fold - 84o has no business calling a 3-bet. Even if Flash is shoving light due to their short stack, my hand has terrible equity and no playability.

Flash (SB) made a large reraise to 1,700 after my open to 400 on the button. I hold Jh–Ts (J‑T offsuit). Calling requires 1,300 more for a total commitment of 1,700. The current pot is 2,200, so I need roughly 1300/(2200+1300) ≈ 37% equity to break even. Versus a short‑stack shove range (wide: pairs, Ax, broadways, suited connectors), JTo has roughly mid‑40s% equity, so this is a +EV call. The risk (1,300) is only ~6% of my ~20,575 stack, so I’m not jeopardizing my tournament life for a good equity spot. A 4‑bet shove is unnecessary here — calling isolates the short stack, keeps position, and lets me realize my equity postflop. I will call and proceed to the flop with position.

I have 50 chips left in a 3,500 pot after my pre-flop three-bet. With Ace-King on a 9-4-2 board, I have two overcards and am essentially all-in. I will bet my remaining 50 chips now to commit my stack and attempt to win the pot. Given the tournament situation and the size of the pot, I have no choice but to commit my last chips and hope to catch an Ace or King if I am currently behind.

Flash was effectively all‑in preflop for 1,700 and I called. Flop comes 2♠‑4♣‑9♣ and he posts a tiny bet of 50 into a ~3,550 pot. Calling 50 is trivial — pot odds ≈ 50/3600 ≈ 1.4% — and my J‑T has reasonable showdown equity against a short‑stack shove range (pairs, broadways, Axs, suited connectors). Raising is pointless (he’s all‑in / no fold equity to gain) and folding after already investing 1,700 would be conceding value. I will call the small bet to realize equity and see the runout. Total committed after this call = 1,750.

4-handed game so open-raising ranges should be much wider than full-ring. K8 offsuit (Kh8s) is marginal UTG in a full ring but perfectly acceptable to open in a 4‑handed pot — it plays well postflop with initiative. I want to seize the betting lead, get fold equity against the Button and the blinds, and avoid limping which surrenders initiative. Raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) — big enough to apply pressure but small enough to keep the pot manageable if called. Plan: c-bet favorable flops; fold to a large 3-bet from Pro/Claude (dominated), and only call a shove from Flash after quickly evaluating his shove range and pot odds.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I currently have 3,600 chips, which puts me in 4th place. My goal is to maximize my stack size. Minni (UTG/CO) has raised to 300. Ace-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand to play against an UTG raise, even in a 4-handed game. While Minni is active, my priority on the last hand is to protect my remaining chips unless I have a clearly profitable opportunity to increase them. Calling 300 with A7o out of position against the original raiser or facing potential squeezes from the blinds is not ideal. Since I cannot realistically overtake the next player (Claude at ~13k) even with a double-up, and the hand is marginal, I will fold to preserve my current stack.

Last hand of the tournament. I have Q5o in the small blind facing a UTG raise from Minni. Q5o is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. Against an UTG raise, I'm likely dominated or significantly behind. While I'm getting decent pot odds (~1.8:1), the hand has poor playability postflop and calling here would be -EV. Easy fold.

This is the final hand of the tournament (#150/150). I am in the Big Blind with a pair of Threes (3d 3h). Minni raised to 300 from UTG, and it folded to me. The pot is currently 450 (300 + 50 + 100). I need to call 200 more chips to see a flop. My stack is very deep (23,875), so calling 200 represents less than 1% of my stack. Pocket threes play well as a set-mining hand; if I flop a set, I have huge upside potential to increase my stack significantly. If I miss the flop, I can easily fold to aggression without significant loss. The pot odds (2.25:1) are sufficient for a call with a pocket pair. Given the goal is to maximize stack size, taking a low-risk, high-reward line by calling and trying to hit a set is the optimal play.

This is the final hand (#150) of the tournament. I hold Pocket Threes (3d 3h) and called a pre-flop raise from Minni (UTG) to set mine. The flop comes 9h Js 5d. I have missed my set and now hold an underpair to the board. Minni's range as the UTG raiser connects reasonably well with this board (sets, overpairs, top pair Jacks), and even her missed overcards (AK, AQ) are ahead of my threes. Leading out (donk betting) accomplishes nothing as better hands will call and worse hands will fold. My best strategy is to check to the aggressor. If Minni bets, I will likely fold to preserve my stack, as the chances of improving are minimal (2 outs). If she checks, I get to see a free turn.

Heads‑up against the BB and I opened preflop, so I have the initiative. The flop (9h Js 5d) is dry and favors a continuation bet — many of Pro’s calling hands preflop (weak broadways, small pairs, suited connectors) miss this board and will fold to pressure. My hand K8s has one overcard but no made pair and no realistic flush/straight draw, so my fold equity is my primary asset. I’ll c‑bet about half the pot to take it down now or keep the pot manageable if called. Bet 325 into 650 (~50% pot). Plan: fold to a large check‑raise/shove that strongly represents Jx/sets; if called, re‑evaluate the turn and only continue with improvement or clear equity.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I called pre-flop with Pocket Threes solely to set mine. The flop came J-9-5, missing my set completely. I now hold an underpair to the board. Minni, who raised UTG, has continued with a bet of 325. Her range likely crushes my pair of threes (connects well with Jacks or Nines, or holds overpairs). Even her bluffs (like AK/AQ) have significant equity or are ahead. With only 2 outs to improve to a set and very low equity against her range, continuing is negative expected value. Preserving my stack by folding is the correct play to maximize my final chip count.
